| House Price Prediction | Tabular | Regression | Linear Regression, XGBoost | MSE, RMSE, $R^2$ |
| Credit Card Fraud | Transactional Streams | Anomaly Detection | Isolation Forest, Autoencoder | Recall, F1, AUC-PR |
| Customer Segmentation | Tabular, behavioral | Clustering | K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical | Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin Index |
| Self-Driving Cars | Camera + LiDAR + GPS | RL + Regression + CV | DQN, PPO, YOLO, Kalman Filters | Mean Average Distance, Collision Rate |
| Movie/Product Recommendation | User-Item Matrix | Collaborative Filtering | Matrix Factorization, Neural CF, LightFM | RMSE, NDCG@K, Hit Rate |
| Machine Translation | Text sequences | Sequence-to-Sequence | RNN, LSTM, Transformer | BLEU, ROUGE |
| Chatbots / Conversational AI | Text | Generation + Retrieval | GPT, T5, RAG, BERT-Retrieval | Perplexity, Human Eval, BLEU |
| Game Playing (Chess, Go) | State-action pairs | Reinforcement Learning | MCTS + DNN (AlphaZero), DQN | Win Rate, Elo Rating |
| Image Synthesis (Art, Deepfakes) | Images | Generative | GAN, Diffusion Models, VAE | FID, Inception Score |
| Predictive Maintenance (Industry 4.0) | Sensor time-series | Binary/Multi-class | LSTM, Random Forest, XGBoost | F1, Recall, ROC-AUC |

### 3.4 Analytical Framework: Choosing the Right Application Model

**Step 1 — Identify the output type.**
- Discrete categorical → Classification.
- Continuous numerical → Regression.
- Group structure → Clustering.

**Step 2 — Identify the data modality.**
- Images → CNN family.
- Text/Sequence → RNN / LSTM / Transformer.
- Tabular → Tree-based (XGBoost, Random Forest) or Linear.
- Graph → GNN.

**Step 3 — Identify the volume and labeling.**
- Large + labeled → Deep Learning.
- Small + labeled → Classical ML (SVM, kNN, Logistic Regression).
- Unlabeled → Clustering, Self-Supervised, or Generative.

**Step 4 — Identify the interpretability requirement.**
- High (medical, legal) → Decision Trees, Logistic Regression, SHAP-based explainers.
- Low (ad ranking, recsys) → Deep Learning acceptable.

**Step 5 — Identify deployment constraints.**
- Edge/Mobile → Lightweight (MobileNet, TFLite, quantization).
- Cloud → Heavy models (LLMs, large CNNs).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End AI/ML Application Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Problem Definition] --> A1[Data Acquisition]
    A1 --> A2[Data Preprocessing]
    A2 --> A3[Feature Engineering]
    A3 --> A4[Model Selection]
    A4 --> A5[Training with Loss J]
    A5 --> A6[Validation and Testing]
    A6 --> A7{Performance OK?}
    A7 -- No --> A4
    A7 -- Yes --> A8[Deployment]
    A8 --> A9[Monitoring and MLOps]
    A9 --> A10[Drift Detected?]
    A10 -- Yes --> A1
    A10 -- No --> A11[Continuous Inference]
```

### 4.2 Application Domain Taxonomy (Mermaid Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[AI and ML Applications] --> R1[Classification Apps]
    R0 --> R2[Regression Apps]
    R0 --> R3[Clustering Apps]
    R0 --> R4[Reinforcement Learning Apps]
    R0 --> R5[Generative Apps]
    R1 --> R1a[Medical Diagnosis]
    R1 --> R1b[Spam Detection]
    R1 --> R1c[Image Recognition]
    R2 --> R2a[Stock Prediction]
    R2 --> R2b[Weather Forecasting]
    R2 --> R2c[Demand Estimation]
    R3 --> R3a[Customer Segmentation]
    R3 --> R3b[Document Grouping]
    R3 --> R3c[Anomaly Detection]
    R4 --> R4a[Robotics]
    R4 --> R4b[Game Playing]
    R4 --> R4c[Autonomous Vehicles]
    R5 --> R5a[Text Generation]
    R5 --> R5b[Image Synthesis]
    R5 --> R5c[Music Composition]
```

### 4.3 Recommendation System Architecture (Mermaid Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[User Behavior Log] --> B1[Data Pipeline]
    B1 --> B2[User Item Interaction Matrix]
    B2 --> B3[Matrix Factorization Engine]
    B3 --> B4[Latent User Vector p]
    B3 --> B5[Latent Item Vector q]
    B4 --> B6[Scoring Module r = p transpose q]
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B7[Top K Recommendations]
    B7 --> B8[User Interface]
    B8 --> B0
```

### 4.4 Reinforcement Learning Loop for Autonomous Systems (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Agent] --> C1[Action a]
    C1 --> C2[Environment]
    C2 --> C3[Reward r and Next State s]
    C3 --> C0
    C0 --> C4[Policy Update via Bellman]
    C4 --> C0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** All Mermaid diagrams above use purely alphanumeric node identifiers (e.g., `A0`, `B3`, `R1a`) and clean uppercase text labels to comply with KTU-Premier-Engine rendering safeguards. No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define an AI/ML Application. List any four major application domains of Machine Learning with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An AI/ML Application is the deployment of a learned computational model to perform a specific real-world task such as classification, prediction, clustering, or generation by leveraging patterns in data.

**Four major application domains:**

| Domain | Example |
|--------|---------|
| Healthcare | Cancer detection from MRI images using CNN |
| Finance | Credit card fraud detection using anomaly detection |
| Transportation | Autonomous driving using reinforcement learning + CV |
| Natural Language Processing | Machine translation using Transformer models |

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for domain–example pair.)*

---

**Q2. Differentiate between Classification, Regression, and Clustering applications with a one-line example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Task | Output Type | Example Application |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| Classification | Discrete categorical label | Email spam detection (spam / not-spam) |
| Regression | Continuous numerical value | Predicting house prices in INR |
| Clustering | Group/cluster ID (unsupervised) | Customer segmentation for marketing |

*(1 mark per correct row.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, End-Semester Pattern, Module 1 Choice Block]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose A or B*

**(a)** Explain in detail the major **application domains of AI and Machine Learning** with at least one real-world use case for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **medical diagnosis application** (e.g., cancer detection from X-ray images), describe the **end-to-end pipeline** including problem formulation, data preprocessing, model selection, training, and evaluation metrics. Justify why **Recall** is the most important metric in this context. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Application Domains of AI/ML (7 marks):**

1. **Healthcare (1 mark):** AI is used in medical imaging for detecting tumors, classifying skin lesions, and predicting patient readmission. *Example:* Google DeepMind's model for detecting eye disease from retinal scans.

2. **Finance (1 mark):** Used for credit scoring, algorithmic trading, portfolio optimization, and fraud detection. *Example:* PayPal uses ML models to flag fraudulent transactions in real time.

3. **Transportation (1 mark):** Autonomous vehicles use sensor fusion and reinforcement learning. *Example:* Tesla's Autopilot uses CNNs to detect lanes, signs, and pedestrians.

4. **Natural Language Processing (1 mark):** Chatbots, sentiment analysis, machine translation, and summarization. *Example:* Google Translate uses Transformer-based models.

5. **Computer Vision (1 mark):** Facial recognition, object detection, surveillance, and quality control in manufacturing. *Example:* Amazon Go stores use CV for cashier-less checkout.

6. **Recommendation Systems (1 mark):** Personalization of content, products, and services. *Example:* Netflix recommends shows based on collaborative filtering.

7. **Robotics & Industrial Automation (1 mark):** Robotic arms in assembly lines use RL and imitation learning. *Example:* Amazon warehouse robots (Kiva) for order picking.

---

**(b) End-to-End Pipeline for Medical Diagnosis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Problem Formulation (1 mark):**
Binary classification — Input: chest X-ray image. Output: $y \in \{0, 1\}$ where $1$ = malignant, $0$ = benign.

**Step 2 — Data Acquisition (1 mark):**
Collect labeled X-ray images from hospitals; dataset size $n$ (e.g., 100,000 images) split into training (80%), validation (10%), and test (10%).

**Step 3 — Preprocessing (1 mark):**
- Resize images to a fixed resolution (e.g., 224×224).
- Normalize pixel values to $[0, 1]$.
- Apply data augmentation: rotation, flipping, contrast adjustment.
- Address class imbalance using **SMOTE** or weighted loss.

**Step 4 — Model Selection (1 mark):**
Use a **Convolutional Neural Network** such as ResNet-50, optionally with **Transfer Learning** pretrained on ImageNet. Output layer uses **Softmax** for 2 classes.

**Step 5 — Training (1 mark):**
Minimize **Cross-Entropy Loss**:

$$J_{CE} = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{c=1}^{2} y_{i,c}\log(\hat{p}_{i,c})$$

Optimized using **Adam optimizer** with learning rate $\alpha = 10^{-4}$ and early stopping based on validation loss.

**Step 6 — Evaluation (1 mark):**
Use **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **Recall**, **F1-Score**, and **ROC-AUC**.

**Step 7 — Why Recall is Critical (1 mark):**
In cancer detection, a **False Negative** means a sick patient is sent home undiagnosed — potentially fatal. A **False Positive** only leads to further tests. Therefore, **Recall** ($R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$) must be maximized, even at the cost of slightly lower precision.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss in detail the role of AI/ML in **Recommender Systems** and **Natural Language Processing**. For each, explain the working principle, key algorithms, and one industry use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable examples, explain the differences between **Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning** applications. For each category, give two real-world applications and the corresponding evaluation metric. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Recommender Systems and NLP (7 marks):**

**Recommender Systems (3.5 marks):**
- **Working Principle:** Predict a user's preference for an item based on past behavior, similarity to other users (collaborative filtering), or item attributes (content-based filtering).
- **Key Algorithms:**
  - **Matrix Factorization (MF)** — Decomposes user-item matrix $R \approx P \cdot Q^{\top}$ into latent factors.
  - **Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF)** — Uses deep neural networks to model nonlinear user-item interactions.
  - **LightFM** — Hybrid model combining MF and content features.
- **Industry Use Case:** Netflix uses MF + deep learning to recommend shows, accounting for ~80% of viewer activity.

**Natural Language Processing (3.5 marks):**
- **Working Principle:** Convert text into numerical representations (embeddings) and model sequential/semantic relationships.
- **Key Algorithms:**
  - **RNN / LSTM** — For sequential text modeling.
  - **Transformer (BERT, GPT)** — Self-attention mechanism for context-aware embeddings.
  - **Seq2Seq Models** — For translation and summarization.
- **Industry Use Case:** Google Translate uses a Transformer-based architecture to translate between 100+ languages in real time.

---

**(b) Supervised vs Unsupervised vs Reinforcement Learning Applications (7 marks):**

**1. Supervised Learning Applications (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Learning a mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ from labeled data $(x_i, y_i)$.
- **Application 1:** Email spam classification — Algorithm: Naive Bayes / SVM — Metric: **F1-Score**.
- **Application 2:** House price prediction — Algorithm: Linear Regression / XGBoost — Metric: **RMSE**.

**2. Unsupervised Learning Applications (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Discovering hidden structure in unlabeled data.
- **Application 1:** Customer segmentation — Algorithm: K-Means — Metric: **Silhouette Score**.
- **Application 2:** Anomaly detection in network traffic — Algorithm: Isolation Forest / Autoencoder — Metric: **AUC-PR**.

**3. Reinforcement Learning Applications (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** An agent learns a policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ by interacting with an environment to maximize cumulative reward.
- **Application 1:** Game playing (AlphaGo) — Algorithm: MCTS + DNN — Metric: **Win Rate / Elo Rating**.
- **Application 2:** Autonomous driving — Algorithm: DQN / PPO — Metric: **Safety Score / Mean Distance Between Interventions**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on "Applications" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing algorithm and application** — e.g., writing "CNN is an application" is wrong. CNN is an *algorithm*; "Skin cancer detection" is the *application*. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Memorizing use cases without explaining the working principle** — Always pair the application with the **task type** (classification / regression / RL) and **metric**.
> 3. **Forgetting evaluation metrics** — A 7-mark application question without a metric is considered incomplete; expect a **1–2 mark deduction**.
> 4. **Writing vague domain names** — "AI is used in business" carries **zero marks**. Be specific: "AI in **finance** for **fraud detection** using **Isolation Forest** with **Recall** as the metric."
> 5. **Ignoring ethical/societal context** — KTU 2024 Scheme NEP-aligned questions often include a 1-mark sub-part on **bias, fairness, or explainability** of the application. Don't skip it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Applications of AI/ML"**

- ✅ **Definition**: An AI/ML application is the real-world deployment of a learned model to perform a task (classify, predict, cluster, generate, or control).
- ✅ **Six Pillars of Applications**: Healthcare, Finance, Transportation, NLP, Computer Vision, Recommendation Systems.
- ✅ **Task-to-Algorithm Mapping**:
    - Classification → CNN, SVM, BERT
    - Regression → Linear, Random Forest, LSTM
    - Clustering → K-Means, DBSCAN
    - Reinforcement Learning → DQN, PPO
    - Generation → GAN, VAE, LLM
- ✅ **Key Metrics**:
    - Classification → Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, ROC-AUC
    - Regression → MSE, RMSE, $R^2$
    - Clustering → Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin
    - Generation → FID, BLEU, Perplexity
- ✅ **Pipeline Stages**: Problem → Data → Preprocess → Features → Model → Train → Evaluate → Deploy → Monitor.
- ✅ **Cross-Entropy Loss** $J_{CE} = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \sum_c y_{i,c} \log \hat{p}_{i,c}$ is the universal classification loss.
- ✅ **F1-Score** = Harmonic mean of Precision & Recall; use when classes are imbalanced.
- ✅ **Recall > Precision** in medical/security applications; **Precision > Recall** in spam/ad applications.
- ✅ **Recommender System** formula: $\hat{r}_{u,i} = p_u^{\top} q_i$ — dot product of latent user and item vectors.
- ✅ **Reinforcement Learning** core update: $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$.
- ✅ **GAN Loss**: $L_{GAN} = \mathbb{E}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ — adversarial minimax objective.
- ✅ **Transformer / BERT / GPT** powers modern NLP applications (translation, chatbots, summarization).
- ✅ **Transfer Learning** is the go-to technique when labeled data is scarce (e.g., medical imaging).
- ✅ **Ethical AI** considerations: bias, fairness, explainability, privacy — mandatory in KTU 2024 NEP-aligned answers.
- ✅ **Industry-Standard Frameworks**: Scikit-learn, TensorFlow, PyTorch, Hugging Face, XGBoost, LightGBM.
- ✅ **Always state the metric with the application** — a common 1–2 mark deduction in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intelligent Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Intelligent Agents # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Agent? In the formal vocabulary of **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus, an **agent** is anything that can be viewed as **perceiving its environment through sensors** and **acting upon that environment through actuators**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (Russell & Norvig Framework):** > An **agent** is an autonomous entity that observes its environment via **sensors**, reasons about what it perceives, and responds by performing actions through **effectors** (also called actuators), with the goal of maximizing a defined **performance measure**. Mathematically, an agent is modelled as a function: $$f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$ where $\mathcal{P}^*$ is the set of all possible **percept sequences** the agent can experience, and $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of all **actions** the agent can perform. ## 1.2 What Makes an Agent "Intelligent"? An **intelligent agent** is not just any reactive system — it must exhibit some form of **rationality**. According to KTU Module 1: > [!NOTE] > **Rational Agent:** For each possible percept sequence, a rational agent should select an action that is expected to **maximize its performance measure**, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and the agent's built-in knowledge. The four pillars of rationality are: 1. **Performance Measure** — defines success. 2. **Prior Knowledge** — what the agent knows before acting. 3. **Possible Actions** — what the agent *can* do. 4. **Percept Sequence** — what the agent has *seen so far*. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **Analogy: The Self-Driving Car** > Imagine a self-driving car navigating through Kerala's traffic. Its **sensors** (cameras, LiDAR, GPS) perceive the road, pedestrians, and other vehicles. Its **actuators** (steering wheel, accelerator, brakes) act on the environment. Its **performance measure** could be "reach the destination safely, quickly, and within fuel limits." The car's AI software is the **agent program** that maps sensor input to actuator output — that whole package is the **intelligent agent**. Other everyday examples students will recognize: - **Thermostat** → simple reflex agent (temperature sensor + heater switch) - **Roomba vacuum** → model-based reflex agent - **Google Maps route planner** → utility-based agent - **ChatGPT** → learning agent - **Human brain** → ultimate general-purpose intelligent agent ## 1.4 Agent vs. Plain Program — The Critical Distinction | Property | Plain Program | Intelligent Agent | |---|---|---| | Operates under direct user control | ✔ Yes | ✘ Autonomous | | Senses its own environment | ✘ No | ✔ Yes | | Acts to change environment | ✘ Rarely | ✔ Yes | | Has a performance measure | ✘ Implicit | ✔ Explicit | | Operates continuously | ✘ Finishes a task | ✔ Continuous loop | --- ## 2. Key Terminology to Memorise for KTU Exams - **Sensors** — devices for perception (cameras, microphones, keyboards, thermometers). - **Actuators / Effectors** — devices for action (motors, robotic arms, screen output, speakers). - **Percept** — the agent's perceptual input at a single instant. - **Percept Sequence** — the complete history of everything the agent has perceived. - **Agent Function** — the abstract mapping $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$. - **Agent Program** — the concrete implementation of the agent function that runs on the physical architecture. - **Rationality** — choosing actions that maximize expected performance. - **Autonomy** — the ability to act based on own experience, not just prior knowledge. - **Omniscience vs Rationality** — omniscience means knowing the *actual* outcome; rationality means choosing the *expected best* action. They differ because environments are stochastic. # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The PEAS Framework (High-Yield for KTU) For any agent design question in KTU exams, you must specify the agent using the **PEAS** descriptor: > [!IMPORTANT] > **PEAS = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors** This is a direct, frequently-asked 7–14 mark question in the KTU ESE. ### PEAS for a Self-Driving Car | PEAS Component | Description | |---|---| | **Performance Measure** | Safety, speed, fuel efficiency, passenger comfort, legal compliance | | **Environment** | Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather, other vehicles, road signs | | **Actuators** | Steering, accelerator, brake, signal lights, horn, display | | **Sensors** | Cameras, LiDAR, radar, GPS, ultrasonic sensors, speedometer, accelerometer | ### PEAS for a Medical Diagnosis System (Classic KTU Question) | PEAS Component | Description | |---|---| | **Performance Measure** | Correct diagnosis, patient health, cost of tests, minimized false negatives | | **Environment** | Patient, hospital, staff, medical records, test results | | **Actuators** | Display questions, print reports, prescribe medication, send alerts | | **Sensors** | Keyboard input, touch screen, microphone (symptom input) | ## 2.2 Environment Properties — The 7 Dimensions KTU exams frequently test environment classification. Memorize the following **7 properties**: > [!NOTE] > **The 7 Environment Dimensions:** > 1. **Fully Observable vs. Partially Observable** > 2. **Deterministic vs. Stochastic** > 3. **Episodic vs. Sequential** > 4. **Static vs. Dynamic** > 5. **Discrete vs. Continuous** > 6. **Single-Agent vs. Multi-Agent** > 7. **Known vs. Unknown** ### Definitions of Each Dimension - **Fully Observable** — Sensors capture the complete state of the environment at every time step. - **Partially Observable** — Sensors miss some relevant state (e.g., poker with hidden cards). - **Deterministic** — Next state is fully determined by current state + action. - **Stochastic** — Next state has uncertainty / randomness. - **Episodic** — Each action-decision is independent of previous ones. - **Sequential** — Current decision affects all future decisions. - **Static** — Environment does not change while the agent deliberates. - **Dynamic** — Environment changes while the agent deliberates. - **Discrete** — Finite / countable states and actions. - **Continuous** — Infinite / real-valued states and actions. - **Single-Agent** — Only one agent operating in the environment. - **Multi-Agent** — Multiple agents interacting (cooperative or competitive). - **Known** — Agent knows the rules / outcomes of its actions. - **Unknown** — Agent must learn the rules by exploration. ### KTU Cheat Sheet — Environment Classification Table | Environment Type | Observable? | Deterministic? | Episodic? | Static? | Discrete? | Agent Count? | Known? | Example | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Crossword Puzzle | Fully | Deterministic | Sequential | Static | Discrete | Single | Known | – | | Chess (with clock) | Fully | Deterministic | Sequential | Semi | Discrete | Multi | Known | – | | Poker | Partial | Stochastic | Sequential | Static | Discrete | Multi | Known | – | | Taxi Driving | Partial | Stochastic | Sequential | Dynamic | Continuous | Multi | Known | – | | Medical Diagnosis | Partial | Stochastic | Episodic | Dynamic | Continuous | Single | Known | – | | Image Analysis | Fully | Deterministic | Episodic | Semi | Continuous | Single | Known | – | | Part-picking Robot | Partial | Stochastic | Episodic | Dynamic | Continuous | Single | Known | – | | Refinery Controller | Partial | Stochastic | Sequential | Dynamic | Continuous | Single | Known | – | | English Tutor | Partial | Stochastic | Sequential | Dynamic | Discrete | Multi | Known | – | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Agent Theory | Concept | Mathematical Form | Plain Meaning | |---|---|---| | Agent Function | $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | Maps entire percept history to a single action | | Rational Action | $a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}[\text{Performance} \mid p, a]$ | Choose action maximizing expected performance | | Percept Sequence at time $t$ | $p_t = (s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_t)$ | All sensor readings up to time $t$ | | Conditional Action Choice | $a = f(p_t)$ | Action depends only on current percept history | | Expected Utility | $\mathbb{E}[U] = \sum_i P(o_i) \cdot U(o_i)$ | Probability-weighted sum of outcome utilities | | Boltzmann Exploration (for learning) | $P(a) = \dfrac{e^{Q(a)/T}}{\sum_{a'} e^{Q(a')/T}}$ | Soft-max action selection for exploration | > [!WARNING] > **Avoid the pipe symbol inside LaTeX tables!** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value, conditional probability, etc., so the markdown table parser does not break. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Intelligent Agents - **Industrial Automation** — Robotic arms in manufacturing (KUKA, Fanuc). - **Autonomous Vehicles** — Tesla FSD, Waymo. - **Healthcare** — IBM Watson for Oncology, diagnostic chatbots. - **Finance** — Algorithmic trading agents. - **Cybersecurity** — Intrusion detection agents. - **Smart Grids** — Adaptive energy distribution. - **Customer Service** — Conversational AI agents. # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: The Vacuum-Cleaner World (Classic KTU Problem) This is the **single most important example** for KTU Module 1. Memorize it completely. ### Environment Specification - **World** — Two squares: $A$ and $B$. - **Percepts** — `[Location, Status]` where - Location $\in \{A, B\}$ - Status $\in \{\text{Clean}, \text{Dirty}\}$ - **Actions** — $\mathcal{A} = \{\text{Left}, \text{Right}, \text{Suck}, \text{NoOp}\}$ - **Performance Measure** — +1 per clean square per time step; −1 for each movement. - **Environment type** — Fully observable, deterministic, episodic, static, discrete, single-agent, known. ### Step 1: Enumerate the Percept Space Each percept is one of the following tuples: $$ p \in \{A_{\text{Clean}},\ A_{\text{Dirty}},\ B_{\text{Clean}},\ B_{\text{Dirty}}\} $$ So $\vert \mathcal{P} \vert = 4$. ### Step 2: Enumerate the Percept Sequence Space The agent's percept history up to time $t$ is a sequence: $$ p_t = (p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_t), \quad p_i \in \mathcal{P} $$ The total number of possible percept sequences of length $t$ is $\vert \mathcal{P} \vert^t = 4^t$. Since the percept sequence can be of *any* length, the full percept-sequence space is: $$ \mathcal{P}^* = \bigcup_{t=0}^{\infty} \mathcal{P}^t $$ ### Step 3: Define the Agent Function Table A *complete* agent function $f$ specifies an action for every possible percept sequence. For a simple reflex agent that only looks at the *current* percept, we have: | Percept | Action | |---|---| | $(A, \text{Clean})$ | Right | | $(A, \text{Dirty})$ | Suck | | $(B, \text{Clean})$ | Left | | $(B, \text{Dirty})$ | Suck | > [!NOTE] > The number of distinct agent functions possible is $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert^{\vert \mathcal{P}^* \vert}$. Since $\mathcal{P}^*$ is *infinite*, the number of possible agent functions is **infinite**. This is why we use compact **agent programs** rather than look-up tables. ### Step 4: Compute the Rational Action for State (A, Dirty) Let $P(\text{state})$ denote the probability of each state and $U(a)$ the utility of action $a$. $$ \mathbb{E}[U(\text{Suck})] = P(\text{A,Dirty}) \cdot U(\text{clean}) + P(\text{B, anything}) \cdot U(\text{stay}) $$ $$ \mathbb{E}[U(\text{Right})] = P(\text{A,Dirty}) \cdot U(\text{unclean}) + P(\text{B,Clean}) \cdot U(\text{clean}) - 1 $$ The rational action is: $$ a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}[U(a)] $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Agent Type to Environment Type > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix is a **direct scoring rubric** for the KTU "Compare agent types" question. | Agent Type | Internal State? | Goal Knowledge? | Utility Function? | Learning? | Best Suited Environment | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Simple Reflex | No | No | No | No | Fully observable, episodic | | Model-Based Reflex | Yes | No | No | No | Partially observable | | Goal-Based | Yes | Yes | No | No | Sequential, goal-driven | | Utility-Based | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Stochastic, conflicting goals | | Learning Agent | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Unknown, dynamic | ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Rationality vs. Omniscience A common KTU conceptual question: *Distinguish between rationality and omniscience.* ### Step 1: Define Omniscience An **omniscient** agent knows the *actual* outcome of its action in the real world. $$ a_{\text{omni}} = \arg\max_{a} \text{ActualPerformance}(a) $$ ### Step 2: Define Rationality A **rational** agent knows the *expected* outcome based on its percept history and prior knowledge. $$ a_{\text{rational}} = \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}\bigl[\text{Performance} \mid p_t, a\bigr] $$ ### Step 3: Show the Gap $$ \Delta = a_{\text{omni}} - a_{\text{rational}} = \arg\max_{a} \text{Actual} - \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E} $$ > [!TIP] > **Why the gap exists:** Because the environment may be **stochastic** or **partially observable**, the actual outcome may differ from the expected outcome. A rational agent therefore **does not need to be omniscient** — it only needs to maximize *expected* performance. **Conclusion:** Omniscience is impossible in real environments; rationality is the achievable engineering ideal. # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Agent–Environment Interaction > [!IMPORTANT] > This block diagram is the **single most-sketched figure** in KTU AI/ML exams. Practice drawing it. ```mermaid flowchart LR envNode["ENVIRONMENT"] -->|"Sensor Input / Percept"| sensorNode["SENSORS"] sensorNode -->|"Raw Percept"| agentCore["AGENT CORE
Agent Function or Agent Program"] agentCore -->|"Action Command"| actuatorNode["ACTUATORS"] actuatorNode -->|"Physical Action"| envNode perfNode["PERFORMANCE MEASURE"] -.->|"Evaluates outcome of"| envNode perfNode -.->|"Feedback to agent"| agentCore style envNode fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b style sensorNode fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#2471a3 style agentCore fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b7950b style actuatorNode fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449 style perfNode fill:#e8daef,stroke:#7d3c98 ``` ## 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Learning Agent ```mermaid flowchart TB critNode["CRITIC
Feedback on Performance"] learnNode["LEARNING ELEMENT
Improves Agent"] perfNode2["PERFORMANCE ELEMENT
Selects Actions"] probNode["PROBLEM GENERATOR
Suggests Exploratory Actions"] sensorIn["Percept from Sensors"] --> perfNode2 perfNode2 -->|"Action"| actOut["Action to Actuators"] critNode -->|"Reward / Penalty"| learnNode learnNode -->|"Updated Knowledge"| perfNode2 probNode -->|"Exploratory Suggestions"| perfNode2 style critNode fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21 style learnNode fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d style perfNode2 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d style probNode fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#9a7d0a ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flow for Classifying an Agent Type ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode{"Does the agent
maintain internal state?"} stateNo{"Does it use
a goal test?"} utilNo{"Does it use a
utility function?"} learnNo{"Does it improve
from experience?"} out1["SIMPLE REFLEX AGENT"] out2["MODEL BASED REFLEX AGENT"] out3["GOAL BASED AGENT"] out4["UTILITY BASED AGENT"] out5["LEARNING AGENT"] startNode -->|No| out1 startNode -->|Yes| stateNo stateNo -->|No| out2 stateNo -->|Yes| utilNo utilNo -->|No| out3 utilNo -->|Yes| learnNo learnNo -->|No| out4 learnNo -->|Yes| out5 style startNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b7950b style stateNo fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b7950b style utilNo fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b7950b style learnNo fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b7950b style out1 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d style out2 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d style out3 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d style out4 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d style out5 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d ``` ## 4.4 PEAS Application — Medical Diagnosis System (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart LR patient["PATIENT & HOSPITAL
Environment"] s1["Symptoms Input"] s2["Lab Reports"] s3["Imaging Data"] kb["KNOWLEDGE BASE
+ Inference Engine"] a1["Diagnosis Output"] a2["Prescription"] a3["Test Recommendation"] patient --> s1 patient --> s2 patient --> s3 s1 --> kb s2 --> kb s3 --> kb kb --> a1 kb --> a2 kb --> a3 a1 --> patient a2 --> patient a3 --> patient style patient fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b style kb fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b7950b style s1 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d style s2 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d style s3 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d style a1 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d style a2 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d style a3 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d ``` # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank > [!NOTE] > All questions below mirror the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: 3-mark Part A and 14-mark Part B with internal choice. Bloom's levels are tagged per question. --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define an intelligent agent. Differentiate between an agent function and an agent program.** *(Bloom: Remember / Understand)* **Model Answer:** An **intelligent agent** is any entity that perceives its environment through **sensors** and acts upon it through **actuators**, with the goal of maximizing a defined **performance measure**. | Aspect | Agent Function | Agent Program | |---|---|---| | Nature | Abstract mathematical mapping $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | Concrete implementation on hardware | | Form | A table or function description | Executable code | | Existence | Theoretic ideal | Runs on the architecture | | Mapping | Maps *entire* percept history → action | Maps *current* percept → action | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Writing "agent function and agent program are the same." They are not — the function is the *mathematical ideal*, the program is the *practical execution*. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Explain the term "rationality" with respect to intelligent agents. Is a rational agent necessarily omniscient? Justify.** *(Bloom: Understand / Analyze)* **Model Answer:** A **rational agent** selects, for each percept sequence, the action that **maximizes its expected performance measure** based on its percepts and built-in knowledge. $$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}\bigl[\text{Performance} \mid p_t, a\bigr]$$ **A rational agent is NOT necessarily omniscient.** Omniscience requires knowing the *actual* outcome, which is impossible in stochastic or partially observable environments. Rationality only requires the agent to choose the **best expected** action — not the best actual one. Hence, rationality is the **achievable engineering ideal**, while omniscience is a theoretical upper bound that real agents can never reach. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Define the **PEAS framework** and use it to fully specify an **automatic taxi driver** agent. Classify its environment on all 7 dimensions. *(7 Marks — Bloom: Apply)* **(b)** Compare and contrast the **five major types of intelligent agents** (Simple Reflex, Model-Based Reflex, Goal-Based, Utility-Based, Learning) with suitable diagrams and one real-world example for each. *(7 Marks — Bloom: Analyze)* --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **PEAS Specification for Automatic Taxi Driver:** | PEAS Element | Specification | |---|---| | **Performance Measure** | Safety (no accidents), speed (reach destination fast), fuel efficiency, passenger comfort, legal compliance, profit per trip | | **Environment** | Roads, traffic signals, other vehicles, pedestrians, weather, road signs, passengers, potholes | | **Actuators** | Steering wheel, accelerator, brake, gear, turn signals, horn, display screen, speaker | | **Sensors** | Cameras, LiDAR, radar, GPS, speedometer, odometer, accelerometer, ultrasonic sensors, microphone | **Environment Classification (7 Dimensions):** | Dimension | Classification | Justification | |---|---|---| | Observability | **Partially observable** | Cannot see around blind corners; other drivers' intentions hidden | | Determinism | **Stochastic** | Traffic, pedestrians, weather introduce randomness | | Episode structure | **Sequential** | Current driving action affects all future states | | Dynamism | **Dynamic** | Other cars move while the taxi is computing | | State space | **Continuous** | Position, speed, steering angle are real-valued | | Agent count | **Multi-agent** | Interacts with other vehicles, pedestrians, traffic cops | | Knowledge | **Known** | Road rules are predefined | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic to remember the 7 dimensions:** **O-D-E-D-C-M-K** (Observe, Determinism, Episode, Dynamic, Continuous, Multi-agent, Known). --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Comparison of the Five Agent Types:** | Agent Type | Internal State | Goal? | Utility? | Learns? | Real-World Example | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Simple Reflex** | No | No | No | No | Thermostat, automatic door | | **Model-Based Reflex** | Yes | No | No | No | Roomba vacuum, Mars rover | | **Goal-Based** | Yes | Yes | No | No | GPS navigation, chess engine | | **Utility-Based** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Self-driving car route planner, recommender system | | **Learning Agent** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Spam filter, AlphaGo, Tesla FSD | **Explanation:** 1. **Simple Reflex Agent** — Acts on the *current* percept using condition-action rules. Fails in partially observable environments. $$\text{IF percept THEN action}$$ 2. **Model-Based Reflex Agent** — Maintains an **internal state** that tracks the unobserved aspects of the world, then uses rules on this enriched state. 3. **Goal-Based Agent** — In addition to state, holds a **goal** description and chooses actions that move it closer to the goal (uses search/planning). 4. **Utility-Based Agent** — When goals are insufficient (conflicting objectives, uncertain outcomes), uses a **utility function** that maps states to a real number expressing "happiness." 5. **Learning Agent** — Comprises a **Performance Element**, **Critic**, **Learning Element**, and **Problem Generator**. Improves from feedback over time. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often draw the **same box** for all five agent types. To score full 7 marks, you must **explicitly label** the components inside each agent's architecture (state, goal, utility, learning element). --- ### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** What is the **agent function**? Draw the **agent-environment interaction diagram** and explain each component in detail. *(7 Marks — Bloom: Understand)* **(b)** Consider the **vacuum-cleaner world** with two squares A and B. Specify the agent function table for a rational agent using a performance measure of "+1 per clean square per time step, −1 per movement." Demonstrate with two scenarios. *(7 Marks — Bloom: Apply / Analyze)* --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Agent Function Definition:** The **agent function** is the abstract mathematical description that maps every possible percept sequence to an action. $$f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$ where - $\mathcal{P}^*$ = set of all possible percept sequences - $\mathcal{A}$ = set of all possible actions **Agent-Environment Interaction Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR envNode2["ENVIRONMENT"] -->|"Sensors capture state"| sensorNode2["SENSORS"] sensorNode2 -->|"Percept"| agentNode2["AGENT
Agent Function or Agent Program"] agentNode2 -->|"Action"| actNode2["ACTUATORS"] actNode2 -->|"Modify environment"| envNode2 style envNode2 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b style sensorNode2 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d style agentNode2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b7950b style actNode2 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d ``` **Component Explanation:** 1. **Environment** — The part of the world the agent inhabits and must navigate. 2. **Sensors** — Perceptual devices that translate environment state into percepts (e.g., camera, microphone). 3. **Agent Core** — Implements the agent function; can be a simple lookup, a rule engine, or a neural network. 4. **Actuators** — Effectors that translate the chosen action into physical change (e.g., motors, screen). 5. **Performance Measure** — Evaluates how well the agent is achieving its goal (often external to the diagram). --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Setup:** - Percepts: `(Location, Status)` where Location ∈ {A, B}, Status ∈ {Clean, Dirty} - Actions: {Left, Right, Suck, NoOp} - Performance: +1 per clean square / time step, −1 per move **Agent Function Table:** | Percept | Optimal Action | Justification | |---|---|---| | $(A, \text{Clean})$ | Right | Move to B (no sucking, no cleaning) | | $(A, \text{Dirty})$ | Suck | Clean A immediately, +1 reward | | $(B, \text{Clean})$ | Left | Move to A | | $(B, \text{Dirty})$ | Suck | Clean B immediately, +1 reward | **Scenario 1: Starting at (A, Dirty) → action Suck** - Reward gained: +1 (A is now clean) - Cost incurred: 0 (no move) **Scenario 2: Starting at (A, Clean) → action Right** - Reward gained: 0 (A already clean) - Cost incurred: −1 (moved) - Net step reward: −1, but enables future cleaning of B **Derivation of Rationality:** The rational action maximizes expected performance: $$a^* = \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}[\text{Performance} \mid p_t, a]$$ For percept $(A, \text{Dirty})$: - Suck: gives +1 with probability 1 - Right: gives 0 with probability 1 - Therefore $a^* = \text{Suck}$ ✓ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students **forget to write the justification column**. KTU evaluators award 1 mark per column in the table; missing the reasoning column costs 2–3 marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Intelligent Agents** ### Core Definitions - ☐ **Agent** = Sensors + Actuators + Performance Measure + Agent Program - ☐ **Rational Agent** = maximizes *expected* performance (not actual) - ☐ **Omniscient Agent** = knows *actual* outcome (impossible in real systems) - ☐ **Agent Function** = $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ (mathematical) - ☐ **Agent Program** = concrete implementation of the agent function - ☐ **Autonomy** = ability to act on own experience, not just prior knowledge ### PEAS Framework - ☐ **P**erformance, **E**nvironment, **A**ctuators, **S**ensors - ☐ Always specify **all 4** for full marks in design questions ### 7 Environment Dimensions — Mnemonic: **O-D-E-D-C-M-K** - ☐ **O**bservable (Fully / Partial) - ☐ **D**eterministic / Stochastic - ☐ **E**pisodic / Sequential - ☐ **D**ynamic / Static / Semi-dynamic - ☐ **C**ontinuous / Discrete - ☐ **M**ulti-agent / Single-agent - ☐ **K**nown / Unknown ### 5 Agent Types — Mnemonic: **S-M-G-U-L** - ☐ **S**imple Reflex (no state) - ☐ **M**odel-Based Reflex (state) - ☐ **G**oal-Based (state + goal) - ☐ **U**tility-Based (state + goal + utility) - ☐ **L**earning (state + goal + utility + learning) ### High-Frequency KTU Examples - ☐ **Vacuum Cleaner World** — 2 squares A & B, 4 percepts, 4 actions - ☐ **Taxi Driver** — Partial / Stochastic / Sequential / Dynamic / Continuous / Multi / Known - ☐ **Medical Diagnosis** — Partial / Stochastic / Episodic / Dynamic / Continuous / Single / Known - ☐ **Chess (with clock)** — Fully / Deterministic / Sequential / Semi / Discrete / Multi / Known - ☐ **Image Classification** — Fully / Deterministic / Episodic / Semi / Continuous / Single / Known ### Diagram to Always Practice - ☐ Agent-Environment interaction block diagram (Section 4.1) - ☐ Learning agent 4-component architecture (Section 4.2) - ☐ Agent-type classification flowchart (Section 4.3) ### Formulae to Memorize - ☐ $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ - ☐ $a^* = \arg\max_a \mathbb{E}[\text{Performance} \mid p_t, a]$ - ☐ $\mathbb{E}[U] = \sum_i P(o_i) \cdot U(o_i)$ > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** KTU Module 1 questions almost always combine PEAS + environment classification + agent-type comparison. Master these three together, and you can answer 80% of Module 1 questions confidently.

topics.title: Agents and Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Agents and Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Agent? (KTU 2024 Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Russell & Norvig Framework)**
> An **Agent** is anything that can be viewed as perceiving its **Environment** through **Sensors** and acting upon that environment through **Effectors / Actuators**. Formally, an agent is a function that maps every possible **percept sequence** to an **action**.

$$\boxed{\text{Agent Function: } f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}}$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{P}^*$ = set of all possible **finite percept sequences**
- $\mathcal{A}$ = set of all possible **actions** the agent can execute

The internal working of an agent is called the **Agent Program** ($agent\_program$), which physically implements the abstract agent function $f$.

---

## 1.2 What is an Environment?

The **Environment** is the external world with which the agent interacts. Everything outside the agent that the agent can sense or affect constitutes its environment. The environment provides **percepts** to the agent and receives **actions** from the agent.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Human Body as an Agent**
> Think of a **taxi driver** navigating city traffic:
> - **Sensors** = Eyes (cameras), ears, speedometer (proprioceptors), GPS receiver
> - **Percept** = "I see a red light, my speed is 40 km/h, GPS says turn left in 200m"
> - **Effectors** = Hands on steering wheel, legs on pedals, turn signals
> - **Action** = Apply brake, steer left, accelerate
> - **Environment** = Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather, road signs
> - **Performance Measure** = Safety, speed, comfort, fuel efficiency, legality

The taxi driver is a **rational agent** because it chooses actions to maximize its performance measure based on percept history.

---

## 1.4 The Perception-Action Loop (Core Concept)

The fundamental operation of any agent is a continuous cycle:

$$\text{Perceive} \rightarrow \text{Think/Reason} \rightarrow \text{Act} \rightarrow \text{Environment Changes} \rightarrow \text{New Percept} \rightarrow \cdots$$

This is the **Perception-Action Cycle**, the most fundamental concept in KTU Module 1.

| Component | Mathematical Symbol | Real-World (Taxi) |
|---|---|---|
| Percept | $p \in \mathcal{P}$ | Camera frame |
| Percept Sequence | $\mathcal{P}^*$ | All frames so far |
| Action | $a \in \mathcal{A}$ | Steer left |
| Agent Function | $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | Driver's brain logic |
| Performance Measure | $J: \mathcal{H} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ | Trip rating score |

---

## 1.5 Rationality vs. Omniscience

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rational Agent (KTU High-Yield Distinction)**
> - A **Rational Agent** acts to **maximize the expected value of the performance measure** given the percept sequence to date.
> - **Omniscient Agent** knows the actual outcome of its actions — this is **IMPOSSIBLE** in reality.
> - Rationality includes **information gathering** (exploration), **learning** (from percepts), and **autonomy** (acting without prior knowledge of the environment).

$$\boxed{\text{Rational Action} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}[\text{Performance} \mid \text{Percept Sequence}]}$$

Where **$\mathbb{E}$** denotes **expectation** (weighted average over uncertainty).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PEAS Framework (Mandatory KTU Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **PEAS** is the standardized description framework for any task environment. It is a **guaranteed 3-mark question** on KTU exams.

**PEAS = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors**

| Component | Question It Answers | Taxicab Driver Example | Medical Diagnosis Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P**erformance Measure | How do we judge success? | Safety, speed, profit | Correct diagnosis %, cost, patient outcome |
| **E**nvironment | What surrounds the agent? | Roads, traffic, weather | Hospital, patient, staff, equipment |
| **A**ctuators | How does it act? | Steering, brake, accelerator | Display, prescription printer, alert |
| **S**ensors | How does it perceive? | Camera, sonar, GPS, speedometer | X-ray, blood test, thermometer, patient interview |

---

## 2.2 Properties of Task Environments (KTU High-Yield)

The **8 Standard Properties** of task environments — frequently asked in KTU Module 1:

### Property 1: Fully Observable vs. Partially Observable
- **Fully Observable**: Sensors capture the complete state of the environment at every point in time.
- **Partially Observable**: Sensors miss critical information (due to noise, occlusion, or limited sensors).
- *Example*: Chess (fully observable) vs. Poker (partially observable).

### Property 2: Deterministic vs. Stochastic
- **Deterministic**: The next state of the environment is completely determined by the current state and the agent's action.
- **Stochastic**: There is inherent uncertainty; multiple possible next states.
- **Strategic** is the term used when the environment is deterministic except for the actions of other agents.

### Property 3: Episodic vs. Sequential
- **Episodic**: Each action-decision is independent; the next episode does not depend on previous ones.
- **Sequential**: Current decision affects all future decisions.

### Property 4: Static vs. Dynamic
- **Static**: Environment does not change while the agent deliberates.
- **Dynamic**: Environment changes continuously (with or without the agent's actions).
- **Semidynamic**: Environment does not change but the agent's performance score changes with time.

### Property 5: Discrete vs. Continuous
- **Discrete**: Finite (or countable) number of percepts and actions.
- **Continuous**: Real-valued percepts/actions (e.g., steering angle).

### Property 6: Single Agent vs. Multi-Agent
- **Single Agent**: Only one agent operating.
- **Multi-Agent**: Multiple agents cooperating (with shared goals) or competing (with conflicting goals).

### Property 7: Known vs. Unknown
- **Known**: Agent knows the rules/outcomes of the environment (e.g., rules of chess).
- **Unknown**: Agent must learn how the environment behaves.

### Property 8: Accessible vs. Inaccessible (Optional)
- The agent's sensors have access to all relevant state variables.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Agent Function | $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | Maps percept history to action | Foundation of all AI systems |
| 2 | Rational Action | $a^* = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \cdot U(s')$ | Best action by expected utility | Decision-making under uncertainty |
| 3 | Percept Sequence | $\mathcal{P}^* = \{(p_1), (p_1,p_2), \ldots, (p_1,\ldots,p_t)\}$ | All prefixes of perception | Memory representation |
| 4 | Performance Measure | $J: \mathcal{H} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ | Score over agent history | Evaluation of agent quality |
| 5 | Task Environment Tuple | $\langle \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{A}, T, R \rangle$ | $T$=transition, $R$=reward | MDP formalism |
| 6 | Autonomy Ratio | $\rho = \frac{\text{Knowledge from Experience}}{\text{Total Knowledge}}$ | 0 = no autonomy, 1 = full autonomy | ML-driven system design |
| 7 | Expected Utility | $\mathbb{E}[U] = \sum_i p_i \cdot u_i$ | Utility weighted by probability | Risk-aware AI design |
| 8 | Sensor Accuracy | $\eta = \frac{\text{True Positives} + \text{True Negatives}}{\text{Total Observations}}$ | Perceptual reliability | Robotic vision systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The notation $T: S \times A \rightarrow P(S)$ represents the **transition function**, and $R: S \times A \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is the **reward function**. Together they form the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** framework, which is the mathematical backbone of reinforcement learning (covered in later modules).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Agent-Environment framework is the **universal abstraction** for:
- **Autonomous Vehicles** (Tesla, Waymo) — continuous, partially observable, multi-agent
- **Industrial Robotics** (KUKA, ABB arms) — discrete, deterministic, single-agent
- **Medical AI** (IBM Watson Health) — episodic, known rules
- **Smart Grids** — dynamic, multi-agent, partially observable
- **Game AI** (AlphaGo, Chess engines) — fully observable, deterministic, sequential
- **Conversational AI** (Chatbots) — episodic, partially observable, stochastic

The choice of **agent architecture** is dictated by the **task environment properties**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Derivation: From Agent Function to Agent Program

The **Agent Function** is a mathematical idealization. In practice, we implement it as an **Agent Program** running on a physical architecture. The KTU-required logical chain is:

### Step 1: Define the Perception Space
The agent receives percepts from sensors. Each percept is drawn from a perception set $\mathcal{P}$.

$$\mathcal{P} = \{p_1, p_2, p_3, \ldots, p_n\}$$

### Step 2: Construct the Percept Sequence
At time $t$, the agent has observed a sequence:

$$\vec{p}_t = (p_1, p_2, p_3, \ldots, p_t)$$

The set of all possible percept sequences is denoted $\mathcal{P}^*$, which includes the empty sequence $\epsilon$ (agent has seen nothing yet).

$$\mathcal{P}^* = \{\epsilon\} \cup \mathcal{P} \cup \mathcal{P}^2 \cup \mathcal{P}^3 \cup \cdots$$

### Step 3: Define the Action Set
The agent can execute one of a finite (or infinite) number of actions:

$$\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_m\}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Agent Function
The agent function maps each percept sequence to exactly one action:

$$\begin{aligned}
f: \mathcal{P}^* &\longrightarrow \mathcal{A} \\
\vec{p}_t &\longmapsto a_t = f(\vec{p}_t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** Given that the agent has seen the percept sequence $\vec{p}_t$, it consults its internal function $f$ and outputs the corresponding action $a_t$. This action is sent to the effectors.

### Step 5: Measure Performance
The performance measure $J$ evaluates the agent's behavior over the entire history $\mathcal{H} = (\vec{p}_1, a_1, \vec{p}_2, a_2, \ldots)$:

$$J(\mathcal{H}) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} w_t \cdot \text{reward}_t$$

Where $w_t$ is the weighting factor for time step $t$ (often $w_t = \gamma^t$ for discounted reward in reinforcement learning, with $0 \leq \gamma < 1$).

### Step 6: Define Rationality Formally
A rational agent chooses the action that maximizes **expected performance**:

$$\begin{aligned}
a_t^* &= \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}\left[\, J(\mathcal{H}_{t+1}) \mid \vec{p}_t, a \,\right] \\
&= \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $s$ = current state (inferred from percepts)
- $s'$ = next possible state
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$ = probability of transitioning to $s'$ after action $a$ in state $s$
- $U(s')$ = utility (score) of being in state $s'$

**Conversion logic:** The agent does not know the future with certainty, so it averages over all possible outcomes, weighted by their probabilities, and picks the action that yields the highest expected utility.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Environment Properties to Agent Architectures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **KTU high-yield analytical tool** — the mapping between environment properties (rows) and the appropriate agent architecture (columns).

| Environment Property ↓ / Agent Type → | Simple Reflex | Model-Based Reflex | Goal-Based | Utility-Based | Learning Agent |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fully Observable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable |
| Partially Observable | ❌ Fails | ✅ Suitable | ⚠️ Limited | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Suitable |
| Deterministic | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable |
| Stochastic | ❌ Fails | ⚠️ Limited | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable |
| Episodic | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ❌ Overkill | ❌ Overkill | ✅ Suitable |
| Sequential | ❌ Fails | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable |
| Static | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable |
| Dynamic | ❌ Fails | ⚠️ Limited | ⚠️ Limited | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Suitable |
| Discrete | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable |
| Continuous | ❌ Difficult | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable |
| Single Agent | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable |
| Multi-Agent | ❌ Fails | ❌ Fails | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Suitable | ✅ Suitable |

Legend: ✅ = Suitable | ⚠️ = Limited use | ❌ = Inadequate

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Vacuum World Agent Function

Consider a **2-cell vacuum world** with cells A and B. The agent can be in A, B, or it is already clean. Percepts and actions are discrete.

### Percept Set Definition

$$\mathcal{P} = \{\text{[A,Dirty]}, \text{[A,Clean]}, \text{[B,Dirty]}, \text{[B,Clean]}\}$$

### Action Set Definition

$$\mathcal{A} = \{\text{Left}, \text{Right}, \text{Suck}, \text{NoOp}\}$$

### Agent Function Table (Sample KTU Question Pattern)

| Percept Sequence | Action $a = f(\vec{p})$ |
|---|---|
| $[\text{A,Dirty}]$ | $\text{Suck}$ |
| $[\text{A,Clean}]$ | $\text{Right}$ |
| $[\text{B,Dirty}]$ | $\text{Suck}$ |
| $[\text{B,Clean}]$ | $\text{Left}$ |
| $[\text{A,Dirty}], [\text{A,Clean}]$ | $\text{Right}$ |
| $[\text{A,Dirty}], [\text{A,Dirty}]$ | $\text{Suck}$ (must be wrong — impossible state, agent loop) |

### Performance Measure (KTU Board Standard)
$$J = +1 \text{ per clean square per time step}, -1 \text{ per action (optional)}$$

The rational agent maximizes the cumulative reward over the agent's operational horizon.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Perception-Action Cycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This is the **canonical KTU diagram** that examiners expect to see drawn in answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV1[ENVIRONMENT] -->|provides percept p_t| SEN1[SENSORS]
    SEN1 -->|raw signal| PRE1[PERCEPT MODULE]
    PRE1 -->|percept p_t| AGT1[AGENT CORE]
    AGT1 -->|queries| INT1[INTERNAL STATE / MODEL]
    INT1 -->|updates state| AGT1
    AGT1 -->|action a_t| EFF1[EFFECTORS / ACTUATORS]
    EFF1 -->|executes a_t| ENV2[ENVIRONMENT MODIFIED]
    ENV2 -->|new state s_t+1| ENV1
    AGT1 -.->|evaluated by| PM1[PERFORMANCE MEASURE J]
    PM1 -.->|feedback signal| AGT1
```

**Flow Explanation:**
1. The **Environment** generates state information that is captured by **Sensors**.
2. **Percept Module** translates raw sensor signals into a usable percept $p_t$.
3. **Agent Core** (the brain) processes $p_t$ along with internal state to decide on action $a_t$.
4. **Effectors** physically execute $a_t$, modifying the environment.
5. The **Performance Measure** evaluates the agent's behavior and provides a feedback signal.

---

## 4.2 The Five Agent Architectures (Block Diagrams)

### A. Simple Reflex Agent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PERCEPT p_t] --> COND1{CONDITION-ACTION RULE}
    RULES1[IF-THEN RULES] --> COND1
    COND1 --> A1[ACTION a_t]
```

**Logic:** Sensor percept $\rightarrow$ directly map to action via pre-defined rules. No memory.

### B. Model-Based Reflex Agent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P2[PERCEPT p_t] --> ST2[STATE UPDATE]
    HIST2[HOW THE WORLD EVOLVES] --> ST2
    SENS2[HOW SENSORS WORK] --> ST2
    ST2 --> INFER2[INFER WORLD STATE s_t]
    INFER2 --> COND2{CONDITION-ACTION RULE}
    RULES2[IF-THEN RULES] --> COND2
    COND2 --> A2[ACTION a_t]
```

**Logic:** Maintains an internal model of how the world evolves and how sensors work, to handle partial observability.

### C. Goal-Based Agent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P3[PERCEPT p_t] --> ST3[STATE UPDATE]
    ST3 --> GOAL3[GIVEN GOAL G]
    GOAL3 --> SEARCH3[SEARCH / PLANNING]
    SEARCH3 --> A3[ACTION a_t]
```

**Logic:** Uses search/planning to find sequences of actions that achieve the goal.

### D. Utility-Based Agent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P4[PERCEPT p_t] --> ST4[STATE UPDATE]
    ST4 --> UTIL4[UTILITY FUNCTION U]
    UTIL4 --> EVAL4[EVALUATE & COMPARE]
    EVAL4 --> A4[ACTION a_t = argmax U]
```

**Logic:** Uses a utility function $U: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ to rank states/actions and pick the maximum-utility action.

### E. Learning Agent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P5[PERCEPT p_t] --> PERF5[PERFORMANCE ELEMENT]
    CRIT5[CRITIC] -->|reward feedback| PERF5
    PERF5 --> ACT5[ACTION a_t]
    ACT5 --> ENV5[ENVIRONMENT]
    ENV5 -->|new percept| P5
    PERF5 --> LEARN5[LEARNING ELEMENT]
    LEARN5 -->|updates| PROBGEN5[PROBLEM GENERATOR]
    PROBGEN5 -->|suggests exploratory actions| PERF5
```

**Logic:** The performance element chooses actions; the critic evaluates them; the learning element modifies the performance element; the problem generator suggests exploratory actions to improve learning.

---

## 4.3 Agent Type Decision Tree (Environment Properties → Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[NEW AI TASK] --> Q1{Environment Fully Observable?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Only Current Percept Sufficient?}
    Q1 -->|No| MBA[Use Model-Based Reflex Agent]
    Q2 -->|Yes| SRA[Use Simple Reflex Agent]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need to Achieve a Specific Goal?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| GBA[Use Goal-Based Agent]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need to Compare Trade-offs?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| UBA[Use Utility-Based Agent]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Environment Changes Over Time?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| LA[Use Learning Agent]
    Q5 -->|No| SRA
```

---

## 4.4 PEAS Framework Visualization (Cross-Domain Map)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TASK1[AUTONOMOUS TAXI DRIVER]
        P1E[Performance: Safety, Speed, Profit] --> P1A
        P1A[Environment: Roads, Traffic, Weather] --> P1AC
        P1AC[Actuators: Steering, Brake, Signal] --> P1S
        P1S[Sensors: Camera, GPS, Lidar]
    end
    subgraph TASK2[MEDICAL DIAGNOSIS SYSTEM]
        P2E[Performance: Correct Diagnosis Rate] --> P2A
        P2A[Environment: Hospital, Patient Records] --> P2AC
        P2AC[Actuators: Display, Alert, Prescription] --> P2S
        P2S[Sensors: Blood Test, X-ray, Thermometer]
    end
    subgraph TASK3[CHESS PLAYING AI]
        P3E[Performance: Win/Draw/Loss Rate] --> P3A
        P3A[Environment: 8x8 Board, Opponent] --> P3AC
        P3AC[Actuators: Move Selector] --> P3S
        P3S[Sensors: Board Position Encoder]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Part A) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q1.** Define an **Intelligent Agent**. List and briefly explain any **four properties of task environments**.

**Model Answer:**

An **Intelligent Agent** is any entity that perceives its environment through sensors and acts upon it through effectors to achieve specific goals. It maps percept sequences to actions in a way that maximizes a performance measure.

Four properties of task environments:

1. **Fully Observable vs. Partially Observable**: Whether sensors capture the complete state of the environment.
2. **Deterministic vs. Stochastic**: Whether the next state is fully determined by current state and action.
3. **Episodic vs. Sequential**: Whether each decision is independent of previous decisions.
4. **Static vs. Dynamic**: Whether the environment changes while the agent deliberates.

*(Any four properties accepted, with at least one-word distinction clearly explained.)*

---

### Question 2 (Part A) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q2.** What is the **PEAS framework**? Apply it to a **Part-Picking Robot** in a manufacturing assembly line.

**Model Answer:**

**PEAS** stands for **Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors** — it is a systematic method to specify a task environment for an AI agent.

For a **Part-Picking Robot**:

| PEAS Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Performance** | Number of parts correctly picked per hour, accuracy, low error rate, energy efficiency |
| **Environment** | Conveyor belt, parts of varying shapes/sizes, factory lighting, other robots |
| **Actuators** | Robotic arm, gripper, suction cup, conveyor control |
| **Sensors** | Camera, infrared sensor, force sensor, position encoders |

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q(A). (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **structure of a Simple Reflex Agent** and **Model-Based Reflex Agent**. Compare their performance in a partially observable environment. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Simple Reflex Agent
The Simple Reflex Agent selects actions based only on the **current percept**, ignoring the rest of the percept history. It uses a set of **condition-action rules** of the form:

$$\text{IF } \text{condition} \text{ THEN } \text{action}$$

For example: `IF car-in-front-is-braking THEN initiate-braking`.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[PERCEPT p_t] --> SC1{CONDITION-ACTION RULES}
    SR1[Rule Base] --> SC1
    SC1 --> SA1[ACTION a_t]
```

#### Model-Based Reflex Agent
The Model-Based Reflex Agent maintains an **internal state** that depends on the percept history and uses two models:
- **Model of how the world evolves**: $T: S \times A \rightarrow P(S')$
- **Model of how the sensors work**: noise model for missing data.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P2[PERCEPT p_t] --> SU[STATE UPDATE]
    HW[HOW THE WORLD EVOLVES] --> SU
    HS[HOW SENSORS WORK] --> SU
    SU --> IS[INFERRED WORLD STATE s_t]
    IS --> CR{CONDITION-ACTION RULES}
    RB[Rule Base] --> CR
    CR --> AC[ACTION a_t]
```

#### Comparison in Partially Observable Environment

| Aspect | Simple Reflex | Model-Based Reflex |
|---|---|---|
| Memory required | None | Requires state tracking |
| Performance in partial observability | ❌ **Poor** — current percept alone insufficient | ✅ **Good** — internal model infers hidden state |
| Computational cost | Low | Moderate |
| Example failure | Same percept in two different states yields same (wrong) action | Maintains internal state, can differentiate percepts with same observation |

**Conclusion**: A Model-Based Reflex Agent is strictly superior in partially observable environments because the internal state compensates for missing information.

---

**Q(A). (b)** Explain the **Goal-Based Agent** and **Utility-Based Agent** architectures with diagrams. Under what conditions should one prefer utility-based over goal-based? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Goal-Based Agent
A Goal-Based Agent uses **goal information** to choose actions that lead to a desired state. Goals are described as a set of states to be achieved. The agent uses **search** and **planning** algorithms.

$$a_t = \arg\min_{a} \text{distance}(\text{current\_state}, \text{goal\_state})$$

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P3[PERCEPT p_t] --> ST3[STATE UPDATE]
    GOAL3[GIVEN GOAL G] --> SEARCH3[SEARCH / PLANNING]
    ST3 --> SEARCH3
    SEARCH3 --> A3[ACTION a_t]
```

#### Utility-Based Agent
When there are **conflicting goals** or **trade-offs** (e.g., speed vs. safety), a Utility-Based Agent uses a **utility function** $U: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that maps each state to a real number representing its "happiness."

$$a_t^* = \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}[U(s') \mid s, a]$$

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P4[PERCEPT p_t] --> ST4[STATE UPDATE]
    ST4 --> UFN[UTILITY FUNCTION U]
    UFN --> EVAL[EVALUATE & COMPARE]
    EVAL --> A4[ACTION a_t = argmax U]
```

#### When to Prefer Utility-Based Over Goal-Based

1. **Multiple conflicting goals**: When objectives trade off (e.g., delivery time vs. fuel cost vs. safety).
2. **Goal uncertainty**: When the goal cannot be specified precisely.
3. **Stochastic environments**: When actions have probabilistic outcomes, expected utility is the proper optimization criterion.
4. **Likelihood of success matters**: When two paths reach the goal but one is more likely to succeed.
5. **Need for balanced trade-offs**: When the agent must balance short-term vs. long-term rewards.

**Conclusion**: Utility-Based Agents are more general and powerful, but require a well-defined utility function (which can be hard to elicit). Goal-Based Agents are sufficient when goals are well-defined and there are no trade-offs.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q(B). (a)** Define **Rational Agent**. Explain why rationality does not imply omniscience. Give the formal rationality condition with equation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Definition of Rational Agent
> [!NOTE]
> **A Rational Agent is one that for each possible percept sequence, selects an action that is expected to maximize its performance measure, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and any prior built-in knowledge.**

Formally:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Rational Action } a^* &= \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}\left[\, \text{Performance}(a) \mid \text{percept history } \vec{p}_t \,\right] \\
&= \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')
\end{aligned}$$

#### Rationality ≠ Omniscience

| Aspect | Rational Agent | Omniscient Agent |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge | Knows what it has perceived | Knows the **actual** outcome of its actions |
| Performance | Maximizes **expected** performance | Maximizes **actual** performance |
| Real-world applicability | ✅ Achievable | ❌ Impossible |
| Use of information gathering | ✅ Yes, can perform exploratory actions to reduce uncertainty | ❌ Not needed |
| Example | Self-driving car anticipates a pedestrian may step out | Self-driving car knows with certainty that no pedestrian will appear |

**Why Rationality ≠ Omniscience:**

1. **Limited sensors**: An agent cannot perceive what is not measurable.
2. **Stochastic environments**: Even perfect information about the past cannot predict the future with certainty.
3. **Computational limits**: Real agents have finite memory and processing time.
4. **Exploration cost**: Sometimes the rational action is to perform an **information-gathering action** that may seem suboptimal in the short run but improves long-term performance.

A rational agent acts optimally **given the information it has**, not the information it wishes it had.

---

**Q(B). (b)** Compare the **five agent architectures** (Simple Reflex, Model-Based Reflex, Goal-Based, Utility-Based, Learning) using a comparison matrix and explain the role of the **Performance Element**, **Critic**, **Learning Element**, and **Problem Generator** in a Learning Agent. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Comparison Matrix of Five Agent Architectures

| Criterion | Simple Reflex | Model-Based | Goal-Based | Utility-Based | Learning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Uses percept history | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Handles partial observability | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Considers future consequences | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Handles conflicting objectives | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Improves over time | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Internal state required | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Computational complexity | Low | Medium | High | High | Very High |

#### Components of a Learning Agent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P5[PERCEPT p_t] --> PE[PERFORMANCE ELEMENT]
    CR[CRITIC] -->|performance feedback| PE
    PE --> ACT[ACTION a_t]
    ACT --> ENV[ENVIRONMENT]
    ENV -->|new percept| P5
    PE --> LE[LEARNING ELEMENT]
    LE -->|updates| PG[PROBLEM GENERATOR]
    PG -->|exploratory actions| PE
```

**1. Performance Element:**
- The **"brain"** of the agent — selects actions based on percepts.
- Equivalent to the entire agent in the previous four architectures.
- Takes percepts and current knowledge, returns actions.

**2. Critic:**
- Evaluates how well the agent is doing with respect to a fixed **performance standard**.
- Takes as input the percepts and outputs a **reward signal** (a scalar measure of performance).
- Essential because the percepts themselves do not indicate success or failure.

**3. Learning Element:**
- Modifies the **performance element** so that future actions lead to better rewards.
- Uses feedback from the critic to improve internal knowledge/representations.
- Implements the actual learning algorithm (e.g., neural network weight updates, decision tree modification).

**4. Problem Generator:**
- Suggests **exploratory actions** that may lead to new, informative experiences.
- Causes the agent to take actions that are not necessarily optimal in the short term but lead to better learning in the long term.
- Responsible for the **exploration vs. exploitation trade-off** in reinforcement learning.

**Conclusion**: Learning Agents are the most powerful and flexible because they can improve their own performance over time. They combine all the capabilities of the other four architectures with the additional ability to learn from experience.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks on Agents & Environments Questions**
>
> 1. **Conflating Rationality with Omniscience**: Many students define a rational agent as one that "always makes the best decision." Wrong! It must include the qualifier **"given the percept sequence to date."** (-1 to -2 marks)
>
> 2. **Confusing Agent Function with Agent Program**: Agent function is the abstract mathematical mapping $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$. Agent program is its physical implementation. Examiners test this distinction directly. (-1 mark)
>
> 3. **Incomplete PEAS Description**: Forgetting any one of the four elements (especially Actuators or Sensors) is a **2-mark deduction** on a 7-mark question.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Episodic and Sequential**: Students often claim chess is episodic because each move is a "decision." It is **sequential** because early moves determine the strategic context of late moves.
>
> 5. **Forgetting diagrams in 7-mark questions**: A block diagram is **mandatory** for any question about agent architecture. A textual answer without a diagram typically receives only **5 of 7 marks**.
>
> 6. **Static vs. Dynamic confusion**: Crossword puzzle is **static** (the letters don't move while you think). Driving a car is **dynamic** (other cars keep moving).
>
> 7. **Not differentiating Fully vs. Partially Observable**: A taxi driver is **partially observable** (cannot see behind blind corners). A chess board is **fully observable** (the entire state is visible).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Agents and Environments: High-Density Revision Summary**

### Core Definitions
- **Agent**: Anything that perceives its environment via sensors and acts upon it via effectors.
- **Agent Function**: $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ — maps every possible percept sequence to an action.
- **Agent Program**: The internal implementation of the agent function.
- **Rationality**: Acting to maximize expected performance measure given the percept history.
- **Autonomy**: Ability to act based on own experience, not just built-in knowledge.

### PEAS Framework (Always Specify All Four)
- **P**erformance, **E**nvironment, **A**ctuators, **S**ensors

### 8 Task Environment Properties
- Fully / Partially Observable
- Deterministic / Stochastic
- Episodic / Sequential
- Static / Dynamic / Semidynamic
- Discrete / Continuous
- Single / Multi-Agent
- Known / Unknown
- Accessible / Inaccessible

### Five Agent Architectures (Increasing Capability)
1. **Simple Reflex** — current percept only, condition-action rules
2. **Model-Based Reflex** — internal state, world model
3. **Goal-Based** — uses goals, search/planning
4. **Utility-Based** — uses utility function for trade-offs
5. **Learning Agent** — performance element + critic + learning element + problem generator

### Critical Equations
- Agent Function: $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$
- Rational Action: $a^* = \arg\max_a \mathbb{E}[J \mid \vec{p}_t, a]$
- Transition Model: $T: S \times A \rightarrow P(S)$
- Utility Maximization: $a^* = \arg\max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \cdot U(s')$
- Performance Score: $J = \sum_{t=1}^T w_t \cdot \text{reward}_t$

### Key Distinctions (Frequently Tested)
- Rational ≠ Omniscient (expected vs. actual)
- Agent Function (abstract) vs. Agent Program (concrete)
- Static ≠ Discrete (static is about time change, discrete is about state space)
- Stochastic vs. Strategic (strategic = deterministic except for other agents)

### Recommended Study Order for Exam Day
1. Memorize the agent function notation $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$.
2. Practice PEAS for 5–6 random task environments (taxi, medical, chess, vacuum, tutoring system).
3. Draw all five agent block diagrams from memory.
4. Be ready to apply all 8 environment properties to any new scenario given in the exam.
5. Be able to compare any two agent architectures in a matrix format.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Intelligent Agent?

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with Russell & Norvig's foundational AI framework), an **Intelligent Agent** is defined as:

> **Definition (KTU / Russell & Norvig):** *An agent is anything that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through sensors and acting upon that environment through actuators. An Intelligent Agent is one that does the "right thing" — selects actions that maximize its performance measure, based on the percept sequence and built-in knowledge.*

Mathematically expressed as:

$$ f : P^{*} \rightarrow A $$

Where:
- $P$ = Set of all possible percepts at any given instant
- $P^{*}$ = Set of all finite sequences of percepts (history)
- $A$ = Set of all possible actions the agent can perform
- $f$ = Agent function (mapping from percept history to action)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The agent function is a *theoretical* abstraction. The *agent program* is the concrete implementation of this function running on a physical architecture. Examiners frequently test the distinction between **Agent Function** (mathematical ideal) and **Agent Program** (real-world implementation).

## 1.2 What are "Properties" of Intelligent Agents?

The term **Properties** in Module 1 of PEMUT755 refers to the **essential characteristics** that distinguish a *rational* (intelligent) agent from a trivial, mechanical program. These properties define *what it means* to be an intelligent entity in the AI sense.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Properties (KTU 2024 Module 1 Syllabus):**
> 1. **Autonomy**
> 2. **Reactivity**
> 3. **Pro-activeness (Goal-Directedness)**
> 4. **Social Ability**
> 5. **Adaptivity / Learning**
> 6. **Mobility** (for software agents)
> 7. **Rationality**
> 8. **Veracity / Benevolence** (optional properties)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Autonomous Delivery Robot" 🤖

Imagine a **food delivery robot** navigating a busy college canteen:
