| Robot Component | Mapping to AI Property | Explanation |
|-----------------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Sensors (cameras, LIDAR)** | Perception | Robot *sees* tables, students, walls |
| **Decides own path** | **Autonomy** | Operates without constant human control |
| **Reacts to "STOP" raised hand** | **Reactivity** | Responds to dynamic changes immediately |
| **Plans shortest route to Table 7** | **Pro-activeness** | Takes initiative to *achieve goals*, not just react |
| **Says "Excuse me" to crowd** | **Social Ability** | Interacts with other agents (humans, robots) |
| **Learns which table orders most chai** | **Adaptivity/Learning** | Improves performance over time |
| **Moves between floors** | **Mobility** | Can relocate in its environment |

This single analogy maps all 8 properties — making it the most powerful memory anchor for the KTU exam.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "list properties of an intelligent agent," always begin with the **Wooldridge triad (Autonomy + Reactivity + Pro-activeness + Social Ability)** — this is the textbook grouping KTU examiners expect.

## 1.4 Why These Properties Matter in AI Engineering

These properties are **not arbitrary** — they are the *minimum operational criteria* that separate an AI system from a conventional deterministic program. A simple calculator that adds 2+2 is **not** an agent (it has no autonomy, no goal, no environment perception). A self-driving car that navigates traffic **is** an agent because it exhibits *all* core properties.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Core Properties — Deep Analysis

### 🔹 Property 1: Autonomy
- **Definition:** The ability of an agent to operate **without direct human (or other agent) intervention**, controlling its own actions and internal state.
- **Why it matters:** Distinguishes true AI from teleoperated systems. A Mars rover acting on its own rock-avoidance logic is autonomous; a drone controlled via joystick is not.
- **Mathematical Ideal:** The agent function $f$ should depend on *its own percept history*, not on external commands after deployment.

$$ \text{Autonomy} \propto \frac{\text{Decisions made by agent}}{\text{Total decisions required}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Full autonomy is a *spectrum*, not a binary. A thermostat has *limited* autonomy; a self-driving Tesla has *high* autonomy.

### 🔹 Property 2: Reactivity
- **Definition:** The ability to **perceive the environment and respond in a timely fashion** to changes that affect its goal.
- **Logical Condition:**
$$ \text{Reactive} \iff \forall \Delta E \neq 0, \exists \text{action } a \text{ at time } t \leq T_{\text{deadline}} $$
- **Engineering Example:** A collision-avoidance system in an autonomous vehicle reacts to an obstacle within **milliseconds** (real-time reactivity).

### 🔹 Property 3: Pro-activeness (Goal-Directedness)
- **Definition:** The ability to **exhibit goal-directed behavior** by taking the initiative rather than merely reacting to the environment.
- **Contrast with Reactivity:** Reactivity = "respond to what happens." Pro-activeness = "anticipate and plan."
- **Example:** A chess engine doesn't just react to your move; it *plans* 10 moves ahead toward a checkmate.

### 🔹 Property 4: Social Ability
- **Definition:** The capacity to **interact with other agents (or humans)** through communication, cooperation, coordination, and negotiation.
- **Communication Languages:** FIPA-ACL, KQML, or natural language.
- **Example:** Multi-agent systems in logistics (FedEx, Amazon) where delivery bots *coordinate* to avoid path collisions.

### 🔹 Property 5: Adaptivity / Learning
- **Definition:** The ability to **improve performance over time** based on experience. Formalized as a feedback loop:

$$ \text{Performance}(t+1) > \text{Performance}(t) \text{ given more data} $$

- **Implementation:** Reinforcement Learning, Neural Networks, Bayesian updates, Genetic Algorithms.

### 🔹 Property 6: Mobility
- **Definition:** The ability of a *software agent* to **travel across distributed network environments** to fulfill its goal.
- **Example:** An Internet bot migrating from one server to another to balance load or gather data.

### 🔹 Property 7: Rationality
- **Definition:** For each possible percept sequence, a **rational agent should select an action that is expected to maximize its performance measure**, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and the agent's built-in knowledge.
- **Russell & Norvig Definition:**
$$ a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in A} \sum_{p \in P^{*}} \Pr(p \mid a) \cdot U(p) $$
  Where $U(p)$ is the utility of the outcome.

### 🔹 Property 8: Veracity & Benevolence (Optional)
- **Veracity:** Agent will not knowingly communicate false information.
- **Benevolence:** Agent will do what is asked without conflicting goals.
- **Use Case:** Cooperative multi-agent systems, human-robot interaction in healthcare.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Symbol / Condition | Core Question Answered | Real-World Example |
|---|----------|-------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Autonomy** | $f : P^{*} \to A$ independent of $H_{\text{ext}}$ | "Can it act alone?" | Mars Rover |
| 2 | **Reactivity** | $\Delta E \Rightarrow \text{action within } T_{d}$ | "Does it respond to changes?" | Anti-lock Braking |
| 3 | **Pro-activeness** | $\exists \text{ plan } \pi : \text{lead to goal } G$ | "Does it take initiative?" | Chess Engine |
| 4 | **Social Ability** | $\text{Comm}(Agent_{1}, Agent_{2}) \neq \emptyset$ | "Can it cooperate?" | Swarm Drones |
| 5 | **Adaptivity** | $\text{Perf}(t+1) > \text{Perf}(t)$ | "Does it learn?" | Netflix Recommender |
| 6 | **Mobility** | $\text{Migrate}(\text{Agent}, \text{Node}_{i} \to \text{Node}_{j})$ | "Can it travel in networks?" | Internet Crawler |
| 7 | **Rationality** | $a^{*} = \arg\max E[U]$ | "Does it pick best action?" | Google Maps Routing |
| 8 | **Veracity** | $\text{Trust}(m) = \text{True}$ for all $m$ sent | "Does it tell truth?" | Medical AI Assistant |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical pipe symbols used in mathematics above (e.g., in $\arg\max$ expressions and conditional notation) have been written outside the table to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Relationship Between Properties — Engineering Utility

These properties are **not independent** — they form a hierarchy:

$$ \text{Rationality} \supset \text{Autonomy} \cup \text{Reactivity} \cup \text{Pro-activeness} \cup \text{Social Ability} $$

In real engineering:
- A **reactive agent** is the *simplest* (e.g., thermostat) → lacks pro-activeness.
- A **deliberative agent** is *goal-based* → adds pro-activeness.
- A **learning agent** adds adaptivity → most powerful and complex.
- A **hybrid agent** combines reactive + deliberative → state-of-the-art robotics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Generic Program to Intelligent Agent

This derivation walks through the **logical progression** used to justify why these properties are *necessary* — a common KTU exam framing.

### Step 1: Define a Generic Computational Program

$$ \text{Program} : I \rightarrow O $$

Where $I$ = set of all inputs and $O$ = set of all outputs. This is a *pure function* with no notion of environment.

### Step 2: Introduce an Environment

$$ \text{Environment} : E = \{e_{1}, e_{2}, e_{3}, \dots, e_{n}\} $$

The program must now interact with an environment $E$ that *changes over time* and is not fully under the program's control.

### Step 3: Define Perception

The program gains *sensors* and begins perceiving the environment. At time $t$, the percept is $p_{t} \in P$.

$$ \text{Percept Sequence} = \langle p_{1}, p_{2}, p_{3}, \dots, p_{t} \rangle \in P^{*} $$

This step **introduces the need for Reactivity** — because the environment changes, the program must respond.

### Step 4: Define Action

The program gains *actuators* and produces an action $a_{t} \in A$ that modifies the environment.

$$ \text{Action at time } t = f(p_{1}, p_{2}, \dots, p_{t}) $$

### Step 5: Introduce a Performance Measure (Utility)

To make the program "intelligent," we must define what "doing well" means:

$$ U : E \times A^{*} \rightarrow \mathbb{R} $$

The agent must choose $a_{t}$ to **maximize expected cumulative utility**.

### Step 6: Derive the Properties as Necessary Conditions

For a program to be *called* an intelligent agent given Steps 1–5, the following **must** hold:

1. **Autonomy** — derived from Step 5: utility maximization requires independent decision-making, not external commands.
2. **Reactivity** — derived from Step 3: changing percepts require response.
3. **Pro-activeness** — derived from Step 5: long-term utility maximization requires *planning* (acting for future reward).
4. **Social Ability** — derived from the observation that environments often contain *other agents* whose actions affect $E$.
5. **Adaptivity** — derived from the assumption that $E$ is partially unknown; the agent must *learn* $E$'s dynamics.
6. **Rationality** — this is the *overarching axiom*: $f$ must select the action that maximizes $E[U]$.

### Step 7: Formal Rational Action Selection

$$ a_{t}^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in A} E\left[ U(s_{t+1}) \mid p_{1:t}, a \right] $$

Where $s_{t+1}$ is the next state of the environment, $p_{1:t}$ is the percept history, and $U$ is the utility function.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Property Fulfillment Across Agent Types

This matrix is a **favorite KTU 14-mark question** because it tests both theoretical knowledge and application.

| Agent Type | Autonomy | Reactivity | Pro-activeness | Social Ability | Adaptivity |
|------------|----------|------------|----------------|----------------|------------|
| **Simple Reflex Agent** | Low | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Model-Based Reflex Agent** | Medium | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Goal-Based Agent** | Medium | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Utility-Based Agent** | High | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Learning Agent** | High | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Multi-Agent System (MAS)** | High | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Autonomous Vehicle (Full Stack)** | High | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** When asked "Which agent has property X?", always quote a *real-world* example. Examiners award bonus marks for engineering context.

---

## 3.3 Property Deduction from PEAS Framework

The **PEAS** acronym (Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors) is the standard way to *operationalize* the properties. Given any problem, you can derive which properties the agent must have.

**Example Derivation — Autonomous Vacuum Cleaner:**

1. **Performance:** Clean floor, minimize time, avoid collisions.
2. **Environment:** Room with furniture, pets, humans, dirt.
3. **Actuators:** Wheels, brushes, vacuum motor.
4. **Sensors:** Bump sensors, dirt sensor, camera.

**Properties derived:**
- *Autonomy* → No human control after "start."
- *Reactivity* → Bump sensor triggers immediate stop/turn.
- *Pro-activeness* → Plans a coverage path using SLAM.
- *Adaptivity* → Learns room layout over time.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture — The Intelligent Agent Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENV["ENVIRONMENT"]
        stateA["Current State S_t"]
        stateB["Updated State S_t+1"]
    end
    subgraph AGENT["INTELLIGENT AGENT"]
        sensorA["Sensors"]
        perceptA["Percept P_t"]
        perfA["Performance Measure"]
        funcA["Agent Function F"]
        progA["Agent Program"]
        actA["Actuators"]
        learnA["Learning Module"]
    end
    stateA --> sensorA
    sensorA --> perceptA
    perceptA --> funcA
    funcA --> progA
    progA --> actA
    actA --> stateB
    stateB --> stateA
    learnA -.feedback.-> funcA
    perfA -.evaluates.-> actA
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **Environment** changes state from $S_t$ to $S_{t+1}$ based on the agent's action.
- **Sensors** perceive the current state → produce a **Percept**.
- The **Agent Function** $f$ maps the percept to a desired action.
- The **Agent Program** implements $f$ in hardware/software.
- **Actuators** execute the action, modifying the environment.
- The **Learning Module** provides feedback to improve $f$ over time (→ Adaptivity).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Property Hierarchy & Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["RATIONALITY"] --> node2["AUTONOMY"]
    node1 --> node3["REACTIVITY"]
    node1 --> node4["PRO ACTIVENESS"]
    node1 --> node5["SOCIAL ABILITY"]
    node2 --> node6["SELF GOVERNED DECISIONS"]
    node3 --> node7["REAL TIME RESPONSE"]
    node4 --> node8["GOAL DRIVEN PLANNING"]
    node5 --> node9["AGENT TO AGENT COMMUNICATION"]
    node8 --> node10["LEARNING AND ADAPTATION"]
    node9 --> node10
    node6 --> node10
    node7 --> node10
```

**Explanation of the flow:**
- **RATIONALITY** is the *umbrella property* from which all others are derived.
- The four core operational properties (Autonomy, Reactivity, Pro-activeness, Social Ability) are *first-class* characteristics.
- All four converge into **LEARNING AND ADAPTATION**, which is the *meta-property* that enables long-term intelligence.

---

## 4.3 Agent-Type Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Simple Reflex Agent"] --> A2["Model Based Reflex Agent"]
    A2 --> A3["Goal Based Agent"]
    A3 --> A4["Utility Based Agent"]
    A4 --> A5["Learning Agent"]
    A5 --> A6["Full Intelligent Agent with ALL Properties"]
    style A1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style A6 fill:#90EE90
```

This shows the **evolution of intelligence** — each subsequent agent type adds properties to the previous one. The final "Full Intelligent Agent" satisfies all 8 properties discussed in Section 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define an intelligent agent. List any four essential properties of an intelligent agent.**

**Model Answer:**

An intelligent agent is anything that can perceive its environment through sensors, and act upon that environment through actuators, in a way that maximizes a performance measure over time.

The four essential properties are:
1. **Autonomy** — operates without direct external intervention.
2. **Reactivity** — responds in a timely manner to changes in the environment.
3. **Pro-activeness** — exhibits goal-directed behavior by taking initiative.
4. **Social Ability** — interacts and cooperates with other agents.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing properties with brief explanation)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between reactivity and pro-activeness in an intelligent agent. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Reactivity | Pro-activeness |
|--------|------------|----------------|
| Trigger | External change in environment | Internal goal/plan |
| Time horizon | Immediate / short-term | Long-term |
| Nature | Reactive (response-based) | Deliberative (plan-based) |
| Example | Anti-lock Braking System stops car when wheel slips | Chess engine plans 10 moves ahead for checkmate |

In short, **reactivity** is *responding* to the environment, whereas **pro-activeness** is *initiating* actions to achieve future goals.

*(3 marks: 2 for differentiation table + 1 for examples)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse *agent function* with *agent program* — this is a 2-mark deduction point.
> - Always **define** a property before *explaining* it; blindly listing properties = partial credit only.
> - Avoid writing "AI" or "intelligent" without a technical anchor — examiners expect engineering rigor.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Define an intelligent agent formally. With a neat diagram, explain the structure of an intelligent agent and describe how it interacts with the environment using the agent function. (7 marks)

**(b)** List and explain any seven essential properties of an intelligent agent. For each property, provide one real-world engineering example. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Definition:**
An intelligent agent is a system that perceives its environment through sensors, maintains an internal state, and acts upon the environment through actuators, where its action selection is governed by an **agent function** that maps percept history to action, with the goal of maximizing a **performance measure** (or utility).

Mathematically:
$$ f : P^{*} \rightarrow A $$

**Structure (Refer to Mermaid Diagram in Section 4.1):**
1. **Sensors** capture raw data from the environment.
2. The **Percept** module interprets the raw sensor data.
3. The **Agent Function** $f$ decides the action based on percept history.
4. The **Agent Program** implements $f$ within a physical architecture.
5. **Actuators** execute the chosen action in the environment.
6. The environment transitions to a new state $S_{t+1}$.

**Environment Interaction Loop:**
- At time $t$, environment is in state $S_t$.
- Sensors produce percept $P_t$.
- Agent function computes $a_t = f(P_1, P_2, \dots, P_t)$.
- Actuator performs $a_t$.
- Environment transitions to $S_{t+1}$ → loop continues.

**Distinction between Agent Function and Agent Program:**
- The **agent function** is an *abstract mathematical mapping* (theoretical ideal).
- The **agent program** is the *concrete implementation* running on physical hardware.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

| # | Property | Definition | Real-World Example |
|---|----------|------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Autonomy** | Operates without direct human intervention | Self-driving Tesla |
| 2 | **Reactivity** | Responds to environmental changes in real time | Emergency braking system |
| 3 | **Pro-activeness** | Takes initiative to achieve goals | Google Maps re-routing proactively |
| 4 | **Social Ability** | Communicates and cooperates with other agents | Swarm of delivery drones |
| 5 | **Adaptivity/Learning** | Improves performance with experience | AlphaGo (learned from self-play) |
| 6 | **Mobility** | Migrates across network nodes | Web crawler agent |
| 7 | **Rationality** | Always chooses action maximizing expected utility | Medical diagnosis AI |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a suitable real-world example, explain the concept of an intelligent agent using the **PEAS framework**. Show how PEAS can be used to derive the required properties of the agent. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare and contrast: (i) Simple Reflex Agent vs Goal-Based Agent, (ii) Single-Agent vs Multi-Agent System, in terms of the properties they exhibit. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**PEAS Framework Definition:**
PEAS stands for **Performance measure, Environment, Actuators, Sensors** — a standard descriptive framework for specifying an intelligent agent.

**Real-World Example — Autonomous Taxi Driver:**

| PEAS Component | Specification |
|----------------|---------------|
| **Performance** | Safe trip, fast arrival, fuel efficiency, legal driving, comfort |
| **Environment** | Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather, signs, other vehicles |
| **Actuators** | Steering wheel, accelerator, brake, signal lights, horn, display |
| **Sensors** | Cameras, GPS, LIDAR, speedometer, accelerometer, microphones |

**Property Derivation from PEAS:**
- **Autonomy** → Performance measure requires independent decisions; agent cannot be teleoperated.
- **Reactivity** → Environment changes constantly (traffic, pedestrians) → must respond in real time.
- **Pro-activeness** → Goal of "fast arrival" requires route planning (not just reaction).
- **Social Ability** → Must interact with human passengers, signal to other drivers.
- **Adaptivity** → Must learn from new traffic patterns and passenger preferences.
- **Mobility** → N/A (physical, not software, agent).

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**(i) Simple Reflex Agent vs Goal-Based Agent:**

| Property | Simple Reflex Agent | Goal-Based Agent |
|----------|---------------------|------------------|
| Reactivity | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Pro-activeness | ❌ No (rule-based) | ✅ Yes (plan-based) |
| Memory | Minimal (current percept only) | Maintains goal state |
| Example | Thermostat | GPS navigation |
| Complexity | Low | High |

**(ii) Single-Agent vs Multi-Agent System:**

| Property | Single-Agent | Multi-Agent (MAS) |
|----------|--------------|-------------------|
| Social Ability | ❌ Not required | ✅ Essential |
| Communication | N/A | Required (FIPA-ACL, KQML) |
| Coordination | None | Negotiation protocols |
| Cooperation | None | Cooperative/Competitive |
| Example | Standalone chess engine | Smart grid with 1000s of nodes |

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **Intelligent Agent** is defined by the tuple: $\langle P, A, f, U \rangle$ (Percepts, Actions, Function, Utility).
- ✅ The **agent function** $f : P^{*} \rightarrow A$ is theoretical; the **agent program** is its implementation.
- ✅ The **8 core properties** are: **Autonomy, Reactivity, Pro-activeness, Social Ability, Adaptivity, Mobility, Rationality, Veracity/Benevolence**.
- ✅ **Rationality** is the *overarching property**; all others are *operational instantiations** of it.
- ✅ **Reactivity ≠ Pro-activeness**: Reactivity is *response*; Pro-activeness is *initiative**.
- ✅ **PEAS framework** is the standard way to specify an agent (Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors).
- ✅ **Agent type hierarchy** (in increasing intelligence): Simple Reflex → Model-Based → Goal-Based → Utility-Based → Learning Agent.
- ✅ In KTU 2024, **always start with the definition** before listing properties — examiners reward structured answers.
- ✅ Use **real-world engineering examples** in every answer for higher marks (Tesla, AlphaGo, GPS, Mars Rover).
- ✅ Avoid the trap of confusing *agent function* (math) with *agent program* (code) — this is a frequent deduction point.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Mnemonic for the 8 Properties:** **"A R P S A M R V"** → *Autonomy, Reactivity, Pro-activeness, Social Ability, Adaptivity, Mobility, Rationality, Veracity.* Memorize using the **"Autonomous Delivery Robot"** analogy from Section 1.3.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solving Problems by Searching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solving Problems by Searching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Solving in Artificial Intelligence** is the systematic process of reaching a desired **goal state** from a given **initial state** by applying a sequence of well-defined **actions (operators)**, where the AI agent must intelligently explore the **state space** to find a valid solution path.

In the context of KTU's outcome-based curriculum, *Solving Problems by Searching* is formalized as the **PEAS framework instantiation**, where:
- **Performance Measure**: Optimality of the path found
- **Environment**: Fully observable, deterministic, discrete, known
- **Actuators**: Action-execution operators
- **Sensors**: State-evaluation functions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every search problem in AI is rigorously defined by **four mandatory components**: *Initial State*, *Successor Function (Actions + Transition Model)*, *Goal Test*, and *Path Cost Function*. The fifth component, **Heuristic Function**, distinguishes **Informed** from **Uninformed Search**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Mountain Hiker

Imagine you are **blindfolded** at the base of a mountain range and told that a treasure is hidden on *some peak*. You can:
1. Feel the slope under your feet (local gradient).
2. Remember which slopes you have already tried (visited states).
3. Optionally, be given a **map with approximate elevations** (a heuristic).

You must find the treasure in the *fewest possible steps* without falling off cliffs (invalid states). This is *exactly* what an AI search algorithm does:
- **Blindfolded + no map** → **Uninformed Search (BFS, DFS, UCS)**
- **Blindfolded + map** → **Informed Search (Greedy, A\*)**
- **Climbing the highest slope visible** → **Hill Climbing (Local Search)**
- **Checking GPS directions** → **Heuristic-Guided Search**

### 1.3 Core Search Vocabulary

| Term | Definition | KTU Notation |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **State** | A configuration of the world | $s$ |
| **State Space** | Set of all reachable states | $S$ |
| **Node** | A data structure used in the search tree | $n$ |
| **Initial State** | Where the agent starts | $s_0$ |
| **Action** | Operator that transitions between states | $a$ |
| **Transition Model** | Result of applying an action | $\text{Result}(s, a)$ |
| **Goal Test** | Boolean function: is this state a goal? | $\text{Goal}(s)$ |
| **Path Cost** | Cumulative cost of actions | $g(n)$ |
| **Step Cost** | Cost of a single action | $c(s, a, s')$ |
| **Branching Factor** | Maximum successors per node | $b$ |
| **Depth** | Number of steps from root to node | $d$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of a Well-Defined Search Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A search problem is **mathematically well-defined** if and only if it specifies all five components. KTU exam questions frequently ask students to *formulate* toy problems (e.g., 8-puzzle, vacuum world, Romania tour) using these five components.

1. **Initial State** $s_0$ — The starting configuration. *Example*: In the 8-puzzle, $s_0$ is the arbitrary arrangement of tiles.
2. **Actions** $\text{Actions}(s)$ — The set of legal moves from state $s$. *Example*: Tile slides producing $\{Up, Down, Left, Right\}$.
3. **Transition Model** $\text{Result}(s, a) \rightarrow s'$ — Deterministic mapping from state-action pair to next state.
4. **Goal Test** $\text{Goal}(s) \rightarrow \{\text{True}, \text{False}\}$ — Either an **explicit** set of states or a **property-based** test.
5. **Path Cost** $g(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} c(s_{i-1}, a_i, s_i)$ — Additive cost of traversing the path.

### 2.2 Search Strategy — The Four Evaluation Properties

Every search algorithm is judged on exactly **four properties** (the *gold standard* of KTU exam answers):

- **Completeness**: Is the algorithm guaranteed to find a solution *if one exists*?
- **Optimality**: Does it find the *least-cost* solution?
- **Time Complexity**: How many nodes are generated/expanded?
- **Space Complexity**: How many nodes must be held in memory simultaneously?

### 2.3 The Taxonomy of Search Algorithms

```
                    Search in AI
                         |
            +------------+------------+
            |                         |
      UNINFORMED                 INFORMED
      (Blind Search)        (Heuristic Search)
            |                         |
   +--------+--------+       +---------+---------+
   |   |    |    |   |       |         |         |
  BFS UCS DFS DLS IDS  Greedy-BFS   A*       IDA*
                         Best-First
```

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table — it covers **90% of Part B answers** on search algorithms. The notation $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of shallowest goal, $m$ = maximum depth of state space, $C^*$ = cost of optimal solution, $\varepsilon$ = minimum step cost.

| Algorithm | Completeness | Optimality | Time Complexity | Space Complexity |
|-----------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **BFS** (Breadth-First) | Yes (if $b$ finite) | Yes (uniform cost) | $O(b^{d+1})$ | $O(b^{d+1})$ |
| **UCS** (Uniform-Cost) | Yes (if $\varepsilon > 0$) | Yes | $O(b^{\lfloor C^*/\varepsilon \rfloor + 1})$ | Same as time |
| **DFS** (Depth-First) | No (infinite spaces fail) | No | $O(b^{m})$ | $O(bm)$ |
| **DLS** (Depth-Limited) | No (if limit $< d$) | No | $O(b^{\ell})$ | $O(b\ell)$ |
| **IDS** (Iterative Deepening) | Yes | Yes (uniform cost) | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(bd)$ |
| **Bidirectional** | Yes | Yes | $O(b^{d/2})$ | $O(b^{d/2})$ |
| **Greedy Best-First** | No | No | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ worst | $O(\vert V \vert)$ |
| **A\*** | Yes (if tree-search) | Yes (admissible $h$) | Exponential in error | Keeps all nodes |
| **IDA\*** | Yes | Yes (admissible $h$) | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(bd)$ |
| **SMA\*** | Yes (until memory) | Yes (until memory) | Same as A\* | Limited by memory |

### 2.5 The A\* Master Formula — The Heart of KTU 2024

The single most-tested equation in KTU Module 1:

$$
f(n) \;=\; g(n) \;+\; h(n)
$$

Where:
- $f(n)$ = **Estimated cost of the cheapest solution passing through node $n$**
- $g(n)$ = **Actual cost from start to node $n$** (known exactly)
- $h(n)$ = **Heuristic estimated cost from $n$ to nearest goal** (estimated)

### 2.6 Heuristic Properties (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Admissibility:** A heuristic $h$ is **admissible** if it *never overestimates* the true cost. Formally:
> 
> $$0 \;\leq\; h(n) \;\leq\; h^*(n)$$
> 
> where $h^*(n)$ is the *true* minimum cost from $n$ to any goal. Admissibility guarantees **A\* optimality** in tree search.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consistency (Monotonicity):** A heuristic is **consistent** if for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$ of $n$ generated by action $a$ with cost $c(n, a, n')$:
> 
> $$h(n) \;\leq\; c(n, a, n') \;+\; h(n')$$

> Consistency is a *stronger* property than admissibility. If $h$ is consistent, then $f(n)$ along any path is **non-decreasing**, and A\* using graph search does not need to re-expand nodes.

### 2.7 Dominance Relation Between Heuristics

For two admissible heuristics $h_1$ and $h_2$:
$$
h_2(n) \;\geq\; h_1(n) \quad \text{for all } n \quad \Longrightarrow \quad h_2 \; \text{dominates} \; h_1
$$

The dominating heuristic **always expands fewer or equal nodes**, making A\* more efficient.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Google Maps / Uber / Ola**: Variant of A\* with road-network heuristics.
- **Robotics Path Planning (ROS Navigation Stack)**: A\* and D\* (Dynamic A\*) on occupancy grids.
- **Video Game AI**: BFS/DFS for grid-based pathing; A\* for NavMeshes.
- **Network Routing (OSPF, BGP)**: Dijkstra (a special UCS variant).
- **VLSI Chip Design**: A\* for component placement and wire routing.
- **SAT Solvers & Theorem Provers**: Iterative Deepening variants for proof search.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why A\* is Optimal with an Admissible Heuristic

**Setup:** We are running **A\*** tree search. At expansion time, two candidates exist in the frontier:
- $n$ — an *unexpanded* (off-path) goal node with path cost $g(n)$.
- $n'$ — a *non-goal* node on the frontier with $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$.

We must prove that any goal returned has $f(n) \leq C^*$.

**Step 1: Initial bound on the goal.**
Let $G$ be any goal node on the frontier. Then:
$$
f(G) \;=\; g(G) \;+\; h(G) \;=\; g(G) \;+\; 0 \;=\; g(G) \;=\; C_G
$$
since the heuristic of a goal is **0 by definition** (it costs nothing more from the goal to itself).

**Step 2: Bound on the cheapest *unexpanded* node.**
Let $n^*$ be the node with the smallest $f$-value on the frontier, *just before* A\* selects it. Two cases:

**Case A — $n^*$ is a goal:**
A\* terminates and returns $n^*$. We have:
$$
f(n^*) \;=\; g(n^*) \;\leq\; f(n') \quad \text{for every other frontier node } n'
$$
Since any goal on the optimal path satisfies $f(G^*) = C^*$, and the smallest $f$ is selected:
$$
g(n^*) \;\leq\; C^*
$$
Because $g(n^*)$ is a path cost, $g(n^*) \geq C^*$ trivially. Therefore:
$$
g(n^*) \;=\; C^*
$$
A\* returns an *optimal* solution. **Q.E.D. for Case A.**

**Case B — $n^*$ is not a goal:**
Before $n^*$ is expanded, suppose there exists some goal $G'$ on the optimal path still in the frontier. Its $f$-value is:
$$
f(G') \;=\; g(G') \;=\; C^*
$$
Since $n^*$ is the minimum-$f$ node:
$$
f(n^*) \;\leq\; f(G') \;=\; C^*
$$
But also $f(n^*) = g(n^*) + h(n^*)$. Since $h$ is **admissible**:
$$
f(n^*) \;=\; g(n^*) + h(n^*) \;\leq\; g(n^*) + h^*(n^*) \;\leq\; C^*
$$
So whenever A\* pops a node $n^*$ with $f(n^*) < C^*$, that node *cannot* be a goal on the optimal path — A\* correctly continues to expand.

**Step 3: Termination condition.**
Eventually all nodes with $f(n) < C^*$ are exhausted, and the next selected node must have $f(n) = C^*$. This node is necessarily a goal (otherwise $h(n) > 0$ would push it past $C^*$, contradicting admissibility). A\* returns it — and the cost equals $C^*$.

**Conclusion:** Tree-search A\* with an **admissible heuristic** is **optimal**.

### 3.2 Derivation: The Effective Branching Factor

KTU problems often ask: *"If A\* expanded 10,000 nodes to depth 12, what is the effective branching factor?"*

The effective branching factor $b^*$ is defined implicitly by:
$$
N \;+\; 1 \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{d} (b^*)^{i} \;=\; \frac{(b^*)^{d+1} - 1}{b^* - 1}
$$

where $N$ = total nodes expanded and $d$ = solution depth. Solve iteratively or read from a precomputed table (e.g., Russell & Norvig Table 3.5).

**Worked example:** $N = 10{,}000$, $d = 12$.

Trial $b^* = 1.5$: $\sum_{i=0}^{12} 1.5^i = \frac{1.5^{13} - 1}{0.5} \approx 194.6$ — *too small*.

Trial $b^* = 2.0$: $\sum_{i=0}^{12} 2^i = 2^{13} - 1 = 8191$ — *close but low*.

Trial $b^* = 2.1$: $\frac{2.1^{13} - 1}{1.1} \approx 11625$ — *slightly over*.

Interpolating: $b^* \approx 2.05$. **Hence, $b^* \approx 2.05$**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What (KTU 14-Mark Favorite)

| Situation | Recommended Algorithm | Why |
|-----------|----------------------|-----|
| All actions have identical cost | **BFS** | Simplest, optimal, complete |
| Step costs differ, want optimal | **UCS / Dijkstra** | Generalization of BFS to weighted edges |
| Memory is tight, solution expected deep | **IDS** | BFS-like optimality with DFS-like memory |
| Heuristic available, need optimal | **A\*** | Optimal + efficient if $h$ is admissible & consistent |
| Heuristic available, optimality not critical | **Greedy Best-First** | Fast, but can be misled by bad heuristics |
| State space is huge, must be space-efficient | **IDA\*** or **SMA\*** | Memory-bounded A\* variants |
| We know the goal's location roughly | **Bidirectional Search** | Two BFS/DFS frontiers meet in the middle |

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Romania Tour (Partial)

Given the straight-line distance heuristic $h_{SLD}$ from Arad:
- $h_{SLD}(\text{Arad}) = 366$
- $h_{SLD}(\text{Sibiu}) = 253$
- $h_{SLD}(\text{Timisoara}) = 329$

A\* frontier after first expansion: $\{Sibiu (g=140, h=253, f=393), \; Timisoara (g=118, h=329, f=447), \; Zerind (g=75, h=374, f=449)\}$.

**Select:** Sibiu (lowest $f = 393$). This shows the A\* algorithm **greedily heads toward the goal** but maintains optimality through $g(n)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — The General Tree-Search Algorithm (Russell & Norvig Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START]) --> initQ[Initialize Frontier with Initial Node]
    initQ --> initE[Initialize Explored Set as Empty]
    initE --> loopCheck{Is Frontier Empty?}
    loopCheck -- YES --> failReturn([Return FAILURE])
    loopCheck -- NO --> popNode[Pop Node n from Frontier]
    popNode --> goalTest{Goal Test on n?}
    goalTest -- YES --> goalReturn([Return SOLUTION = n])
    goalTest -- NO --> expandNode[Generate Successors of n]
    expandNode --> forEach[For each successor s of n]
    forEach --> checkS{Is s in Frontier or Explored?}
    checkS -- NO --> addFrontier[Add s to Frontier with Parent Pointer]
    checkS -- YES --> checkBetter{Is new path to s cheaper?}
    checkBetter -- YES --> updateFrontier[Update s in Frontier with Better Parent and g cost]
    checkBetter -- NO --> skipS[Skip s and Continue]
    addFrontier --> addExplored[Add n to Explored Set]
    updateFrontier --> addExplored
    skipS --> addExplored
    addExplored --> loopCheck
```

### 4.2 Figure 2 — State Space Tree of a Toy Problem (3-Node Goal at Depth 2)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeR((R)) --> nodeA((A))
    nodeR --> nodeB((B))
    nodeR --> nodeC((C))
    nodeA --> nodeD((D))
    nodeA --> nodeE((E))
    nodeB --> nodeF((F))
    nodeB --> nodeG((G GOAL))
    nodeC --> nodeH((H))
    nodeC --> nodeI((I GOAL))
    nodeD --> nodeJ((J))
    nodeD --> nodeK((K GOAL))
    nodeE --> nodeL((L))
    nodeE --> nodeM((M))
    nodeF --> nodeN((N))
    nodeF --> nodeO((O))
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the above tree, BFS would discover all three goals at depth 2 and return **G** (lexicographically first). DFS, choosing the leftmost child first, would reach **K** at depth 3 instead. A* with a heuristic would skip entire subtrees and return the *optimal* goal.

### 4.3 Figure 3 — BFS vs DFS Traversal Order on the Same Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BFS_Traversal["BFS Level-Order Traversal"]
        direction LR
        bf1[R] --> bf2[A] --> bf3[B] --> bf4[C] --> bf5[D] --> bf6[E] --> bf7[F] --> bf8[G]
    end
    subgraph DFS_Traversal["DFS Pre-Order Traversal"]
        direction LR
        df1[R] --> df2[A] --> df3[D] --> df4[J] --> df5[K] --> df6[E] --> df7[L] --> df8[M]
    end
```

### 4.4 Figure 4 — A\* Node Expansion Order with Heuristic Guidance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    astarStart([Start S]) --> n1[Node n1: f equals 10]
    n1 --> n2[Node n2: f equals 12]
    n1 --> n3[Node n3: f equals 8]
    n1 --> n4[Node n4: f equals 15]
    n3 --> n5[Node n5: f equals 9]
    n3 --> n6[Node n6: f equals 11 GOAL]
    n5 --> n7[Node n7: f equals 20]
    n5 --> n8[Node n8: f equals 14]
    n6 --> finalReturn([Return Optimal Path])
    classDef goalNode fill:lightgreen,stroke:darkgreen,stroke-width:2px
    class n6 goalNode
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** A\* always expands the node with the **lowest $f$-value** from the frontier. Even though node $n_1$ was expanded first (as the root), $n_3$ is chosen next because $f(n_3) = 8 < f(n_2) = 12$. The goal $n_6$ is then reached optimally at $f = 11$.

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: A Search Module in Production

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Formulation Module] --> B[State Representation Encoder]
    B --> C[Successor Function Library]
    C --> D[Heuristic Function Estimator]
    A --> E[Search Strategy Selector]
    E --> F[Open List Priority Queue]
    E --> G[Closed List Hash Set]
    F --> H[Node Expansion Engine]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Goal Test Verifier]
    I -- GOAL --> J[Path Reconstruction via Parent Pointers]
    I -- NOT GOAL --> H
    D --> F
    J --> K[Solution Output]
    classDef module fill:lightblue,stroke:navy,stroke-width:2px
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K module
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> Define the term **"state space"** in the context of AI problem solving. How is it represented formally?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **state space** is the set of all configurations (states) that the world can be in, along with the legal transitions between them. Formally, it is represented as a **directed graph** $\mathcal{G} = (S, E)$, where $S$ is the set of all reachable states from the initial state $s_0$, and $E \subseteq S \times S$ is the set of edges representing legal actions, each labelled with a step cost $c(s, a, s')$. The **search algorithm** explores this graph to find a path from $s_0$ to any goal state $G \subseteq S$. *(Award full marks for graph formalism; 1 mark for the intuitive part.)*

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> Differentiate between **"tree search"** and **"graph search"** with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Tree Search | Graph Search |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| **Visited Tracking** | No `explored` set | Maintains `explored`/`closed` set |
| **Repeated States** | Re-expands them freely | Detects and prunes them |
| **Completeness** | May loop infinitely | Guaranteed for finite graphs |
| **Optimality** | A* needs admissible $h$ | A* needs *consistent* $h$ |
| **Example Use** | Trees without cycles | Maps, puzzles with loops |

---

### 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice**: Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the **A\*** search algorithm with the help of its evaluation function. Discuss the role of the **heuristic function** in making A\* optimal and efficient. *(7 marks)*

> **(b)** Consider the following graph where each edge is labelled with its step cost. Use **A\*** to find the optimal path from **S** to **G**, given the heuristic values: $h(S)=8$, $h(A)=5$, $h(B)=6$, $h(C)=4$, $h(D)=5$, $h(E)=3$, $h(G)=0$. *(7 marks)*

```
        S
       / \
    2 /   \ 4
     /     \
    A ----- B
    |\2    /|
   3| \   /3|2
    |  \ /  |
    C---D---E
    1\  |2  /1
      \ |  /
       \| /
        G
```

##### 📝 Model Solution

**Part (a) — A\* Search Algorithm (7 marks):**

**A\* Algorithm Definition (1.5 marks):**
A\* is a *best-first* search algorithm that combines the cost-so-far $g(n)$ with an estimated cost-to-go $h(n)$ via the evaluation function:

$$
f(n) \;=\; g(n) \;+\; h(n)
$$

The node with the **lowest $f$-value** is expanded first. A\* is **complete** (finds a solution if one exists) and **optimal** (returns the least-cost solution) when the heuristic $h$ is **admissible** for tree search and **consistent** for graph search.

**Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
1. Insert the initial node $n_0$ into the *frontier* with $g(n_0)=0$ and $f(n_0)=h(n_0)$.
2. **Loop:** If frontier is empty, return *failure*.
3. Pop node $n$ with the **minimum $f(n)$**.
4. If $n$ satisfies the goal test, return $n$ (reconstruct path via parent pointers).
5. Mark $n$ as *expanded*.
6. For each successor $n'$ of $n$:
   - Compute $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, a, n')$.
   - If $n'$ not in frontier and not expanded, add it.
   - If $n'$ already in frontier with a *higher* $g$, update it.

**Role of Heuristic (2.5 marks):**
- **Admissibility** ($0 \leq h(n) \leq h^*(n)$) ensures that A\* never *over-estimates* the cost to goal, preserving **optimality**.
- **Consistency** ($h(n) \leq c(n,a,n') + h(n')$) ensures the $f$-cost is non-decreasing along any path, which is required for **graph search** optimality and avoids re-expansion.
- A heuristic with **higher values** (closer to $h^*$) is said to *dominate* weaker ones and causes A\* to **expand fewer nodes**, improving efficiency without sacrificing optimality.
- **Example:** For the 8-puzzle, $h_1(n)$ = number of misplaced tiles, $h_2(n)$ = sum of Manhattan distances. Since $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$ for all $n$, $h_2$ **dominates** $h_1$, and A\* with $h_2$ expands **strictly fewer** nodes.

**Part (b) — Numerical A\* Execution (7 marks):**

| Step | Frontier (sorted by $f$) | Expanded |
|------|---------------------------|----------|
| Init | $\{(S, f=0+8=8)\}$ | $\{\}$ |
| 1 | $\{(A, g=2, f=7), (B, g=4, f=10)\}$ | $\{S\}$ |
| 2 | $\{(A, f=7), (B, f=10)\}$ — expand $A$ next | $\{S, A\}$ |
| 3 | Expand $A$: successors $C, D, B$ <br> - $C$: $g=2+3=5$, $f=5+4=9$ <br> - $D$: $g=2+2=4$, $f=4+5=9$ <br> - $B$ (via $A$): $g=2+2=4$, $f=4+6=10$ <br> Frontier: $\{(D,f=9), (C,f=9), (B,f=10), (B,f=10)\}$ | $\{S, A\}$ |
| 4 | Expand $D$ (or $C$ — tie): <br> - $D \to C$: $g=4+1=5$, $f=5+4=9$ <br> - $D \to E$: $g=4+2=6$, $f=6+3=9$ <br> - $D \to G$: $g=4+2=6$, $f=6+0=\mathbf{6}$ <br> Frontier: $\{(G, f=6), \ldots\}$ | $\{S, A, D\}$ |
| 5 | Pop $G$ — **goal reached!** | Return path |

**Optimal Path:** $S \to A \to D \to G$ with **total cost $g(G) = 6$**. *(2 marks for cost; 1 mark for path reconstruction; 2 marks for table; 1 mark for final verification that no other goal had $f < 6$ at termination.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Compare and contrast **Breadth-First Search (BFS)**, **Depth-First Search (DFS)**, and **Iterative Deepening Search (IDS)** in terms of completeness, optimality, time complexity, and space complexity. Use a branching factor $b$ and depth $d$. *(7 marks)*

> **(b)** Construct the **search tree** for the 8-puzzle problem starting from the initial state given below and apply **BFS** until the goal is reached. Show all expanded nodes and the frontier at each step. The goal is to reach the configuration:
> 
> ```
> 1 2 3
> 4 5 6
> 7 8 _
> ```
> 
> Initial state:
> ```
> 1 _ 3
> 4 2 6
> 7 5 8
> ``` *(7 marks)*

##### 📝 Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparative Table (7 marks):**

| Property | BFS | DFS | IDS |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Complete?** | Yes (finite $b$) | No (infinite-depth spaces) | Yes |
| **Optimal?** | Yes (uniform cost) | No | Yes (uniform cost) |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^{d+1})$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(b^{d+1})$ | $O(bm)$ | $O(bd)$ |
| **Backtracking** | Not needed | LIFO stack | LIFO with depth limit |
| **Use Case** | Shallow solutions, abundant memory | Deep solutions, tight memory | Best of both worlds |

**Derivation of IDS Time Complexity (1 mark bonus):**
Total nodes expanded by IDS = $\sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k = b^0 + b^1 + b^2 + \cdots + b^d = \frac{b^{d+1}-1}{b-1} \leq \frac{b^{d+1}}{b-1} = O(b^d)$. The lower-order terms dominate by a constant factor $(b-1)^{-1}$, so IDS matches BFS in asymptotic time while retaining DFS's *linear* space complexity.

**Why IDS is Preferred (1 mark):**
- IDS uses $O(bd)$ memory — the **same as DFS** — but inherits BFS's **completeness and optimality**.
- The redundant expansion of shallower levels is bounded by a constant factor $\frac{1}{b-1}$, which is negligible for $b \geq 2$.
- This is why IDS is the **preferred uninformed search** when the depth of the solution is unknown.

**Part (b) — BFS on 8-Puzzle (7 marks):**

**Notation:** Each state is encoded as a 3×3 grid. The blank is moved Up, Down, Left, Right where legal.

**Step-by-step BFS execution (1 mark per significant step):**

**Level 0 (Initial):** Expand $S_0$.
```
1 _ 3
4 2 6
7 5 8
```
Three legal moves: blank goes **Down** ($S_1$), **Right** ($S_2$), **Left** (illegal — wall).

**Level 1 — Frontier:** $\{S_1, S_2\}$

$S_1$ (blank Down):
```
1 2 3
4 _ 6
7 5 8
```

$S_2$ (blank Right):
```
1 3 3   <- invalid
```
Wait — moving blank *right* requires swapping with tile 3, giving:
```
1 3 _
4 2 6
7 5 8
```
**Level 2 — Frontier (after expanding $S_1$ first, since BFS is FIFO):**

Expand $S_1$: successors include blank moving Up, Down, Right, Left.
- $S_1 \to$ Down: invalid (would clash with bottom row)
- $S_1 \to$ Up: returns to $S_0$ (already in *explored*) — **pruned**
- $S_1 \to$ Left: returns to $S_0$ — **pruned**
- $S_1 \to$ Right: new state $S_3$:
```
1 2 3
4 6 _
7 5 8
```

Expand $S_2$: successors.
- $S_2 \to$ Down: new state $S_4$:
```
1 _ 3
4 2 6
7 5 8
```
This is $S_0$ — **pruned**.
- $S_2 \to$ Right: invalid (wall)
- $S_2 \to$ Up: invalid (wall)
- $S_2 \to$ Left: new state $S_5$:
```
1 3 _
4 2 6
7 5 8
```
Wait — $S_5$ is the same as $S_2$ — **pruned**.

**Level 3 — Frontier:** $\{S_3, S_5, S_4\}$
... and BFS continues level-by-level until the goal state is reached. The BFS path length is $d=5$ for this instance (typical for 8-puzzle).

**Important annotation (1 mark):** The student must explicitly mention that BFS maintains a **FIFO queue** for the frontier and an *explored set* to avoid revisiting states — this is graph-search BFS, which is the standard variant for 8-puzzle since the state space has cycles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Confusing Nodes and States (–1 mark):** In a search tree, the same *state* may appear as multiple *nodes*. Always specify which version (path) you are tracking.
> 2. **Forgetting the Explored Set (–1 mark):** BFS without an explored set re-expands states exponentially. Always mark the explored set at each step.
> 3. **Confusing $h$ with $h^*$ (–1 mark):** $h(n)$ is the *estimate* (given); $h^*(n)$ is the *true* optimal cost. Admissibility is defined with respect to $h^*$.
> 4. **Forgetting the Goal Test Returns on Pop, Not on Insert (–1 mark):** A\* tests for goal *after* popping, not when inserting. Late goal-test in BFS/A\* can break optimality.
> 5. **Mis-stating Space Complexity of BFS (–1 mark):** BFS space is $O(b^{d+1})$, NOT $O(b^d)$. The "+1" is from the frontier at level $d$ being half-full plus the explored set.
> 6. **Calling DFS "complete":** DFS is *not* complete in infinite spaces. State this explicitly in comparative answers.
> 7. **Ignoring the Heuristic's Effect on A\* (–1 mark):** A\* without an admissible heuristic is just UCS. Always justify why your $h$ is admissible (e.g., "Manhattan distance ≤ true tile-swap cost").
> 8. **Path Reconstruction Error (–2 marks):** When reporting the optimal path, always *reconstruct* it from goal to start using parent pointers, not from memory. KTU examiners verify path consistency.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Problem formulation has 4 (or 5 with heuristic) components** — Initial state, Actions, Transition Model, Goal Test, Path Cost.
- [ ] **Search algorithms are evaluated on exactly 4 properties** — Completeness, Optimality, Time, Space.
- [ ] **BFS** is complete and optimal for uniform step costs, but space-hungry $O(b^{d+1})$.
- [ ] **DFS** is space-efficient $O(bm)$ but incomplete and not optimal.
- [ ] **UCS** = Dijkstra = BFS generalized to weighted graphs; uses *path cost* $g(n)$ for ordering.
- [ ] **DLS** = DFS with depth limit $\ell$; **IDS** = DLS with $\ell = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ — combines BFS and DFS strengths.
- [ ] **Bidirectional search** reduces effective depth from $d$ to $d/2$, but only if both forward and backward searches are tractable.
- [ ] **A\* evaluation function:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.
- [ ] **Admissible heuristic:** $0 \leq h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ — never overestimates the true cost. Ensures A\* tree-search optimality.
- [ ] **Consistent (monotonic) heuristic:** $h(n) \leq c(n,a,n') + h(n')$ — implies $f$ is non-decreasing along paths. Required for A\* graph-search optimality.
- [ ] **Dominance:** If $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$ for all $n$, then $h_2$ dominates $h_1$ and A\* with $h_2$ expands $\leq$ nodes than A\* with $h_1$.
- [ ] **Effective branching factor $b^*$** measures heuristic quality — closer to 1 is better.
- [ ] **8-puzzle heuristics:** $h_1$ = misplaced tiles, $h_2$ = Manhattan distance. $h_2$ dominates $h_1$.
- [ ] **Tree search vs Graph search:** Graph search uses an *explored set* to prune revisited states.
- [ ] **A\* termination:** Returns the first popped goal node — this is the optimal one.
- [ ] **Memory-bounded variants:** IDA\* (iterative deepening on $f$-cost), SMA\* (uses all available memory).
- [ ] **KTU buzzwords to memorize for 14-mark answers:** *admissibility, consistency, dominance, effective branching factor, completeness, optimality*.

> **🎓 Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** In Part B, when asked to "compare search algorithms", always use a **4-column table** with the four properties (C, O, T, S) and a 5th column for *example use case*. Examiners in KTU reward this structure with full marks. Always draw a *mermaid-style or hand-drawn search tree* to earn the visualization marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Search Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Search Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Search Algorithm in AI?

A **Search Algorithm** in Artificial Intelligence is a problem-solving technique that systematically explores a **state-space graph** to find a sequence of actions (a *path*) that transitions the system from an **initial state** to a **goal state**. It is the foundational computational paradigm upon which reasoning, planning, and game-playing agents are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> "Search is the process of finding a sequence of actions that leads from an initial state to a goal state. In AI, search algorithms navigate a state-space represented as a graph, where nodes represent states and edges represent transitions (actions)."

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Maze Explorer"

Imagine you are dropped blindfolded into a maze (your *initial state*). You have no map, but you can feel the walls and step forward, left, right, or back (your *successor function*). Your goal is to reach the exit (the *goal state*).

- **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** = You and 100 friends spread out in *all directions simultaneously*, layer by layer, until one of you finds the exit. It guarantees the shortest path but is expensive.
- **Depth-First Search (DFS)** = You pick a single path and keep walking *as deep as possible* until you hit a dead-end, then you backtrack. It is memory-efficient but might take the long route.
- **A\* Search** = You use a *smart compass* (a heuristic) that always points you toward the exit. You combine "distance already traveled" with "estimated distance to the goal" — the most efficient strategy.

## 1.3 Formal Problem Formulation (5-Tuple Definition)

Every search problem in AI is formally defined as a tuple:

$$P = (S, s_0, A, T, G)$$

Where:
- $S$ = Set of all possible **states** in the world.
- $s_0 \in S$ = The **initial state** where the agent starts.
- $A(s)$ = Set of **actions** available in state $s$.
- $T(s, a) \rightarrow s'$ = **Transition model** — the result of applying action $a$ in state $s$.
- $G \subseteq S$ = The **goal test** — a boolean function checking if $s$ is a goal state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Path Cost Function ($g(n)$):** Some problems also include a step-cost function $c(s, a, s')$ that assigns a numeric cost to each step, so the algorithm can find the *cheapest* path, not just *any* path.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Search Algorithms (KTU High-Yield Classification)

Search algorithms are broadly classified into two families:

| Category | Description | Example Algorithms |
|---|---|---|
| **Uninformed (Blind) Search** | No extra domain knowledge; only uses the problem definition. | BFS, DFS, Uniform-Cost Search, Depth-Limited, IDS, Bidirectional |
| **Informed (Heuristic) Search** | Uses a heuristic function $h(n)$ to estimate distance to goal. | Greedy Best-First, A*, IDA*, AO* |
| **Adversarial Search** | Used in multi-agent competitive environments (games). | Minimax, Alpha-Beta Pruning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** *Uninformed* search is "blind" because it has **no idea** how close it is to the goal. *Informed* search uses a **heuristic** $h(n)$ — an educated guess — to prioritize promising paths.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Uninformed (Blind) Search Strategies

### 2.1.1 Breadth-First Search (BFS)
- **Strategy:** Expand the *shallowest* unexpanded node first. Uses a **FIFO Queue**.
- **Why it works:** It explores all nodes at depth $d$ before any node at depth $d+1$. This guarantees the *shortest path* in terms of number of steps.
- **Data Structure:** FIFO Queue — enqueue successors at the tail, dequeue from the head.

### 2.1.2 Depth-First Search (DFS)
- **Strategy:** Expand the *deepest* unexpanded node first. Uses a **LIFO Stack**.
- **Why it works:** It dives down one path completely before backtracking. Memory-efficient because it only stores the current path.
- **Data Structure:** LIFO Stack — push successors, pop the most recent.

### 2.1.3 Uniform-Cost Search (UCS)
- **Strategy:** Expand the node with the *lowest path cost* $g(n)$ first. Uses a **Priority Queue** ordered by $g(n)$.
- **Why it works:** It is a generalization of BFS that handles variable step costs. It always finds the *cheapest* path, not just the shortest in steps.

### 2.1.4 Depth-Limited Search (DLS) & Iterative Deepening Search (IDS)
- **DLS:** DFS with a predetermined depth cutoff $\ell$ — avoids infinite descent in infinite trees.
- **IDS:** Repeatedly applies DLS with increasing limits $\ell = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ — combines BFS's optimality with DFS's low memory.

## 2.2 Informed (Heuristic) Search Strategies

### 2.2.1 Greedy Best-First Search
- **Strategy:** Expand the node that *appears* closest to the goal according to heuristic $h(n)$. Uses a Priority Queue keyed on $h(n)$.
- **Why it works:** Fast in practice but **not optimal** — can be misled by a bad heuristic.

### 2.2.2 A\* Search (The Star of the Show ⭐)
- **Strategy:** Combines UCS's actual cost so far with Greedy's heuristic estimate. Uses an **evaluation function**:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

- **Why it works:** $g(n)$ ensures optimality (like UCS), $h(n)$ focuses the search (like Greedy). Together, they yield the *best of both worlds*.
- **Admissibility:** A heuristic is *admissible* if it never overestimates the true cost, i.e., $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$.
- **Consistency (Monotonicity):** A heuristic is *consistent* if for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$ of $n$:

$$h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$$

## 2.3 Adversarial Search — Game Playing

### 2.3.1 Minimax Algorithm
- **Strategy:** Two players — **MAX** (the AI) and **MIN** (the opponent). MAX assumes MIN will play optimally to *minimize* MAX's score.
- **Why it works:** Backward induction from the leaves of the game tree, alternating min/max layers.
- **Recursion depth:** Equal to the lookahead depth of the game.

### 2.3.2 Alpha-Beta Pruning
- **Strategy:** An *optimization* over Minimax that prunes branches that cannot possibly influence the final decision.
- **Why it works:** Maintains two bounds:
  - $\alpha$ = the best value MAX can guarantee so far (lower bound).
  - $\beta$ = the best value MIN can guarantee so far (upper bound).
- **Pruning rule:** Stop exploring a branch when $\alpha \geq \beta$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Algorithm | Evaluation Function | Data Structure | Complete? | Optimal? | Time | Space |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BFS** | — | FIFO Queue | Yes (if $b$ finite) | Yes (unit costs) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| **DFS** | — | LIFO Stack | No | No | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ |
| **UCS** | $g(n)$ | Priority Queue ($g$) | Yes | Yes | $O(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ |
| **DLS** | depth $\leq \ell$ | Stack | No | No | $O(b^\ell)$ | $O(b\ell)$ |
| **IDS** | iteratively $\ell$ | Stack | Yes | Yes (unit costs) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bd)$ |
| **Greedy** | $h(n)$ | Priority Queue ($h$) | No | No | worst $O(b^m)$ | worst $O(b^m)$ |
| **A\*** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Priority Queue ($f$) | Yes (if consistent) | Yes (if admissible) | exponential in error | keeps all nodes |
| **Minimax** | $\max / \min$ | Recursion | — | Yes (vs optimal) | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ |
| **Alpha-Beta** | prune when $\alpha \geq \beta$ | Recursion | — | Yes (vs optimal) | $O(b^{m/2})$ best | $O(bm)$ |

Where:
- $b$ = **branching factor** (max number of successors per node).
- $d$ = depth of the shallowest goal.
- $m$ = maximum depth of the state space.
- $C^*$ = cost of the optimal solution.
- $\epsilon$ = minimum step cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Search algorithms are the backbone of **GPS navigation** (A*), **puzzle solvers** (BFS for Rubik's cube), **chess engines** (Alpha-Beta), **robot path planning**, **logistics optimization**, and **automated theorem proving**. In modern AI, A\* and its variants power everything from warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva) to autonomous vehicles.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — BFS Traversal

**Problem:** Find the shortest path from $S$ (Start) to $G$ (Goal) in the following graph:

$$
S \to A,\ S \to B,\ A \to C,\ A \to D,\ B \to D,\ C \to G,\ D \to G
$$

**Step 1 — Initialize:**
- Frontier Queue: $[S]$
- Explored Set: $\emptyset$

**Step 2 — Dequeue $S$, expand it:**
- Successors: $\{A, B\}$
- Frontier: $[A, B]$
- Explored: $\{S\}$

**Step 3 — Dequeue $A$, expand it:**
- Successors: $\{C, D\}$ (skip already in frontier)
- Frontier: $[B, C, D]$
- Explored: $\{S, A\}$

**Step 4 — Dequeue $B$, expand it:**
- Successor: $\{D\}$ (already in frontier)
- Frontier: $[C, D]$
- Explored: $\{S, A, B\}$

**Step 5 — Dequeue $C$, expand it:**
- Successor: $\{G\}$ — **GOAL REACHED!**
- Path: $S \to A \to C \to G$

**Conclusion:** BFS found a path of length **3** — which is optimal.

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — A\* Search

**Problem:** Use A\* to find the shortest path from $S$ to $G$. The heuristic $h(n)$ and step costs are given below:

| Node | $h(n)$ |
|---|---|
| $S$ | 7 |
| $A$ | 5 |
| $B$ | 6 |
| $C$ | 4 |
| $D$ | 2 |
| $G$ | 0 |

Edges and costs: $S{-}A: 1$, $S{-}B: 4$, $A{-}C: 3$, $A{-}D: 2$, $B{-}D: 5$, $C{-}G: 4$, $D{-}G: 3$.

**Step 1 — Initialize with $S$:**
- $f(S) = g(S) + h(S) = 0 + 7 = 7$
- Frontier: $\{S(7)\}$

**Step 2 — Expand $S$ (lowest $f$):**
- Add $A$: $g(A) = 1$, $f(A) = 1 + 5 = 6$
- Add $B$: $g(B) = 4$, $f(B) = 4 + 6 = 10$
- Frontier: $\{A(6),\ B(10)\}$

**Step 3 — Expand $A$ (lowest $f$):**
- Add $C$: $g(C) = 1 + 3 = 4$, $f(C) = 4 + 4 = 8$
- Add $D$: $g(D) = 1 + 2 = 3$, $f(D) = 3 + 2 = 5$
- Frontier: $\{D(5),\ C(8),\ B(10)\}$

**Step 4 — Expand $D$ (lowest $f$):**
- Add $G$: $g(G) = 3 + 3 = 6$, $f(G) = 6 + 0 = 6$
- Frontier: $\{G(6),\ C(8),\ B(10)\}$

**Step 5 — Expand $G$ (lowest $f$):**
- **GOAL REACHED!**
- Path: $S \to A \to D \to G$ with total cost $6$.

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example — Minimax with Alpha-Beta Pruning

**Problem:** Evaluate the following game tree using Minimax, then show Alpha-Beta pruning.

The tree has MAX at the root, MIN at depth 1, MAX at depth 2, and leaves with values. From left to right, the leaf values are: $3, 12, 2, 8, 14, 5, 6, 2$.

**Step 1 — Standard Minimax (bottom-up):**
- At each MAX node: take the max of children.
- At each MIN node: take the min of children.

Leaf layer: $[3, 12, 2, 8, 14, 5, 6, 2]$

**Level 2 (MAX nodes — 4 nodes, each with 2 children):**
- $\max(3, 12) = 12$
- $\max(2, 8) = 8$
- $\max(14, 5) = 14$
- $\max(6, 2) = 6$

Result: $[12, 8, 14, 6]$

**Level 1 (MIN nodes — 2 nodes, each with 2 children):**
- $\min(12, 8) = 8$
- $\min(14, 6) = 6$

Result: $[8, 6]$

**Root (MAX):**
- $\max(8, 6) = 8$

**Final Minimax value = 8**, reached via the left subtree.

**Step 2 — Alpha-Beta Pruning:**

We initialize $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$.

Traversal (left to right):

**Left subtree first:**
- Left MAX node: examines leaf $3$ → value updates to 3. Examines leaf $12$ → value becomes 12. Returns 12.
- Left MIN node: now $\alpha = -\infty, \beta = 12$ initially, after left child returns 12, $\beta = 12$.
  - Right MAX node: examines leaf $2$ → value = 2. Since $2 \leq \alpha = -\infty$? No, but we check pruning. Actually, we are at MAX so we update $\alpha$. Initial $\alpha = -\infty$. After leaf 2, value = 2, so $\alpha = 2$. Now $\alpha \geq \beta$? ($2 \geq 12$?) No. Examine leaf 8. Value becomes $\max(2, 8) = 8$. $\alpha = 8$. $\alpha \geq \beta$? $8 \geq 12$? No. Return 8.
  - Back to MIN: $\min(12, 8) = 8$. $\alpha$ updates to 8.

**Right subtree:**
- $\alpha = 8, \beta = +\infty$ at root, passed down.
- Right MIN node: $\alpha = 8, \beta = +\infty$.
  - Left MAX node (third subtree): examines leaf 14 → value = 14. $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 14) = 14$. Examine leaf 5 → value = 14. Return 14.
  - Right MIN: $\min(14, \text{?}) = ?$ with $\alpha = 14$. We need to explore the next MAX child.
  - Right MAX node (fourth subtree): $\alpha = 14, \beta = +\infty$. Examines leaf 6 → value = 6. Check pruning: $6 \geq \beta$? No. But we are at MAX, so $\alpha = \max(14, 6) = 14$. $\alpha \geq \beta$? No. Examine leaf 2 → value = 14. Return 14.
  - Right MIN: $\min(14, 14) = 14$. $\beta = 14$.

**Back to root:** $\max(8, 14) = 14$.

Wait — this gives a different answer than standard Minimax! That means I made a re-derivation. Let me redo this carefully.

**Re-derivation (correct):**

The game tree: Root (MAX) has 2 children (MIN nodes). Each MIN node has 2 children (MAX nodes). Each MAX node has 2 children (leaves).

Leaves left to right: $3, 12, 2, 8, 14, 5, 6, 2$.

**Standard Minimax:**
- Subtree 1 leaves: $\{3, 12\}$ → MAX picks 12
- Subtree 2 leaves: $\{2, 8\}$ → MAX picks 8
- Subtree 3 leaves: $\{14, 5\}$ → MAX picks 14
- Subtree 4 leaves: $\{6, 2\}$ → MAX picks 6
- MIN node 1: $\min(12, 8) = 8$
- MIN node 2: $\min(14, 6) = 6$
- Root MAX: $\max(8, 6) = 8$ ✓

**Alpha-Beta Pruning (correct execution):**

$\alpha = -\infty, \beta = +\infty$.

**Path: Root → MIN-1 → MAX-1 → leaves 3, 12:**
- MAX-1 examines leaf 3: $v = 3$, $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 3) = 3$.
- MAX-1 examines leaf 12: $v = \max(3, 12) = 12$, $\alpha = 12$.
- $\alpha \geq \beta$? ($12 \geq \infty$?) No. Continue.
- MAX-1 returns 12. MIN-1 has $v = 12$, $\beta = \min(\infty, 12) = 12$.

**Path: MIN-1 → MAX-2 → leaves 2, 8:**
- $\alpha = -\infty$ (passed from root to MIN-1, then to MAX-2), $\beta = 12$.
- MAX-2 examines leaf 2: $v = 2$, $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 2) = 2$.
- Check pruning at MAX: $\alpha \geq \beta$? ($2 \geq 12$?) No. Continue.
- MAX-2 examines leaf 8: $v = \max(2, 8) = 8$, $\alpha = 8$.
- Check: $8 \geq 12$? No. Return 8.
- MIN-1: $v = \min(12, 8) = 8$, $\beta = \min(12, 8) = 8$.

**Back at root:** $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 8) = 8$. Continue to MIN-2.

**Path: Root → MIN-2 → MAX-3 → leaves 14, 5:**
- $\alpha = 8, \beta = +\infty$ at MIN-2, passed to MAX-3.
- MAX-3 examines leaf 14: $v = 14$, $\alpha = \max(8, 14) = 14$.
- Check: $14 \geq \infty$? No. Continue.
- MAX-3 examines leaf 5: $v = \max(14, 5) = 14$, $\alpha = 14$.
- Return 14.
- MIN-2: $v = 14$, $\beta = \min(\infty, 14) = 14$.

**Check pruning at MIN-2:** $\alpha \geq \beta$? ($8 \geq 14$?) No. Continue.

**Path: MIN-2 → MAX-4 → leaves 6, 2:**
- $\alpha = 8, \beta = 14$.
- MAX-4 examines leaf 6: $v = 6$, $\alpha = \max(8, 6) = 8$.
- Check: $8 \geq 14$? No. Continue.
- MAX-4 examines leaf 2: $v = \max(8, 2) = 8$, $\alpha = 8$.
- Check: $8 \geq 14$? No. Return 8.
- MIN-2: $v = \min(14, 8) = 8$, $\beta = 8$.

**Back at root:** $\alpha = \max(8, 8) = 8$. **Final value = 8.** ✓

**Pruning Efficiency:** No pruning occurred in this particular tree because the leaf ordering was unfavorable. If we reorder leaves (e.g., $12, 3, 8, 2, 14, 5, 6, 2$), heavy pruning would occur, reducing complexity from $O(b^m)$ to $O(b^{m/2})$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Problem Characteristic | Recommended Algorithm |
|---|---|
| Uniform step costs, need shortest path | BFS |
| Deep state space, memory limited | DFS or IDS |
| Variable step costs, need optimal path | Uniform-Cost Search |
| Good heuristic available, need optimal path | A\* with admissible $h$ |
| Need fast, near-optimal path in games | Greedy Best-First |
| Two-player zero-sum game | Minimax + Alpha-Beta Pruning |
| Tree is too deep for full Minimax | Depth-limited Minimax + Evaluation Function |
