# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define supervised learning. List any two differences between supervised and unsupervised learning.

**Model Answer:**
Supervised learning is a machine learning paradigm where the model is trained on a labeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$, learning a mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ under the guidance of ground-truth labels provided by an external supervisor.

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| Labels | Present ($y^{(i)}$ known) | Absent |
| Goal | Predict $y$ for new $x$ | Discover hidden structure in $X$ |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for table + 1 for clarity)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between classification and regression with one real-world example each.

**Model Answer:**

- **Classification** predicts a *discrete categorical label*. Example: classifying emails as "Spam" or "Not Spam" ($Y = \{0, 1\}$).
- **Regression** predicts a *continuous real-valued output*. Example: predicting the price of a house in lakhs ($Y \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$).

The mathematical distinction: classification outputs pass through a discrete mapping (e.g., $\arg\max$) while regression outputs lie on the real line.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the supervised learning workflow with a neat block diagram. Discuss any **three** major challenges faced during model training. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a dataset with $N = 5$ training points: $x = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]^{T}$, $y = [2, 4, 5, 4, 5]^{T}$, fit a linear regression model $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_{0} + \theta_{1}x$ using the **Ordinary Least Squares Normal Equation**. Compute the predicted values and the MSE on the training set. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Supervised Learning Workflow (7 marks):**

The supervised learning pipeline comprises the following sequential stages:

1. **Data Acquisition:** Collect a labeled dataset with ground-truth annotations.
2. **Preprocessing:** Clean missing values, normalize features, encode categoricals.
3. **Train/Validation/Test Split:** Typically 70/15/15 or 60/20/20.
4. **Model Training:** Minimize empirical risk $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)})$.
5. **Hyperparameter Tuning:** Use cross-validation on the validation set.
6. **Evaluation:** Measure performance using metrics (accuracy, MSE, F1).
7. **Deployment:** Apply trained model to unseen real-world data.

**Three major challenges:**

- **Overfitting:** Model memorizes training data; fails to generalize.
- **Underfitting:** Model too simple to capture underlying pattern.
- **Curse of Dimensionality:** Performance degrades as feature dimensionality $d$ grows without proportionally growing $N$.

**(b) OLS Solution (7 marks):**

Augment $x$ with a column of ones to include the intercept:

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \\ 5 \\ 4 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $X^{T}X$:

$$
X^{T}X = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 15 \\ 15 & 55 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $X^{T}y$:

$$
X^{T}y = \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 60 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The determinant of $X^{T}X$ is $5 \cdot 55 - 15 \cdot 15 = 275 - 225 = 50$. The inverse is:

$$
(X^{T}X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{50}\begin{bmatrix} 55 & -15 \\ -15 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.1 & -0.3 \\ -0.3 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply the Normal Equation $\theta^{*} = (X^{T}X)^{-1} X^{T} y$:

$$
\theta^{*} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.1 & -0.3 \\ -0.3 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 60 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 22 - 18 \\ -6 + 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.7 \\ 0.7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $\theta_{0} = 2.7$ and $\theta_{1} = 0.7$. The fitted model is:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = 2.7 + 0.7x$$

**Predicted values and MSE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y} &= [3.4,\ 4.1,\ 4.8,\ 5.5,\ 6.2]^{T} \\
\text{Residuals } (y - \hat{y}) &= [-1.4,\ -0.1,\ 0.2,\ -1.5,\ -1.2]^{T} \\
\text{SSE} &= 1.96 + 0.01 + 0.04 + 2.25 + 1.44 = 5.70 \\
\text{MSE} &= \frac{5.70}{5} = 1.14
\end{aligned}
$$

**(Total: 7 marks = 2 setup + 2 matrix inversion + 2 prediction + 1 MSE)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Alternate]**

**(a)** What is the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff**? Derive the decomposition $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f})^{2}] = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \sigma^{2}$ step-by-step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the following supervised learning algorithms: **(i)** k-Nearest Neighbors, **(ii)** Support Vector Machines (with linear kernel), and **(iii)** Decision Trees. Compare their computational complexity at training and inference time. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**(a) Bias-Variance Decomposition (7 marks):**

Let $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ where $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$ and let $\hat{f}(x)$ be the learned predictor trained on a dataset $\mathcal{D}$. Define the expectation over training sets: $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)] = \bar{f}(x)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^{2}\right] &= \mathbb{E}\left[(f + \varepsilon - \hat{f})^{2}\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\left[(f - \hat{f} + \varepsilon)^{2}\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\left[(f - \bar{f} + \bar{f} - \hat{f} + \varepsilon)^{2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding and subtracting $\bar{f}(x)$ and expanding, the cross-terms vanish because $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f} - \bar{f}] = 0$ and $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f})^{2}\right] &= (f - \bar{f})^{2} + \mathbb{E}\left[(\bar{f} - \hat{f})^{2}\right] + \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^{2}] \\
&= \text{Bias}^{2}(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Variance}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Algorithm Comparison (7 marks):**

**(i) k-NN:** Instance-based, non-parametric. Predicts by majority vote (classification) or mean (regression) over $k$ nearest neighbors in feature space. Training: $O(1)$ (just stores data). Inference: $O(Nd)$.

**(ii) SVM:** Finds the maximum-margin hyperplane $\theta^{T}x + \theta_{0} = 0$ separating classes. Uses hinge loss $\max(0, 1 - y^{(i)}(\theta^{T}x^{(i)} + \theta_{0}))$. Training: $O(N^{2})$ to $O(N^{3})$. Inference: $O(d)$ for linear kernel.

**(iii) Decision Trees:** Recursively partition feature space using greedy splits maximizing Information Gain (classification) or MSE reduction (regression). Training: $O(N d \log N)$. Inference: $O(\text{depth}) \approx O(\log N)$.

| Algorithm | Training Cost | Inference Cost | Parametric? |
|---|---|---|---|
| k-NN | $O(1)$ | $O(Nd)$ | No |
| Linear SVM | $O(N^{2})$ | $O(d)$ | Yes |
| Decision Tree | $O(Nd\log N)$ | $O(\log N)$ | No |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Bias with Variance** — Bias = error from wrong assumptions (underfitting); Variance = error from data sensitivity (overfitting). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for swapped definitions.
> 2. **Forgetting to augment $X$ with a bias column** when applying the Normal Equation — this yields wrong $\theta_{0}$.
> 3. **Stating k-NN has a "training phase"** — k-NN is lazy; training is just storing the dataset.
> 4. **Mixing up Precision and Recall formulas** — Precision cares about predicted positives; Recall cares about actual positives.
> 5. **Not specifying the loss function** when asked about an algorithm — every KTU 14-mark question demands explicit loss-function identification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Supervised Learning = Learning from labeled examples.** The dataset is $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}$.
- **Two flavors:** Classification (discrete $y$) and Regression (continuous $y$).
- **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** is the foundational learning principle.
- **MSE Loss** for regression; **Cross-Entropy Loss** for classification.
- **OLS closed-form:** $\theta^{*} = (X^{T}X)^{-1} X^{T} y$. Requires $X^{T}X$ invertible.
- **Gradient Descent** is used when closed-form is infeasible (large $N$ or non-invertible $X^{T}X$).
- **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Total Error = Bias² + Variance + Irreducible Noise.
- **Evaluation Metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 (classification); MSE, MAE, RMSE, $R^{2}$ (regression).
- **Curse of Dimensionality:** As $d$ grows, supervised models require exponentially more data.
- **KTU favorite questions:** Normal Equation derivation, Gradient Descent update rule, Bias-Variance decomposition, Algorithm comparison table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unsupervised Learning** is a paradigm of Machine Learning in which an algorithm is trained on a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$ consisting of **input vectors without corresponding target labels**. The objective is to discover hidden structure, intrinsic patterns, groupings, or low-dimensional representations within the data.

Mathematically, the learning problem is formulated as:

$$f : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}_{\text{latent}}$$

where $\mathcal{Y}_{\text{latent}}$ is an unknown output space (clusters, manifolds, latent codes) that the model must infer directly from $\mathcal{X}$.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Librarian Without a Catalog**
> Imagine a librarian is handed **1 million unlabeled books** dumped in a giant hall. There are no genre stickers, no author tags, no pre-written summaries. The librarian's job is to *organize* them into meaningful shelves purely by **reading similarities** — books with similar words go together, math books cluster with math books, cooking books cluster with cooking books.
> - The librarian is the **unsupervised algorithm**.
> - The books are the **unlabeled data points**.
> - The shelves that emerge are the **clusters**.
> - The librarian never saw an example of "what a correctly sorted shelf looks like" — they discovered structure on their own.

This is the essence of unsupervised learning: **learning without a teacher, only from the geometry and statistics of the data itself.**

---

## 1.3 Why Unsupervised Learning Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real engineering and industry applications, **labeled data is expensive, scarce, or impossible to obtain**. For instance:
> - Satellite imagery over a continent (no labels for forests vs. water vs. urban)
> - Customer transaction logs (no explicit "this customer is X type" tag)
> - Gene expression data (function of most genes is unknown)
>
> Unsupervised learning unlocks value from this **vast pool of unlabeled information**.

**Key Standard Metrics (used throughout KTU evaluation):**
- **Intra-cluster distance** — should be **minimized**
- **Inter-cluster distance** — should be **maximized**
- **Silhouette Score** $\in [-1, 1]$ — higher is better
- **Reconstruction Error** (for autoencoders/PCA) — should be **minimized**

---

## 1.4 Major Families of Unsupervised Learning

| Family | Purpose | KTU-Standard Algorithm Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Clustering** | Group similar data points | K-Means, Hierarchical, DBSCAN |
| **Dimensionality Reduction** | Compress features while preserving structure | PCA, t-SNE, Autoencoders |
| **Association Rule Mining** | Discover co-occurrence patterns | Apriori, FP-Growth |
| **Density Estimation** | Model underlying data distribution | GMM, KDE |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Identify outliers | Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Unsupervised Learning Pipeline (Structured Logic Steps)

1. **Input Acquisition:** Collect raw feature matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ with **no label vector $\mathbf{y}$**.
2. **Preprocessing:** Standardize features using **Z-score normalization** so that no single feature dominates distance computations.
3. **Model Selection:** Choose a hypothesis class (e.g., K centroids, a low-rank subspace, a mixture of Gaussians).
4. **Objective Definition:** Specify a **loss / objective function** that quantifies "goodness" of structure discovered.
5. **Optimization:** Iterate to minimize (or maximize) the objective — usually via gradient descent, EM, or iterative assignment.
6. **Evaluation:** Use **internal metrics** (no labels available!) such as inertia, silhouette score, or reconstruction error.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundations

### 2.2.1 Distance Metrics (Core to Clustering)

For two points $\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j \in \mathbb{R}^d$, the most widely used distance is the **Euclidean distance**:

$$d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d} (x_{ik} - x_{jk})^2}$$

Other variants in the KTU syllabus:

$$d_{\text{Manhattan}}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \sum_{k=1}^{d} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert$$

$$d_{\text{Minkowski}}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{d} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert^{p} \right)^{\frac{1}{p}}$$

### 2.2.2 K-Means Objective

Given $K$ clusters with centroids $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \dots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_K\}$ and assignment set $C_k$, K-Means minimizes:

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^{2}$$

This is the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** — also called **inertia**.

### 2.2.3 Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

Given centered data $\mathbf{X}_c$, PCA finds orthogonal directions (principal components) that maximize variance. The covariance matrix:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c$$

Eigen-decomposition: $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i$.

Total variance explained by top $k$ components:

$$\text{Explained Variance Ratio} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MASTER THIS FOR KTU EXAMS**

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Euclidean Distance | $d = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d} (x_{ik} - x_{jk})^2}$ | $d$ features; indices $i,j$ |
| Manhattan Distance | $d = \sum_{k=1}^{d} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert$ | L1 norm |
| Minkowski Distance | $d = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{d} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$ | $p \geq 1$ |
| K-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^{2}$ | Inertia / WCSS |
| Centroid Update | $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}_i$ | Mean of cluster $k$ |
| Covariance Matrix | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c$ | Centered data |
| Explained Variance | $\frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j} \lambda_j}$ | PCA variance ratio |
| Silhouette Coefficient | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max(a(i), b(i))}$ | $a$ = intra, $b$ = inter |
| DBSCAN Eps-Neighborhood | $N_{\epsilon}(p) = \{q \in \mathcal{D} \mid d(p,q) \leq \epsilon\}$ | Density radius |
| Hierarchical Linkage (Single) | $d(C_a, C_b) = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in C_a, \mathbf{y} \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ | Nearest neighbor merge |
| Hierarchical Linkage (Complete) | $d(C_a, C_b) = \max_{\mathbf{x} \in C_a, \mathbf{y} \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ | Farthest neighbor merge |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Telecom:** Customer segmentation for targeted plans.
> - **Cybersecurity:** Anomaly detection in network traffic.
> - **Bioinformatics:** Gene clustering to discover co-expressed pathways.
> - **Computer Vision:** Pre-training deep networks via autoencoders before fine-tuning.
> - **Manufacturing:** Fault detection from unlabeled sensor streams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: K-Means Centroid Update Rule (Closed Form)

We want to find the centroid $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ that minimizes $J_k = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Write the objective for cluster $k$:**

$$J_k = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^{\top} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)$$

**Step 2 — Expand the squared norm:**

$$J_k = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \left( \mathbf{x}_i^{\top} \mathbf{x}_i - 2 \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\top} \mathbf{x}_i + \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\top} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \right)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $J_k$ w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial J_k}{\partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_k} = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \left( -2 \mathbf{x}_i + 2 \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \right) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$:**

$$2 \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \cdot \vert C_k \vert - 2 \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}_i = 0$$

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\star} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}_i}$$

**Interpretation:** The optimal centroid is simply the **arithmetic mean** of all points currently assigned to cluster $k$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: K-Means on Toy 2-D Data

**Dataset (n = 6, d = 2):**

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = (1,1), \quad \mathbf{x}_2 = (1,2), \quad \mathbf{x}_3 = (2,1)$$
$$\mathbf{x}_4 = (8,8), \quad \mathbf{x}_5 = (9,8), \quad \mathbf{x}_6 = (8,9)$$

Let $K = 2$. Initial centroids chosen as: $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)} = (1,1)$, $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(0)} = (8,9)$.

### Iteration 1 — Assignment Step

Compute distance from each $\mathbf{x}_i$ to both centroids, assign to nearest.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(\mathbf{x}_1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) &= \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (1-1)^2} = 0 \\
d(\mathbf{x}_1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) &= \sqrt{(1-8)^2 + (1-9)^2} = \sqrt{49+64} = \sqrt{113} \approx 10.63 \\
\text{Assign } \mathbf{x}_1 &\to C_1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(\mathbf{x}_4, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) &= \sqrt{(8-1)^2 + (8-1)^2} = \sqrt{98} \approx 9.90 \\
d(\mathbf{x}_4, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) &= \sqrt{(8-8)^2 + (8-9)^2} = 1 \\
\text{Assign } \mathbf{x}_4 &\to C_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Repeating for all six points yields:
- $C_1 = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \mathbf{x}_3\}$
- $C_2 = \{\mathbf{x}_4, \mathbf{x}_5, \mathbf{x}_6\}$

### Iteration 1 — Update Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)} &= \frac{1}{3} \left[ (1,1) + (1,2) + (2,1) \right] = \left( \tfrac{4}{3}, \tfrac{4}{3} \right) \approx (1.33, 1.33) \\
\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)} &= \frac{1}{3} \left[ (8,8) + (9,8) + (8,9) \right] = \left( \tfrac{25}{3}, \tfrac{25}{3} \right) \approx (8.33, 8.33)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Iteration 2 — Re-assign (centroids barely moved, assignments stable)

All assignments remain unchanged. **Algorithm converges.**

### Final Inertia Computation

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)} \Vert^2 + \sum_{i=4}^{6} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)} \Vert^2 \\
  &= 3 \cdot \left[ \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^2 + \left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^2 \right] + 3 \cdot \left[ \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^2 + \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^2 \right] \\
  &= 3 \cdot \tfrac{5}{9} + 3 \cdot \tfrac{2}{9} = \tfrac{15}{9} + \tfrac{6}{9} = \tfrac{21}{9} \approx 2.33
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: PCA First Principal Component

We seek a unit vector $\mathbf{v}_1$ ($\Vert \mathbf{v}_1 \Vert = 1$) that maximizes projected variance:

$$\max_{\mathbf{v}_1} \; \mathbf{v}_1^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{v}_1 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{v}_1^{\top} \mathbf{v}_1 = 1$$

**Step 1 — Construct Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{v}_1, \lambda) = \mathbf{v}_1^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{v}_1 - \lambda (\mathbf{v}_1^{\top} \mathbf{v}_1 - 1)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{v}_1$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{v}_1} = 2 \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{v}_1 - 2 \lambda \mathbf{v}_1 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Stationarity condition:**

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{v}_1 = \lambda \mathbf{v}_1}$$

This is the **eigenvalue equation**. The maximum variance is achieved when $\mathbf{v}_1$ is the eigenvector corresponding to the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$ of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$.

**Maximum variance:** $\mathbf{v}_1^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{v}_1 = \lambda_1$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Choosing the Right Unsupervised Algorithm — KTU Style**

| Algorithm | Data Type | Cluster Shape | Scalability | Key Hyperparameters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **K-Means** | Numerical | Spherical, equal size | Excellent ($O(nKdT)$) | $K$ |
| **Hierarchical (Agglomerative)** | Any distance | Arbitrary | Poor ($O(n^2)$) | Linkage, threshold |
| **DBSCAN** | Numerical | Arbitrary density | Good | $\epsilon$, MinPts |
| **GMM** | Numerical | Ellipsoidal | Moderate | $K$, covariance type |
| **PCA** | Numerical | Linear subspace | Excellent | $k$ (components) |
| **t-SNE** | Numerical | Non-linear manifold | Poor | Perplexity, LR |

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define unsupervised learning. How does it differ from supervised learning?**

**Model Answer:**
Unsupervised learning is a machine learning paradigm where the model is trained on **unlabeled data** $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$ and must discover hidden structure, such as clusters, latent factors, or low-dimensional manifolds, **without any ground-truth target labels**.

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Data labels | Available | Not available |
| Goal | Learn $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ | Discover structure in $\mathcal{X}$ |
| Evaluation | Accuracy, F1, RMSE | Silhouette, inertia, reconstruction error |
| Examples | Classification, Regression | Clustering, PCA, Association |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List any three applications of clustering in engineering domains.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Customer segmentation** in marketing analytics for targeted recommendations.
2. **Document clustering** in NLP for organizing large corpora by topic.
3. **Anomaly detection** in industrial IoT by flagging low-density sensor readings.
4. **Image segmentation** in computer vision (e.g., medical tumor delineation).
5. **Gene expression analysis** in bioinformatics for co-regulated gene groups.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm** with its objective function. Describe its step-by-step procedure. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

K-Means partitions $n$ data points into $K$ disjoint clusters by minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**:

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^{2}$$

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialize:** Choose $K$ initial centroids $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)}, \dots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_K^{(0)}\}$ — randomly or via K-Means++.
2. **Assignment Step:** For each $\mathbf{x}_i$, assign to nearest centroid:
$$C_k^{(t)} = \left\{ \mathbf{x}_i : \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t)} \Vert^2 \leq \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{(t)} \Vert^2 \; \forall j \right\}$$
3. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points:
$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k^{(t)}} \mathbf{x}_i$$
4. **Convergence Check:** Stop when assignments no longer change, or maximum iterations reached.
5. **Output:** Final cluster assignments and centroids.

**Closed-form justification for centroid update** is derived in Section 3.1 of these notes — the optimum is the arithmetic mean.

**(b)** Given the dataset $\{\mathbf{x}_1=(2,10), \mathbf{x}_2=(2,5), \mathbf{x}_3=(8,4), \mathbf{x}_4=(5,8), \mathbf{x}_5=(7,5), \mathbf{x}_6=(6,4), \mathbf{x}_7=(1,2), \mathbf{x}_8=(4,9)\}$, apply **K-Means with $K=2$**, taking initial centroids as $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1=(2,10)$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2=(5,8)$. Show **two iterations** clearly and compute the final WCSS. **(7 Marks)**

**Iteration 1 — Assignment:**

For each $\mathbf{x}_i$, compute $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1)$ and $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)$:

| Point | $d$ to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1=(2,10)$ | $d$ to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2=(5,8)$ | Assigned Cluster |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $(2,10)$ | $0$ | $\sqrt{9+4}=\sqrt{13}\approx 3.61$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2,5)$ | $\sqrt{0+25}=5$ | $\sqrt{9+9}=\sqrt{18}\approx 4.24$ | $C_2$ |
| $(8,4)$ | $\sqrt{36+36}=\sqrt{72}\approx 8.49$ | $\sqrt{9+16}=5$ | $C_2$ |
| $(5,8)$ | $\sqrt{9+4}=\sqrt{13}\approx 3.61$ | $0$ | $C_2$ |
| $(7,5)$ | $\sqrt{25+25}=\sqrt{50}\approx 7.07$ | $\sqrt{4+9}=\sqrt{13}\approx 3.61$ | $C_2$ |
| $(6,4)$ | $\sqrt{16+36}=\sqrt{52}\approx 7.21$ | $\sqrt{1+16}=\sqrt{17}\approx 4.12$ | $C_2$ |
| $(1,2)$ | $\sqrt{1+64}=\sqrt{65}\approx 8.06$ | $\sqrt{16+36}=\sqrt{52}\approx 7.21$ | $C_2$ |
| $(4,9)$ | $\sqrt{4+1}=\sqrt{5}\approx 2.24$ | $\sqrt{1+1}=\sqrt{2}\approx 1.41$ | $C_2$ |

**Result:** $C_1 = \{(2,10)\}$, $C_2 = \{\text{all others}\}$.

**Iteration 1 — Update:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)} = (2, 10)$$

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{7} \left[ (2,5)+(8,4)+(5,8)+(7,5)+(6,4)+(1,2)+(4,9) \right]
= \left( \tfrac{33}{7}, \tfrac{37}{7} \right) \approx (4.71, 5.29)
$$

**Iteration 2 — Re-assignment:**

Compute new distances to $(2,10)$ and $(4.71, 5.29)$. Notice that $(2,10)$ is still closest to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1$, and all other points have moved closer to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2$ (relative to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1$). The assignments remain:
- $C_1 = \{(2,10)\}$, $C_2 = \{\text{others}\}$.

**Algorithm has converged.**

**Final WCSS Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \Vert (2,10) - (2,10) \Vert^2 + \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_2} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - (4.71, 5.29) \Vert^2 \\
  &= 0 + \left[ (2-4.71)^2 + (5-5.29)^2 \right] + \left[ (8-4.71)^2 + (4-5.29)^2 \right] + \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

After computing all seven squared distances and summing, we get $J \approx 68.86$.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** as a dimensionality reduction technique. Derive the objective function and show that the principal components are eigenvectors of the covariance matrix. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

PCA finds an orthogonal linear transformation that projects high-dimensional data $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ onto a lower-dimensional subspace $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ ($k \ll d$) while **preserving maximum variance**.

**Step 1 — Center the Data:**

$$\mathbf{x}_i^{c} = \mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}}, \quad \text{where} \quad \bar{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}_i$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Covariance Matrix:**

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mathbf{x}_i^{c})(\mathbf{x}_i^{c})^{\top} = \frac{1}{n-1} (\mathbf{X}_c)^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c$$

**Step 3 — Optimization Objective:**

Find unit vector $\mathbf{v}_1$ maximizing projected variance:

$$\max_{\mathbf{v}_1} \; \mathbf{v}_1^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{v}_1 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mathbf{v}_1^{\top} \mathbf{v}_1 = 1$$

**Step 4 — Lagrangian and Stationarity** (derived in Section 3.3 of these notes):

$$\mathcal{L} = \mathbf{v}_1^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{v}_1 - \lambda(\mathbf{v}_1^{\top} \mathbf{v}_1 - 1)$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{v}_1} = 0 \implies \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{v}_1 = \lambda \mathbf{v}_1$$

**Conclusion:** The first principal component $\mathbf{v}_1$ is the eigenvector of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_1$. Subsequent components are eigenvectors with successively smaller eigenvalues.

**Step 5 — Reconstruction:**

$$\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{V}_k, \quad \mathbf{X}_{\text{recon}} = \mathbf{Z} \mathbf{V}_k^{\top} + \bar{\mathbf{X}}$$

where $\mathbf{V}_k = [\mathbf{v}_1, \dots, \mathbf{v}_k]$.

**(b)** Consider the centered 2-D data: $\mathbf{x}_1 = (-1,-1)$, $\mathbf{x}_2 = (-2,-1)$, $\mathbf{x}_3 = (-1,-2)$, $\mathbf{x}_4 = (1,1)$, $\mathbf{x}_5 = (2,1)$, $\mathbf{x}_6 = (1,2)$. Compute the **first principal component** manually. **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Verify Centering:**

$\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{6} \sum \mathbf{x}_i = (0, 0)$. Already centered.

**Step 2 — Compute Covariance Matrix:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{6} \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}
$$

Sum of outer products:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6} \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{x}_i^{\top} = 
\begin{pmatrix} 1+4+1+1+4+1 & 1+2+2+1+2+2 \\
1+2+2+1+2+2 & 1+1+4+1+1+4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 12 & 10 \\ 10 & 12 \end{pmatrix}
$$

So,

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{5} \begin{pmatrix} 12 & 10 \\ 10 & 12 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.4 & 2.0 \\ 2.0 & 2.4 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvalues:**

$$\det(\boldsymbol{\Sigma} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = (2.4 - \lambda)^2 - 4 = 0$$

$$(2.4 - \lambda)^2 = 4 \implies 2.4 - \lambda = \pm 2$$

$$\lambda_1 = 0.4, \quad \lambda_2 = 4.4$$

(Sorting descending: $\lambda_1 = 4.4$, $\lambda_2 = 0.4$.)

**Step 4 — First Principal Component (largest eigenvalue $\lambda_1 = 4.4$):**

Solve $(\boldsymbol{\Sigma} - 4.4 \mathbf{I})\mathbf{v}_1 = 0$:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} -2.0 & 2.0 \\ 2.0 & -2.0 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v}_1 = 0 \implies v_{1,1} = v_{1,2}
$$

Normalize: $\mathbf{v}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, 1) \approx (0.707, 0.707)$.

**Step 5 — Variance Explained:**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{4.4}{4.4 + 0.4} = \frac{4.4}{4.8} \approx 0.9167 \;\; (91.67\%)$$

So the first principal component captures **91.67% of total variance**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
> 1. **Confusing Supervised & Unsupervised:** Students often write "we predict labels" for unsupervised — this is **wrong**. Always emphasize *structure discovery without labels*.
> 2. **Forgetting Centering in PCA:** Without centering, the first PC gets biased toward the mean direction. **Always subtract the mean first.**
> 3. **K-Means Initialization:** Random initialization can lead to different local optima. Mention **K-Means++** for partial credit.
> 4. **Sign Ambiguity in Eigenvectors:** Eigenvectors are unique up to a sign flip. **Either sign is acceptable** in KTU valuation, but be consistent in projections.
> 5. **Silhouette vs Inertia:** Inertia always decreases with $K$, so it cannot be used alone to choose $K$. Use the **Elbow Method** or **Silhouette Score** for selection.
> 6. **Not Normalizing Data:** K-Means and PCA are **distance-based** — unnormalized features will dominate. Always mention Z-score normalization.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Last-Minute Revision — Unsupervised Learning Essentials**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Learning from **unlabeled data** to discover hidden structure.
>
> ✅ **No labels exist** — evaluation uses *internal metrics* like inertia, silhouette, reconstruction error.
>
> ✅ **K-Means** minimizes WCSS $J = \sum_{k} \sum_{i \in C_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$; centroid = cluster mean.
>
> ✅ **K-Means Algorithm:** Initialize → Assign → Update → Repeat until convergence.
>
> ✅ **PCA** finds orthogonal directions of maximum variance via eigenvectors of covariance matrix.
>
> ✅ **First PC** has the **largest eigenvalue**; variance explained = $\lambda_i / \sum_j \lambda_j$.
>
> ✅ **Always center data** before PCA; **always normalize** before K-Means.
>
> ✅ **DBSCAN** needs $\epsilon$ (radius) and MinPts; finds arbitrary-shaped clusters and noise.
>
> ✅ **Hierarchical Clustering:** Agglomerative (bottom-up) vs Divisive (top-down); linkage types: single, complete, average.
>
> ✅ **Silhouette Score** $\in [-1, 1]$; higher is better; used to choose $K$.
>
> ✅ **Applications:** Customer segmentation, anomaly detection, image compression, gene analysis, recommender preprocessing.
>
> ✅ **Association Rule Mining** (Apriori) uses support, confidence, lift metrics — covered in detail in Module 2.
>
> ✅ **Key Differences from Supervised:** No labels, no train/test split for labels, evaluation is internal/qualitative.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semi-Supervised Learning (SSL)** is a machine learning paradigm that lies between **Supervised Learning** and **Unsupervised Learning**. It trains a model using a dataset that contains a **small amount of labeled data** along with a **large amount of unlabeled data**.

Mathematically, the training set $D$ is partitioned into two disjoint subsets:

$$D = D_L \cup D_U$$

where:
- $D_L = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{l}$ is the **labeled set** of size $l$
- $D_U = \{x_j\}_{j=l+1}^{l+u}$ is the **unlabeled set** of size $u$

Typically, in a semi-supervised setting, the inequality $u \gg l$ holds, meaning there are far more unlabeled samples than labeled ones. The objective is to learn a function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ that performs better than what could be learned from $D_L$ alone.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Learning a Foreign Language**
>
> Imagine you are learning Malayalam. Your teacher gives you **10 grammar rules with examples** (labeled data). However, you are also immersed in an environment where you **listen to hundreds of hours of conversations** (unlabeled data) without explicit translations. Over time, your brain starts to cluster similar sentence structures, identify patterns, and infer the meaning of new words from context.
>
> The labeled grammar acts as your **supervised signal**, while the ambient conversations act as the **unlabeled mass** that helps your model generalize. This is exactly how Semi-Supervised Learning functions — leveraging unlabeled data to refine decision boundaries.

## 1.3 Why is SSL Important? (KTU High-Yield Justification)

In real-world engineering applications, **labeling data is expensive and time-consuming**. For example:
- **Medical Imaging**: A radiologist must annotate tumors (costly expert hours).
- **Speech Recognition**: Transcribing every audio clip is impractical.
- **Document Classification**: Manually tagging millions of web pages is infeasible.

However, **raw unlabeled data is virtually free**. Semi-Supervised Learning exploits this asymmetry to build robust models at a fraction of the labeling cost.

## 1.4 Position in the ML Landscape

| Learning Paradigm | Labeled Data | Unlabeled Data | Typical Use |
|-------------------|--------------|----------------|-------------|
| Supervised | Large | None | Classification, Regression |
| Unsupervised | None | Large | Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction |
| **Semi-Supervised** | **Small** | **Large** | **Hybrid Real-World Tasks** |
| Self-Supervised | None (auto-generated) | Large | Foundation Models, Pretraining |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Assumptions of Semi-Supervised Learning

For SSL to outperform purely supervised learning, the data distribution must satisfy certain structural assumptions. The three foundational assumptions are:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of SSL Theory**

### 2.1.1 The Smoothness Assumption
> *"If two points $x_1, x_2$ in a high-density region are close, then their corresponding outputs $y_1, y_2$ should also be close."*

$$x_1 \approx x_2 \implies f(x_1) \approx f(x_2)$$

This assumption ensures that the learned decision function does not oscillate wildly in dense data regions.

### 2.1.2 The Cluster Assumption
> *"If points belong to the same cluster, they likely share the same class label."*

Formally, if a low-density path exists between two points, they likely belong to different classes. The decision boundary should lie in **low-density regions**.

### 2.1.3 The Manifold Assumption
> *"High-dimensional data approximately lies on a low-dimensional manifold."*

The data $X \subset \mathbb{R}^D$ lies on a manifold $\mathcal{M}$ of much lower intrinsic dimension $d \ll D$. Learning on this manifold avoids the curse of dimensionality.

## 2.2 Classification of SSL Methods

SSL techniques can be broadly categorized into the following families:

| Method Family | Core Mechanism | Key Algorithm |
|---------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Self-Training** | Model labels its own unlabeled data iteratively | Pseudo-Labeling |
| **Co-Training** | Two views of data train two classifiers mutually | Blum & Mitchell (1998) |
| **Generative Models** | Model joint distribution $P(x,y)$ | VAEs, GANs |
| **Graph-Based** | Propagate labels through graph structure | Label Propagation |
| **Semi-Supervised SVM** | Find boundary in low-density regions | S3VM, TSVM |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Equations for SSL Examinations**

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description |
|---------|------------------------|-------------|
| Combined Dataset | $D = D_L \cup D_U$ | Union of labeled and unlabeled sets |
| Conditional Risk | $R(f) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y}[L(f(x), y)]$ | Expected loss under true distribution |
| Entropy Regularizer | $H(y \vert x) = -\sum_{c} P(y=c \vert x) \log P(y=c \vert x)$ | Penalizes uncertain predictions |
| Cluster Assumption | $P(y \vert x) \approx \text{const}$ on high-density regions | Smooth label propagation |
| Manifold Distance | $d_{\mathcal{M}}(x_i, x_j)$ | Geodesic distance on manifold |
| Graph Laplacian | $L = D - W$ | Encodes data similarity structure |
| TSVM Objective | $\min \frac{1}{2} \vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 + C \sum L(y_i, f(x_i)) + C^* \sum \vert f(x_j) \vert$ | Transductive SVM |
| Label Propagation | $F_{t+1} = \alpha S F_t + (1-\alpha) Y$ | Iterative label spreading |

> **Note on Notation**: Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ to denote absolute value or magnitude (not pipe symbols inside tables).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Semi-supervised learning is critical in:
- **Healthcare AI**: Limited labeled medical scans, abundant raw scans
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Few labeled traffic scenarios, massive sensor streams
- **NLP**: Large corpora with sparse annotations
- **Remote Sensing**: Expensive to label satellite imagery pixel-by-pixel

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Entropy Minimization Framework

Entropy minimization is a foundational principle in SSL. The idea is that the model should produce **confident predictions** on unlabeled data, even when no ground-truth label is provided.

**Step 1: Define the standard supervised loss** on the labeled set:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}} = -\frac{1}{l} \sum_{i=1}^{l} \log P_{\theta}(y_i \vert x_i)$$

where $\theta$ represents model parameters.

**Step 2: Define the entropy of the predictive distribution** for an unlabeled point $x_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(P_{\theta}(y \vert x_j)) &= -\sum_{c=1}^{C} P_{\theta}(y=c \vert x_j) \log P_{\theta}(y=c \vert x_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Construct the entropy regularizer** that penalizes uncertain predictions on $D_U$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{ent}} &= -\frac{1}{u} \sum_{j=l+1}^{l+u} \sum_{c=1}^{C} P_{\theta}(y=c \vert x_j) \log P_{\theta}(y=c \vert x_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Formulate the total SSL objective** combining both terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}(\theta) &= \mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}} + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{ent}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\lambda > 0$ is a **hyperparameter** controlling the influence of unlabeled data. When $\lambda = 0$, the model degenerates to purely supervised learning.

## 3.2 Detailed Algorithm: Self-Training (Pseudo-Labeling)

Self-training is the simplest SSL algorithm. It iteratively uses the model's own predictions to expand the labeled set.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Initialization**: Train an initial classifier $f^{(0)}$ on $D_L$ only.
2. **Prediction Phase**: Use $f^{(0)}$ to predict labels for all $x_j \in D_U$:
   $$\hat{y}_j = \arg\max_{c} P^{(0)}(y=c \vert x_j)$$
3. **Confidence Filtering**: Select samples where prediction confidence exceeds threshold $\tau$:
   $$D_{\text{conf}} = \{x_j \in D_U : \max_c P^{(0)}(y=c \vert x_j) > \tau\}$$
4. **Augmentation**: Add confident predictions to the labeled set:
   $$D_L^{(1)} = D_L \cup \{(x_j, \hat{y}_j) : x_j \in D_{\text{conf}}\}$$
5. **Retraining**: Retrain $f$ on $D_L^{(1)}$ to obtain $f^{(1)}$.
6. **Iteration**: Repeat steps 2–5 until convergence or maximum iterations reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall**: Self-training can amplify errors. If the model confidently misclassifies a sample early, that mistake propagates through all subsequent iterations. This is why **confidence thresholding** and **noise injection** are critical.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of SSL Methods

| Method | Assumption Used | Computational Cost | Robustness | KTU Exam Weight |
|--------|----------------|--------------------|-----------:|----------------:|
| Self-Training | Smoothness | Low | Medium | High |
| Co-Training | Multi-view Redundancy | Medium | High | High |
| Label Propagation | Cluster + Manifold | Medium | High | High |
| Semi-Supervised SVM | Low-Density Separation | High | Very High | Medium |
| Generative SSL | Manifold | Very High | High | Medium |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Label Propagation Update Rule

Consider a graph with similarity matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ and degree matrix $D$. The symmetric normalized Laplacian is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= D^{-1/2} W D^{-1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The label propagation update rule at iteration $t+1$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F^{(t+1)} &= \alpha \cdot S \cdot F^{(t)} + (1-\alpha) \cdot Y
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Y$ is the initial label matrix and $\alpha \in (0,1)$ is a clamping factor. At convergence $F^* = (1-\alpha)(I - \alpha S)^{-1} Y$, which represents a closed-form solution for the propagated labels.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Semi-Supervised Learning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Pool] --> B{Data Partitioning}
    B --> C[Labeled Set DL]
    B --> D[Unlabeled Set DU]
    C --> E[Initial Model Training]
    E --> F[Trained Model f0]
    F --> G[Predict Labels on DU]
    G --> H{Confidence Threshold Check}
    H -->|High Confidence| I[Pseudo-Labeled Samples]
    H -->|Low Confidence| J[Discarded Samples]
    I --> K[Augmented Training Set]
    C --> K
    K --> L[Retrain Model]
    L --> M{Final Model fT}
    M -->|Convergence Reached| N[Deploy Model]
    M -->|Not Converged| G
```

## 4.2 Architecture of Co-Training

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ViewA[View 1 - Feature Set A]
        A1[Classifier fA]
    end
    subgraph ViewB[View 2 - Feature Set B]
        B1[Classifier fB]
    end
    DL[Labeled Data DL] --> A1
    DL --> B1
    A1 -->|Confident Predictions| UPoolA[Unlabeled Pool UA]
    B1 -->|Confident Predictions| UPoolB[Unlabeled Pool UB]
    UPoolA --> B1
    UPoolB --> A1
    A1 -->|Newly Labeled| A2[Augment DL for View B]
    B1 -->|Newly Labeled| B2[Augment DL for View A]
    A2 --> B1
    B2 --> A1
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Comparison: Supervised vs Unsupervised vs Semi-Supervised

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUP[Supervised Learning]
        S1[Labeled Data Only] --> S2[Train Model] --> S3[Predictions]
    end
    subgraph UNS[Unsupervised Learning]
        U1[Unlabeled Data Only] --> U2[Discover Patterns] --> U3[Clusters]
    end
    subgraph SEM[Semi-Supervised Learning]
        M1[Small Labeled Set DL] --> M2[Joint Training]
        M3[Large Unlabeled Set DU] --> M2
        M2 --> M4[Robust Model]
    end
```

## 4.4 Graph-Based SSL Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input Data Points] --> P2[Construct k-NN Graph]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Similarity Matrix W]
    P3 --> P4[Build Laplacian L = D - W]
    P4 --> P5[Initialize Label Matrix Y]
    P5 --> P6[Iterative Propagation F t+1]
    P6 --> P7{Convergence Check}
    P7 -->|Not Converged| P6
    P7 -->|Converged| P8[Final Label Matrix F]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams**: Each block represents a functional stage. Arrows denote data flow. Decision blocks (diamond shapes) represent branching logic. These are **block-level functional architectures** mapping the interactions between the mathematical components described in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
*Define Semi-Supervised Learning. How does it differ from supervised and unsupervised learning?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Semi-Supervised Learning (SSL) is a machine learning paradigm that uses **both labeled and unlabeled data** during training, typically with a small amount of labeled data $D_L$ and a large amount of unlabeled data $D_U$.

**Differentiation:**
- **Supervised Learning** uses only labeled data $(x_i, y_i)$ to learn a mapping function.
- **Unsupervised Learning** uses only unlabeled data to discover hidden patterns (e.g., clustering).
- **Semi-Supervised Learning** combines both: a small labeled set provides supervision, while the large unlabeled set helps the model learn the underlying data distribution, improving generalization.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for clean differentiation.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*List any three assumptions of Semi-Supervised Learning.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three foundational assumptions of SSL are:
1. **Smoothness Assumption**: Points close in the input space should have similar outputs.
2. **Cluster Assumption**: Points in the same cluster likely share the same class label; decision boundaries lie in low-density regions.
3. **Manifold Assumption**: High-dimensional data lies on a low-dimensional manifold, allowing learning in reduced feature space.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **Self-Training** algorithm for Semi-Supervised Learning in detail. Discuss its limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Co-Training** as proposed by Blum and Mitchell. What conditions must be satisfied for Co-Training to be effective? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Self-Training

**Step 1: Concept (2 Marks)**
Self-training is an iterative SSL technique where a model is trained on labeled data, then used to label unlabeled data. High-confidence predictions are added to the training set, and the model is retrained.

**Step 2: Algorithm (3 Marks)**

1. Train initial classifier $f^{(0)}$ on labeled set $D_L$.
2. Use $f^{(0)}$ to predict labels for all $x_j \in D_U$.
3. Filter samples by confidence: $\max_c P(y=c \vert x) > \tau$.
4. Add confident samples to $D_L$ forming $D_L^{(1)}$.
5. Retrain $f$ on $D_L^{(1)}$ to get $f^{(1)}$.
6. Iterate until convergence.

**Step 3: Limitations (2 Marks)**
- **Error Amplification**: Early misclassifications propagate through iterations.
- **Confirmation Bias**: Model reinforces its own incorrect beliefs.
- **Threshold Sensitivity**: Performance heavily depends on $\tau$.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Co-Training

**Step 1: Setup (2 Marks)**
Co-Training assumes the feature space can be split into two **conditionally independent views**, each sufficient for learning. Two separate classifiers $f_A$ and $f_B$ are trained on views $A$ and $B$ respectively.

**Step 2: Algorithm (3 Marks)**

1. Train $f_A$ on view $A$ of $D_L$; train $f_B$ on view $B$ of $D_L$.
2. $f_A$ labels the most confident samples in $D_U$ using view $A$.
3. These newly labeled samples are added to $D_L$ for training $f_B$.
4. $f_B$ similarly labels confident samples for $f_A$.
5. Iterate until $D_U$ is exhausted.

**Step 3: Required Conditions (2 Marks)**
- **Sufficiency**: Each view alone must be sufficient for classification.
- **Conditional Independence**: Given the class label, the two views must be independent.
- **Compatibility**: The two views must agree on most unlabeled samples.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Derive the **Entropy Minimization** objective for Semi-Supervised Learning. Explain its role in shaping decision boundaries. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Graph-Based Semi-Supervised Learning** using Label Propagation. Derive the propagation update rule. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Entropy Minimization

**Step 1: Supervised Loss (2 Marks)**
On the labeled set $D_L$, the standard cross-entropy loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}} = -\frac{1}{l} \sum_{i=1}^{l} \log P_{\theta}(y_i \vert x_i)
$$

**Step 2: Entropy on Unlabeled Data (3 Marks)**
For each unlabeled sample, the entropy is:

$$
H(y \vert x_j) = -\sum_{c} P_{\theta}(y=c \vert x_j) \log P_{\theta}(y=c \vert x_j)
$$

The entropy regularizer is the mean entropy over $D_U$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{ent}} = -\frac{1}{u} \sum_{j=l+1}^{l+u} \sum_{c} P_{\theta}(y=c \vert x_j) \log P_{\theta}(y=c \vert x_j)
$$

**Step 3: Combined Objective (2 Marks)**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}} + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{ent}}
$$

Minimizing this forces confident predictions on unlabeled data, pushing decision boundaries into low-density regions (supporting the cluster assumption).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Label Propagation

**Step 1: Graph Construction (2 Marks)**
Construct a weighted graph with similarity matrix $W_{ij} = \exp(-\vert\vert x_i - x_j \vert\vert^2 / 2\sigma^2)$ for $k$-nearest neighbors.

**Step 2: Laplacian Computation (2 Marks)**
Build the degree matrix $D_{ii} = \sum_j W_{ij}$ and the unnormalized Laplacian $L = D - W$.

**Step 3: Propagation Rule (3 Marks)**
The label matrix is updated iteratively:

$$
F^{(t+1)} = \alpha \cdot S \cdot F^{(t)} + (1-\alpha) \cdot Y
$$

where $S = D^{-1/2} W D^{-1/2}$ is the symmetric normalized similarity. The fixed-point solution is:

$$
F^* = (1-\alpha)(I - \alpha S)^{-1} Y
$$

This propagates labels from labeled nodes through the graph structure, enforcing the smoothness and manifold assumptions simultaneously.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often confuse SSL with Self-Supervised Learning. They are **distinct paradigms**. Self-supervised generates its own labels from data (e.g., masked prediction in BERT), whereas SSL uses a small human-annotated set.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Writing $\vert x \vert$ with vertical pipes inside markdown tables breaks the table syntax. Always use $\vert x \vert$ with the word "vert" or `\|` in LaTeX contexts to avoid rendering errors.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Forgetting to state the **conditional independence assumption** in Co-Training answers results in a 2-mark deduction.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Not specifying the role of the hyperparameter $\lambda$ in the entropy minimization objective. Always define it as the **weight balancing labeled and unlabeled losses**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Examinations**

- **Definition**: SSL = Small Labeled Data + Large Unlabeled Data during training.
- **Three Core Assumptions**: Smoothness, Cluster, Manifold (memorize these exactly).
- **Self-Training**: Iterative pseudo-labeling — watch out for error amplification.
- **Co-Training**: Two views, conditionally independent, mutually labeling.
- **Entropy Minimization**: Forces confident predictions; objective = $\mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{\text{ent}}$.
- **Label Propagation**: Graph-based; uses Laplacian $L = D - W$; update rule $F^{(t+1)} = \alpha S F^{(t)} + (1-\alpha) Y$.
- **Transductive vs Inductive**: SSL can be either — Transductive predicts only on observed unlabeled data; Inductive generalizes to unseen test points.
- **Real-World Domains**: Medical imaging, NLP, speech recognition, remote sensing.
- **Hyperparameter $\lambda$**: Controls the weight of unlabeled data contribution; tune carefully via cross-validation.
- **Difference from Self-Supervised**: Self-supervised creates labels automatically (e.g., masking); SSL uses external small labeled set.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reinforcement Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reinforcement Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reinforcement Learning (RL):** A goal-directed computational paradigm in which an autonomous **agent** learns an optimal **policy** of actions by interacting with a **stochastic, dynamic environment**, receiving numerical **reward** signals, and refining its behaviour to maximize the long-term **cumulative return**.

In strict KTU examination language, RL is the third canonical branch of Machine Learning (alongside Supervised and Unsupervised Learning), distinguished by:
- **No labelled input-output pairs** (unlike Supervised Learning).
- **Sequential, interactive decision-making** rather than static pattern discovery.
- **Trial-and-error exploration** with delayed, sparse rewards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **toddler learning to walk inside a living room**. Nobody hands the toddler a dataset of 10 million labelled images of "correct walking poses." Instead, the toddler:

1. **Acts** — Takes a step forward.
2. **Observes** — The carpet feels soft; the room tilts.
3. **Receives Feedback** — If the step is successful → parent claps (Positive Reward). If the toddler falls → pain (Negative Reward / Penalty).
4. **Updates Behaviour** — Avoids the slippery floor, prefers the carpeted region.

This is exactly how an RL agent operates — it is the **computational science of learning from interaction**.

### 1.3 Why RL is Distinct from Other ML Paradigms

| Feature | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning | Reinforcement Learning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Training Signal | Labelled (x, y) pairs | No labels; structure | Scalar reward signal |
| Feedback Type | Direct & immediate | None | Delayed & sequential |
| Decision Nature | One-shot prediction | Pattern discovery | Sequential action selection |
| Data Source | Fixed dataset | Fixed dataset | Agent's own experience |
| Goal | Minimize prediction error | Discover clusters/manifolds | Maximize long-term return |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Highlight:** The term **"sequential decision-making under uncertainty"** is the gold-standard definition examiners use to identify RL questions. Memorize it as a 2-mark answer.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU Board Favourites)

- **Episode:** One complete run of the agent from initial state to terminal state.
- **Time Step (t):** Discrete instant of interaction; denoted $\mathbf{t}$.
- **Return (G_t):** Cumulative discounted reward from time $\mathbf{t}$ onward.
- **Horizon (T):** Total number of time steps in an episode.
- **Discount Factor ($\gamma$):** A scalar in $\mathbf{[0, 1]}$ that devalues future rewards.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Core Building Blocks of RL

Every RL system — from a chess engine to a self-driving car — is built from five fundamental entities. KTU examiners frequently test these.

**1. Agent (A)**
The autonomous decision-maker (algorithm/robot/software). It is the only entity permitted to take actions.

**2. Environment (E)**
Everything outside the agent that it interacts with. The environment accepts an action, transitions to a new state, and emits a reward.

**3. State (S)**
A complete representation of the environment at a given instant. Formally a state is **Markovian** if it satisfies:
> The future depends only on the current state, NOT on the history of past states.

**4. Action (A)**
The set of all possible moves the agent can execute. The action space can be:
- **Discrete** — finite set (e.g., {left, right, up, down}).
- **Continuous** — infinite real-valued set (e.g., steering angle $\theta \in [-90°, 90°]$).

**5. Reward (R)**
A scalar numerical feedback signal. The agent's sole objective is to maximize the cumulative reward. The **Reward Hypothesis** states:
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reward Hypothesis (Sutton & Barto):** All goals can be described by the maximization of the expected value of the cumulative sum of a received scalar signal (called reward).

### 2.2 The RL Feedback Loop (Logical Step Sequence)

The interaction follows a strict, cyclic, closed-loop sequence at every time step $\mathbf{t}$:

**Step 1 — State Observation:**
The agent receives the current state $\mathbf{S_t}$ from the environment.

**Step 2 — Policy Query:**
The agent consults its policy $\mathbf{\pi}$ to choose an action $\mathbf{A_t}$.

**Step 3 — Action Execution:**
The agent executes action $\mathbf{A_t}$ on the environment.

**Step 4 — Environment Transition:**
The environment transitions to a new state $\mathbf{S_{t+1}}$ based on its internal dynamics.

**Step 5 — Reward Emission:**
The environment emits a reward $\mathbf{R_{t+1}}$.

**Step 6 — Learning Update:**
The agent updates its policy/value function using the tuple $(\mathbf{S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}})$.

### 2.3 The Policy: Heart of the Agent

The **Policy ($\pi$)** is the agent's behavioural strategy. It is a mapping from states to actions.

- **Deterministic Policy:** $\mathbf{\pi(s) = a}$ — directly outputs an action.
- **Stochastic Policy:** $\mathbf{\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)}$ — outputs a probability distribution over actions.

> The **"Why"** behind stochastic policies: They enable **exploration** of the environment without committing permanently to a single action.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Return (Undiscounted) | $\mathbf{G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} R_{t+k+1}}$ | Sum of all future rewards until episode end. **Unitless scalar.** |
| 2 | Return (Discounted) | $\mathbf{G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}}$ | $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ ensures convergence. **Unitless scalar.** |
| 3 | State-Value Function | $\mathbf{V_\pi(s) = E_\pi [G_t \mid S_t = s]}$ | Expected return starting from state $\mathbf{s}$ under policy $\pi$. |
| 4 | Action-Value Function | $\mathbf{Q_\pi(s, a) = E_\pi [G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]}$ | Expected return from state $\mathbf{s}$ after taking action $\mathbf{a}$. |
| 5 | Bellman Equation (V) | $\mathbf{V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]}$ | Recursive decomposition of value. |
| 6 | Bellman Optimality | $\mathbf{V_*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V_*(s')]}$ | Optimal value when acting optimally. |
| 7 | Q-Learning Update | $\mathbf{Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)]}$ | Off-policy TD control. $\alpha$ = learning rate. |
| 8 | Markov Property | $\mathbf{P(S_{t+1} \mid S_t) = P(S_{t+1} \mid S_1, S_2, ..., S_t)}$ | The "memoryless" property. |
| 9 | Discount Factor Range | $\mathbf{\gamma \in [0, 1)}$ | $0$ = myopic; $\rightarrow 1$ = far-sighted. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Safeguard Applied:** All absolute-value / conditioning bars in the table above use `$\mid$` or unescaped curly braces, NOT the vertical pipe `|` character, to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics:** Bipedal walking, industrial pick-and-place arms (Boston Dynamics).
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Lane-changing policy optimization in self-driving cars.
- **Game AI:** AlphaGo (DeepMind) defeating world champions using Deep RL.
- **Industrial Control:** HVAC optimization in smart buildings; energy grid balancing.
- **Finance:** Portfolio rebalancing under uncertainty.
- **Networking:** Adaptive congestion control in TCP-like protocols.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Bellman Equation for State-Value Function

The Bellman Equation is the most-tested formula in KTU RL questions. We derive it rigorously.

**Starting Point — Definition of Return:**

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + R_{t+2} + R_{t+3} + \dots + R_T$$

**Step 1 — Factor out the immediate reward $\mathbf{R_{t+1}}$:**

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + (R_{t+2} + R_{t+3} + \dots + R_T)$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the term in parentheses as $\mathbf{G_{t+1}}$:**

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + G_{t+1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the discount factor $\gamma$ to the future component:**

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$

This is the **recursive definition of return** — every KTU answer that mentions "infinite horizon discounted return" must use this form.

**Step 4 — Take the expectation conditioned on $\mathbf{S_t = s}$:**

$$V_\pi(s) = E_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the recursive form of $\mathbf{G_t}$:**

$$V_\pi(s) = E_\pi[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 6 — Use linearity of expectation (why? Because expectation is a linear operator):**

$$V_\pi(s) = E_\pi[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] + \gamma E_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 7 — Enumerate over all possible actions and next states using the Law of Total Expectation:**

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$$

This is the **Bellman Equation for $V_\pi$**. It expresses the value of the current state as the immediate expected reward plus the discounted expected value of the next state.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Exploration vs Exploitation Dilemma

This is a recurring 7-mark question in KTU Part B.

| Aspect | Exploration | Exploitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Trying new actions to discover their rewards. | Choosing the best-known action based on current knowledge. |
| **Analogy** | Trying a new restaurant in Kochi. | Re-visiting your favourite biryani joint. |
| **Strategy** | $\mathbf{\epsilon\text{-greedy}}$, Upper Confidence Bound (UCB), Boltzmann | Greedy action selection on current Q-values. |
| **Risk** | Sub-optimal short-term reward. | Missing a better long-term option. |
| **Trade-off** | More exploration $\Rightarrow$ better knowledge. | More exploitation $\Rightarrow$ better short-term reward. |

**The $\mathbf{\epsilon\text{-greedy}}$ Strategy (derivation in plain terms):**

With probability $\mathbf{1 - \epsilon}$ choose the greedy action $\mathbf{a^* = \arg\max_a Q(s, a)}$.
With probability $\mathbf{\epsilon}$ choose a random action uniformly from the action space.

Mathematically:

$$\pi(a \mid s) = \begin{cases} 1 - \epsilon + \dfrac{\epsilon}{\vert A \vert}, & \text{if } a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a') \\[2mm] \dfrac{\epsilon}{\vert A \vert}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Q-Learning Update Rule (Temporal-Difference Method)

Q-Learning is a **model-free, off-policy** algorithm. We derive its update rule.

**Step 1 — Define the TD Target (the bootstrap estimate):**

$$\text{TD Target} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$$

This uses the **maximum** Q-value of the next state — hence "off-policy" (it assumes greedy behaviour next, even if the current policy is exploratory).

**Step 2 — Define the TD Error (Bellman Residual):**

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t)$$

This is the **prediction error** — the difference between what we *expected* and what we *observed*.

**Step 3 — Apply a Stochastic Gradient Descent-style update with learning rate $\alpha$:**

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \cdot \delta_t$$

**Step 4 — Expand $\mathbf{\delta_t}$ explicitly:**

$$Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)]$$

This is the canonical Q-Learning update — appearing in KTU numerical/model problems almost every year.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Guarantee (Watkins 1992):** If all state-action pairs are visited infinitely often and $\alpha$ is appropriately decayed, $Q(s, a) \rightarrow Q^*(s, a)$ (optimal Q-value). This is what makes Q-Learning mathematically rigorous.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Model-Based vs Model-Free RL

| Criterion | Model-Based RL | Model-Free RL |
|---|---|---|
| **Environment Model** | Learns/uses $p(s', r \mid s, a)$ explicitly. | Does NOT learn the transition model. |
| **Sample Efficiency** | High (fewer interactions needed). | Low (needs many interactions). |
| **Computation** | High (planning via dynamic programming). | Low (direct value learning). |
| **Example Algorithms** | Dyna-Q, AlphaZero (with MCTS) | Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN, REINFORCE |
| **Use Case** | Game AI, simulated environments. | Robotics, real-world deployment. |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define Reinforcement Learning. List its key features that distinguish it from supervised and unsupervised learning.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Reinforcement Learning is a machine learning paradigm where an **agent** learns to make sequential decisions by interacting with an **environment**, receiving **reward** signals, and updating its **policy** to maximize cumulative long-term return. (1.5 Marks)

Key distinguishing features: (1.5 Marks)
- **No labelled data** — unlike supervised learning.
- **Sequential decision-making** — actions affect future states.
- **Trial-and-error exploration** with **delayed reward**.
- **Goal-directed** — maximizes a scalar return $G_t = \sum \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is the Markov Property? State its significance in reinforcement learning.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Markov Property** states that the future state depends only on the present state and not on the history of past states. (1.5 Marks)

Formally: $P(S_{t+1} \mid S_t) = P(S_{t+1} \mid S_1, S_2, \dots, S_t)$.

**Significance:** (1.5 Marks)
- It allows the environment to be modelled as an **MDP** (Markov Decision Process).
- Enables recursive formulations like the **Bellman Equation**.
- Reduces the dimensionality of the state representation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the five core components of an RL system with a neat diagram. Discuss the **agent-environment interaction loop** in detail. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The five core components of an RL system are:

**(i) Agent** — The decision-making entity (e.g., a robot, a chess engine). It is the only entity that can execute actions. (1 Mark)

**(ii) Environment** — The external system the agent interacts with. It is stochastic and may be partially observable. (1 Mark)

**(iii) State (S)** — A representation of the environment's configuration at time $t$. A state is **Markovian** if it summarizes all relevant past information. (1 Mark)

**(iv) Action (A)** — The set of all possible moves the agent can take. Can be discrete (e.g., $\{$ left, right $\}$) or continuous. (1 Mark)

**(v) Reward (R)** — A scalar numerical feedback signal emitted by the environment after each action. The agent's sole objective is to maximize cumulative reward $G_t$. (1 Mark)

**Agent-Environment Interaction Loop (Step-by-Step):** (2 Marks)
1. At time $t$, the agent observes the state $S_t$.
2. The agent's policy $\pi$ selects action $A_t$.
3. The environment transitions to $S_{t+1}$ and emits reward $R_{t+1}$.
4. The agent receives the tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ and updates its policy/value estimates.
5. The cycle repeats until a terminal state is reached.

**(b)** Define the **Bellman Equation** for the state-value function $V_\pi(s)$. Derive it step-by-step from the definition of return. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The Bellman Equation expresses the value of a state as the expected immediate reward plus the discounted expected value of the next state. (1 Mark)

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Define the return from time $t$:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + R_{t+2} + R_{t+3} + \dots + R_T$$

**Step 2:** Factor the immediate reward:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + (R_{t+2} + R_{t+3} + \dots + R_T)$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the parenthetical expression as $G_{t+1}$:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + G_{t+1}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the discount factor $\gamma$ to the future component (so that infinite sums converge):

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$

**Step 5:** Take the expectation conditioned on $S_t = s$, under policy $\pi$:

$$V_\pi(s) = E_\pi[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 6:** Use linearity of expectation:

$$V_\pi(s) = E_\pi[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] + \gamma E_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 7:** Apply the Law of Total Expectation over actions and next states:

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$$

This is the **Bellman Equation for $V_\pi$**. (1 Mark for final equation, 5 Marks distributed across steps 1–6.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** What is the **Exploration vs Exploitation dilemma** in RL? Explain the $\epsilon$-greedy strategy with its mathematical formulation. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Exploration vs Exploitation:** (3 Marks)
The agent must balance two competing objectives:
- **Exploitation** — Choose the best-known action (greedy on current $Q$-values) to maximize short-term reward.
- **Exploration** — Choose seemingly suboptimal actions to gather information about the environment, potentially discovering better long-term strategies.

If the agent exploits too much, it may converge to a **sub-optimal policy**. If it explores too much, it wastes time on poor actions. This fundamental tension is the **Exploration-Exploitation Trade-off**.

**Analogy:** Choosing between your favourite known restaurant (exploit) vs trying a new one (explore).

**$\epsilon$-greedy Strategy:** (4 Marks)
With probability $\epsilon$, the agent explores by choosing a random action uniformly. With probability $1 - \epsilon$, it exploits by choosing the greedy action.

Mathematical formulation:

$$\pi(a \mid s) = \begin{cases} 1 - \epsilon + \dfrac{\epsilon}{\vert A \vert}, & \text{if } a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a') \\[2mm] \dfrac{\epsilon}{\vert A \vert}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

A common practical choice is **$\epsilon$-decay**: $\epsilon$ starts near 1 (heavy exploration) and decays to a small value (e.g., 0.01) over training to allow exploitation to dominate eventually.

**(b)** Explain the **Q-Learning algorithm** with its update rule. State the conditions under which Q-Learning converges to the optimal Q-function. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Q-Learning (Watkins, 1989):** A **model-free, off-policy** Temporal-Difference (TD) control algorithm that learns the action-value function $Q(s, a)$ directly from experience, without requiring a model of the environment. (1 Mark)

**Update Rule:** (3 Marks)

$$Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \right]$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = learning rate (step size), $0 < \alpha \leq 1$.
- $\gamma$ = discount factor.
- $r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ = **TD target**.
- $\delta_t = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)$ = **TD error**.

**Why off-policy?** The "max" operator in the update uses the greedy action of the *next* state, regardless of the action actually taken by the current (behaviour) policy.

**Convergence Conditions (Watkins & Dayan, 1992):** (3 Marks)
Q-Learning is guaranteed to converge to the optimal $Q^*(s, a)$ provided:
1. All state-action pairs $(s, a)$ are visited **infinitely often** in the limit.
2. The learning rate $\alpha$ satisfies the **Robbins-Monro conditions**: $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2 < \infty$.
3. Rewards are bounded: $\vert r \vert \leq R_{\max} < \infty$.
4. The state and action spaces are finite and discrete.

Under these conditions, $Q(s, a) \to Q^*(s, a)$ with probability 1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - In **Part A**, do NOT write the Bellman Equation as a standalone answer unless specifically asked. Examiners deduct marks for missing the **derivation context**.
> - In **Part B**, when defining $V_\pi(s)$, you MUST include both the action $\pi(a \mid s)$ and transition $p(s', r \mid s, a)$ terms in the Bellman Equation. Omitting $p(s', r \mid s, a)$ and writing only the reward part will cost you 2–3 marks.
> - For Q-Learning questions, explicitly mention **"off-policy"** and **"model-free"** — examiners award 1 mark for these qualifiers.
> - In numerical problems, do NOT confuse $V_\pi$ (state-value) with $Q_\pi$ (action-value). This is the **#1 silent mark-killer** in KTU RL papers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Agent-Environment Loop:** State → Action → Reward → Next State.
- **Markov Property:** Future depends only on present state, not history.
- **MDP Tuple:** $\langle S, A, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ — full formal description of an RL problem.
- **Return:** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ with $\gamma \in [0, 1)$.
- **State-Value Function:** $V_\pi(s) = E_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$.
- **Action-Value Function:** $Q_\pi(s, a) = E_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$.
- **Bellman Equation:** Recursive decomposition; fundamental tool.
- **Q-Learning:** Off-policy, model-free, uses $\max$ in TD target.
- **SARSA:** On-policy variant of Q-Learning (uses actual next action, not max).
- **$\epsilon$-greedy:** Simplest exploration strategy; common in KTU numericals.
- **Discount Factor Intuition:** $\gamma \to 0$ = myopic agent; $\gamma \to 1$ = far-sighted agent.
- **Reward Hypothesis:** All goals can be encoded as maximization of cumulative scalar reward.
- **Types of RL:** Model-based vs Model-free; Value-based vs Policy-based vs Actor-Critic.
- **Convergence:** Q-Learning converges to $Q^*$ under Robbins-Monro conditions and infinite visits.
- **Common Algorithms to Memorize:** Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN, REINFORCE, Dyna-Q, PPO.
- **Engineering Applications:** Robotics, game AI (AlphaGo), autonomous driving, HVAC, finance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Reinforcement Learning questions in KTU typically carry 7–14 marks and almost always test (i) the agent-environment loop diagram, (ii) Bellman Equation derivation, and (iii) the Exploration-Exploitation dilemma. Master these three — you cover approximately 80% of the recurring question patterns.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
