topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supervised Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Supervised Learning

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Supervised Learning** is a paradigm of Machine Learning in which an algorithm learns a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from a labeled training dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where each input $x^{(i)} \in \mathcal{X}$ (feature space) is paired with a corresponding ground-truth output $y^{(i)} \in \mathcal{Y}$ (label space). The objective is to learn a hypothesis $h_{\theta}(x)$ parameterized by $\theta$ that minimizes a predefined loss function $\mathcal{L}(h_{\theta}(x), y)$ over the training data, such that the learned model **generalizes** well to unseen instances drawn from the same underlying data distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The two defining pillars of Supervised Learning are (1) the availability of *labeled training data* and (2) the existence of a *measurable loss function* to optimize.

### 1.1.1 Taxonomy of Supervised Learning Problems

| Problem Type | Target Variable $y$ | Output Space | Example Tasks |
|--------------|---------------------|--------------|---------------|
| **Binary Classification** | Discrete (2 classes) | $y \in \{0, 1\}$ | Spam detection, disease diagnosis |
| **Multiclass Classification** | Discrete ($K > 2$ classes) | $y \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ | Digit recognition, image classification |
| **Regression** | Continuous real-valued | $y \in \mathbb{R}$ | House price prediction, temperature forecasting |
| **Multilabel Classification** | Multiple binary labels | $y \in \{0,1\}^{K}$ | Movie genre tagging, medical imaging |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Student–Teacher" Paradigm

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a student preparing for an examination. The teacher provides the student with thousands of solved problems — each problem (input) comes with the correct answer (label). By studying these solved examples, the student learns a *general rule* (a function) that allows them to solve **new, unseen** problems accurately. The student never memorizes the answers — they extract the underlying pattern. This is exactly what a Supervised Learning algorithm does.

The mapping to formal ML terminology:

- **Student** $\rightarrow$ the **model** (e.g., neural network, decision tree)
- **Teacher's solved problems** $\rightarrow$ the **labeled training dataset**
- **Exam (unseen questions)** $\rightarrow$ the **test set**
- **General rule learned** $\rightarrow$ the **hypothesis function** $h_{\theta}(x)$
- **Scoring well on the exam** $\rightarrow$ **generalization** (low test error)

## 1.3 Core Terminology — The Vocabulary of Supervised Learning

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| **Instance / Sample** | $x^{(i)}$ | A single data point, typically a feature vector |
| **Feature** | $x_j$ | The $j$-th measurable attribute of an instance |
| **Label / Target** | $y^{(i)}$ | The ground-truth output associated with $x^{(i)}$ |
| **Feature Space** | $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | The $d$-dimensional space where inputs reside |
| **Label Space** | $\mathcal{Y}$ | The space where targets reside (discrete or continuous) |
| **Hypothesis** | $h_{\theta}: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | The function learned by the model |
| **Loss Function** | $\mathcal{L}$ | Quantifies the discrepancy between prediction and truth |
| **Training Set** | $\mathcal{D}_{train}$ | Data used to fit the model parameters $\theta$ |
| **Validation Set** | $\mathcal{D}_{val}$ | Data used for hyperparameter tuning |
| **Test Set** | $\mathcal{D}_{test}$ | Held-out data for final unbiased evaluation |
| **Epoch** | — | One complete pass over the entire training set |
| **Batch Size** | $B$ | Number of samples processed before one weight update |
| **Learning Rate** | $\eta$ | Step size of the optimization algorithm |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Examiners often ask students to distinguish between **training error** $\mathcal{L}_{train}$ and **generalization error** $\mathcal{L}_{gen}$. Always remember: a good model has low training error **AND** low generalization error. A low training error with high test error signals **overfitting**.

## 1.4 The Supervised Learning Pipeline (End-to-End)

The complete workflow — from raw data to a deployable model — consists of **seven canonical stages**:

1. **Data Collection** — Gathering representative samples.
2. **Data Preprocessing** — Handling missing values, normalization, encoding.
3. **Feature Engineering / Selection** — Constructing informative features.
4. **Model Selection** — Choosing an appropriate algorithm (e.g., SVM, neural net).
5. **Training** — Optimizing parameters $\theta$ to minimize $\mathcal{L}$.
6. **Evaluation** — Measuring performance on validation/test sets.
7. **Hyperparameter Tuning & Deployment** — Refining and serving the model.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation — Learning as Optimization

The crux of supervised learning is to solve the following **empirical risk minimization (ERM)** problem:

$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\left(h_{\theta}\left(x^{(i)}\right), y^{(i)}\right)$$

The quantity being minimized is the **empirical risk** (average training loss). The true objective — the **expected risk** — is:

$$R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{P}_{data}} \left[ \mathcal{L}\left(h_{\theta}(x), y\right) \right]$$

Since the true distribution $\mathcal{P}_{data}$ is unknown, we approximate $R(\theta)$ using the training set, giving us the ERM objective.

## 2.2 Key Algorithmic Families in Supervised Learning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes the following families. Understand both the **intuition** and the **mathematics** of each.

### 2.2.1 Linear Regression
Models a linear relationship between inputs and a continuous target.

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^{T} x = \sum_{j=0}^{d} \theta_j x_j$$

where $x_0 = 1$ is the bias term.

### 2.2.2 Logistic Regression
Despite its name, this is a **classification** algorithm. It applies the **sigmoid (logistic) function** to a linear combination of features:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \sigma(\theta^{T} x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^{T} x}}$$

The decision boundary is defined by $\theta^{T} x = 0$ (i.e., $P(y=1 \mid x) = 0.5$).

### 2.2.3 Decision Trees
A non-parametric model that recursively partitions the feature space using axis-aligned splits, seeking to maximize **information gain** (or minimize Gini impurity / entropy).

### 2.2.4 Support Vector Machines (SVM)
Finds the **maximum-margin hyperplane** separating classes. The decision function is:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y^{(i)} K(x^{(i)}, x) + b\right)$$

where $K(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a kernel function and $\alpha_i$ are dual coefficients.

### 2.2.5 k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)
A **lazy learner** that classifies a query point based on the majority label of its $k$ nearest training samples (using Euclidean or other distance metrics).

### 2.2.6 Naive Bayes
A probabilistic classifier based on **Bayes' theorem** with a strong (naive) independence assumption among features:

$$P(y \mid x) \propto P(y) \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y)$$

### 2.2.7 Ensemble Methods
Combines multiple weak learners to produce a strong learner. Key variants:
- **Bagging** (e.g., Random Forest) — reduces variance
- **Boosting** (e.g., AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost) — reduces bias

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery Rule:** Memorize the formulas below. They appear in **80%+** of KTU Module 2 numerical questions.

### 2.3.1 Regression Loss Functions

| Loss Function | Formula | Use Case |
|---------------|---------|----------|
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$ | Standard regression, penalizes large errors quadratically |
| **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)} \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same units as target $y$ |
| **R-squared (Coefficient of Determination)** | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2}{\sum (y^{(i)} - \bar{y})^2}$ | Proportion of variance explained |

### 2.3.2 Classification Loss Functions

| Loss Function | Formula | Use Case |
|---------------|---------|----------|
| **Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)** | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$ | Binary classification with sigmoid output |
| **Categorical Cross-Entropy** | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}_k^{(i)})$ | Multiclass with softmax output |
| **Hinge Loss (SVM)** | $\mathcal{L} = \max(0, 1 - y^{(i)} \cdot \hat{y}^{(i)})$ | Maximum-margin classification |
| **0-1 Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{1}[\hat{y}^{(i)} \neq y^{(i)}]$ | Theoretical, non-differentiable |

### 2.3.3 Classification Evaluation Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Fraction of correct predictions |
| **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of predicted positives, how many are correct? |
| **Recall (Sensitivity / TPR)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of actual positives, how many were caught? |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True negative rate |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $1 - \text{Specificity}$ |

where **TP** = True Positive, **TN** = True Negative, **FP** = False Positive, **FN** = False Negative.

### 2.3.4 Optimization — Gradient Descent Update Rule

The **batch gradient descent** update rule is:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_j}$$

For MSE loss in linear regression, this becomes:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \eta \cdot \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

### 2.3.5 The Normal Equation (Closed-Form Solution for Linear Regression)

$$\theta^{*} = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y$$

This gives the **global optimum** in one shot — no iterative optimization needed. However, it has **$O(d^3)$** computational cost due to the matrix inverse, making it impractical for high dimensions.

### 2.3.6 Sigmoid Function and Its Derivative

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

$$\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z) \cdot (1 - \sigma(z))$$

### 2.3.7 Softmax Function (Multiclass Generalization)

$$\text{softmax}(z_k) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

### 2.3.8 Gini Impurity (Decision Trees)

$$Gini = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k^2$$

### 2.3.9 Entropy (Decision Trees / Information Theory)

$$H(S) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log_2(p_k)$$

### 2.3.10 Bayes' Theorem (Foundation of Naive Bayes)

$$P(y \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid y) \cdot P(y)}{P(x)}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Application Context:** Supervised learning underpins virtually every modern AI product.

- **Healthcare:** Cancer detection from histopathology images (CNN-based classification), drug response prediction (regression).
- **Finance:** Credit scoring (logistic regression), stock price forecasting (LSTM/regression), fraud detection (anomaly-aware classifiers).
- **Autonomous Systems:** Pedestrian detection, lane departure warning (SVM / deep nets).
- **NLP:** Sentiment analysis (Naive Bayes / transformers), spam filtering.
- **Manufacturing:** Predictive maintenance (regression on sensor time-series).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Form Solution (Normal Equation) for Linear Regression

**Problem Setup:** Given a training set with $N$ instances, each having $d$ features, we want to find $\theta$ that minimizes the MSE cost function.

**Step 1:** Define the design matrix $X$ (an $N \times (d+1)$ matrix with a column of ones for the bias) and the target vector $y$ (an $N \times 1$ vector):

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1^{(1)} & x_2^{(1)} & \cdots & x_d^{(1)} \\ 1 & x_1^{(2)} & x_2^{(2)} & \cdots & x_d^{(2)} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_1^{(N)} & x_2^{(N)} & \cdots & x_d^{(N)} \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} y^{(1)} \\ y^{(2)} \\ \vdots \\ y^{(N)} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Write the cost function $J(\theta)$ in matrix form. The vector of predictions is $X\theta$, so the residual vector is $X\theta - y$:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} (X\theta - y)^{T} (X\theta - y)$$

**Step 3:** Expand the quadratic form. Let $e = X\theta - y$:

$$\begin{aligned} J(\theta) &= \frac{1}{2N} (X\theta - y)^{T} (X\theta - y) \\ &= \frac{1}{2N} \left[ \theta^{T} X^{T} X \theta - \theta^{T} X^{T} y - y^{T} X \theta + y^{T} y \right] \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Note that $\theta^{T} X^{T} y$ is a scalar, and a scalar equals its transpose: $\theta^{T} X^{T} y = y^{T} X \theta$. So:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \left[ \theta^{T} X^{T} X \theta - 2 \theta^{T} X^{T} y + y^{T} y \right]$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate $J(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$ and set to zero (using standard matrix calculus rules: $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} (\theta^{T} A \theta) = 2A\theta$ for symmetric $A$, and $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} (\theta^{T} b) = b$):

$$\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \left[ 2 X^{T} X \theta - 2 X^{T} y \right] = 0$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the gradient:

$$\frac{1}{N} \left[ X^{T} X \theta - X^{T} y \right] = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad X^{T} X \theta = X^{T} y$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $\theta$ by left-multiplying both sides by $(X^{T} X)^{-1}$ (assuming $X^{T} X$ is invertible / non-singular):

$$\boxed{\theta^{*} = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The term $(X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T}$ is called the **Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse** of $X$, often written $X^{+}$. When $X^{T} X$ is singular (e.g., due to multicollinearity), we use **ridge regression** by adding a regularizer: $\theta^{*} = (X^{T} X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^{T} y$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gradient Descent Update Rule for Linear Regression (MSE Loss)

**Step 1:** Hypothesis: $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^{T} x$.

**Step 2:** Cost function: $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$.

**Step 3:** Compute the partial derivative of $J(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta_j$. Apply the chain rule:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} &= \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2 \\ &= \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2 \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) \\ &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the gradient descent update rule $\theta_j := \theta_j - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$:

$$\boxed{\theta_j := \theta_j - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Trick Question:** Examiners may ask: "Why do we use the factor $\frac{1}{2}$ in the cost function?" The answer: the $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels the 2 from the derivative of the squared term, yielding cleaner gradient expressions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Logistic Regression Cost Function (Cross-Entropy Loss)

**Step 1:** The model outputs a probability: $h_{\theta}(x) = \hat{y} = P(y = 1 \mid x; \theta) = \sigma(\theta^{T} x)$.

**Step 2:** For a single training example, we want a cost function that heavily penalizes confident wrong predictions. Define the cost for one example:

$$\text{Cost}(h_{\theta}(x), y) = \begin{cases} -\log(\hat{y}) & \text{if } y = 1 \\ -\log(1 - \hat{y}) & \text{if } y = 0 \end{cases}$$

This can be compactly written as:

$$\text{Cost}(h_{\theta}(x), y) = -\left[ y \log(\hat{y}) + (1 - y) \log(1 - \hat{y}) \right]$$

**Step 3:** Verify behavior:
- If $y = 1$ and $\hat{y} \to 0$ (confident wrong): cost $\to +\infty$ (severe penalty). ✓
- If $y = 1$ and $\hat{y} \to 1$ (correct): cost $\to 0$. ✓
- If $y = 0$ and $\hat{y} \to 1$ (confident wrong): cost $\to +\infty$. ✓

**Step 4:** Average over all $N$ training examples to get the full cost function:

$$\boxed{J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the gradient. Let $a = \sigma(z)$ where $z = \theta^{T} x$. The derivative of the sigmoid is $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$. After applying chain rule:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)} \end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Beautiful Symmetry:** Notice that the gradient of logistic regression has the **same elegant form** as that of linear regression — the prediction error $(\hat{y} - y)$ multiplied by the feature $x_j$. This is one of the most satisfying results in classical ML.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for Logistic Regression

**Step 1:** Assume training examples are i.i.d. The likelihood of the data under the model is:

$$L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}; \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} (\hat{y}^{(i)})^{y^{(i)}} (1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})^{1 - y^{(i)}}$$

**Step 2:** Take the log-likelihood (log is monotonic, so maximizing log-likelihood = maximizing likelihood):

$$\ell(\theta) = \log L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Step 3:** Maximizing $\ell(\theta)$ is equivalent to minimizing $-\ell(\theta)$:

$$\min_{\theta} \; -\ell(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$$

This is exactly $N \cdot J(\theta)$ from Section 3.3. Hence the cross-entropy loss is the **negative log-likelihood** — a direct consequence of MLE under the Bernoulli assumption.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Supervised Learning Algorithms

| Algorithm | Type | Parametric? | Key Strength | Key Weakness | Loss Function |
|-----------|------|-------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| Linear Regression | Regression | Yes | Simple, interpretable | Limited to linear relationships | MSE |
| Logistic Regression | Classification | Yes | Probabilistic, fast | Linear decision boundary | Cross-Entropy |
| Decision Tree | Both | No | Handles non-linearity, interpretable | Prone to overfitting | Gini / Entropy |
| Random Forest | Both | No (ensemble) | Robust, low variance | Less interpretable, slow | Voting / Averaging |
| SVM | Classification | Yes (with kernel) | Effective in high dimensions | Sensitive to kernel choice | Hinge Loss |
| k-NN | Both | No | No training phase | Slow at inference, curse of dimensionality | Distance-based |
| Naive Bayes | Classification | Yes | Fast, works with small data | Strong independence assumption | Negative log-likelihood |
| AdaBoost | Both | Ensemble | Reduces bias | Sensitive to noisy data | Exponential loss |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Supervised Learning Workflow (End-to-End Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Source] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Feature Engineering and Scaling]
    C --> D[Train-Validation-Test Split]
    D --> E[Model Training with Labels]
    E --> F[Loss Computation]
    F --> G[Gradient Based Optimization]
    G --> H{Convergence Check}
    H -->|Not Converged| E
    H -->|Converged| I[Hyperparameter Tuning on Validation Set]
    I --> J[Final Evaluation on Test Set]
    J --> K[Model Deployment]
    K --> L[Monitor and Retrain Periodically]
```

## 4.2 Classification vs. Regression — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Supervised Learning Problem] --> B{Is the Target Variable Continuous or Discrete?}
    B -->|Continuous Real Value| C[Regression Problem]
    B -->|Discrete Category| D[Classification Problem]
    C --> E[Use Linear Regression or Decision Tree Regressor]
    D --> F{Number of Classes?}
    F -->|2 Classes| G[Binary Classification: Logistic Regression or SVM]
    F -->|More than 2| H[Multiclass Classification: Softmax Regression or Random Forest]
    E --> I[Evaluate with MSE, MAE, R-squared]
    G --> J[Evaluate with Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1]
    H --> J
```

## 4.3 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Bias Underfitting] --> B[Sweet Spot Optimal Model]
    B --> C[High Variance Overfitting]
    A --> A1[Training Error High, Test Error High]
    B --> B1[Training Error Low, Test Error Low]
    C --> C1[Training Error Very Low, Test Error High]
    A1 --> D[Increase Model Complexity]
    D --> B
    C1 --> E[Reduce Model Complexity or Add Regularization]
    E --> B
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Supervised Learning System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A1[Feature Vector x]
    end
    subgraph MODEL[Model Block h_theta]
        B1[Linear Combination z = theta Transpose x]
        B2[Activation Function sigma or identity]
        B1 --> B2
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Prediction Block]
        C1[Predicted Label y_hat]
    end
    subgraph LOSS[Loss and Optimization Block]
        D1[Compute Loss L y_hat, y]
        D2[Compute Gradient of L w.r.t theta]
        D3[Update theta using Gradient Descent]
        D1 --> D2
        D2 --> D3
        D3 --> B1
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    C1 --> E1[Compare with Ground Truth y]
    E1 --> D1
```

## 4.5 Confusion Matrix Structure (Classification Evaluation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PRED[Predicted Class]
        A[Predicted Positive]
        B[Predicted Negative]
    end
    subgraph ACT[Actual Class]
        C[Actual Positive]
        D[Actual Negative]
    end
    C --> A
    C --> B
    D --> A
    D --> B
    A --> A1[True Positive TP if A matches C]
    A --> A2[False Positive FP if A matches D]
    B --> B1[False Negative FN if B matches C]
    B --> B2[True Negative TN if B matches D]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Supervised Learning. Differentiate between Classification and Regression with suitable examples. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Supervised Learning** is a machine learning paradigm where the algorithm learns a mapping from inputs to outputs using a labeled training dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$. The model learns by minimizing a loss function that measures the discrepancy between its predictions and the ground-truth labels, with the goal of generalizing to unseen data.

| Aspect | Classification | Regression |
|--------|----------------|------------|
| **Target type** | Discrete (categories) | Continuous (real-valued) |
| **Output** | Class label | Numerical value |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 | MSE, MAE, RMSE, R² |
| **Example** | Email spam detection (spam / not spam) | Predicting house price from area |
| **Example** | MNIST digit recognition (0–9) | Forecasting daily temperature |

### Question 2: What is Overfitting? Explain two techniques to prevent it. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Overfitting** occurs when a model learns the training data — including its noise and random fluctuations — too well, resulting in **low training error** but **high generalization error** (poor performance on unseen data). It typically happens when the model is excessively complex relative to the size of the training set.

**Two techniques to prevent overfitting:**

1. **Regularization (L1 / L2):** Add a penalty term to the cost function that discourages large parameter values. For L2 (Ridge):
$$J_{reg}(\theta) = J(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{d} \theta_j^2$$
where $\lambda$ is the regularization hyperparameter. This forces the model to prefer simpler hypotheses.

2. **Cross-Validation:** Use techniques like **k-fold cross-validation** to estimate generalization error reliably and select hyperparameters that perform well on unseen data, preventing the model from being tuned to a single train-test split.

*(Other acceptable techniques: early stopping, dropout for neural networks, reducing model complexity, data augmentation, increasing training data.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Linear & Logistic Regression `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the normal equation for linear regression. Show all steps clearly. Compare gradient descent with the normal equation approach.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Define the design matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (d+1)}$ with a bias column of ones, and the target vector $y \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1}$ as shown in Section 3.1.

**Step 2:** Write the cost function in matrix form:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} (X\theta - y)^{T} (X\theta - y)$$

**Step 3:** Expand and use the identity $\theta^{T} X^{T} y = y^{T} X \theta$:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \left[ \theta^{T} X^{T} X \theta - 2 \theta^{T} X^{T} y + y^{T} y \right]$$

**Step 4:** Take the gradient using $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}(\theta^{T} A \theta) = 2A\theta$ for symmetric $A$:

$$\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ X^{T} X \theta - X^{T} y \right]$$

**Step 5:** Set gradient to zero and solve:

$$X^{T} X \theta = X^{T} y \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \theta^{*} = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y$$

**Comparison Table (3 marks for the comparison):**

| Aspect | Normal Equation | Gradient Descent |
|--------|----------------|------------------|
| **Solution type** | Closed-form (analytical) | Iterative |
| **Computational cost** | $O(d^3)$ for matrix inverse | $O(N d)$ per iteration |
| **Choice of learning rate** | Not required | Requires tuning $\eta$ |
| **Scalability** | Poor for large $d$ | Scales well to large $d$ |
| **Convergence** | Exact in one step | Approximate, may need many iterations |
| **Feature scaling** | Not strictly needed | Highly recommended |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain Logistic Regression with the sigmoid function. Derive the cost function (cross-entropy) and its gradient.**

**Model Solution:**

**Sigmoid Function:**

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad \text{with derivative} \quad \sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$$

The sigmoid squashes any real-valued input $z = \theta^{T} x$ into the range $(0, 1)$, making it interpretable as a probability.

**Hypothesis:** $h_{\theta}(x) = \sigma(\theta^{T} x) = P(y=1 \mid x; \theta)$.

**Cost Function (Cross-Entropy) Derivation:** For a single training example, we need a function that heavily penalizes confident wrong predictions. The cost for one example is:

$$\text{Cost}(h_{\theta}(x), y) = -\left[ y \log(h_{\theta}(x)) + (1 - y) \log(1 - h_{\theta}(x)) \right]$$

Averaging over $N$ training examples:

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)})) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_{\theta}(x^{(i)})) \right]$$

**Gradient Derivation:** Differentiating $J(\theta)$ w.r.t. $\theta_j$ using the chain rule and the fact that $\frac{d}{dz} \log(\sigma(z)) = 1 - \sigma(z)$ and $\frac{d}{dz} \log(1 - \sigma(z)) = -\sigma(z)$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ -y^{(i)} \frac{\sigma'(z^{(i)})}{\sigma(z^{(i)})} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \frac{\sigma'(z^{(i)})}{1 - \sigma(z^{(i)})} \right] x_j^{(i)} \\ &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ -y^{(i)}(1 - \sigma(z^{(i)})) + (1 - y^{(i)})\sigma(z^{(i)}) \right] x_j^{(i)} \\ &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \sigma(z^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right] x_j^{(i)} \\ &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)} \end{aligned}$$

**Update rule:** $\theta_j := \theta_j - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Decision Trees, SVM & Evaluation `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working of the k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) algorithm. Discuss the effect of choosing a small versus a large value of $k$. How is the optimal $k$ selected?**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of k-NN:**

k-NN is a **non-parametric, instance-based (lazy) learning** algorithm. For a query point $x_q$:

1. Compute the distance from $x_q$ to every training point $x^{(i)}$ using a metric, typically **Euclidean distance**:
$$d(x_q, x^{(i)}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_{q,j} - x_j^{(i)})^2}$$

2. Identify the **$k$ training points** with the smallest distances to $x_q$ (the "neighbors").

3. For **classification**: assign the **majority class label** among the $k$ neighbors (majority voting). For **regression**: output the **average** of the $k$ neighbors' target values.

**Effect of $k$:**

| Value of $k$ | Behavior | Effect |
|--------------|----------|--------|
| **$k = 1$** | Decision boundary is highly jagged, follows training data closely | High variance, prone to overfitting, sensitive to noise |
| **Small $k$ (e.g., 3–5)** | Smooth boundary, captures local patterns | Reasonable balance, but still somewhat noisy |
| **Large $k$ (e.g., $N$)** | Prediction becomes constant (majority class) | High bias, underfitting |
| **Optimal $k$** | Minimizes test error via cross-validation | Best generalization |

**Selecting the optimal $k$:** Use **k-fold cross-validation**. For each candidate $k$ in a range (e.g., $k = 1, 3, 5, \dots, 21$), compute the average validation error across all $f$ folds. Choose the $k$ that **minimizes the cross-validation error**.

**Curse of Dimensionality:** k-NN performs poorly in high-dimensional spaces because distances become less meaningful as dimensionality increases.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the confusion matrix for a binary classification problem. Define Precision, Recall, and F1-score. A classifier produces the following predictions on 1000 samples: TP=80, FP=20, FN=40, TN=860. Compute all relevant metrics.**

**Model Solution:**

**Confusion Matrix Structure:**

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP = 80 | FN = 40 |
| **Actual Negative** | FP = 20 | TN = 860 |

**Definitions:**

- **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ — Of all samples predicted as positive, what fraction is truly positive?
- **Recall (Sensitivity / TPR)** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — Of all actual positives, what fraction did we catch?
- **F1-Score** = $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ — Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- **Accuracy** = $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$
- **Specificity (TNR)** = $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$

**Numerical Computation:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{80 + 860}{1000} = \frac{940}{1000} = 0.94 \text{ (94\%)}$$

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80 \text{ (80\%)}$$

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{80}{80 + 40} = \frac{80}{120} \approx 0.6667 \text{ (66.67\%)}$$

$$\text{F1-Score} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.80 \times 0.6667}{0.80 + 0.6667} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5333}{1.4667} \approx 0.7273 \text{ (72.73\%)}$$

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{860}{860 + 20} = \frac{860}{880} \approx 0.9773 \text{ (97.73\%)}$$

$$\text{False Positive Rate} = 1 - 0.9773 = 0.0227 \text{ (2.27\%)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do NOT use Accuracy alone** for imbalanced datasets. In the above example, a naive "always predict negative" classifier would achieve 90% accuracy (900/1000) but zero recall — clearly useless.
> - **F1-Score is the harmonic mean, NOT the arithmetic mean.** A common mistake is to write $\frac{P+R}{2}$. The harmonic mean penalizes extreme imbalances: if Precision = 1 and Recall = 0, F1 = 0, not 0.5.
> - **Always show substitution steps** in your computation — partial marks are awarded for substituting correct values into the correct formulas.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Memorize These:**

- ✅ **Supervised Learning** requires **labeled data** $(x, y)$ and a **loss function** to optimize.
- ✅ The two grand divisions are **Classification** (discrete $y$) and **Regression** (continuous $y$).
- ✅ **Linear Regression** minimizes **MSE**; its closed-form solution is the **Normal Equation** $\theta^{*} = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y$.
- ✅ **Logistic Regression** uses the **sigmoid** to output probabilities in $(0,1)$ and is optimized using **cross-entropy loss**.
- ✅ The **gradient of MSE** (linear regression) and **cross-entropy** (logistic regression) share the elegant form $\frac{1}{N} \sum (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent variants**: Batch (uses all $N$ samples), Stochastic (1 sample), Mini-batch (size $B$).
- ✅ **Overfitting** = low training error + high test error. **Underfitting** = high training error + high test error.
- ✅ **Regularization** (L1/L2) adds a penalty $\lambda \sum \theta_j^2$ to the cost to combat overfitting.
- ✅ **Confusion Matrix** components: TP, TN, FP, FN. From these we compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, Specificity.
- ✅ **Precision** answers: "Of predicted positives, how many are right?" **Recall** answers: "Of actual positives, how many did we catch?"
- ✅ **F1-Score** is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall: $F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R}$.
- ✅ **Bias-Variance Tradeoff**: Simple models have high bias (underfit); complex models have high variance (overfit). The optimal model balances both.
- ✅ **Sigmoid** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, range $(0,1)$, derivative $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$.
- ✅ **Softmax** generalizes sigmoid to $K$ classes: $\text{softmax}(z_k) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$.
- ✅ **Naive Bayes** assumes conditional feature independence and applies **Bayes' theorem**.
- ✅ **k-NN** is a **lazy learner** — no training phase; all work happens at inference. Suffers from the **curse of dimensionality**.
- ✅ **Decision Trees** split on features to maximize **information gain** (or minimize **Gini impurity** / **entropy**).
- ✅ **SVMs** find the **maximum-margin hyperplane**; the kernel trick enables non-linear boundaries.
- ✅ **Ensemble methods** (Bagging / Boosting) combine weak learners to reduce **variance** / **bias** respectively.
- ✅ The **Universal ML workflow**: Data → Preprocess → Split → Train → Validate → Test → Deploy → Monitor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Parameter Estimation - Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Maximum A Posteriori Estimation (MAP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basics of Parameter Estimation - Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Maximum A Posteriori Estimation (MAP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Parameter Estimation?

In Machine Learning and Statistics, **Parameter Estimation** is the formal process of inferring the unknown parameters of a probability distribution (or model) from observed data. Once the underlying distribution is assumed (e.g., Gaussian, Bernoulli, Poisson), the parameters such as mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$, or probability $p$ must be estimated to make the model fit the data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Parameter estimation is the procedure of determining the values of the parameters of a statistical model based on observed data. The two principal frequentist–Bayesian paradigms are **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** and **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Estimation**.

## 1.2 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

**MLE** is a *frequentist* approach that finds the parameter values which **maximize the likelihood function** — i.e., the probability of observing the given data, assuming a specific parameter value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ drawn i.i.d. from a distribution $P(x \vert \theta)$, the MLE estimate is
> $$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \; P(\mathcal{D} \vert \theta)$$

### Intuitive Analogy: The Biased Coin Problem
Imagine you are handed a coin and asked: *Is this coin fair?* You flip it **10 times** and observe **8 heads**. Now ask:
- *Which value of the bias $p$ (probability of heads) makes the observed data most probable?*

If $p = 0.8$, the chance of getting 8 heads in 10 tosses is highest. So intuitively, $\hat{p} = 0.8$ is the MLE.

In simple words: **MLE asks — "Given the data, what parameter makes this data most likely?"**

## 1.3 Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Estimation

**MAP** is a *Bayesian* approach that incorporates **prior beliefs** about the parameter before observing data, then updates these beliefs using evidence from the data via **Bayes' Theorem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** The MAP estimate is the mode of the posterior distribution:
> $$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \; P(\theta \vert \mathcal{D}) = \arg\max_{\theta} \; P(\mathcal{D} \vert \theta) \, P(\theta)$$

### Intuitive Analogy: The Weather Foreaster
A weather forecaster in Kerala knows that *historically, the chance of rain in July is 80%*. Today, the satellite data strongly suggests heavy clouds. A purely frequentist (MLE) forecaster would output only what the data says. A Bayesian (MAP) forecaster would *combine* the prior belief (80%) with the new sensor evidence to make a balanced prediction.

In simple words: **MAP asks — "Given the data AND my prior belief, what is the most plausible parameter value?"**

## 1.4 Why Are These Important in AI/ML?

- **Model fitting** (regression, classification) hinges on estimating weights $\theta$.
- **Neural networks** use MLE-derived loss functions (cross-entropy, MSE).
- **Bayesian deep learning** uses MAP to inject prior regularization (L2 regularization = Gaussian prior!).
- **Generative models** (GANs, VAEs) rely heavily on parameter estimation theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction at a Glance:**
> - MLE uses only data: $P(\mathcal{D} \vert \theta)$
> - MAP uses data + prior: $P(\mathcal{D} \vert \theta) \cdot P(\theta)$
> - When the prior is **uniform**, MAP reduces to MLE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Frequentist vs Bayesian Paradigm

| Aspect | MLE (Frequentist) | MAP (Bayesian) |
|---|---|---|
| Treats $\theta$ as | Fixed unknown constant | Random variable with prior |
| Objective | Maximize likelihood $P(\mathcal{D} \vert \theta)$ | Maximize posterior $P(\theta \vert \mathcal{D})$ |
| Uses prior? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (via $P(\theta)$ ) |
| Output | Single point estimate $\hat{\theta}$ | Single point estimate (mode of posterior) |
| Overfitting | Prone when data is scarce | Controlled by prior (regularization) |
| Computes $P(\theta \vert \mathcal{D})$? | No | Yes (proportional to) |

## 2.2 Foundational Mathematical Setup

Let $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ be i.i.d. samples from $P(x \vert \theta)$.

**Step 1 — Likelihood Function:**
$$L(\theta) = P(\mathcal{D} \vert \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert \theta)$$

**Step 2 — Log-Likelihood (for numerical stability):**
$$\ell(\theta) = \log L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \vert \theta)$$

**Step 3 — MLE Optimization:**
$$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \ell(\theta)$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent condition (set derivative to zero):**
$$\frac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Bayes' Theorem (foundation of MAP):**
$$P(\theta \vert \mathcal{D}) = \frac{P(\mathcal{D} \vert \theta) \, P(\theta)}{P(\mathcal{D})}$$

**Step 6 — MAP Optimization (dropping evidence $P(\mathcal{D})$ since it is independent of $\theta$):**
$$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \left[ \log P(\mathcal{D} \vert \theta) + \log P(\theta) \right]$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert \theta)$ | Likelihood function | MLE |
| 2 | $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \vert \theta)$ | Log-likelihood | MLE |
| 3 | $\frac{d\ell}{d\theta} = 0$ | Optimality condition | MLE / MAP |
| 4 | $P(\theta \vert \mathcal{D}) \propto P(\mathcal{D} \vert \theta) \, P(\theta)$ | Posterior proportionality | MAP |
| 5 | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta}\left[\ell(\theta) + \log P(\theta)\right]$ | MAP objective | MAP |
| 6 | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}}^{\text{Bernoulli}} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | MLE for Bernoulli | Coin flips |
| 7 | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}}^{\text{Bernoulli+Beta}} = \frac{\alpha - 1 + \sum x_i}{\alpha + \beta - 2 + n}$ | MAP with Beta$(\alpha,\beta)$ prior | Bayesian coins |
| 8 | $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ | MLE mean (Gaussian) | Mean estimation |
| 9 | $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \hat{\mu})^2$ | MLE variance (Gaussian) | Variance estimation |
| 10 | $\log P(\theta) = -\frac{(\theta-\mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} + C$ | Gaussian prior log | L2 regularization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** L2 regularization in deep learning (ridge regression, weight decay) is *mathematically equivalent* to MAP estimation with a zero-mean Gaussian prior on the weights. This is a favorite KTU interview question!

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Estimating disease prevalence from clinical trials (MAP with epidemiological priors).
- **Speech Recognition:** Hidden Markov Model (HMM) parameter estimation via Baum–Welch (an EM algorithm based on MLE).
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Bayesian sensor fusion (Kalman Filter = recursive MAP/ML estimate).
- **Spam Filtering:** Naive Bayes classifiers use MAP for class probability estimation.
- **A/B Testing:** Conversion rate estimation in e-commerce platforms (Beta–Bernoulli MAP model).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: MLE for Bernoulli Distribution

**Problem Setup:** A coin has unknown bias $p$ (probability of heads). We observe $n$ independent flips with $k$ heads and $(n-k)$ tails. Find the MLE of $p$.

**Step 1 — Define the likelihood.**
For a single Bernoulli trial, $P(x_i \vert p) = p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i}$. For $n$ i.i.d. trials:
$$L(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i} = p^{\sum x_i}(1-p)^{n - \sum x_i}$$

**Step 2 — Take the logarithm to convert product to sum.**
$$\ell(p) = \log L(p) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right) \log p + \left(n - \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right) \log(1-p)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $p$ and set to zero.**
$$\frac{d\ell}{dp} = \frac{\sum x_i}{p} - \frac{n - \sum x_i}{1 - p} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically.**
$$\frac{\sum x_i}{p} = \frac{n - \sum x_i}{1-p}$$

$$\left(\sum x_i\right)(1-p) = (n - \sum x_i)\,p$$

$$\sum x_i - p \sum x_i = np - p \sum x_i$$

$$\sum x_i = np$$

**Step 5 — Final MLE solution.**
$$\boxed{\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n} = \frac{k}{n}}$$

**Step 6 — Verify second-order condition (maximum).**
$$\frac{d^2 \ell}{dp^2} = -\frac{\sum x_i}{p^2} - \frac{n - \sum x_i}{(1-p)^2} < 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (concave, confirmed maximum)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example:** Out of 10 coin flips, we get 7 heads. Then $\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}} = 7/10 = 0.7$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: MAP for Bernoulli with Beta Prior

**Problem Setup:** Same coin problem, but we now assume a **prior belief** that $p \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ with PDF:
$$P(p) = \frac{p^{\alpha-1}(1-p)^{\beta-1}}{B(\alpha, \beta)}$$

**Step 1 — Write the posterior (using Bayes' theorem).**
$$P(p \vert \mathcal{D}) = \frac{P(\mathcal{D} \vert p) \, P(p)}{P(\mathcal{D})}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute likelihood and prior.**
$$P(p \vert \mathcal{D}) \propto \left[ p^k (1-p)^{n-k} \right] \cdot \left[ p^{\alpha-1}(1-p)^{\beta-1} \right]$$

$$P(p \vert \mathcal{D}) \propto p^{k + \alpha - 1}(1-p)^{n - k + \beta - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Take log of the posterior.**
$$\log P(p \vert \mathcal{D}) = (k + \alpha - 1)\log p + (n - k + \beta - 1)\log(1-p) + C$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate and set to zero.**
$$\frac{d}{dp}\log P(p \vert \mathcal{D}) = \frac{k + \alpha - 1}{p} - \frac{n - k + \beta - 1}{1 - p} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $p$.**
$$(k + \alpha - 1)(1-p) = (n - k + \beta - 1)p$$

$$k + \alpha - 1 = p\,(n + \alpha + \beta - 2)$$

**Step 6 — Final MAP solution.**
$$\boxed{\hat{p}_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{k + \alpha - 1}{n + \alpha + \beta - 2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Observation:** When $\alpha = \beta = 1$ (uniform prior), MAP gives $\frac{k + 0}{n + 0} = \frac{k}{n}$, which equals MLE. **This proves MAP generalizes MLE with a uniform prior.**

**Numerical Example:** Suppose $n=10$, $k=7$, prior $\text{Beta}(2, 2)$:
$$\hat{p}_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{7 + 2 - 1}{10 + 2 + 2 - 2} = \frac{8}{12} = 0.667$$

Compared to MLE $= 0.7$, the prior *pulled* the estimate slightly toward 0.5 (the prior mean).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: MLE for Gaussian Mean $\mu$ (Variance Known)

**Step 1 — Likelihood for Gaussian $x_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.**
$$L(\mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Log-likelihood.**
$$\ell(\mu) = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $\mu$.**
$$\frac{d\ell}{d\mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve.**
$$\sum x_i = n\mu \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = \bar{x}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: MLE for Gaussian Variance $\sigma^2$ (Mean Known)

**Step 1 — Log-likelihood (with mean $\mu$ known).**
$$\ell(\sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\sigma^2$.**
$$\frac{d\ell}{d\sigma^2} = -\frac{n}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve.**
$$\frac{n}{2\sigma^2} = \frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2}\sum (x_i - \mu)^2$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The MLE variance is *biased*; the unbiased estimator uses $n-1$ in the denominator. KTU frequently tests this distinction.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | MLE | MAP |
|---|---|---|
| Objective function | $\ell(\theta)$ | $\ell(\theta) + \log P(\theta)$ |
| Sensitive to prior? | No | Yes |
| Asymptotic behavior | Converges to true $\theta$ | Converges to true $\theta$ (if prior proper) |
| Regularization effect | None inherent | Equivalent to adding regularizer |
| Requires prior specification? | No | Yes |
| Computational ease | Simpler (no prior tuning) | Slightly more complex |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: MLE vs MAP Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Observed Data D"] --> B{"Prior Belief P theta available?"}
    B -- No --> C["Use MLE"]
    B -- Yes --> D["Use MAP"]
    C --> E["Form Likelihood L theta = Product P xi given theta"]
    D --> F["Form Posterior P theta given D proportional to L theta times P theta"]
    E --> G["Take Log Likelihood ell theta"]
    F --> H["Take Log Posterior = ell theta + log P theta"]
    G --> I["Differentiate and Set to Zero"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Solve for theta hat"]
    J --> K["Output Point Estimate theta hat"]
    K --> L["Use Estimate for Prediction or Inference"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Architecture of Bayesian Parameter Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PriorKnowledge["Prior Knowledge Module"]
        PR["Prior Distribution P theta"]
    end
    subgraph DataLayer["Observed Data Layer"]
        DS["Dataset D = x1 to xn"]
    end
    subgraph InferenceEngine["Bayesian Inference Engine"]
        LK["Likelihood P D given theta"]
        BTH["Bayes Theorem: Posterior proportional to Likelihood times Prior"]
    end
    subgraph Estimator["MAP Estimator"]
        OP["argmax over theta of Posterior"]
        OUT["Output: theta hat MAP"]
    end
    PR --> BTH
    DS --> LK
    LK --> BTH
    BTH --> OP
    OP --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map: Relationship Between MLE, MAP, and Bayesian Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PEB["Parameter Estimation"] --> FRQ["Frequentist Approach"]
    PEB --> BAY["Bayesian Approach"]
    FRQ --> MLE["Maximum Likelihood Estimation MLE"]
    BAY --> MAPE["Maximum A Posteriori MAP"]
    BAY --> FULL["Full Bayesian Posterior Inference"]
    MLE -. "Uniform prior reduces MAP to MLE" .-> MAPE
    MAPE -. "Take full distribution not just mode" .-> FULL
    MLE --> APP1["Logistic Regression Weights"]
    MLE --> APP2["Linear Regression Coefficients"]
    MAPE --> APP3["Ridge Regression L2 regularized"]
    MAPE --> APP4["Naive Bayes Classifier"]
    FULL --> APP5["Bayesian Neural Networks"]
    FULL --> APP6["Gaussian Processes"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: MLE Optimization Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Researcher
    participant D as Data Source
    participant M as Model
    participant O as Optimizer
    U->>D: Collect n i.i.d. samples
    D-->>M: Provide dataset D
    M->>M: Assume distribution family P x given theta
    M->>O: Build log likelihood ell theta
    O->>O: Compute gradient d ell by d theta
    O->>O: Set gradient equal to zero
    O->>O: Solve equation for theta hat
    O-->>U: Return theta hat MLE
    U->>M: Plug theta hat into model for predictions
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | MLE | MAP |
|---|---|---|
| Philosophy | Frequentist | Bayesian |
| Objective | Maximize $P(\mathcal{D} \vert \theta)$ | Maximize $P(\theta \vert \mathcal{D}) \propto P(\mathcal{D} \vert \theta) P(\theta)$ |
| Prior required? | No | Yes, $P(\theta)$ |
| Output | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}}$ | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}}$ (mode of posterior) |
| Special case | MAP with uniform prior reduces to MLE | — |
| Overfitting | High with small data | Controlled via prior |

*(1 mark each for objective, prior usage, and special relationship)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Bayes' Theorem and explain each term with a parameter estimation context.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$P(\theta \vert \mathcal{D}) = \frac{P(\mathcal{D} \vert \theta) \, P(\theta)}{P(\mathcal{D})}$$

- $P(\theta \vert \mathcal{D})$ — **Posterior** : updated belief about parameter $\theta$ after seeing data.
- $P(\mathcal{D} \vert \theta)$ — **Likelihood** : probability of data given parameter $\theta$.
- $P(\theta)$ — **Prior** : initial belief about $\theta$ before data.
- $P(\mathcal{D})$ — **Evidence / Marginal likelihood** : normalizing constant (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the Maximum Likelihood Estimate of the parameter $p$ of a Bernoulli distribution given $n$ independent observations. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same Bernoulli setup, derive the MAP estimate assuming the prior $p \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$. Show that MAP reduces to MLE when $\alpha = \beta = 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — MLE Derivation for Bernoulli (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Likelihood function for $n$ i.i.d. Bernoulli trials with $k$ successes:
$$L(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i} = p^k(1-p)^{n-k}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Log-likelihood:
$$\ell(p) = k \log p + (n-k)\log(1-p)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** First derivative:
$$\frac{d\ell}{dp} = \frac{k}{p} - \frac{n-k}{1-p}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Set to zero and solve:
$$\frac{k}{p} = \frac{n-k}{1-p} \;\Rightarrow\; k(1-p) = (n-k)p \;\Rightarrow\; k = np$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** MLE solution:
$$\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{k}{n}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Second derivative test:
$$\frac{d^2 \ell}{dp^2} = -\frac{k}{p^2} - \frac{n-k}{(1-p)^2} < 0 \;\; \checkmark \text{ maximum confirmed}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Worked example: For $n=20$, $k=12$, $\hat{p} = 0.6$.

---

**Solution (b) — MAP with Beta Prior (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Prior $P(p) \propto p^{\alpha-1}(1-p)^{\beta-1}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Posterior by Bayes' theorem:
$$P(p \vert \mathcal{D}) \propto p^k(1-p)^{n-k} \cdot p^{\alpha-1}(1-p)^{\beta-1} = p^{k+\alpha-1}(1-p)^{n-k+\beta-1}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Log-posterior:
$$\log P(p \vert \mathcal{D}) = (k+\alpha-1)\log p + (n-k+\beta-1)\log(1-p) + C$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Differentiate and set to zero:
$$\frac{k+\alpha-1}{p} - \frac{n-k+\beta-1}{1-p} = 0$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve:
$$(k+\alpha-1)(1-p) = (n-k+\beta-1)p$$
$$k+\alpha-1 = p(n + \alpha + \beta - 2)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** MAP estimate:
$$\hat{p}_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{k + \alpha - 1}{n + \alpha + \beta - 2}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Setting $\alpha = \beta = 1$:
$$\hat{p}_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{k + 0}{n + 0} = \frac{k}{n} = \hat{p}_{\text{MLE}} \;\; \blacksquare$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Given a sample $\{2, 4, 4, 6, 8\}$ drawn from a Gaussian distribution with known variance $\sigma^2 = 4$, derive and compute the MLE of the mean $\mu$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the relationship between L2 regularization in machine learning and MAP estimation with a Gaussian prior. Derive the equivalence mathematically. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — MLE of Gaussian Mean (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** PDF: $P(x_i \vert \mu) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Likelihood:
$$L(\mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Log-likelihood:
$$\ell(\mu) = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\mu)^2$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Differentiate:
$$\frac{d\ell}{d\mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = 0$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve:
$$\sum x_i = n\mu \;\Rightarrow\; \hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Substitute values: $\bar{x} = \frac{2+4+4+6+8}{5} = \frac{24}{5} = 4.8$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final answer: $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = 4.8$.

---

**Solution (b) — L2 Regularization ≡ MAP with Gaussian Prior (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** In linear regression, model: $y_i = w^T x_i + \epsilon_i$, with $\epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. Likelihood:
$$P(y_i \vert w, x_i) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{(y_i - w^T x_i)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Assume Gaussian prior on weights: $P(w) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda \Vert w \Vert^2}{2}\right)$ (zero mean, variance $1/\lambda$).

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Posterior:
$$P(w \vert \mathcal{D}) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum (y_i - w^T x_i)^2\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda \Vert w \Vert^2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Log-posterior:
$$\log P(w \vert \mathcal{D}) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum (y_i - w^T x_i)^2 - \frac{\lambda}{2}\Vert w \Vert^2 + C$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Maximizing is equivalent to minimizing the negative:
$$J(w) = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum (y_i - w^T x_i)^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2}\Vert w \Vert^2$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The first term is the **MSE loss**, the second term is **L2 regularization** (ridge penalty).

**Step 7 (1 mark):** **Conclusion:** Minimizing regularized MSE = MAP estimation with Gaussian prior on weights. Hence, *L2 regularization is the Bayesian MAP counterpart* of frequentist least squares.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **(2 marks lost commonly):** Forgetting to convert the product of likelihoods into a *sum* via logarithm before differentiating. **Always take log first** when the data is i.i.d.
> - **(2 marks lost commonly):** In MAP derivations, students drop the prior $P(\theta)$ and only maximize likelihood. **You must explicitly include** $P(\theta)$ and show how it appears in the final estimate.
> - **(1 mark lost):** Not verifying the second-order condition (concave/convex) — always confirm whether the critical point is a maximum.
> - **(1 mark lost):** For Bernoulli MAP, algebraic errors when rearranging — show every step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorize):**

- **MLE formula:** $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert \theta)$.
- **MAP formula:** $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \left[ \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert \theta) \right] \cdot P(\theta)$.
- **Bayes' Theorem:** Posterior $\propto$ Likelihood $\times$ Prior.
- **Bernoulli MLE:** $\hat{p} = \frac{\text{number of successes}}{\text{total trials}} = \frac{k}{n}$.
- **Bernoulli MAP (Beta prior):** $\hat{p}_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{k + \alpha - 1}{n + \alpha + \beta - 2}$.
- **Gaussian MLE mean:** $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$ (sample mean).
- **Gaussian MLE variance:** $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ (biased; unbiased uses $n-1$).
- **MAP with uniform prior $\equiv$ MLE.** This is the single most-tested statement.
- **L2 regularization $\equiv$ MAP with zero-mean Gaussian prior** on weights.
- **Log-likelihood trick:** Always convert products to sums to avoid numerical underflow.
- **Log-posterior = log-likelihood + log-prior** (this is what you actually maximize).
- **MLE is consistent** (converges to true $\theta$ as $n \to \infty$) and **asymptotically efficient**, but may be biased for finite $n$ (e.g., variance).
- **MAP with Beta(1,1) prior** = uniform prior on [0,1] = MLE for Bernoulli.
- **Gradient condition:** Set $\frac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$ to find the optimum.
- **Concave log-likelihood** $\Rightarrow$ unique global maximum guaranteed for exponential family distributions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra for KTU 2024:**
> *"Take log → Differentiate → Set to zero → Solve algebraically → Verify second derivative < 0 for MLE; for MAP, include $\log P(\theta)$ in the same steps."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Bayesian Formulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Bayesian Formulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bayesian Formulation** is a mathematical framework for statistical inference that updates the probability of a hypothesis as new evidence or data becomes available, by applying **Bayes' Theorem**. In the context of Machine Learning, it provides a principled mechanism for combining **prior beliefs** (domain knowledge) with **observed evidence** (training data) to compute the **posterior probability** of model parameters, hypotheses, or class labels.

Mathematically, the most general form of Bayes' Theorem is stated as:

$$
P(H \mid E) \;=\; \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}
$$

Where every term has a precise statistical identity. The terminology used in the KTU 2024 scheme and standard pattern recognition texts is tabulated below.

| Symbol | Standard KTU Term | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $P(H)$ | **Prior Probability** | Initial belief about hypothesis $H$ *before* seeing evidence |
| $P(E)$ | **Evidence / Marginal Likelihood** | Total probability of observing evidence $E$ under all hypotheses |
| $P(E \mid H)$ | **Likelihood** | Probability of evidence $E$ *given* that hypothesis $H$ is true |
| $P(H \mid E)$ | **Posterior Probability** | Updated belief about $H$ *after* observing evidence $E$ |

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Medical Testing Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Disease Test Paradox"**
>
> Imagine a patient undergoes a screening test for a rare disease. The disease affects only **1 in every 1000** people, so the prior $P(\text{Disease}) = 0.001$. The test is highly accurate: it correctly identifies the disease 99% of the time (sensitivity), and correctly clears healthy people 99% of the time (specificity).
>
> The patient receives a **POSITIVE** test result. Naively, the patient panics believing they almost certainly have the disease. However, applying Bayesian formulation reveals a striking result — the actual probability of having the disease, given a positive test, is only about **9%** (≈ 0.0901).
>
> *Why?* Because the disease is so rare (small prior) that the *base rate* dominates. Most positive results come from the **vast pool of healthy people** getting false positives. This is the famous **Base Rate Fallacy**, and Bayesian Formulation is the precise mathematical cure for it.

## 1.3 Why Bayesian Formulation Matters in Machine Learning

In KTU 2024 AI & ML coursework, Bayesian formulation underpins:

- **Naive Bayes Classifiers** — A direct application used in spam filtering, sentiment analysis, and document classification.
- **Bayesian Networks** — Probabilistic graphical models for reasoning under uncertainty.
- **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Estimation** — Regularized parameter estimation.
- **Bayesian Linear/Logistic Regression** — Probabilistic treatment of regression with uncertainty quantification.
- **Hidden Markov Models (HMMs)** — Speech recognition, POS tagging.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Keyword:** Always remember the four pillars — *Prior, Likelihood, Evidence, Posterior*. Examiners frequently award partial marks just for correctly naming and identifying each term in a formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Concept — Conditional Probability

Bayesian formulation is rooted in the definition of **conditional probability**. For two events $A$ and $B$ where $P(B) > 0$:

$$
P(A \mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}
$$

This expresses the probability of $A$ occurring *given* that $B$ has already occurred. The joint probability $P(A \cap B)$ can be written symmetrically:

$$
P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(A \mid B)\,P(B) \;=\; P(B \mid A)\,P(A)
$$

This symmetric decomposition is the **logical root** of Bayes' Theorem.

## 2.2 Structured Logical Derivation of Bayes' Theorem

- **Step 1 — Start with the product rule of probability.**
  By definition of conditional probability:
  $$
  P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)
  $$

- **Step 2 — Swap the conditioning variable.**
  The same joint event can be expressed by conditioning on $A$ first:
  $$
  P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
  $$

- **Step 3 — Equate the two expressions.**
  Since both expressions describe the same joint probability:
  $$
  P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) \;=\; P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
  $$

- **Step 4 — Isolate the target conditional probability.**
  Dividing both sides by $P(B)$:
  $$
  P(A \mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
  $$

This is **Bayes' Theorem** in its canonical two-event form. Replacing $A$ with $H$ (hypothesis) and $B$ with $E$ (evidence) yields the machine-learning-friendly notation.

## 2.3 Extension to Multiple Hypotheses — The Law of Total Probability

When the sample space partitions into $n$ mutually exclusive and exhaustive hypotheses $H_1, H_2, \dots, H_n$, the evidence probability expands as:

$$
P(E) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)
$$

The general multi-hypothesis Bayes' Theorem becomes:

$$
P(H_i \mid E) \;=\; \frac{P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the denominator matters:** Without computing $P(E)$, the posterior would not be a valid probability (it would not integrate to 1). For classification, since $P(E)$ is the same for all classes, it can often be treated as a **normalization constant**, allowing us to write: $P(H_i \mid E) \propto P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Table

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU High-Yield — memorize and apply directly.**

| # | Formula | Name | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(A \cap B) = P(A)\,P(B)$ | Independence Rule | Used when events are statistically independent |
| 2 | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ | Conditional Probability | Foundation of all Bayesian reasoning |
| 3 | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}$ | **Bayes' Theorem (Two-Event)** | Direct posterior computation |
| 4 | $P(E) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)$ | Law of Total Probability | Normalization constant for multi-class |
| 5 | $P(H_i \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_j)\,P(H_j)}$ | **Bayes' Theorem (Multi-Hypothesis)** | Naive Bayes, HMMs |
| 6 | $P(H_i \mid E) \propto P(E \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)$ | Posterior Proportionality | Used when only ranking hypotheses |
| 7 | $\arg\max_{H_i}\, P(H_i \mid E) = \arg\max_{H_i}\, P(E \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)$ | MAP Decision Rule | Naive Bayes classifier output |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real-World ML Systems

- **Spam Filtering (Gmail, Outlook):** The Naive Bayes classifier computes $P(\text{Spam} \mid \text{word}_1, \text{word}_2, \dots)$ using Bayesian formulation.
- **Medical Diagnosis Systems:** Bayesian networks reason over symptoms to estimate disease probabilities.
- **Speech Recognition:** HMMs use Bayesian inference to compute the most probable phoneme sequence.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Sensor fusion uses Bayesian filters (Kalman, Particle) for state estimation.
- **Recommendation Engines:** Probabilistic models compute the likelihood of user preference given historical behavior.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Bayes' Theorem from First Principles

**Statement to Prove:** For any two events $A$ and $B$ with non-zero probabilities:

$$
P(A \mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

### **Proof:**

**Step 1 — Apply the definition of conditional probability.**
By the formal definition, the conditional probability of event $A$ given event $B$ is the joint probability of $A$ and $B$ divided by the probability of $B$:

$$
P(A \mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}
$$

This is the foundational axiom from which everything else follows.

**Step 2 — Recognize that joint probability is symmetric.**
By Kolmogorov's axioms, the joint probability of two events can be factorized in *two valid ways*:

$$
P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)
$$

$$
P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two factorizations.**
Both right-hand sides describe the *same* joint probability, hence they must be equal:

$$
P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) \;=\; P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

**Step 4 — Algebraically isolate $P(A \mid B)$.**
Divide both sides of the equation by $P(B)$ (which is guaranteed non-zero by the conditional-probability requirement):

$$
P(A \mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

**Step 5 — Q.E.D.**
This completes the rigorous derivation. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Classic Coin-Die Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A box contains two coins. Coin 1 is fair ($P(\text{Head}) = 0.5$). Coin 2 is biased with $P(\text{Head}) = 0.8$. A coin is selected at random (equal probability) and tossed. Given that the result is **Head**, what is the probability that it was Coin 1?

### **Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the events and priors.**
- Let $H$ = event of observing a Head.
- Let $C_1$ = event that Coin 1 (fair) was chosen.
- Let $C_2$ = event that Coin 2 (biased) was chosen.
- Since the coin is chosen uniformly at random:
  $$
  P(C_1) \;=\; P(C_2) \;=\; 0.5
  $$

**Step 2 — Identify the likelihoods.**
- Likelihood of Head given Coin 1 (fair):
  $$
  P(H \mid C_1) \;=\; 0.5
  $$
- Likelihood of Head given Coin 2 (biased):
  $$
  P(H \mid C_2) \;=\; 0.8
  $$

**Step 3 — Compute the marginal evidence $P(H)$.**
Using the Law of Total Probability over the exhaustive partition $\{C_1, C_2\}$:

$$
P(H) \;=\; P(H \mid C_1)\,P(C_1) \;+\; P(H \mid C_2)\,P(C_2)
$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$
P(H) \;=\; (0.5 \times 0.5) \;+\; (0.8 \times 0.5)
$$

$$
P(H) \;=\; 0.25 \;+\; 0.40
$$

$$
P(H) \;=\; 0.65
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' Theorem.**
We seek $P(C_1 \mid H)$, the posterior probability that Coin 1 was selected given Head was observed:

$$
P(C_1 \mid H) \;=\; \frac{P(H \mid C_1) \cdot P(C_1)}{P(H)}
$$

Substitute:

$$
P(C_1 \mid H) \;=\; \frac{0.5 \times 0.5}{0.65}
$$

$$
P(C_1 \mid H) \;=\; \frac{0.25}{0.65}
$$

$$
P(C_1 \mid H) \;=\; \frac{5}{13} \;\approx\; 0.3846
$$

**Step 5 — Verify normalization.**
The complementary posterior must be:

$$
P(C_2 \mid H) \;=\; 1 - \frac{5}{13} \;=\; \frac{8}{13} \;\approx\; 0.6154
$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check.**
Since Coin 2 is biased toward Heads, observing Head *should* push the posterior mass toward $C_2$. Indeed, $P(C_2 \mid H) \approx 0.6154 > P(C_1 \mid H) \approx 0.3846$. ✓

**Final Answer:** $P(C_1 \mid H) = \dfrac{5}{13} \approx 0.3846$

## 3.3 The Bayesian Inference Loop — Recursive Posterior Update

In sequential learning (e.g., online spam filtering), Bayesian formulation naturally supports **iterative belief updating**. If evidence $E_1, E_2, \dots, E_n$ arrives one at a time:

$$
P(H \mid E_1, E_2) \;=\; \frac{P(E_2 \mid H, E_1)\,P(H \mid E_1)}{P(E_2 \mid E_1)}
$$

The posterior after observing $E_1$ becomes the **prior** before observing $E_2$. This recursive structure is the foundation of:

- **Kalman Filters** (robotics, GPS)
- **Particle Filters** (computer vision tracking)
- **Bayesian Online Learning** (streaming ML models)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Bayesian Inference

The diagram below depicts the **information flow** in Bayesian reasoning. Observations (evidence) update prior beliefs into posterior beliefs through the Bayes engine.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["PRIOR BELIEF P(H)"] --> node2["BAYES ENGINE"]
    node3["LIKELIHOOD P(E|H)"] --> node2
    node2 --> node4["POSTERIOR P(H|E)"]
    node4 --> node5{"DECISION RULE"}
    node5 --> node6["CLASSIFICATION OR PARAMETER ESTIMATE"]
    node4 --> node7["FEEDBACK LOOP"]
    node7 -.-> node1

    classDef input fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#0D47A1
    classDef engine fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#E65100
    classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20
    classDef decision fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,color:#880E4F

    class node1,node3 input
    class node2 engine
    class node4,node6 output
    class node5 decision
```

## 4.2 Multi-Hypothesis Bayesian Decision Architecture

This block diagram illustrates the architectural pipeline used in **Naive Bayes classifiers** and **Bayesian diagnostic systems**. Multiple hypotheses are evaluated in parallel against the same evidence stream, and the hypothesis with the maximum posterior wins.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    obs["OBSERVED EVIDENCE E"] --> split{"ROUTE TO ALL HYPOTHESES"}
    split --> h1["H1: CLASS A"]
    split --> h2["H2: CLASS B"]
    split --> h3["H3: CLASS C"]
    split --> h4["H4: CLASS D"]

    h1 --> p1["LIKELIHOOD P(E|H1)"]
    h2 --> p2["LIKELIHOOD P(E|H2)"]
    h3 --> p3["LIKELIHOOD P(E|H3)"]
    h4 --> p4["LIKELIHOOD P(E|H4)"]

    pr1["PRIOR P(H1)"] --> p1
    pr2["PRIOR P(H2)"] --> p2
    pr3["PRIOR P(H3)"] --> p3
    pr4["PRIOR P(H4)"] --> p4

    p1 --> n1["NUMERATOR N1"]
    p2 --> n2["NUMERATOR N2"]
    p3 --> n3["NUMERATOR N3"]
    p4 --> n4["NUMERATOR N4"]

    n1 --> sum["NORMALIZATION CONSTANT Z = N1+N2+N3+N4"]
    n2 --> sum
    n3 --> sum
    n4 --> sum

    sum --> post1["POSTERIOR P(H1|E)"]
    sum --> post2["POSTERIOR P(H2|E)"]
    sum --> post3["POSTERIOR P(H3|E)"]
    sum --> post4["POSTERIOR P(H4|E)"]

    post1 --> arg["ARGMAX OPERATOR"]
    post2 --> arg
    post3 --> arg
    post4 --> arg

    arg --> winner["WINNING HYPOTHESIS H*"]

    classDef ev fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1
    classDef hyp fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F9A825,color:#F57F17
    classDef calc fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#4A148C
    classDef win fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20

    class obs,split ev
    class h1,h2,h3,h4,pr1,pr2,pr3,pr4 hyp
    class p1,p2,p3,p4,n1,n2,n3,n4,sum,post1,post2,post3,post4,arg calc
    class winner win
```

## 4.3 The Bayesian Updating Cycle

A conceptual schematic of **iterative belief revision** — how posterior at time $t$ becomes prior at time $t+1$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t1P["PRIOR at t=0"] --> t1B["BAYES UPDATE"]
    t1E["EVIDENCE E1"] --> t1B
    t1B --> t1Post["POSTERIOR at t=1"]
    t1Post --> t2P["PRIOR at t=1"]
    t2P --> t2B["BAYES UPDATE"]
    t2E["EVIDENCE E2"] --> t2B
    t2B --> t2Post["POSTERIOR at t=2"]
    t2Post --> t3P["PRIOR at t=2"]
    t3P --> t3B["BAYES UPDATE"]
    t3E["EVIDENCE E3"] --> t3B
    t3B --> t3Post["POSTERIOR at t=3"]

    classDef stage fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,color:#01579B
    class t1P,t1E,t1B,t1Post,t2P,t2E,t2B,t2Post,t3P,t3E,t3B,t3Post stage
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Bayesian formulation. State and explain Bayes' Theorem with its components.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** **Bayesian formulation** is a probabilistic framework that updates the probability of a hypothesis based on new evidence by combining prior beliefs with observed data.
- **(1 Mark)** **Bayes' Theorem** states:
  $$
  P(H \mid E) \;=\; \frac{P(E \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(E)}
  $$
- **(1 Mark)** Components: $P(H)$ = Prior, $P(E \mid H)$ = Likelihood, $P(E)$ = Evidence, $P(H \mid E)$ = Posterior.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between prior probability and posterior probability with an example.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1.5 Marks)** **Prior probability** $P(H)$ is the initial belief about a hypothesis before observing evidence. **Posterior probability** $P(H \mid E)$ is the revised belief *after* evidence $E$ has been observed.
- **(1.5 Marks)** **Example:** Before testing, $P(\text{Disease}) = 0.001$ (prior). After a positive test, $P(\text{Disease} \mid \text{Positive}) = 0.09$ (posterior). The test updates the belief, but the posterior is always conditioned on observed evidence.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive Bayes' Theorem from the definition of conditional probability. Clearly explain each term with suitable notation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a factory, two machines M1 and M2 produce 60% and 40% of the total output respectively. The defect rates are: 2% for M1 and 3% for M2. If a randomly selected item is found to be defective, find the probability that it was produced by Machine M1.

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Conditional Probability.**
For two events $A$ and $B$, the conditional probability of $A$ given $B$ is defined as:

$$
P(A \mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}
$$

**Step 2 — Joint Probability Factorization (First Way).**
From the definition:

$$
P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)
$$

**Step 3 — Joint Probability Factorization (Symmetry).**
The same joint event can also be written as:

$$
P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and Solve.**
Equating both expressions:

$$
P(A \mid B)\,P(B) \;=\; P(B \mid A)\,P(A)
$$

Dividing by $P(B)$:

$$
P(A \mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret each term in ML context.**
- $P(A)$ — Prior probability of hypothesis.
- $P(B \mid A)$ — Likelihood of evidence given hypothesis.
- $P(B)$ — Marginal evidence (normalizing constant).
- $P(A \mid B)$ — Posterior probability (updated belief).

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Define the events.**
- $M_1$ = item produced by Machine 1.
- $M_2$ = item produced by Machine 2.
- $D$ = item is defective.

**Step 2 — Identify the priors.**
$$
P(M_1) \;=\; 0.60, \qquad P(M_2) \;=\; 0.40
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the likelihoods.**
$$
P(D \mid M_1) \;=\; 0.02, \qquad P(D \mid M_2) \;=\; 0.03
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total probability of defect (Law of Total Probability).**

$$
P(D) \;=\; P(D \mid M_1)\,P(M_1) \;+\; P(D \mid M_2)\,P(M_2)
$$

$$
P(D) \;=\; (0.02 \times 0.60) \;+\; (0.03 \times 0.40)
$$

$$
P(D) \;=\; 0.012 \;+\; 0.012 \;=\; 0.024
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Bayes' Theorem.**

$$
P(M_1 \mid D) \;=\; \frac{P(D \mid M_1)\,P(M_1)}{P(D)}
$$

$$
P(M_1 \mid D) \;=\; \frac{0.02 \times 0.60}{0.024} \;=\; \frac{0.012}{0.024}
$$

$$
P(M_1 \mid D) \;=\; 0.50
$$

**Final Answer:** $P(M_1 \mid D) = 0.50$ or **50%**. The defective item has an equal chance of having come from either machine.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Bayesian inference. Describe how prior, likelihood, evidence, and posterior interact in updating beliefs with a real-world example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A doctor knows that the disease affects 1% of the population. A screening test gives a positive result for 95% of diseased patients (true positive rate) and a negative result for 90% of healthy patients (true negative rate). If a patient tests positive, what is the probability that they actually have the disease?

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Bayesian Inference** is the process of using Bayes' Theorem to update the probability of a hypothesis as new evidence accumulates.

**The Four Components:**
1. **Prior $P(H)$** — Initial belief (e.g., from historical data, expert opinion).
2. **Likelihood $P(E \mid H)$** — Probability of observing the evidence under each hypothesis.
3. **Evidence $P(E)$** — Total probability of the observation across all hypotheses (normalizer).
4. **Posterior $P(H \mid E)$** — The updated belief after incorporating evidence.

**Real-World Example — Email Spam Detection:**
- **Prior $P(\text{Spam})$** — Proportion of spam emails historically (e.g., 30%).
- **Likelihood $P(\text{Word} \mid \text{Spam})$** — Frequency of "lottery" appearing in spam emails.
- **Evidence** — Probability of the word appearing in any email.
- **Posterior $P(\text{Spam} \mid \text{Word})$** — Updated probability that this email is spam given the word was found.

The interaction forms a cycle: posterior $\to$ prior when new evidence arrives.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Define events.**
- $D$ = patient has disease.
- $\bar{D}$ = patient is healthy.
- $+$ = test is positive.
- $-$ = test is negative.

**Step 2 — Identify priors.**
$$
P(D) \;=\; 0.01, \qquad P(\bar{D}) \;=\; 0.99
$$
