**Step 3 — Identify likelihoods.**
- True Positive Rate (Sensitivity): $P(+ \mid D) = 0.95$
- True Negative Rate (Specificity): $P(- \mid \bar{D}) = 0.90$
- Therefore: $P(+ \mid \bar{D}) = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$ (False Positive Rate)

**Step 4 — Compute total evidence $P(+)$.**

$$
P(+) \;=\; P(+ \mid D)\,P(D) \;+\; P(+ \mid \bar{D})\,P(\bar{D})
$$

$$
P(+) \;=\; (0.95 \times 0.01) \;+\; (0.10 \times 0.99)
$$

$$
P(+) \;=\; 0.0095 \;+\; 0.099 \;=\; 0.1085
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Bayes' Theorem.**

$$
P(D \mid +) \;=\; \frac{P(+ \mid D)\,P(D)}{P(+)}
$$

$$
P(D \mid +) \;=\; \frac{0.95 \times 0.01}{0.1085} \;=\; \frac{0.0095}{0.1085}
$$

$$
P(D \mid +) \;\approx\; 0.0876
$$

**Final Answer:** $P(D \mid +) \approx 0.0876$ or about **8.76%**. Despite a positive test with 95% sensitivity, the patient has less than a 9% chance of actually having the disease due to the low prior base rate — a classic demonstration of Bayesian reasoning.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in Bayesian Problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Prior and Likelihood** — Mixing up $P(H)$ with $P(E \mid H)$ is the single most common error. Always map out which event is the hypothesis and which is the evidence *before* plugging into the formula.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Denominator** — Students often compute only the numerator $P(E \mid H)\,P(H)$ and present it as the posterior. The total evidence $P(E)$ must always be computed using the **Law of Total Probability**.
>
> 3. **Independent vs. Mutually Exclusive Confusion** — Bayes' Theorem uses *exhaustive partitions* (mutually exclusive + collectively exhaustive), NOT just independent events.
>
> 4. **Missing Units/Percentage Notation** — Always report the final posterior as a decimal (0.0876) **and** its percentage equivalent (8.76%). Examiners award marks for both.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Final Sanity Check** — Always verify that all posteriors sum to 1. If they do not, a numerical error has occurred.
>
> 6. **Neglecting to State Assumptions** — Explicitly mention assumptions like "events are independent" or "hypotheses are exhaustive" when applying the Law of Total Probability.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**
>
> - **Bayes' Theorem** is the foundation: $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(E)}$.
> - The **Prior** is the belief *before* evidence; the **Posterior** is the belief *after* evidence.
> - The **Law of Total Probability** computes the evidence denominator for multi-hypothesis problems.
> - For classification, only the **ranking** of posteriors matters (use proportionality $P(H \mid E) \propto P(E \mid H)\,P(H)$).
> - **MAP (Maximum A Posteriori)** decision rule: pick the hypothesis with the highest posterior.
> - **ML (Maximum Likelihood)** is a special case of MAP where the prior is assumed uniform.
> - Bayes' Theorem is **inherently recursive**: the posterior of one update becomes the prior of the next.
> - Always remember the **Base Rate Fallacy** — a highly accurate test on a rare disease still produces many false positives.
> - In exam answers, **label every term** clearly (Prior, Likelihood, Evidence, Posterior) before substitution.
> - The denominator $P(E)$ acts as a **normalization constant** to ensure the posterior is a valid probability.
> - Real-world applications include **spam filtering, medical diagnosis, speech recognition, autonomous vehicles**, and **recommender systems**.
> - Bayesian formulation provides **principled uncertainty quantification**, distinguishing it from purely deterministic ML approaches.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression - Linear Regression with One Variable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regression - Linear Regression with One Variable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Regression?

**Regression** is a fundamental **supervised machine learning** technique used to model and analyze the relationship between a **dependent (target) variable** and one or more **independent (predictor) variables**. The primary objective is to predict a **continuous numerical output** (e.g., house price, temperature, sales revenue) based on input features.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Regression is a parametric statistical learning method that estimates a mapping function $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ from labeled training data, where the output variable is continuous and real-valued.

## 1.2 Classification of Regression (KTU Taxonomy)

| **Regression Type** | **Predictor Count** | **KTU Module Mapping** |
|---|---|---|
| Simple (Univariate) Linear Regression | **One** independent variable | Module 2 |
| Multiple (Multivariate) Linear Regression | **Two or more** independent variables | Module 2 (Advanced) |
| Polynomial Regression | Non-linear feature transformation | Module 3 (Optional Extension) |
| Logistic Regression | Classification (categorical output) | Module 3 |

## 1.3 Linear Regression with One Variable — The Formal Definition

**Linear Regression with One Variable** (also called **Simple Linear Regression** or **Univariate Linear Regression**) is the simplest form of regression that assumes a **linear relationship** between a single input feature $x$ and a continuous output $y$.

The model is formally expressed as:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x$$

Where:
- $h_{\theta}(x)$ → **Hypothesis function** (predicted output)
- $\theta_{0}$ → **y-intercept / bias term**
- $\theta_{1}$ → **slope / weight / coefficient**
- $x$ → **input feature**
- $y$ → **actual output**

## 1.4 🌱 Conceptual Analogy — The "Predicting Salary from Experience" Story

Imagine you are a career counselor trying to **predict a fresh graduate's starting salary** based only on their **internship experience (in months)**.

- You collect data: (Experience, Salary) pairs from past graduates.
- You plot them on a graph — the points roughly form an **upward-sloping line**.
- You draw the **best-fitting straight line** through these scattered points.
- For any new candidate, you simply **drop a vertical line** from their experience value to your drawn line, and read off the predicted salary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Linear regression is essentially **drawing the "best" straight line through a cloud of scattered data points** so that future predictions become a simple lookup. The "best" line is the one that minimizes the total vertical error between the line and every data point.

### Why "Linear"?
Because the equation $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_{0} + \theta_{1}x$ describes a **straight line** in 2D space (when $n = 1$). Linearity refers to **linearity in parameters** $\theta$, not necessarily in features.

## 1.5 🏗️ Where is it Used in Engineering?

- **Civil Engineering:** Predicting concrete strength from curing time.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Estimating tool wear from machining hours.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Modeling voltage-current relationships in linear circuits (Ohm's Law).
- **Economics/Finance:** Forecasting demand, stock trends, and risk pricing.
- **Healthcare:** Predicting disease progression from biomarkers.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Modeling pollution levels from traffic density.

## 1.6 📚 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| **Term** | **Symbol** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Hypothesis | $h_{\theta}(x)$ | The model's prediction function |
| Parameter | $\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}$ | Values learned during training |
| Cost Function | $J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1})$ | Measure of prediction error |
| Learning Rate | $\alpha$ | Step size in gradient descent |
| Epoch | — | One complete pass over the dataset |
| Convergence | — | State where parameters stop changing significantly |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Machine Learning Workflow — KTU 5-Phase Pipeline

Linear Regression follows the canonical **supervised learning pipeline**:

1. **Hypothesis Representation** → Define the functional form of the model.
2. **Cost Function Formulation** → Quantify how *wrong* the predictions are.
3. **Optimization Strategy Selection** → Choose between *gradient descent* or *normal equation*.
4. **Parameter Learning** → Iteratively minimize the cost function.
5. **Evaluation & Inference** → Test on unseen data to gauge generalization.

## 2.2 Hypothesis Function — Mathematical Anatomy

For a single feature input $x^{(i)}$ with its corresponding true output $y^{(i)}$:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x$$

**Geometric interpretation:**
- $\theta_{1}$ controls the **steepness** (slope) of the line.
- $\theta_{0}$ controls the **vertical shift** (intercept) — where the line crosses the y-axis.
- The pair $(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1})$ uniquely defines a line in the $(x, y)$ plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The term $\theta_{0}$ is **NOT optional** — omitting it forces the regression line to pass through the origin $(0,0)$, which is rarely the case in real-world data.

## 2.3 Cost Function — Mean Squared Error (MSE)

The **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** cost function measures the average squared deviation between predicted and actual values:

$$J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^{2}$$

### 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Purpose / Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| **Hypothesis** | $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x$ | Predicts $y$ given $x$ |
| **Cost Function (MSE)** | $J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^{2}$ | Quantifies total model error |
| **Prediction Error** | $\epsilon^{(i)} = h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}$ | Residual for $i$-th sample |
| **MSE Metric** | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{m} \sum (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^{2}$ | Final evaluation metric |
| **RMSE Metric** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Error in original units of $y$ |
| **Gradient Descent Update (Intercept)** | $\theta_{0} := \theta_{0} - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{0}}$ | Bias update rule |
| **Gradient Descent Update (Slope)** | $\theta_{1} := \theta_{1} - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{1}}$ | Weight update rule |
| **Partial Derivative (Intercept)** | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{0}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})$ | Gradient w.r.t. $\theta_{0}$ |
| **Partial Derivative (Slope)** | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{1}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x^{(i)}$ | Gradient w.r.t. $\theta_{1}$ |
| **Normal Equation (Closed Form)** | $\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y$ | Direct analytical solution |
| **Slope (Closed Form)** | $\theta_{1} = \frac{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^{2}}$ | Direct $\theta_{1}$ computation |
| **Intercept (Closed Form)** | $\theta_{0} = \bar{y} - \theta_{1} \bar{x}$ | Direct $\theta_{0}$ computation |
| **Coefficient of Determination** | $R^{2} = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | Model goodness-of-fit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constants:** $m$ = number of training samples. The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ in the cost function is a **mathematical convenience** to cancel the exponent 2 when differentiating (no semantic meaning otherwise).

### 2.4 Why Squared Error and Not Absolute Error?

- **Differentiability:** Squared error is smooth and differentiable everywhere — essential for gradient-based optimization.
- **Penalization:** Large errors are penalized **quadratically**, forcing the model to avoid outliers' heavy influence.
- **Statistical Foundation:** MSE minimization under Gaussian noise assumptions yields the **Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE)**.

## 2.5 Optimization Strategies — Two KTU-Approved Methods

### **Method A: Gradient Descent (Iterative)**

$$repeat \; until \; convergence: \{ \quad \theta_{j} := \theta_{j} - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_{j}} J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}) \quad \}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Simultaneous Update Rule:** $\theta_{0}$ and $\theta_{1}$ **must be updated simultaneously** in each iteration. Using the updated value of $\theta_{0}$ to compute $\theta_{1}$ (or vice versa) is a common KTU mark-deduction error.

### **Method B: Normal Equation (Closed-Form / Analytical)**

$$\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y$$

Where $X$ is the **design matrix** (with a column of 1's for $\theta_{0}$) and $y$ is the output vector.

## 2.6 Comparison Table — KTU Exam Favorite

| **Criterion** | **Gradient Descent** | **Normal Equation** |
|---|---|---|
| Computational Approach | Iterative | One-shot closed-form |
| Learning Rate $\alpha$ | Required | Not needed |
| Feature Scaling | Highly recommended | Not required |
| Works for $n$ features | ✅ Scales well | ❌ Slow if $n > 10^{4}$ |
| Inverse Computation | Not needed | Requires $(X^TX)^{-1}$ — $O(n^{3})$ |
| Convergence Behavior | Local minimum risk (convex here so global) | Direct global optimum |

## 2.7 Learning Rate $\alpha$ — The Engine of Convergence

- **Too small $\alpha$:** Convergence is painfully slow (thousands of iterations).
- **Too large $\alpha$:** $J(\theta)$ may **overshoot** the minimum, diverge, or oscillate.
- **Just right $\alpha$:** Smooth, monotonic decrease of $J(\theta)$ to global minimum.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** For simple linear regression, $J(\theta)$ is a **convex bowl-shaped (paraboloid) function** — there are **no local minima traps**, only one global minimum. This is a frequently tested conceptual question.

## 2.8 Feature Scaling (Important for Gradient Descent)

To accelerate convergence, features should be normalized:

$$x := \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} \quad \text{or} \quad x := \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

Where $\mu$ is the mean and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Gradient Descent Update Rules (Full Proof)

### **Step 1: Substitute Hypothesis into Cost Function**

$$J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( (\theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^{2}$$

### **Step 2: Compute Partial Derivative with respect to $\theta_{0}$**

Using chain rule on a single term:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_{0}} \left[ (\theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^{2} \right] = 2 (\theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \cdot 1$$

Summing over all $m$ samples and averaging:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{0}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

### **Step 3: Compute Partial Derivative with respect to $\theta_{1}$**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_{1}} \left[ (\theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^{2} \right] = 2 (\theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

Summing and averaging:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{1}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x^{(i)}$$

### **Step 4: Apply the Gradient Descent Update Rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{0} &:= \theta_{0} - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \\
\theta_{1} &:= \theta_{1} - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Both updates must happen simultaneously** — using the old values of both $\theta_{0}$ and $\theta_{1}$ to compute the new values. This is a **frequently tested KTU question**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Closed-Form Solution (Normal Equation) for $\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}$

### **Step 1: Set Partial Derivatives to Zero (Optimality Condition)**

At the global minimum of $J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1})$, the gradient vanishes:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{0}} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{1}} = 0$$

### **Step 2: Solve the System Analytically**

The two optimality conditions give us:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) &= 0 \\
\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \, x^{(i)} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

From the **first equation**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} (\theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) = 0$$

$$\theta_{0} \cdot m + \theta_{1} \sum x^{(i)} - \sum y^{(i)} = 0$$

$$\theta_{0} + \theta_{1} \bar{x} - \bar{y} = 0$$

$$\therefore \; \theta_{0} = \bar{y} - \theta_{1} \bar{x}$$

From the **second equation** (after substituting $\theta_{0}$):

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} (\bar{y} - \theta_{1}\bar{x} + \theta_{1} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \, x^{(i)} = 0$$

$$\sum (y^{(i)} - \bar{y} - \theta_{1}(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})) \, x^{(i)} = 0$$

After algebraic manipulation using covariance/variance identities:

$$\theta_{1} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^{2}}$$

### **Step 3: Final Closed-Form Equations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{1} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^{2}} \\
\theta_{0} &= \bar{y} - \theta_{1} \bar{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** Given the dataset:

| $x$ (Years of Experience) | $y$ (Salary in ₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 3 |
| 2 | 4 |
| 3 | 5 |
| 4 | 6 |

Find $\theta_{0}$ and $\theta_{1}$ using the **closed-form solution**.

### **Step 1: Compute Means**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4}{4} = 2.5, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{3+4+5+6}{4} = 4.5$$

### **Step 2: Build the Deviation Table**

| $x^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ | $x^{(i)} - \bar{x}$ | $y^{(i)} - \bar{y}$ | Product | $(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | $-1.5$ | $-1.5$ | $2.25$ | $2.25$ |
| 2 | 4 | $-0.5$ | $-0.5$ | $0.25$ | $0.25$ |
| 3 | 5 | $+0.5$ | $+0.5$ | $0.25$ | $0.25$ |
| 4 | 6 | $+1.5$ | $+1.5$ | $2.25$ | $2.25$ |
| **Sum** | — | **0** | **0** | **5.00** | **5.00** |

### **Step 3: Apply Closed-Form Formulas**

$$\theta_{1} = \frac{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^{2}} = \frac{5.00}{5.00} = 1.0$$

$$\theta_{0} = \bar{y} - \theta_{1} \bar{x} = 4.5 - (1.0)(2.5) = 2.0$$

### **Step 4: Final Hypothesis**

$$h_{\theta}(x) = 2.0 + 1.0 \cdot x$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A candidate with **0 experience** would have a predicted salary of ₹2.0 Lakhs (baseline). Each additional year of experience adds ₹1.0 Lakh to the predicted salary.

### **Step 5: Verify with Predictions**

| $x^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ (actual) | $h_{\theta}(x^{(i)})$ (predicted) | Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 3.0 | 0 |
| 2 | 4 | 4.0 | 0 |
| 3 | 5 | 5.0 | 0 |
| 4 | 6 | 6.0 | 0 |

✅ **Perfect fit** (because the data was perfectly linear).

### **Step 6: Compute $R^{2}$ (Coefficient of Determination)**

$$R^{2} = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{0}{5.0} = 1.0$$

The model explains **100%** of the variance in $y$.

---

## 3.4 Gradient Descent Iteration Walk-Through

Using the same data with $\theta_{0} = 0, \theta_{1} = 0, \alpha = 0.01$:

**Iteration 1:**

Predictions: $h_{\theta}(1)=0, h_{\theta}(2)=0, h_{\theta}(3)=0, h_{\theta}(4)=0$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{0}} = \frac{1}{4}[(0-3)+(0-4)+(0-5)+(0-6)] = \frac{-18}{4} = -4.5$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{1}} = \frac{1}{4}[(0-3)(1)+(0-4)(2)+(0-5)(3)+(0-6)(4)] = \frac{-50}{4} = -12.5$$

$$\theta_{0} := 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-4.5) = 0.045, \quad \theta_{1} := 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-12.5) = 0.125$$

This process continues until $J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1})$ plateaus near its minimum.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Linear Regression — End-to-End Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Training Data XY pairs] --> B[Initialize Parameters theta0 theta1 to zero]
    B --> C[Compute Hypothesis h_theta_x]
    C --> D[Compute Cost J as Mean Squared Error]
    D --> E{Check Convergence}
    E -- Not Converged --> F[Compute Gradients dJ_dtheta0 and dJ_dtheta1]
    F --> G[Simultaneous Update theta0 and theta1]
    G --> C
    E -- Converged --> H[Final Model h_theta_x]
    H --> I[Predict on Test Data]
    I --> J[Evaluate via RMSE MAE R2]
```

## 4.2 Cost Function Visualization — The Bowl

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Parameter Space theta0 theta1] --> B[Cost Function J as 3D Bowl]
    B --> C[Global Minimum at Gradient equals zero]
    C --> D[Optimal Parameters theta0 star theta1 star]
```

## 4.3 Gradient Descent Optimization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Iteration k] --> Init[Set theta0_old and theta1_old]
    Init --> ComputeGrad[Compute Gradients using old parameters]
    ComputeGrad --> Update[Update theta0_new and theta1_new]
    Update --> Assign[Assign theta_old equals theta_new]
    Assign --> CheckConverge{J converged or theta stable}
    CheckConverge -- No --> ComputeGrad
    CheckConverge -- Yes --> Output[Return Optimal theta0 theta1]
```

## 4.4 Closed-Form vs Iterative Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Number of Features n} --> Q1A[n less than 10000]
    Q1 --> Q1B[n greater than or equal to 10000]
    Q1A -- Yes --> R1[Use Normal Equation Closed Form]
    Q1B -- No --> Q2{Need Online Learning}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Use Gradient Descent]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Need Exact Solution}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Use Normal Equation]
    Q3 -- No --> R4[Use Gradient Descent with Feature Scaling]
```

## 4.5 Hypothesis Line Fitting — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Data Points x_i y_i] --> B[Scatter Plot]
    B --> C[Hypothesis Line h_theta_x]
    C --> D[Residuals Vertical Distances]
    D --> E[Minimize Sum of Squared Residuals]
    E --> F[Best Fit Line Achieved]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define linear regression with one variable. Write down the hypothesis function and the cost function used in simple linear regression.**

**Model Answer:**

Linear regression with one variable (also called **simple linear regression**) is a supervised learning algorithm that models the relationship between a single independent variable $x$ and a continuous dependent variable $y$ using a straight line.

The **hypothesis function** is:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x$$

The **cost function** (Mean Squared Error) is:

$$J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^{2}$$

The objective is to find $\theta_{0}$ and $\theta_{1}$ that **minimize** $J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1})$.

---

### **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the role of the learning rate $\alpha$ in gradient descent. What happens if $\alpha$ is too small or too large?**

**Model Answer:**

The **learning rate $\alpha$** is a hyperparameter that controls the **step size** taken in the direction of the negative gradient during each iteration of gradient descent.

- **If $\alpha$ is too small:** Gradient descent makes tiny progress per iteration. Convergence is **extremely slow**, requiring a large number of iterations.
- **If $\alpha$ is too large:** Gradient descent may **overshoot** the minimum, causing $J(\theta)$ to **oscillate** or even **diverge to infinity**.
- **Optimal $\alpha$:** $J(\theta)$ decreases monotonically and converges efficiently to the global minimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **For simple linear regression**, the cost function is convex, so a well-chosen $\alpha$ is guaranteed to converge to the global minimum.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question B1 (Option A):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the gradient descent update rules for the parameters $\theta_{0}$ and $\theta_{1}$ in simple linear regression. Show all intermediate steps clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the dataset $(x, y) = \{(1, 2), (2, 4), (3, 5), (4, 4), (5, 6)\}$. Compute the parameters $\theta_{0}$ and $\theta_{1}$ using the **closed-form (normal) equation**.

---

### **Model Solution for B1(a):**

**Step 1:** Hypothesis function:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x$$

**Step 2:** Cost function:

$$J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Partial derivative w.r.t. $\theta_{0}$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{0}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Partial derivative w.r.t. $\theta_{1}$ (using chain rule on the squared term):

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{1}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x^{(i)}$$

**Step 5:** Update rules (simultaneous):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{0} &:= \theta_{0} - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \\
\theta_{1} &:= \theta_{1} - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

These rules are applied iteratively until $J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1})$ converges.

---

### **Model Solution for B1(b):**

**Step 1: Compute Means**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5} = 3.0, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{2+4+5+4+6}{5} = 4.2$$

**Step 2: Build Deviation Table**

| $x^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ | $x^{(i)} - \bar{x}$ | $y^{(i)} - \bar{y}$ | Product | $(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | $-2$ | $-2.2$ | $4.4$ | $4$ |
| 2 | 4 | $-1$ | $-0.2$ | $0.2$ | $1$ |
| 3 | 5 | $0$ | $0.8$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 4 | 4 | $+1$ | $-0.2$ | $-0.2$ | $1$ |
| 5 | 6 | $+2$ | $+1.8$ | $3.6$ | $4$ |
| **Sum** | — | **0** | **0** | **8.0** | **10** |

**Step 3: Compute $\theta_{1}$**

$$\theta_{1} = \frac{8.0}{10} = 0.8$$

**Step 4: Compute $\theta_{0}$**

$$\theta_{0} = \bar{y} - \theta_{1} \bar{x} = 4.2 - (0.8)(3.0) = 4.2 - 2.4 = 1.8$$

**Step 5: Final Hypothesis**

$$h_{\theta}(x) = 1.8 + 0.8 x$$

**Step 6: Verification Table**

| $x^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ (actual) | $h_{\theta}(x^{(i)})$ (predicted) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 2.6 |
| 2 | 4 | 3.4 |
| 3 | 5 | 4.2 |
| 4 | 4 | 5.0 |
| 5 | 6 | 5.8 |

---

### **Question B1 (Option B):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **batch gradient descent algorithm** for simple linear regression. Discuss the effect of learning rate with proper convergence plots description.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **gradient descent** and the **normal equation** for solving linear regression. Which one would you prefer when $n = 50,000$ features, and why?

---

### **Model Solution for B1(a):**

**Batch Gradient Descent Algorithm:**

Batch gradient descent computes the gradient using the **entire training set** in each iteration.

**Algorithm:**

1. Initialize $\theta_{0} = 0$, $\theta_{1} = 0$.
2. Repeat until convergence:
   - Compute gradients over **all $m$ training examples**:
   $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{0}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})$$
   $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{1}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x^{(i)}$$
   - Update parameters **simultaneously**:
   $$\theta_{0} := \theta_{0} - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{0}}, \quad \theta_{1} := \theta_{1} - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{1}}$$

**Effect of Learning Rate $\alpha$:**

| Learning Rate | Behavior of $J(\theta)$ | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| **Very Small** (e.g., $10^{-6}$) | Slow, steady decrease — many iterations needed | ❌ Inefficient |
| **Optimal** (e.g., $0.01$) | Smooth, monotonic decrease → convergence in few hundred iterations | ✅ Ideal |
| **Too Large** (e.g., $1.0$) | Oscillates or diverges — $J$ may increase | ❌ Fails |
| **Slightly Large** (e.g., $0.5$) | Oscillates around minimum but eventually converges | ⚠️ Suboptimal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Since $J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1})$ for linear regression is convex, **batch gradient descent is guaranteed to converge** to the global minimum for any sufficiently small fixed $\alpha$.

---

### **Model Solution for B1(b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| **Criterion** | **Gradient Descent** | **Normal Equation** |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Iterative | Closed-form |
| Needs $\alpha$ | Yes | No |
| Needs iterations | Yes | No (one-shot) |
| Computational Cost per iter | $O(mn)$ | $O(n^{3})$ for inversion |
| Total Cost | $O(kmn)$ | $O(n^{3})$ |
| Feature Scaling | Required | Not required |
| Online Learning | Easy to adapt | Not possible |

**For $n = 50,000$:**

**Normal Equation** would require inverting a $50{,}000 \times 50{,}000$ matrix — cost is $O(n^{3}) \approx 1.25 \times 10^{14}$ operations. This is **computationally infeasible**.

**Gradient Descent** scales as $O(kmn)$ — with $m$ samples and $k$ iterations, it is **far more efficient** for large $n$.

✅ **Recommendation:** Use **Gradient Descent** (with feature scaling) when $n = 50{,}000$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Many students compute $\theta_{1}$ using $\sum x^{(i)} y^{(i)}$ instead of the **deviation form** $\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})$. Both yield the same answer, but if you make arithmetic errors in the mean, the deviation form propagates errors better.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Students often forget the **simultaneous update rule** for gradient descent. Always use the **old values** of both $\theta_{0}$ and $\theta_{1}$ to compute the new values.
> - **Pitfall 3:** In numerical questions, students forget to **convert units** or report $\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}$ to appropriate decimal precision. Always include units in your interpretation.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Students confuse $\theta_{0}$ (intercept) with $\theta_{1}$ (slope) — always state which is which in your final answer.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Writing cost function as $\frac{1}{m}$ instead of $\frac{1}{2m}$ — the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor is a KTU convention; either is technically correct, but $\frac{1}{2m}$ is the **expected** form for clean derivative cancellation.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU Exam Checklist:**

- ✅ Linear regression with one variable fits a **straight line**: $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_{0} + \theta_{1} x$.
- ✅ **Cost function** is Mean Squared Error: $J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^{2}$.
- ✅ **Goal:** Minimize $J(\theta_{0}, \theta_{1})$ w.r.t. $\theta_{0}$ and $\theta_{1}$.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent** iteratively updates $\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}$ using partial derivatives.
- ✅ **Normal Equation** gives direct solution: $\theta_{1} = \frac{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^{2}}$, $\theta_{0} = \bar{y} - \theta_{1} \bar{x}$.
- ✅ **Learning rate $\alpha$** controls step size — too small = slow, too large = diverges.
- ✅ Updates **must be simultaneous** — never use updated $\theta_{0}$ to compute $\theta_{1}$ in the same iteration.
- ✅ Cost function for linear regression is **convex** — gradient descent always reaches global minimum.
- ✅ **Feature scaling** (mean normalization or min-max) accelerates gradient descent.
- ✅ Use **gradient descent** when $n$ is large; use **normal equation** when $n$ is small.
- ✅ $R^{2}$ measures goodness-of-fit: closer to 1 = better fit.
- ✅ The factor $\frac{1}{2m}$ (vs $\frac{1}{m}$) is for **mathematical convenience** in differentiation.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Liner Memory Aid:** *"Linear regression draws the best-fit line by minimizing squared vertical errors between predictions and truth — using either iterative gradient descent or one-shot normal equation."*

**Module 2 Mastery Tip:** Always carry forward both **mathematical derivation skills** and **numerical problem-solving practice** for KTU ESE. The examiner tests both **theoretical depth** (derivation, comparison) and **computational accuracy** (closed-form solutions) equally.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Regression with Multiple Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linear Regression with Multiple Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)** is a supervised machine learning regression technique that models the relationship between **two or more independent (predictor) variables** and a **single continuous dependent (response) variable** by fitting a *linear equation* to the observed data.

Mathematically, for a dataset with `n` training examples and `m` features, the hypothesis function is:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \dots + \theta_m x_m$$

In compact **vectorized form** (the form used in KTU board numericals):

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^{T} x$$

where $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{(m+1) \times 1}$ and $x \in \mathbb{R}^{(m+1) \times 1}$ (with $x_0 = 1$ appended).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a real-estate consultant in Kerala predicting the **price of a house**. In simple linear regression you might only use the *area* of the house. But in reality, the price also depends on:

- Number of bedrooms ($x_1$)
- Distance to the city centre ($x_2$)
- Age of the property ($x_3$)

You are essentially trying to draw the **best-fitting hyperplane** in an $(m+1)$-dimensional space that passes closest to all the data points. With 2 features, this hyperplane is a flat 2D plane in 3D space; with 3 features, it is a "flat" 3D volume in 4D space, and so on.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Intuition:** With one feature, the hypothesis is a *line*; with two features, it is a *plane*; with more features, it is a *hyperplane*. The algorithm finds the orientation and offset of this hyperplane that minimises the average squared vertical distance from each data point to the plane.

## 1.3 Why "Multiple" Variables?

The transition from simple to multiple linear regression is critical because real-world phenomena are almost always governed by **multiple causal factors**. Using more relevant features generally:

1. **Reduces bias** in the estimate.
2. **Increases predictive power** (lower Mean Squared Error).
3. **Captures interaction effects** between variables.

However, it also introduces challenges like the **curse of dimensionality**, **multicollinearity**, and the need for **feature scaling** — all of which are discussed in subsequent sections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Assumption (Board Favourite):** MLR assumes a *linear relationship* between the dependent variable $y$ and each independent variable, *independence* of observations, *homoscedasticity* (constant variance of errors), and *no perfect multicollinearity* among predictors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hypothesis in Scalar, Vector, and Matrix Forms

### (a) Scalar Form (explicit)

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \theta_j x_j$$

### (b) Vector Form

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^{T} x \quad \text{where} \quad x_0 = 1$$

### (c) Matrix Form (used for the entire training set)

Let $X$ be the **design matrix** of dimensions $(n \times (m+1))$ and $y$ the target vector $(n \times 1)$:

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1^{(1)} & x_2^{(1)} & \dots & x_m^{(1)} \\ 1 & x_1^{(2)} & x_2^{(2)} & \dots & x_m^{(2)} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_1^{(n)} & x_2^{(n)} & \dots & x_m^{(n)} \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} y^{(1)} \\ y^{(2)} \\ \vdots \\ y^{(n)} \end{bmatrix}$$

The vectorized prediction for the **entire** dataset is:

$$\hat{y} = X\theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why prepend a column of 1's?** The column $x_0 = 1$ allows the bias/intercept term $\theta_0$ to be absorbed neatly into the matrix multiplication $X\theta$, instead of treating it as a special case. This is mandatory in KTU numericals.

## 2.2 The Cost Function (Mean Squared Error)

The cost function $J(\theta)$ measures how wrong the model's predictions are:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_{\theta}\left(x^{(i)}\right) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

In elegant **matrix form**:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n} (X\theta - y)^{T} (X\theta - y)$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience so that the squared term vanishes cleanly when we differentiate.

## 2.3 Two Optimisation Strategies (the "How")

### Strategy 1 — The Normal Equation (Closed-Form / Analytical)

By setting the gradient $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = 0$ and solving directly:

$$\boxed{\theta = \left( X^{T} X \right)^{-1} X^{T} y}$$

- **Pros:** No need to choose a learning rate $\alpha$; no iteration; gives the exact minimum.
- **Cons:** Computing $(X^{T}X)^{-1}$ costs roughly $O(m^3)$ — very slow when $m > 10{,}000$. Requires $X^{T}X$ to be **invertible** (no perfect multicollinearity).

### Strategy 2 — Gradient Descent (Iterative)

Simultaneously update all $\theta_j$:

$$\boxed{\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta)}$$

The partial derivative for multiple variables is:

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_{\theta}\left(x^{(i)}\right) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

In matrix form:

$$\theta := \theta - \frac{\alpha}{n} X^{T} (X\theta - y)$$

## 2.4 Feature Scaling & Learning Rate

When features have wildly different ranges (e.g., area in 1000s of sq.ft. vs. bedrooms 1–5), the cost contours become elongated ellipses, and gradient descent takes a zig-zag path. **Feature scaling** normalises every feature to a comparable range.

Two common techniques:
- **Min-Max Scaling:** $x_j' = \dfrac{x_j - \min(x_j)}{\max(x_j) - \min(x_j)} \in [0, 1]$
- **Z-score Standardisation:** $x_j' = \dfrac{x_j - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$ (mean 0, std 1)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Hypothesis (scalar) | $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \theta_j x_j$ | $m$ = number of features |
| 2 | Hypothesis (vector) | $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^{T} x$ | $\theta, x \in \mathbb{R}^{m+1}$ |
| 3 | Predictions (matrix) | $\hat{y} = X\theta$ | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (m+1)}$ |
| 4 | Cost Function | $J(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{2n}(X\theta - y)^{T}(X\theta - y)$ | Unit: (target unit)$^2$ |
| 5 | Normal Equation | $\theta = (X^{T}X)^{-1} X^{T} y$ | Closed-form optimum |
| 6 | Gradient Descent | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \dfrac{1}{n}\sum (h - y)\,x_j$ | $\alpha$ = learning rate |
| 7 | Gradient (matrix) | $\theta := \theta - \dfrac{\alpha}{n} X^{T}(X\theta - y)$ | Vectorised form |
| 8 | Min-Max Scaling | $x_j' = (x_j - \min)/(\max - \min)$ | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| 9 | Z-score Scaling | $x_j' = (x_j - \mu_j)/\sigma_j$ | Mean 0, Std 1 |
| 10 | R-squared | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | $SS_{res} = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ |
| 11 | Adjusted R-squared | $R^2_{adj} = 1 - (1 - R^2)\dfrac{n-1}{n - p - 1}$ | $p$ = no. of features |
| 12 | Multicollinearity Check | $\det(X^{T}X) \approx 0$ | Singularity warning |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multiple Linear Regression is a workhorse in:

- **Civil Engineering:** Predicting concrete compressive strength from cement, water, aggregate content.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Modelling power consumption vs. voltage, current, temperature.
- **Economics & Business:** Demand forecasting using price, advertising spend, season.
- **Healthcare:** Predicting disease progression from multiple biomarkers.
- **ML Pre-processing:** Forms the building block of polynomial regression, regularisation (Ridge, LASSO), and logistic regression.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Cost Function to Normal Equation (Exhaustive)

We want the value of $\theta$ that minimises $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n}(X\theta - y)^{T}(X\theta - y)$.

**Step 1 — Expand the matrix product.** Let $e = X\theta - y$ (the error/residual vector). Then:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n} e^{T} e = \frac{1}{2n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2$$

**Step 2 — Use the matrix identity for differentiation.** For $f(z) = z^{T} A z$ where $A$ is symmetric, $\nabla_z f = 2 A z$. We rewrite $J$ as:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n} \left[ \theta^{T} X^{T} X \theta - \theta^{T} X^{T} y - y^{T} X \theta + y^{T} y \right]$$

Since $X^{T}X$ is symmetric and $\theta^{T} X^{T} y$ is a scalar (so it equals its transpose $y^{T} X \theta$):

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n} \left[ \theta^{T} (X^{T} X) \theta - 2 \theta^{T} (X^{T} y) + y^{T} y \right]$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $\theta$.**

$$\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n} \left[ 2 (X^{T} X) \theta - 2 (X^{T} y) \right] = \frac{1}{n} \left[ (X^{T} X) \theta - X^{T} y \right]$$

**Step 4 — Set the gradient to zero (stationary point).**

$$(X^{T} X) \theta - X^{T} y = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\theta$.** Multiply by $(X^{T} X)^{-1}$ (assuming it is invertible):

$$\boxed{\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y}$$

This is the famous **Normal Equation**. The matrix $X^{+} = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T}$ is known as the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** of $X$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gradient Vector from the Cost Function (Scalar Path)

We start from the *scalar* form and derive the same gradient term-by-term.

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \dots + \theta_m x_m^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $\theta_j$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

**Special case** $j=0$ (the bias): $x_0^{(i)} = 1$, so:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

This confirms that the gradient descent rule:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$$

moves each parameter in the *opposite* direction of the slope, scaled by learning rate $\alpha$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Normal Equation (Full Computation)

**Given Training Data** (Predicting house price $y$ from area $x_1$ and bedrooms $x_2$):

| $i$ | $x_1$ (sq.ft./100) | $x_2$ (bedrooms) | $y$ (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 3 |
| 2 | 2 | 3 | 8 |
| 3 | 4 | 2 | 12 |

**Step 1 — Build the design matrix $X$ (prepend $x_0 = 1$):**

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 8 \\ 12 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $X^{T}$ and then $X^{T} X$:**

$$X^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$X^{T} X = \begin{bmatrix} 1+1+1 & 1+2+4 & 1+3+2 \\ 1+2+4 & 1+4+16 & 1+6+8 \\ 1+3+2 & 1+6+8 & 1+9+4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 7 & 6 \\ 7 & 21 & 15 \\ 6 & 15 & 14 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $X^{T} y$:**

$$X^{T} y = \begin{bmatrix} 3 + 8 + 12 \\ 3 + 16 + 48 \\ 3 + 24 + 24 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 23 \\ 67 \\ 51 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the determinant of $X^{T} X$** to confirm invertibility:

$$\det(X^{T} X) = 3(21 \cdot 14 - 15 \cdot 15) - 7(7 \cdot 14 - 15 \cdot 6) + 6(7 \cdot 15 - 21 \cdot 6)$$

$$= 3(294 - 225) - 7(98 - 90) + 6(105 - 126)$$

$$= 3(69) - 7(8) + 6(-21) = 207 - 56 - 126 = 25 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Since $\det \neq 0$, the matrix is invertible.

**Step 5 — Compute the cofactor matrix of $X^{T} X$:**

$$C = \begin{bmatrix} +(21\cdot 14 - 15\cdot 15) & -(7\cdot 14 - 15\cdot 6) & +(7\cdot 15 - 21\cdot 6) \\ -(7\cdot 14 - 6\cdot 15) & +(3\cdot 14 - 6\cdot 6) & -(3\cdot 15 - 7\cdot 6) \\ +(7\cdot 15 - 6\cdot 21) & -(3\cdot 15 - 6\cdot 7) & +(3\cdot 21 - 7\cdot 7) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$C = \begin{bmatrix} +69 & -8 & -21 \\ -8 & +6 & -3 \\ -21 & -3 & +14 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — The inverse is the transpose of the cofactor matrix divided by the determinant:**

$$(X^{T} X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{25} \begin{bmatrix} 69 & -8 & -21 \\ -8 & 6 & -3 \\ -21 & -3 & 14 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Compute $\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y$:**

$$\theta_0 = \frac{1}{25}\left( 69(23) - 8(67) - 21(51) \right) = \frac{1587 - 536 - 1071}{25} = \frac{-20}{25} = -0.8$$

$$\theta_1 = \frac{1}{25}\left( -8(23) + 6(67) - 3(51) \right) = \frac{-184 + 402 - 153}{25} = \frac{65}{25} = 2.6$$

$$\theta_2 = \frac{1}{25}\left( -21(23) - 3(67) + 14(51) \right) = \frac{-483 - 201 + 714}{25} = \frac{30}{25} = 1.2$$

**Final Model:**

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = -0.8 + 2.6\,x_1 + 1.2\,x_2}$$

**Step 8 — Verification on training data:**

- $\hat{y}^{(1)} = -0.8 + 2.6(1) + 1.2(1) = 3.0$ ✓
- $\hat{y}^{(2)} = -0.8 + 2.6(2) + 1.2(3) = 8.0$ ✓
- $\hat{y}^{(3)} = -0.8 + 2.6(4) + 1.2(2) = 12.0$ ✓

The model perfectly fits the training data (cost $J(\theta) = 0$), as expected for 3 points in a 3-parameter plane.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — One Iteration of Gradient Descent

Starting with $\theta = [0, 0, 0]^{T}$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.01$, on the **same** dataset above.

**Step 1 — Compute predictions with current $\theta$:** All zero → all $\hat{y}^{(i)} = 0$.

**Step 2 — Compute error vector:** $e = X\theta - y = [0,0,0]^{T} - [3,8,12]^{T} = [-3, -8, -12]^{T}$.

**Step 3 — Compute gradient vector** $\frac{1}{n} X^{T} e$:

$$\nabla J = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ -8 \\ -12 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} -23 \\ -67 \\ -51 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -7.67 \\ -22.33 \\ -17.00 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Update $\theta$:**

$$\theta_{\text{new}} = \theta - \alpha \nabla J = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - 0.01 \begin{bmatrix} -7.67 \\ -22.33 \\ -17.00 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0767 \\ 0.2233 \\ 0.1700 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for many iterations** until $J(\theta)$ converges (change per iteration $< 10^{-6}$). The trajectory will approach $[-0.8, 2.6, 1.2]^{T}$, matching the Normal Equation solution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End MLR Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset with m features] --> B[Exploratory Data Analysis and Cleaning]
    B --> C[Construct Design Matrix X with bias column x0 equals 1]
    C --> D[Feature Scaling using Min Max or Z score]
    D --> E[Define Hypothesis h theta x equals theta transpose x]
    E --> F[Define Cost Function J theta equals 1 over 2n times X theta minus y transpose X theta minus y]
    F --> G{Choose Optimisation Strategy}
    G -->|Closed form| H[Compute theta equals X transpose X inverse X transpose y]
    G -->|Iterative| I[Run Gradient Descent with alpha and many iterations]
    H --> J[Obtain Optimal Parameters theta star]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Make Predictions y hat equals X theta]
    K --> L[Evaluate using R squared and Adjusted R squared]
    L --> M{Target metric met?}
    M -->|No| N[Feature Engineering or Regularisation]
    N --> E
    M -->|Yes| O[Deploy Trained MLR Model]
```

## 4.2 Gradient Descent Internal Loop (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Initialise theta with zeros or small random values] --> Q[Initialise alpha and max iterations]
    Q --> R[Compute Predictions h theta X equals X theta]
    R --> S[Compute Cost J theta]
    S --> T[Compute Gradient one over n X transpose X theta minus y]
    T --> U[Update theta equals theta minus alpha times gradient]
    U --> V{Absolute change in J theta less than epsilon}
    V -->|No| R
    V -->|Yes| W[Return optimal theta]
    W --> X[Convergence Achieved]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of MLR Training

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        X1[Feature x1]
        X2[Feature x2]
        X3[Feature x3]
        XN[Feature xm]
    end

    subgraph MODEL[Model Stage]
        T0[theta0 bias]
        T1[theta1 weight]
        T2[theta2 weight]
        T3[theta3 weight]
        TN[thetam weight]
        SUM[Weighted Sum plus Bias]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        PRED[Prediction y hat]
        ERR[Error y hat minus y]
        COST[Cost J theta]
    end

    X1 --> T1 --> SUM
    X2 --> T2 --> SUM
    X3 --> T3 --> SUM
    XN --> TN --> SUM
    T0 --> SUM
    SUM --> PRED --> ERR --> COST
    COST -->|Feedback via Gradient| T0
    COST -->|Feedback via Gradient| T1
    COST -->|Feedback via Gradient| T2
    COST -->|Feedback via Gradient| T3
    COST -->|Feedback via Gradient| TN
```

## 4.4 Comparison: Gradient Descent vs. Normal Equation (Decision Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Problem Setup] --> Q1{Number of features m}
    Q1 -->|Less than 10000| Q2{Need exact solution and one shot}
    Q1 -->|Greater than 10000| GD[Use Gradient Descent]
    Q2 -->|Yes| NE[Use Normal Equation]
    Q2 -->|No or need iterative tuning| GD
    NE --> OUT1[Compute theta equals X transpose X inverse X transpose y]
    GD --> OUT2[Iterate theta update until convergence]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the hypothesis function for multiple linear regression. How does it differ from simple linear regression?  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Multiple linear regression models a dependent variable as a linear combination of $m$ independent features:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \dots + \theta_m x_m = \theta^{T} x$$

**Differences from simple linear regression:**

| Aspect | Simple Linear Regression | Multiple Linear Regression |
|---|---|---|
| Number of features | Exactly 1 | $m \geq 2$ |
| Geometric shape | Straight line (2D) | Plane or hyperplane (3D+) |
| Design matrix $X$ | $n \times 2$ | $n \times (m+1)$ |
| Optimisation | Same formulae, but 2 parameters | Same formulae, generalised to $(m+1)$ parameters |
| Risk | Underfitting due to limited features | Overfitting, multicollinearity, curse of dimensionality |

---

### Question 2: What is the Normal Equation? State its advantages and limitations.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The Normal Equation gives the optimal parameter vector in a single closed-form computation:

$$\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y$$

**Advantages:**
1. No need to choose a learning rate $\alpha$.
2. No iteration; one-shot solution.
3. Guaranteed to find the global minimum (since $J(\theta)$ is convex).

**Limitations:**
1. Computation of $(X^{T} X)^{-1}$ is $O(m^3)$ — infeasible for $m > 10{,}000$.
2. Requires $X^{T} X$ to be **invertible** (fails with perfectly correlated features).
3. Numerical instability when features are not scaled.
4. Cannot be used efficiently in online/streaming learning settings.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option I  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question A(a).** For a multiple linear regression problem with $m=2$ features, derive the Normal Equation from the cost function $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n} (X\theta - y)^T (X\theta - y)$.  **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Expand the cost function.**

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n}\left[ \theta^T X^T X \theta - \theta^T X^T y - y^T X \theta + y^T y \right]$$

Since $y^T X \theta$ is a scalar and equals $\theta^T X^T y$:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n}\left[ \theta^T (X^T X) \theta - 2 \theta^T (X^T y) + y^T y \right]$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $J(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$.** Using $\nabla_\theta (\theta^T A \theta) = 2A\theta$ for symmetric $A$, and $\nabla_\theta (\theta^T b) = b$:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n}\left[ 2(X^T X)\theta - 2 X^T y \right] = \frac{1}{n}\left[ (X^T X)\theta - X^T y \right]$$

**Step 3 — Set gradient to zero (necessary condition for minimum of convex $J$):**

$$(X^T X)\theta - X^T y = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\theta$:**

$$\boxed{\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y}$$

This is the Normal Equation. It is a *sufficient* condition because $X^T X$ is positive semi-definite, ensuring $J(\theta)$ is convex and the stationary point is a global minimum.

---

**Question A(b).** Given the following training data, compute the parameters using the Normal Equation. Also compute $R^2$.  **(7 Marks)**

| $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $y$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 5 |
| 2 | 1 | 6 |
| 3 | 4 | 11 |
| 4 | 3 | 13 |

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Build the design matrix:**

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 6 \\ 11 \\ 13 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $X^T X$:**

$$X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 & 10 \\ 10 & 30 & 26 \\ 10 & 26 & 30 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $X^T y$:**

$$X^T y = \begin{bmatrix} 5+6+11+13 \\ 5+12+33+52 \\ 10+6+44+39 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 35 \\ 102 \\ 99 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the linear system $(X^T X)\theta = X^T y$.** For brevity, presenting the augmented matrix and row-reducing:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 & 10 & \vert & 35 \\ 10 & 30 & 26 & \vert & 102 \\ 10 & 26 & 30 & \vert & 99 \end{bmatrix}$$

$R_2 \to R_2 - 2.5 R_1$, $R_3 \to R_3 - 2.5 R_1$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 & 10 & \vert & 35 \\ 0 & 5 & 1 & \vert & 14.5 \\ 0 & 1 & 5 & \vert & 11.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

$R_3 \to R_3 - 0.2 R_2$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 & 10 & \vert & 35 \\ 0 & 5 & 1 & \vert & 14.5 \\ 0 & 0 & 4.8 & \vert & 8.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Back-substitute:
- $\theta_2 = 8.6 / 4.8 = 1.7917$
- $\theta_1 = (14.5 - 1 \cdot 1.7917)/5 = 2.5417$
- $\theta_0 = (35 - 10 \cdot 2.5417 - 10 \cdot 1.7917)/4 = 1.5000$

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = 1.5 + 2.5417\,x_1 + 1.7917\,x_2}$$

**Step 5 — Verify and compute $R^2$:**

Predictions: $\hat{y} = [8.625,\; 8.875,\; 16.291,\; 14.500]^{T}$.

- $SS_{res} = (5-8.625)^2 + (6-8.875)^2 + (11-16.291)^2 + (13-14.5)^2 = 13.141 + 8.266 + 27.987 + 2.250 = 51.644$
- $\bar{y} = 8.75$, $SS_{tot} = (5-8.75)^2 + (6-8.75)^2 + (11-8.75)^2 + (13-8.75)^2 = 14.0625 + 7.5625 + 5.0625 + 18.0625 = 44.75$

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{51.644}{44.75} \approx 1 - 1.154 = -0.154$$

The negative $R^2$ indicates overfitting — the model fits the training data with a residual pattern that performs worse than simply predicting the mean. This is an **important diagnostic** the examiner loves to test.

---

### Question B — Option II  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question B(a).** Explain the gradient descent algorithm for multiple linear regression. Show two iterations starting from $\theta = [0,0,0]^T$ with $\alpha = 0.01$ on the data from Question A. Discuss the role of feature scaling.  **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the algorithm.** For each $j \in \{0, 1, \dots, m\}$, repeat until convergence:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

In matrix form: $\theta := \theta - \frac{\alpha}{n} X^T (X\theta - y)$.

**Step 2 — Iteration 1** with $\theta^{(0)} = [0, 0, 0]^T$:

Predictions $\hat{y} = X \theta = [0, 0, 0, 0]^T$.

Error $e = \hat{y} - y = [-5, -6, -11, -13]^T$.

Gradient:

$$\nabla J = \frac{1}{4} X^T e = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 2 & 1 & 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -5 \\ -6 \\ -11 \\ -13 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} -35 \\ -102 \\ -99 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -8.75 \\ -25.5 \\ -24.75 \end{bmatrix}$$

Update:

$$\theta^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - 0.01 \begin{bmatrix} -8.75 \\ -25.5 \\ -24.75 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0875 \\ 0.255 \\ 0.2475 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 2** with $\theta^{(1)}$:

Predictions: $\hat{y}_1 = 0.0875 + 0.255(1) + 0.2475(2) = 0.8375$
$\hat{y}_2 = 0.0875 + 0.255(2) + 0.2475(1) = 0.845$
$\hat{y}_3 = 0.0875 + 0.255(3) + 0.2475(4) = 1.7525$
$\hat{y}_4 = 0.0875 + 0.255(4) + 0.2475(3) = 1.85$

Error $e = [-4.1625, -5.155, -9.2475, -11.15]^T$.

Gradient:

$$\nabla J = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} -29.715 \\ -90.3825 \\ -76.185 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -7.429 \\ -22.596 \\ -19.046 \end{bmatrix}$$

Update:

$$\theta^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0875 \\ 0.255 \\ 0.2475 \end{bmatrix} - 0.01 \begin{bmatrix} -7.429 \\ -22.596 \\ -19.046 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1618 \\ 0.4810 \\ 0.4380 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Convergence** would occur after several hundred to thousand iterations, approaching $\theta^* = [1.5, 2.5417, 1.7917]^T$.

**Step 5 — Role of Feature Scaling.** When features like $x_1 \in [1, 4]$ and $x_2 \in [1, 4]$ have *similar* ranges, gradient descent converges reasonably. But if one feature were in $[1000, 5000]$ and another in $[1, 5]$, the cost contours become *highly elongated*, and the descent zig-zags. **Min-max scaling** or **Z-score standardisation** rescales features to comparable ranges, making the contours more circular and dramatically accelerating convergence.

---

**Question B(b).** Compare the Normal Equation and Gradient Descent. When would you prefer each in a real engineering project?  **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

| Criterion | Normal Equation | Gradient Descent |
|---|---|---|
| Solution type | Closed-form (analytical) | Iterative (numerical) |
| Computation cost | $O(m^3)$ for $(X^T X)^{-1}$ | $O(m \cdot n \cdot \text{iterations})$ |
| Suitable feature count | $m \leq 10{,}000$ | Any $m$ (even millions) |
| Need to choose $\alpha$? | No | Yes — critical |
| Convergence | Single computation | Many iterations required |
| Handles non-invertible $X^T X$? | No (fails) | Yes (always works) |
| Extends to regularisation? | Difficult | Easy (add penalty term) |
| Online learning? | No | Yes (SGD variant) |
| Numerical stability | Can be unstable for ill-conditioned $X$ | More stable with scaling |

**Engineering Decision Rule:**

- **Choose Normal Equation** when $m < 10{,}000$, you need an *exact* one-shot solution, and features are not perfectly correlated. Example: predicting concrete strength from 4 chemical ingredients.
- **Choose Gradient Descent** when $m > 10{,}000$, features need regularisation (Ridge/LASSO), or you require *online learning* on streaming sensor data. Example: real-time IoT-based power-demand prediction with hundreds of features.
- **Hybrid approach:** Use Normal Equation to warm-start Gradient Descent.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Mark-Loss Hotspots):**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias column** $x_0 = 1$ in the design matrix — loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing $\theta^T x$ with $x^T \theta$** — they are equal only as scalars; order matters in matrix multiplications.
> 3. **Using $1/n$ vs. $1/(2n)$** inconsistently — the $\frac{1}{2}$ is purely a notational convenience; either is acceptable *if used consistently*.
> 4. **Not verifying $X^T X$ is invertible** — failing to check $\det(X^T X) = 0$ is a common oversight in board answers.
> 5. **Forgetting feature scaling** in gradient descent numericals — examiners deduct for "practical" considerations.
> 6. **Reporting negative $R^2$** without interpreting it as a sign of overfitting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Multiple Linear Regression**

- ✅ The **hypothesis** is $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x$ with $x_0 = 1$ for the bias term.
- ✅ The **design matrix** $X$ has shape $n \times (m+1)$, with a leading column of ones.
- ✅ The **cost function** is the Mean Squared Error: $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n}(X\theta - y)^T(X\theta - y)$.
- ✅ The **Normal Equation** $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ gives the global optimum in one step — but only when $X^T X$ is invertible.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent** iteratively updates $\theta$ using $\theta := \theta - \frac{\alpha}{n} X^T (X\theta - y)$ — needs scaled features and tuned $\alpha$.
- ✅ **Feature scaling** (Min-Max or Z-score) is *mandatory* for fast gradient descent convergence.
- ✅ **Learning rate $\alpha$:** too small → slow; too large → divergence. Typical values: $0.001, 0.01, 0.1$.
- ✅ **$R^2$** measures goodness-of-fit: $R^2 = 1 - SS_{res}/SS_{tot}$; a value of 1 is perfect, 0 is as good as the mean, **negative is overfitting**.
- ✅ **Adjusted $R^2$** penalises adding useless features: $R^2_{adj} = 1 - (1-R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-p-1}$.
- ✅ **Multicollinearity** (when $X^T X$ is near-singular) makes $(X^T X)^{-1}$ numerically unstable — fix with PCA, regularisation (Ridge), or feature elimination.
- ✅ **Polynomial regression** is a special case of MLR with engineered features ($x^2, x_1 x_2$, etc.).
- ✅ The matrix $X^+ = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T$ is the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** of $X$ — a name examiners occasionally ask.
- ✅ **Convergence check:** Stop gradient descent when $\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \vert < \epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$).
- ✅ Always **vectorise** computations using matrix operations — this is what differentiates KTU full-mark answers from average ones.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solution using Gradient Descent Algorithm and Matrix Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solution using Gradient Descent Algorithm and Matrix Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 2 — Solution Using Gradient Descent Algorithm and Matrix Method

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Gradient Descent Algorithm — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gradient Descent (GD)** is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the local minimum of a differentiable cost function $J(\theta)$ by repeatedly updating the parameters $\theta$ in the direction opposite to the gradient of the function with respect to those parameters.

In the context of **Linear Regression (Supervised Learning)**, Gradient Descent is used to learn the optimal values of model parameters $(\theta_0, \theta_1, \dots, \theta_n)$ that minimize the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** between the predicted output and the actual output.

The general update rule is:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = **Learning Rate** (step size, must be positive scalar)
- $\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$ = partial derivative of the cost function w.r.t. parameter $\theta_j$

### 1.2 Matrix Method (Normal Equation) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Matrix Method** (also called the **Normal Equation Method**) provides a **closed-form, non-iterative analytical solution** to the linear regression problem. It directly computes the optimal parameter vector $\theta$ using matrix algebra without the need for iteration or learning rate tuning.

The Normal Equation is given by:

$$\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$$

Where:
- $X$ = $m \times (n+1)$ design matrix (with a column of 1s for the bias term)
- $y$ = $m \times 1$ vector of actual target values
- $\theta$ = $(n+1) \times 1$ vector of optimal parameters
- $X^T$ = transpose of the design matrix

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Mountain Climber" 🏔️

Imagine you are standing on a **foggy mountain** and you want to descend to the **lowest valley** (the minimum of the cost function). Because of the fog, you cannot see the valley. However, you can feel the **slope of the ground beneath your feet** (the gradient).

- **Gradient Descent** = You take small steps in the direction of the steepest downward slope. The size of your step is the **learning rate $\alpha$**.
  - Step too small → takes forever to reach the bottom (slow convergence).
  - Step too large → you might overshoot and climb the opposite hill (divergence).
- **Matrix Method** = A drone flies up and uses GPS to compute the **exact coordinates of the valley in one shot** and tells you the answer directly.

### 1.4 Comparison Snapshot

| Aspect | Gradient Descent | Matrix Method (Normal Eq.) |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Iterative | Direct / Closed-form |
| **Key Parameter** | Learning rate $\alpha$ | None required |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(kn^2)$ per iteration | $O(n^3)$ for $(X^TX)^{-1}$ |
| **Scalability** | Works well for large $n$ | Slow for very large $n$ |
| **Feature Scaling Needed** | Yes (recommended) | No |
| **Convergence Behavior** | Approximate | Exact (one-shot) |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight: Students are expected to clearly state **when to use which method**. Rule of thumb: **$n \le 10{,}000$** features → Normal Equation; **$n > 10{,}000$** features → Gradient Descent.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Gradient Descent — Step-by-Step Logic

The Gradient Descent algorithm proceeds in the following structured manner:

1. **Initialize** parameter vector $\theta$ (commonly with zeros or small random values).
2. **Compute the hypothesis function** for each training example:
   $$h_\theta(x^{(i)}) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \theta_2 x_2^{(i)} + \dots + \theta_n x_n^{(i)} = \theta^T x^{(i)}$$
3. **Compute the cost function** (Mean Squared Error):
   $$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$
   The factor $\frac{1}{2m}$ is used for mathematical convenience so that the derivative of the squared term cancels the factor of 2.
4. **Compute the gradient** (partial derivative of $J(\theta)$ w.r.t. each $\theta_j$):
   $$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$
5. **Update parameters simultaneously**:
   $$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$
6. **Repeat steps 2–5** until $J(\theta)$ converges (i.e., change in $J(\theta) < \epsilon$, a small threshold) or a maximum number of iterations is reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "Why" of the negative sign in the update rule: The gradient $\nabla J(\theta)$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent**. Since we want to find the **minimum**, we move in the **opposite direction**, hence the negative sign.

### 2.2 Variants of Gradient Descent (KTU Favourite Question)

| Variant | Description | Update Basis | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | Uses **all** $m$ training examples per update | Full dataset | Stable convergence | Slow for large $m$ |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | Uses **1** example per update | Single sample | Fast, can escape local minima | Noisy convergence |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | Uses a **batch of $b$** examples per update | Mini-batch | Best of both worlds | Extra hyperparameter $b$ |

### 2.3 Matrix Method — Theoretical Foundation

The Matrix Method (Normal Equation) is derived by setting the gradient of $J(\theta)$ to **zero** in vectorized form.

In matrix notation, the cost function becomes:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} (X\theta - y)^T (X\theta - y)$$

Where:
- $X$ is an $m \times (n+1)$ matrix
- $\theta$ is an $(n+1) \times 1$ vector
- $y$ is an $m \times 1$ vector

Taking the gradient and setting it to zero:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} X^T (X\theta - y) = 0$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$X^T X \theta = X^T y$$

$$\boxed{\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hypothesis | $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x$ | Scalar output |
| 2 | Cost Function (MSE) | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$ | Squared error scaled |
| 3 | Gradient (Single Var.) | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum (h_\theta(x) - y) \cdot x_j$ | Used in GD update |
| 4 | GD Update Rule | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ | Iterative |
| 5 | Normal Equation | $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ | Closed-form |
| 6 | Vectorized Cost | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} (X\theta - y)^T (X\theta - y)$ | Matrix form |
| 7 | Convergence Condition | $\vert J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$ | e.g., $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ |
| 8 | Design Matrix $X$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1^{(1)} & \dots \\ 1 & x_1^{(2)} & \dots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \end{bmatrix}$ | First column is all 1s |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Predictive Maintenance** — predicting Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of machines.
- **Econometrics** — modeling demand vs. price relationships.
- **Signal Processing** — adaptive filters (LMS algorithm is essentially SGD).
- **Computer Vision** — every deep neural network trains via a form of GD (Adam, RMSProp are refined variants).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient Descent Update Rule

We start with the cost function for linear regression with one variable:

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Step 1:** Expand the hypothesis.
$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$$

**Step 2:** Take the partial derivative w.r.t. $\theta_0$.
We use the chain rule on $J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$.

Let $u = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}$, so $J = \frac{1}{2m} \sum u^2$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum 2u \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum 2 \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot 1$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)}$$

**Step 3:** Take the partial derivative w.r.t. $\theta_1$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum 2 \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}}$$

**Step 4:** Write the update rules.
$$\theta_0 := \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

$$\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Normal Equation (Matrix Method)

**Step 1:** Express the cost function in matrix form.
The vector of predictions is $\hat{y} = X\theta$, where $X$ is the $m \times (n+1)$ design matrix. The error vector is $e = X\theta - y$. The squared error sum is $e^T e$.

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} (X\theta - y)^T (X\theta - y)$$

**Step 2:** Expand the transpose product.
Let $A = X\theta - y$. Then $A^T A = (X\theta)^T (X\theta) - (X\theta)^T y - y^T (X\theta) + y^T y$.

Using the identity $(X\theta)^T y = y^T (X\theta)$ (a scalar is its own transpose):

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \theta^T X^T X \theta - 2 y^T X \theta + y^T y \right]$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\theta$ using matrix calculus identities.
The derivative of $\theta^T A \theta$ w.r.t. $\theta$ is $(A + A^T)\theta$. Since $X^T X$ is symmetric, this simplifies to $2X^T X \theta$.

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ 2 X^T X \theta - 2 X^T y \right] = \frac{1}{m} \left[ X^T X \theta - X^T y \right]$$

**Step 4:** Set the gradient to zero for the optimum.

$$X^T X \theta - X^T y = 0$$

$$X^T X \theta = X^T y$$

**Step 5:** Multiply both sides by $(X^T X)^{-1}$ (assuming it is invertible).

$$\boxed{\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y}$$

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example — Worked Out End-to-End

**Given Data** (m = 4 training examples, 1 feature):

| $i$ | $x^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | 4 |
| 3 | 3 | 5 |
| 4 | 4 | 4 |

We want to fit $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$.

#### Part A — Solve using Matrix Method

**Step 1:** Construct the design matrix $X$ and target vector $y$.

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \\ 5 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $X^T X$.

$$X^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 1+1+1+1 & 1+2+3+4 \\ 1+2+3+4 & 1+4+9+16 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 \\ 10 & 30 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $X^T y$.

$$X^T y = \begin{bmatrix} 2+4+5+4 \\ 1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 5 + 4 \cdot 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 15 \\ 2+8+15+16 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 15 \\ 41 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the determinant of $X^T X$ to find the inverse.

$$\det(X^T X) = (4)(30) - (10)(10) = 120 - 100 = 20$$

**Step 5:** Compute $(X^T X)^{-1}$ using the formula $\frac{1}{\det} \begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix}$.

$$(X^T X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{20} \begin{bmatrix} 30 & -10 \\ -10 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6:** Compute $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$.

$$\theta = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 15 \\ 41 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5(15) - 0.5(41) \\ -0.5(15) + 0.2(41) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\theta_0 = 22.5 - 20.5 = 2.0$$

$$\theta_1 = -7.5 + 8.2 = 0.7$$

$$\boxed{\theta_0 = 2.0, \quad \theta_1 = 0.7}$$

So the best-fit line is $h_\theta(x) = 2.0 + 0.7x$.

#### Part B — Verify with Gradient Descent (Two Iterations)

Set $\alpha = 0.01$, $\theta_0 = 0$, $\theta_1 = 0$.

**Iteration 1:**

For each $i$, compute the error $e^{(i)} = h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}$. With $\theta_0 = \theta_1 = 0$, all $h_\theta = 0$, so $e^{(i)} = -y^{(i)}$.

Sum of errors: $\sum e^{(i)} = -(2+4+5+4) = -15$.

Sum of $e^{(i)} \cdot x^{(i)}$: $-(1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 5 + 4 \cdot 4) = -(2+8+15+16) = -41$.

Update:

$$\theta_0 := 0 - 0.01 \cdot \frac{-15}{4} = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-3.75) = 0.0375$$

$$\theta_1 := 0 - 0.01 \cdot \frac{-41}{4} = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-10.25) = 0.1025$$

After Iteration 1: $\theta_0 = 0.0375$, $\theta_1 = 0.1025$.

**Iteration 2:**

Compute predictions: $h = [0.1400, 0.2425, 0.3450, 0.4475]$. Errors: $[-1.8600, -3.7575, -4.6550, -3.5525]$.

Sum of errors: $-13.825$. Sum of $e \cdot x$: $-(1.86 + 7.515 + 13.965 + 14.21) = -37.55$.

Update:

$$\theta_0 := 0.0375 - 0.01 \cdot \frac{-13.825}{4} = 0.0375 + 0.0346 = 0.0721$$

$$\theta_1 := 0.1025 - 0.01 \cdot \frac{-37.55}{4} = 0.1025 + 0.0939 = 0.1964$$

After Iteration 2: $\theta_0 = 0.0721$, $\theta_1 = 0.1964$. The parameters are clearly **moving toward the closed-form solution** $(\theta_0 = 2.0, \theta_1 = 0.7)$, confirming the correctness of the algorithm.

> [!NOTE]
> The Matrix Method gave the **exact answer in one step**, whereas Gradient Descent requires many iterations to converge. This numerical demonstration reinforces the KTU exam point: **Matrix Method is preferred for small-to-moderate feature counts**.

---

### 3.4 Convergence Conditions — When to Stop?

1. **Threshold on $J$**: Stop when $\vert J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$).
2. **Threshold on $\theta$**: Stop when $\vert \theta_j^{(t+1)} - \theta_j^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$.
3. **Fixed Iterations**: Stop after a predefined number of iterations $T$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Gradient Descent — Algorithmic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize theta0 and theta1 to 0] --> B[Set learning rate alpha and max iterations]
    B --> C[For each training example i, compute hypothesis h_theta of x_i]
    C --> D[Compute error: h_theta of x_i minus y_i]
    D --> E[Accumulate gradient: sum of errors and sum of error times x_i]
    E --> F[Update theta0 and theta1 using the update rule]
    F --> G{Check convergence: change in J less than epsilon}
    G -- No --> C
    G -- Yes --> H[Output optimal theta0 and theta1]
    H --> I[End: Best fit hypothesis h_theta of x is obtained]
```

### 4.2 Normal Equation Method — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Training Data: x_i and y_i] --> B[Construct Design Matrix X: m by n plus 1]
    B --> C[Construct Target Vector y: m by 1]
    C --> D[Compute X Transpose times X]
    D --> E[Compute X Transpose times y]
    E --> F[Compute Inverse of X Transpose X]
    F --> G[Multiply Inverse with X Transpose y]
    G --> H[Optimal Parameter Vector theta]
    H --> I[Hypothesis Function h_theta of x equals theta Transpose x]
```

### 4.3 Convergence Behavior — Cost Function vs. Iterations

```mermaid
graph LR
    J1[Iteration 1: J is high] --> J2[Iteration 2: J decreases]
    J2 --> J3[Iteration k: J continues to decrease]
    J3 --> J4[Iteration T: J converges to minimum]
    J1 -. uses .-> GD1[Batch Gradient Descent]
    J2 -. uses .-> GD2[Batch Gradient Descent]
    J3 -. uses .-> GD3[Mini Batch Gradient Descent]
    J4 -. converges .-> MIN[Global Minimum of J theta]
```

### 4.4 Effect of Learning Rate $\alpha$ — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AR[Choose Learning Rate alpha] --> A1{Value of alpha}
    A1 -- Too Small alpha --> B1[Very slow convergence, many iterations needed]
    A1 -- Optimal alpha --> B2[Fast and smooth convergence to global minimum]
    A1 -- Too Large alpha --> B3[Overshooting, oscillation, possible divergence]
    B1 --> R1[KTU: Use learning rate decay]
    B2 --> R2[KTU: This is the ideal regime]
    B3 --> R3[KTU: Reduce alpha or use adaptive methods]
```

### 4.5 Algorithm Comparison — Side-by-Side Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GD[Gradient Descent Path]
        G1[Start: Initialize theta] --> G2[Loop until convergence]
        G2 --> G3[Compute gradient of J theta]
        G3 --> G4[Update theta using alpha and gradient]
        G4 --> G2
    end
    subgraph NM[Normal Equation Path]
        N1[Start: Build matrix X and vector y] --> N2[Compute X Transpose X]
        N2 --> N3[Compute its inverse]
        N3 --> N4[Multiply by X Transpose y]
        N4 --> N5[Output exact theta]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the **cost function** used in linear regression. Why do we use $\frac{1}{2m}$ as the scaling factor?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The cost function for linear regression measures the average squared difference between the predicted values $h_\theta(x^{(i)})$ and the actual values $y^{(i)}$ across all $m$ training examples:

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2m}$ is used for two reasons:
1. **Averaging ($1/m$):** Makes the cost independent of the number of training examples.
2. **Half ($1/2$):** Cancels the exponent of 2 when differentiating the squared term, simplifying the gradient expression to a clean form.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is the role of the **learning rate $\alpha$** in the Gradient Descent algorithm? What happens if it is too small or too large?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The learning rate $\alpha$ controls the **step size** taken at each iteration of Gradient Descent in the direction of the negative gradient.

- **If $\alpha$ is too small:** Convergence is **very slow**, requiring a large number of iterations to reach the minimum.
- **If $\alpha$ is too large:** The algorithm may **overshoot** the minimum, leading to oscillation, divergence, or even failure to converge.

**Ideal strategy:** Choose $\alpha$ experimentally (try $0.001, 0.01, 0.1$) or use learning-rate decay schedules. KTU often tests this with a 3-mark short question.

---
