### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Explain the **Gradient Descent Algorithm** for linear regression. Derive the update rules for parameters $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ for a univariate linear model $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Define the Hypothesis:**
$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Define the Cost Function:**
$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Derive Partial Derivatives:**
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Write the Update Rules:**
$$\theta_0 := \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

$$\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — State the Algorithm:**
Repeat steps 3–4 until $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ converges. The algorithm moves in the direction of steepest descent (negative gradient) scaled by $\alpha$.

---

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Using the **Matrix Method (Normal Equation)**, find the optimal parameters $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ for the following dataset. Use the formula $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$.

| $x$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 3 | 5 | 7 | 9 |

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Construct Matrices:**
$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 5 \\ 7 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Compute $X^T X$:**
$$X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 \\ 10 & 30 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark) — Compute $X^T y$:**
$$X^T y = \begin{bmatrix} 3+5+7+9 \\ 3+10+21+36 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 24 \\ 70 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Compute the Inverse:**
$$\det = (4)(30) - (10)(10) = 120 - 100 = 20$$

$$(X^T X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{20} \begin{bmatrix} 30 & -10 \\ -10 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks) — Compute $\theta$:**
$$\theta = \frac{1}{20} \begin{bmatrix} 30 & -10 \\ -10 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 24 \\ 70 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{20} \begin{bmatrix} 720 - 700 \\ -240 + 280 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{20} \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 40 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_0 = 1, \quad \theta_1 = 2}$$

The best-fit line is $h_\theta(x) = 1 + 2x$. Verification: predictions are $[3, 5, 7, 9]$ which match the data exactly.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** State and derive the **Normal Equation** for multivariate linear regression using matrix calculus. Clearly define the design matrix $X$, target vector $y$, and the parameter vector $\theta$.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — State the Normal Equation:**
$$\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Define the Matrices:**
- $X$ is the $m \times (n+1)$ design matrix where the first column is all 1s (for $\theta_0$).
- $y$ is the $m \times 1$ target vector.
- $\theta$ is the $(n+1) \times 1$ parameter vector.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Derive from Cost Function:**
The vectorized cost is $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}(X\theta - y)^T (X\theta - y)$. Expanding:
$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \theta^T X^T X \theta - 2 y^T X \theta + y^T y \right]$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Differentiate and Solve:**
$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m}(X^T X \theta - X^T y) = 0$$

$$X^T X \theta = X^T y \implies \theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$$

---

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Compare **Gradient Descent** and the **Matrix Method** for solving linear regression. Mention at least five points of comparison and state when each method is preferred.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

| S.No. | Comparison Point | Gradient Descent | Matrix Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Type of Solution** | Iterative / Approximate | Closed-form / Exact |
| 2 | **Learning Rate $\alpha$** | Required, must be tuned | Not required |
| 3 | **Computational Complexity** | $O(kn^2)$ per iteration | $O(n^3)$ for matrix inverse |
| 4 | **Scalability with Features $n$** | Scales well, works for large $n$ | Becomes slow when $n > 10{,}000$ |
| 5 | **Feature Scaling** | Required (e.g., normalization) | Not required |
| 6 | **Multiple Minima Risk** | Possible with non-convex problems | Convex problem → unique solution |
| 7 | **Preferred When** | $n$ is large, $m$ is large | $n$ is small ($n \le 10{,}000$) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the GD update rule: KTU examiners deduct 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Not using the $1/m$ factor** in the gradient computation: If you write $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \sum (h_\theta(x) - y) \cdot x$ without $1/m$, you lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Inverting $X^T X$ without checking non-singularity**: The matrix $X^T X$ is non-invertible if features are linearly dependent or if $m < n$. Mention this for a bonus mark.
> 4. **Confusing Normal Equation with gradient $= 0$** setup: The Normal Equation is **$X^T X \theta = X^T y$**, NOT $\nabla J = 0$ in scalar form.
> 5. **Computational error in matrix multiplication**: Most marks lost in 14-mark numericals are due to arithmetic mistakes in $(X^TX)^{-1}$. Practice $2 \times 2$ inverses thoroughly.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Gradient Descent is an **iterative, first-order** optimization algorithm that minimizes the cost function $J(\theta)$ by updating parameters in the direction of the **negative gradient**.
- ✅ The **update rule** is $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$, where $\alpha$ is the learning rate.
- ✅ The **cost function** for linear regression is the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**: $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (h_\theta(x) - y)^2$.
- ✅ The **Matrix Method (Normal Equation)** provides a **closed-form solution**: $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$.
- ✅ Normal Equation is preferred when $n$ (number of features) is small ($\le 10{,}000$); Gradient Descent is preferred for large $n$.
- ✅ **Variants of GD**: Batch GD (uses all data), Stochastic GD (uses 1 sample), Mini-Batch GD (uses $b$ samples).
- ✅ **Convergence criteria**: $\vert J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$, where $\epsilon$ is a small positive number (e.g., $10^{-6}$).
- ✅ **Learning rate $\alpha$** must be chosen carefully — too small = slow, too large = divergence.
- ✅ **Design matrix $X$** must have a **column of 1s** as the first column to account for the bias term $\theta_0$.
- ✅ The matrix $X^T X$ must be **non-singular (invertible)** for the Normal Equation to work.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Idea of Overfitting in Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Idea of Overfitting in Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of supervised learning and regression analysis, **overfitting** is formally defined as a modeling phenomenon in which a learned regression function $f_{\theta}(x)$ captures not only the underlying deterministic signal $f(x)$ present in the training data, but also the stochastic noise $\epsilon$ and idiosyncratic fluctuations specific to that particular training sample. Mathematically, an overfitted model satisfies:

$$f_{\theta}(x) \approx f(x) + \hat{\epsilon}$$

where $\hat{\epsilon}$ denotes the noise component spuriously absorbed into the learned parameters $\theta$. This results in a model exhibiting **negligible training error** but **substantially elevated generalization (test) error**, thereby violating the core principle of statistical learning theory that a model must perform consistently on unseen data drawn from the same underlying distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme – Module 2):**
> Overfitting is a foundational concept that bridges regression theory with practical model selection. KTU examiners frequently test this topic together with the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** and **Regularization Techniques**. Mastering this concept is essential for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

> [!NOTE]
> **Companion Concepts (Frequently Tested Together):**
> - **Underfitting** – High bias, low variance, poor performance on both training and test sets.
> - **Just-Right Fit** – Optimal balance between bias and variance.
> - **Overfitting** – Low bias, high variance, excellent training accuracy but poor test accuracy.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

**Analogy: The Rote-Memorizing Student vs. The Understanding Student**

Imagine two students preparing for a Kerala State Board mathematics examination:

| Student Type | Study Strategy | Exam Outcome |
|--------------|----------------|--------------|
| **Understanding Student** (Well-Fit Model) | Learns the underlying theorems, derivations, and core principles. | Solves both practiced and novel problems correctly. |
| **Rote-Memorizing Student** (Overfitted Model) | Memorizes every single solved example verbatim from a single textbook. | Scores 100% on those exact problems, but fails when question numbers or numerical values change. |

The rote-memorizing student behaves identically to an overfitted regression model. It has memorized the *training data* (including its noise) rather than learning the *true underlying function*. When presented with new, unseen data points (the actual exam), its predictions deviate significantly from the true values.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** In real-world engineering applications — such as predicting concrete compressive strength, modeling bridge stress-strain behavior, or forecasting energy consumption — an overfitted model would give near-perfect predictions for historical data but dangerously inaccurate predictions for new structural designs or operational scenarios.

### 1.3 Why Overfitting Occurs — The Three Root Causes

1. **Excessive Model Complexity:** The number of parameters $\vert\theta\vert$ exceeds what is necessary to capture the underlying signal. Example: Fitting a degree-15 polynomial to data generated from a cubic relationship.

2. **Insufficient Training Data:** The sample size $N$ is too small relative to the model complexity. The ratio $\frac{N}{\vert\theta\vert}$ falls below a critical threshold.

3. **Presence of Noisy or Irrelevant Features:** Including noisy, redundant, or irrelevant input variables causes the model to fit spurious patterns that do not generalize.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Training Error (Empirical Risk):** $E_{\text{train}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_i - \hat{f}(x_i)\right)^2$
- **Test (Generalization) Error:** $E_{\text{test}} = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim P_{\text{data}}}\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^2\right]$
- **Model Complexity:** Typically quantified as $\vert\theta\vert$ (number of learnable parameters) or the degree of the polynomial in polynomial regression.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bias-Variance Decomposition — The Mathematical Heart of Overfitting

The expected prediction error of any learned regression model $\hat{f}(x)$ at a fixed input point $x$ can be rigorously decomposed into three irreducible components. This decomposition is the cornerstone analytical tool for understanding overfitting.

$$E\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^2\right] = \underbrace{\left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2}_{\text{Squared Bias}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Error}}$$

Where each term represents a distinct source of error:

- **Squared Bias ($\text{Bias}^2$):** Measures how far the *average prediction* of the model deviates from the true function. High bias indicates the model is consistently missing the target (systematic error). Associated with **underfitting**.

- **Variance ($\text{Var}$):** Measures how much the model's predictions *fluctuate* when trained on different datasets drawn from the same distribution. High variance indicates excessive sensitivity to the specific training sample. Associated with **overfitting**.

- **Irreducible Error ($\sigma^2$):** The inherent noise in the data generation process $y = f(x) + \epsilon$, where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. This component cannot be eliminated by any model.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Bias-Variance Relationship

Let $\hat{f}(x)$ denote the prediction of the learned model. The expected squared error at a single test point decomposes as follows:

1. **Total Error** = Expected squared deviation between the true label $y$ and the prediction $\hat{f}(x)$.
2. **Decomposition Strategy:** Introduce the expected prediction $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$ as a pivot point via the algebraic identity: $y - \hat{f}(x) = \left(y - f(x)\right) + \left(f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\right) + \left(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x)\right)$.
3. **Apply the Squared Expectation Identity:** Expand the square and exploit the fact that the noise term $(y - f(x))$ is statistically independent of the model prediction $\hat{f}(x)$.
4. **Cross-Term Elimination:** All cross-product terms vanish under expectation due to independence.
5. **Result:** The clean three-way decomposition emerges.

### 2.3 How Model Complexity Drives the Tradeoff

| Regime | Model Complexity | Bias | Variance | Total Error | Symptom |
|--------|------------------|------|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Underfitting** | Too Low (e.g., linear fit on cubic data) | **High** | Low | High | Poor on both train & test |
| **Good Fit** | Optimal | Moderate | Moderate | **Minimum** | Balanced performance |
| **Overfitting** | Too High (e.g., degree-15 poly on cubic data) | **Low** | **High** | High | Excellent on train, poor on test |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:** As model complexity increases, bias monotonically *decreases* (the model becomes more flexible and can capture finer patterns), while variance monotonically *increases* (the model becomes more sensitive to the specific training sample). The total expected error exhibits a characteristic **U-shape** with a clear minimum at the optimal complexity level.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **True Data Generating Process** | $y = f(x) + \epsilon$, where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ | Nature's underlying rule plus stochastic measurement noise |
| 2 | **Bias of Estimator** | $\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)] = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)$ | Systematic deviation from the true function |
| 3 | **Variance of Estimator** | $\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] = \mathbb{E}\left[\left(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2\right]$ | Sensitivity of predictions to training data variation |
| 4 | **Bias-Variance-Noise Decomposition** | $\mathbb{E}\left[\left(y-\hat{f}(x)\right)^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Fundamental identity governing generalization error |
| 5 | **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Standard training/test error metric |
| 6 | **Total Expected Prediction Error** | $E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{train}} + \text{Penalty}(\text{Complexity})$ | Captures the tradeoff via structural risk minimization |
| 7 | **Polynomial Regression Hypothesis** | $\hat{f}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x + \theta_2 x^2 + \cdots + \theta_d x^d$ | Increasing degree $d$ increases complexity |
| 8 | **Rule of Thumb Complexity Bound** | $\vert\theta\vert \ll N$ | Number of parameters should be far less than training samples |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The understanding of overfitting is **mission-critical** in numerous engineering domains practiced in Kerala and globally:

- **Civil Engineering:** Structural load prediction models for bridge design must generalize to new loading configurations, not just memorized historical events.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Power consumption forecasting models for Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) grid management must predict future loads accurately.
- **Computer Science (AI/ML):** Neural network training for image classification (e.g., medical diagnosis from X-rays) demands robust generalization to new patient scans.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Predictive models for disease progression must work on new patients, not just the training cohort.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Consequence:** Deploying an overfitted model in a production engineering system can lead to catastrophic failures — from incorrect structural safety margins to misdiagnosed medical conditions to unstable power grids.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Bias-Variance-Noise Decomposition

This derivation is a **high-priority KTU examination topic**. Students are expected to reproduce it step-by-step.

**Setup:** Assume the true relationship is $y = f(x) + \epsilon$, where $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\epsilon) = \sigma^2$. The training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_N, y_N)\}$ is drawn i.i.d. from the joint distribution $P(X, Y)$. The learned model $\hat{f}(x) = \hat{f}(x; \mathcal{D})$ depends on the training data.

**Objective:** Derive a closed-form expression for $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}, \epsilon}\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^2\right]$.

---

**Step 1: Add and subtract the expected prediction.**

To isolate bias and variance, we introduce the pivot term $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)]$, which we denote compactly as $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]$.

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f})^2\right] = \mathbb{E}\left[\left(\left(y - f\right) + \left(f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]\right) + \left(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f}\right)\right)^2\right]$$

**Explanation:** We decompose the total deviation into three orthogonal pieces: (a) the irreducible noise $(y - f)$, (b) the systematic bias $(f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])$, and (c) the model variance $(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f})$.

---

**Step 2: Expand the trinomial square.**

Let $A = (y - f)$, $B = (f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])$, and $C = (\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f})$. Then:

$$\mathbb{E}[(A + B + C)^2] = \mathbb{E}[A^2] + \mathbb{E}[B^2] + \mathbb{E}[C^2] + 2\mathbb{E}[AB] + 2\mathbb{E}[AC] + 2\mathbb{E}[BC]$$

**Explanation:** Standard algebraic expansion of $(A+B+C)^2$ yields three squared terms and three cross-product terms.

---

**Step 3: Evaluate $\mathbb{E}[A^2]$.**

Since $A = y - f = \epsilon$ and $\epsilon$ has zero mean and variance $\sigma^2$:

$$\mathbb{E}[A^2] = \mathbb{E}[\epsilon^2] = \text{Var}(\epsilon) + (\mathbb{E}[\epsilon])^2 = \sigma^2 + 0 = \sigma^2$$

**Explanation:** This term represents the **irreducible noise error** — it cannot be reduced by any model.

---

**Step 4: Evaluate $\mathbb{E}[B^2]$.**

Since $B = f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]$ is a constant with respect to the expectation over $\mathcal{D}$ and $\epsilon$:

$$\mathbb{E}[B^2] = (f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])^2 = \left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}]\right)^2$$

**Explanation:** This is the **squared bias** — measuring systematic deviation of the average prediction from the truth.

---

**Step 5: Evaluate $\mathbb{E}[C^2]$.**

Since $C = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f}$ depends only on the training set $\mathcal{D}$:

$$\mathbb{E}[C^2] = \mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{f} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])^2\right] = \text{Var}(\hat{f})$$

**Explanation:** This is the **variance** — measuring the fluctuation of model predictions across different training sets.

---

**Step 6: Show that all cross-terms vanish.**

**Cross-term $2\mathbb{E}[AB]$:** Since $A = \epsilon$ has zero mean and is independent of everything:

$$2\mathbb{E}[AB] = 2\mathbb{E}[\epsilon \cdot (f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])] = 2(f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])\mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0$$

**Cross-term $2\mathbb{E}[AC]$:** Since $\epsilon$ is independent of the model $\hat{f}$ (which depends only on training inputs/labels):

$$2\mathbb{E}[AC] = 2\mathbb{E}[\epsilon \cdot (\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f})] = 0$$

**Cross-term $2\mathbb{E}[BC]$:** Since $B$ is a constant with respect to $\mathcal{D}$:

$$2\mathbb{E}[BC] = 2(f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]) \cdot \mathbb{E}[(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f})] = 2(f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]) \cdot 0 = 0$$

(The inner expectation is zero by the definition of $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]$.)

---

**Step 7: Combine all surviving terms.**

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^2\right] = \underbrace{\left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2}_{\text{Systematic Error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{Sensitivity to Data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}}$$

**Final Interpretation:**

- As model complexity increases: $\text{Bias}^2 \downarrow$ and $\text{Var} \uparrow$.
- As model complexity decreases: $\text{Bias}^2 \uparrow$ and $\text{Var} \downarrow$.
- The optimal model complexity minimizes the **sum** $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var}$, yielding the minimum achievable generalization error (excluding $\sigma^2$, which is unalterable).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Regression Model Behavior

The following table summarizes the three regression regimes using a canonical example: fitting a polynomial $y = \sin(2\pi x) + \epsilon$ with sample size $N = 30$.

| Polynomial Degree $d$ | Training MSE | Test MSE | Bias | Variance | Regime | Visual Appearance |
|------------------------|--------------|----------|------|----------|--------|---------------------|
| $d = 1$ (Linear) | 0.85 | 0.92 | **High** | Low | **Underfit** | Straight line ignoring curve |
| $d = 3$ (Cubic) | 0.05 | 0.08 | Low | Low | **Good Fit** | Smooth curve through data |
| $d = 15$ (High-Order) | 0.001 | 0.95 | **Very Low** | **Very High** | **Overfit** | Wild oscillations through every point |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When a KTU question provides a plot showing training error continuing to decrease while test error begins to increase, the inflection point of the test error curve marks the onset of overfitting. This is a frequently tested visual interpretation skill.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

Define **overfitting** in the context of regression. List any two circumstances under which a regression model is likely to overfit the training data.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Overfitting is a phenomenon in supervised learning where a regression model learns the training data too closely — including its random noise and idiosyncratic fluctuations — such that it fails to generalize effectively to new, unseen test data. The model exhibits very low training error but significantly high test error.

**Two Circumstances Leading to Overfitting:**

1. **Excessively Complex Model:** When the model has too many parameters relative to the amount of training data (e.g., fitting a degree-20 polynomial to 30 data points). The model becomes flexible enough to memorize individual training samples rather than capturing the underlying trend.

2. **Insufficient or Noisy Training Data:** When the training dataset is too small or contains substantial noise, the model may interpret random fluctuations as genuine patterns, embedding these into its parameters.

*(Other valid circumstances: inclusion of irrelevant features, training for too many iterations without validation, absence of regularization.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

Differentiate between **bias** and **variance** in a regression model. How does each behave as model complexity increases?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bias | Variance |
|--------|------|----------|
| **Definition** | The error introduced by approximating a real-world problem with a simplified model; mathematically $\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)] = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)$ | The amount by which the model's predictions would change if a different training dataset were used; $\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] = \mathbb{E}[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2]$ |
| **As Complexity Increases** | **Decreases** — model becomes more flexible and can fit finer patterns | **Increases** — model becomes more sensitive to the specific training sample |
| **Associated Problem** | Underfitting | Overfitting |
| **Detection** | High training error AND high test error | Low training error BUT high test error |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and provide internal choice. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as independent alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the concepts of **underfitting**, **good fit**, and **overfitting** in polynomial regression. Discuss the role of model complexity in each case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** State and prove the **Bias-Variance Decomposition** of the expected squared prediction error in regression. Clearly identify each component of the decomposition. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Polynomial Regression Hypothesis:** The general form is

$$\hat{f}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x + \theta_2 x^2 + \cdots + \theta_d x^d$$

where $d$ denotes the polynomial degree and controls model complexity.

**Three Regimes:**

1. **Underfitting (Low $d$, e.g., $d=1$):** The model is too simple to capture the underlying curvature. A straight line is fitted to data that follows a curved pattern. Both training error and test error are high. The model suffers from **high bias**.

2. **Good Fit (Optimal $d$, e.g., $d=3$ for cubic data):** The model complexity matches the true complexity of the underlying function. Training error is low, and test error is at its minimum. The **bias-variance tradeoff is balanced**.

3. **Overfitting (High $d$, e.g., $d=15$):** The model is excessively flexible and passes through every training point, capturing noise as if it were signal. Training error becomes nearly zero, but test error is very high due to **high variance**.

**Role of Model Complexity:**

| Complexity | Training Error | Test Error | Diagnosis |
|-----------|----------------|------------|-----------|
| Low | High | High | Underfit |
| Optimal | Low | **Minimum** | Good Fit |
| High | Very Low | High | Overfit |

A well-labeled sketch would show three subplots side by side, each displaying the same data points (following a sinusoidal pattern) with regression curves of increasing complexity.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Statement:** For any learned regression model $\hat{f}(x)$ trained on data generated from $y = f(x) + \epsilon$, the expected squared prediction error at a test point $x$ decomposes as:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^2\right] = \left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2 + \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] + \sigma^2$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Introduce the pivot $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$ and decompose:

$$y - \hat{f}(x) = \underbrace{(y - f(x))}_{\text{Noise } \epsilon} + \underbrace{(f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])}_{\text{Bias Term } B} + \underbrace{(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x))}_{\text{Variance Term } C}$$

**Step 2:** Square both sides and apply expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}[(y-\hat{f})^2] = \mathbb{E}[(y-f)^2] + \mathbb{E}[B^2] + \mathbb{E}[C^2] + 2\mathbb{E}[(y-f)B] + 2\mathbb{E}[(y-f)C] + 2\mathbb{E}[BC]$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each term:

$$\mathbb{E}[(y-f)^2] = \mathbb{E}[\epsilon^2] = \sigma^2 \quad \text{(irreducible error)}$$

$$\mathbb{E}[B^2] = (f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2 = \text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)]$$

$$\mathbb{E}[C^2] = \mathbb{E}[(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f})^2] = \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]$$

**Step 4:** Show cross-terms vanish. Since $\epsilon$ is independent of $\hat{f}$ and has zero mean:

$$2\mathbb{E}[\epsilon \cdot B] = 0, \quad 2\mathbb{E}[\epsilon \cdot C] = 0$$

Since $B$ is constant w.r.t. $\mathcal{D}$ and $\mathbb{E}[C] = 0$:

$$2\mathbb{E}[B \cdot C] = 0$$

**Step 5:** Combine:

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)] + \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] + \sigma^2}$$

**Identification of Components:**

- **Squared Bias:** Measures systematic error — how far the average prediction is from the truth.
- **Variance:** Measures instability — how much predictions fluctuate across different training sets.
- **Irreducible Error ($\sigma^2$):** Inherent noise in the data; cannot be reduced by any model.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** with a suitable graph. Discuss how it guides the selection of an optimal regression model. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A data scientist fits polynomial regression models of degrees $d = 1, 2, 3, 5, 10$ to a dataset of $N = 50$ points generated from $y = \sin(x) + \epsilon$, where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.3^2)$. The following table records the observed errors:

| Degree $d$ | Training MSE | Test MSE |
|-----------|--------------|----------|
| 1 | 0.82 | 0.91 |
| 2 | 0.45 | 0.52 |
| 3 | 0.18 | 0.22 |
| 5 | 0.08 | 0.31 |
| 10 | 0.02 | 1.45 |

Identify which models are **underfitted**, **well-fitted**, and **overfitted**. Justify each classification. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** is a central principle in statistical learning that states: as model complexity increases, bias decreases while variance increases. The goal is to find the complexity level that minimizes the *sum* of these two components (excluding the irreducible error $\sigma^2$).

**Graphical Description:** A plot with model complexity on the X-axis and error magnitude on the Y-axis shows three curves:

1. **Squared Bias Curve:** Monotonically decreasing — high at low complexity, asymptoting toward zero at high complexity.
2. **Variance Curve:** Monotonically increasing — near zero at low complexity, rising sharply at high complexity.
3. **Total Error Curve (Bias² + Variance + $\sigma^2$):** Exhibits a characteristic **U-shape** with a clear minimum at the optimal complexity.
4. **Training Error Curve:** Monotonically decreasing, approaching zero.
5. **Test Error Curve:** U-shaped — mirrors the total error curve.

**Guidance for Model Selection:**

- **Region 1 (Low Complexity):** High total error dominated by bias. Choose a more complex model.
- **Region 2 (Optimal Complexity):** Minimum total error. **Select this model.**
- **Region 3 (High Complexity):** High total error dominated by variance. Choose a simpler model or apply regularization.

The optimal model is identified through techniques such as **k-fold cross-validation**, where the model achieving the lowest validation error is selected.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Classification and Justification:**

| Degree $d$ | Training MSE | Test MSE | Classification | Justification |
|-----------|--------------|----------|----------------|---------------|
| **1** | 0.82 | 0.91 | **Underfitted** | Both training AND test errors are high. The linear model cannot capture the sinusoidal non-linearity. Gap between train and test is small (0.09), indicating **high bias**. |
| **2** | 0.45 | 0.52 | **Underfitted (mild)** | Errors are lower than $d=1$ but still substantially above zero. The quadratic model still lacks flexibility for the sinusoidal pattern. |
| **3** | 0.18 | 0.22 | **Well-Fitted** | Training and test errors are both low and close to each other. The cubic polynomial adequately approximates the sine function in this range. Gap (0.04) is small — **balanced bias-variance**. |
| **5** | 0.08 | 0.31 | **Slightly Overfitted** | Training error drops further (0.08), but test error (0.31) is noticeably larger than train error. The gap of 0.23 indicates the model is starting to fit noise. |
| **10** | 0.02 | 1.45 | **Severely Overfitted** | Training error is near-zero (0.02), but test error has exploded to 1.45 — a gap of 1.43. The high-degree polynomial fits every training point, including noise. This is the classic signature of **high variance / overfitting**. |

**Conclusion:** The optimal model is the **cubic ($d=3$) polynomial**, which achieves the lowest test MSE (0.22). Models beyond $d=3$ exhibit progressively worsening generalization despite lower training error.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing training error with generalization error:** Many students erroneously assume "lower training error = better model." This is the *defining mistake* about overfitting. Always evaluate on held-out test data.
> 2. **Omitting the irreducible error $\sigma^2$:** In the bias-variance decomposition proof, students frequently forget the $\sigma^2$ term or incorrectly merge it with bias/variance.
> 3. **Missing cross-term justifications:** When proving the decomposition, examiners allocate marks for explicitly showing that each cross-term equals zero. Skipping these steps costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Misinterpreting the U-curve:** Students often confuse the *training error curve* (monotonically decreasing) with the *test error curve* (U-shaped). Clearly distinguish between them.
> 5. **Forgetting to label the "good fit" region:** A common error is recognizing only underfitting and overfitting. The optimal intermediate region is equally important and frequently carries dedicated marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Overfitting in Regression:**

- ☐ **Definition:** Overfitting = model fits training data (including noise) too closely, failing to generalize.
- ☐ **Core Signature:** Low training error + High test error = Overfitting.
- ☐ **Bias-Variance Identity:** $\text{Expected Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$.
- ☐ **Bias** decreases with complexity; **Variance** increases with complexity.
- ☐ **Total error** has a U-shape with a unique minimum at optimal complexity.
- ☐ **Three Causes:** Excessive complexity, insufficient data, noisy/irrelevant features.
- ☐ **Three Solutions:** Cross-validation, regularization (L1/L2), collecting more training data.
- ☐ **Polynomial Degree Analogy:** $d=1$ → underfit; $d=3$ (for cubic data) → good fit; $d=15$ → overfit.
- ☐ **Irreducible Error $\sigma^2$:** Cannot be eliminated; represents inherent data noise.
- ☐ **KTU Focus:** Be prepared to (1) state the decomposition, (2) prove it step-by-step, and (3) interpret diagnostic plots showing train vs. test error curves.
- ☐ **Practical Heuristic:** Number of parameters $\vert\theta\vert$ should be substantially less than training set size $N$.
- ☐ **Visual Cue on Plots:** The divergence point between training and test error curves marks the onset of overfitting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Methods for Classification - Logistic Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Linear Methods for Classification - Logistic Regression # SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Logistic Regression** is a **supervised learning classification algorithm** used to predict the probability that an observation belongs to one of two (or more) discrete classes. Despite the name "regression," it is fundamentally a **classification method** because it estimates a categorical (discrete) outcome. Mathematically, it models the **posterior probability** $P(Y=1 \mid X)$ using a linear combination of input features passed through the **logistic (sigmoid) function**: $$P(Y=1 \mid X) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad \text{where } z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_p x_p$$ The predicted class label is assigned by thresholding this probability at a decision boundary (default **0.5**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Logistic Regression belongs to the family of **Generalized Linear Models (GLM)**. It assumes the response variable follows a **Bernoulli distribution** and uses a **logit link function** to map linear predictor outputs $(-\infty, +\infty)$ into valid probabilities $(0, 1)$. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Medical Diagnosis Test":** > Imagine a doctor trying to decide whether a patient has a disease (Yes/No) based on test readings like blood pressure, cholesterol, and age. Instead of giving a hard "Yes" or "No" directly, the doctor estimates a **probability** — say, "there is an 87% chance this patient is positive." Logistic Regression works exactly like this calibrated probabilistic judgment. > > - It takes in many **symptoms (features)**. > - Combines them with **importance weights (coefficients)**. > - Squeezes the resulting score into a probability between **0 and 1** using the **sigmoid curve**. > - If probability $\geq 0.5$, predict **Class 1**; otherwise predict **Class 0**. **Why not use Linear Regression for classification?** - Linear regression outputs unbounded real numbers $(\dots -5, 12, 1000 \dots)$ which are **not valid probabilities**. - It is heavily affected by **outliers**. - It produces nonsensical predictions outside $[0, 1]$. Logistic Regression elegantly **squashes** all real-valued outputs into the bounded interval $[0, 1]$, making it the natural choice for binary classification. --- ## 1.3 Why Logistic Regression Matters (KTU Relevance) | Reason | Engineering Significance | |---|---| | **Probabilistic Output** | Provides confidence levels, not just hard predictions | | **Interpretability** | Coefficients directly indicate feature influence | | **Computationally Efficient** | Fast training even on large datasets | | **Industry Standard** | Used in spam filtering, credit scoring, medical diagnosis, churn prediction | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory KTU Note:** Logistic Regression is a **discriminative classifier** (models $P(Y \mid X)$ directly), in contrast to generative classifiers like Naïve Bayes (which model $P(X \mid Y)$ and $P(Y)$). This distinction is frequently tested in KTU Module 2 exams. --- ## 1.4 The Three Building Blocks (Conceptual Map) 1. **Linear Score Function:** Computes $z = \mathbf{\beta}^T \mathbf{x}$ (a real number). 2. **Sigmoid Activation:** Converts $z$ into probability $\sigma(z) \in (0,1)$. 3. **Thresholding Rule:** Assigns class label using $\hat{y} = \mathbb{1}[\sigma(z) \geq 0.5]$. # SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Logistic (Sigmoid) Function — Foundation The sigmoid function $\sigma: \mathbb{R} \to (0, 1)$ is defined as: $$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$ ### Key Properties (Must-Memorize for KTU) - **Range:** $(0, 1)$ — strictly bounded, never reaches 0 or 1. - **Monotonic:** Strictly increasing function. - **Symmetry:** $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$. - **Sigmoidal Shape:** S-curve, symmetric about origin. - **Derivative (Self-referential property):** $$\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This beautiful derivative identity $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ is the **mathematical reason** logistic regression has clean, closed-form gradient updates — making it computationally elegant. --- ## 2.2 The Logit (Log-Odds) Transformation For a probability $p$, the **odds** are defined as $\frac{p}{1-p}$, and the **log-odds** (logit) as: $$\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$$ The logit function maps $(0, 1) \to (-\infty, +\infty)$. Setting logit$(p)$ equal to a linear function gives the **Logistic Regression Model**: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{P(Y=1 \mid X)}{1 - P(Y=1 \mid X)}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_p x_p$$ Exponentiating both sides yields the **odds ratio interpretation**: $$\frac{P(Y=1 \mid X)}{1 - P(Y=1 \mid X)} = e^{\beta_0} \cdot e^{\beta_1 x_1} \cdots e^{\beta_p x_p}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Coefficient Interpretation:** A one-unit increase in $x_j$ multiplies the odds of $Y=1$ by a factor of $e^{\beta_j}$, holding all other variables constant. This is a high-yield KTU exam point. --- ## 2.3 Decision Boundary The decision boundary is the locus where $P(Y=1 \mid X) = 0.5$, i.e., where $\sigma(z) = 0.5$, which occurs when $z = 0$. $$\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_p x_p = 0$$ This is a **linear (hyperplane) decision boundary** — hence the term *"Linear Methods for Classification."* --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below summarizes **every formula you need** to solve KTU Module 2 questions on Logistic Regression. Memorize the Symbol, Meaning, and Formula columns. | **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula** | |---|---|---| | $\sigma(z)$ | Sigmoid / Logistic function | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | | $\sigma'(z)$ | Sigmoid derivative | $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | | $z$ | Linear predictor (logit) | $z = \mathbf{\beta}^T \mathbf{x} = \beta_0 + \sum_j \beta_j x_j$ | | $h_\theta(x)$ | Hypothesis (predicted prob) | $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\mathbf{\beta}^T \mathbf{x})$ | | $\text{logit}(p)$ | Log-odds of $p$ | $\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$ | | $\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta})$ | Log-likelihood | $\sum_{i=1}^{n}\!\bigl[y_i \ln \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\ln(1-\hat{p}_i)\bigr]$ | | $J(\boldsymbol{\beta})$ | Cost / Negative log-likelihood | $-\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\!\bigl[y_i \ln \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\ln(1-\hat{p}_i)\bigr]$ | | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j}$ | Gradient of cost w.r.t. $\beta_j$ | $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(\hat{p}_i - y_i\bigr)x_{ij}$ | | $\beta_j^{(\text{new})}$ | Updated coefficient | $\beta_j^{(\text{new})} = \beta_j^{(\text{old})} - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j}$ | | $\sigma(-z)$ | Sigmoid symmetry | $1 - \sigma(z)$ | | Odds ratio | Effect of $\Delta x_j = 1$ | $e^{\beta_j}$ | | Decision rule | Class prediction | $\hat{y} = 1 \text{ if } \sigma(z) \geq 0.5, \text{ else } 0$ | | Multinomial LR | Softmax generalisation | $P(Y=k \mid X) = \frac{e^{\boldsymbol{\beta}_k^T \mathbf{x}}}{\sum_{l=1}^{K} e^{\boldsymbol{\beta}_l^T \mathbf{x}}}$ | --- ## 2.5 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) — The "Why" of the Cost Function Because we cannot use **Ordinary Least Squares** (residuals are not Gaussian), we use **Maximum Likelihood Estimation**: **Step 1 — Likelihood for one observation:** $$P(Y=y_i \mid X=x_i) = \hat{p}_i^{\,y_i} \cdot (1-\hat{p}_i)^{\,1-y_i}$$ **Step 2 — Likelihood for the full dataset (assuming i.i.d. samples):** $$L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \hat{p}_i^{\,y_i} (1-\hat{p}_i)^{\,1-y_i}$$ **Step 3 — Take the log (monotonic transform simplifies product to sum):** $$\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl[y_i \ln \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\ln(1-\hat{p}_i)\bigr]$$ **Step 4 — Maximize $\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta})$**, or equivalently **minimize the cost** $J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = -\frac{1}{n}\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta})$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** The cost function is **convex** (single global minimum guaranteed), which is a major advantage over neural networks. This guarantees convergence of gradient descent. --- ## 2.6 Gradient Descent Update Rule There is **no closed-form solution** for logistic regression. We iteratively update parameters: $$\beta_j^{(t+1)} = \beta_j^{(t)} - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(\hat{p}_i^{(t)} - y_i\bigr)x_{ij}$$ where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate**. | Variant | Update Rule | Use Case | |---|---|---| | Batch GD | Full dataset per update | Small datasets | | Stochastic GD | One sample per update | Large datasets, online learning | | Mini-batch GD | Batches of size $b$ | Industry standard (deep learning) | | Newton's Method | Uses Hessian matrix | Faster convergence, expensive per step | | L-BFGS | Quasi-Newton | Default in scikit-learn | --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications | Domain | Use Case | |---|---| | **Healthcare** | Disease prediction (diabetes, cancer, heart disease) | | **Finance** | Credit card fraud detection, loan default prediction | | **Marketing** | Customer churn, click-through rate prediction | | **NLP** | Sentiment classification (binary), spam detection | | **Manufacturing** | Defect detection in production lines | | **Bioinformatics** | Disease-gene association studies | # SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sigmoid Derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ **Goal:** Differentiate $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ from first principles. **Step 1 — Rewrite using a negative exponent:** We can express $\sigma(z)$ as $\sigma(z) = (1 + e^{-z})^{-1}$. **Step 2 — Apply the chain rule:** Let $u = 1 + e^{-z}$, then $\sigma(z) = u^{-1}$, and $\frac{du}{dz} = -e^{-z}$. $$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -1 \cdot u^{-2} \cdot \frac{du}{dz} = -\frac{1}{(1+e^{-z})^2} \cdot (-e^{-z})$$ $$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1+e^{-z})^2}$$ **Step 3 — Factor the result strategically:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{z}$: $$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1+e^{-z})^2} = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1+e^{-z}}$$ **Step 4 — Recognize the two sigmoid terms:** $$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}, \quad 1-\sigma(z) = \frac{e^{-z}}{1+e^{-z}}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\bigl[1 - \sigma(z)\bigr]}$$ > [!NOTE] > This identity is the *single most important identity* in logistic regression — it makes gradient computation simple and elegant. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost Function via Maximum Likelihood Estimation **Setup:** We have $n$ training samples $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}$ with $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$. **Step 1 — Single observation likelihood:** For any sample $i$: $$P(y_i \mid \mathbf{x}_i; \boldsymbol{\beta}) = \hat{p}_i^{\,y_i} (1-\hat{p}_i)^{1-y_i}$$ where $\hat{p}_i = \sigma(\mathbf{\beta}^T \mathbf{x}_i)$. **Step 2 — Verify the expression is consistent with both labels:** - If $y_i = 1$: $P = \hat{p}_i^1 (1-\hat{p}_i)^0 = \hat{p}_i$. ✓ - If $y_i = 0$: $P = \hat{p}_i^0 (1-\hat{p}_i)^1 = 1 - \hat{p}_i$. ✓ **Step 3 — Likelihood across all $n$ independent samples:** $$L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \hat{p}_i^{\,y_i} (1-\hat{p}_i)^{1-y_i}$$ **Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm (monotonic, converts product to sum):** $$\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \ln L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\Bigl[y_i \ln \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\ln(1-\hat{p}_i)\bigr]$$ **Step 5 — The cost function is the negative mean log-likelihood:** $$\boxed{J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\Bigl[y_i \ln \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\ln(1-\hat{p}_i)\bigr]}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why negative?** Maximizing $\ell$ is equivalent to minimizing $-\ell$. Averaging by $\frac{1}{n}$ gives a per-sample loss that is independent of dataset size. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gradient of the Cost Function **Goal:** Compute $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j}$ to enable gradient descent. **Step 1 — Start from the log-likelihood for a single sample:** $$\ell_i = y_i \ln \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\ln(1-\hat{p}_i)$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\hat{p}_i$:** $$\frac{\partial \ell_i}{\partial \hat{p}_i} = \frac{y_i}{\hat{p}_i} - \frac{1-y_i}{1-\hat{p}_i} = \frac{y_i(1-\hat{p}_i) - (1-y_i)\hat{p}_i}{\hat{p}_i(1-\hat{p}_i)} = \frac{y_i - \hat{p}_i}{\hat{p}_i(1-\hat{p}_i)}$$ **Step 3 — Apply chain rule with $\hat{p}_i = \sigma(z_i)$ and $z_i = \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{x}_i$:** $$\frac{\partial \hat{p}_i}{\partial z_i} = \sigma'(z_i) = \hat{p}_i(1-\hat{p}_i)$$ $$\frac{\partial z_i}{\partial \beta_j} = x_{ij}$$ **Step 4 — Combine using the chain rule:** $$\frac{\partial \ell_i}{\partial \beta_j} = \frac{\partial \ell_i}{\partial \hat{p}_i} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{p}_i}{\partial z_i} \cdot \frac{\partial z_i}{\partial \beta_j}$$ $$= \frac{y_i - \hat{p}_i}{\hat{p}_i(1-\hat{p}_i)} \cdot \hat{p}_i(1-\hat{p}_i) \cdot x_{ij}$$ **Step 5 — Massive cancellation gives the elegant result:** $$\frac{\partial \ell_i}{\partial \beta_j} = (\hat{p}_i - y_i)\,x_{ij}$$ (Note: We flipped sign inside for the cost function $J = -\frac{1}{n}\ell$.) **Step 6 — Full gradient for the cost function:** $$\boxed{\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\hat{p}_i - y_i)\,x_{ij}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Pearl:** The gradient has the same form as linear regression, but with $\hat{p}_i = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{x}_i)$ instead of $\boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{x}_i$ itself. This structural similarity is highly testable. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — From Probabilities to Predicted Labels (Decision Rule) **Given:** $\hat{p}_i = \sigma(\mathbf{\beta}^T \mathbf{x}_i) \in (0,1)$. **Step 1 — Apply Bayes-optimal rule (for 0/1 loss):** Predict $\hat{y}_i = 1$ if $P(Y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}_i) > P(Y=0 \mid \mathbf{x}_i)$. **Step 2 — Substitute and simplify:** $$\hat{p}_i > 1 - \hat{p}_i \iff 2\hat{p}_i > 1 \iff \hat{p}_i > 0.5$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $z$:** $$\sigma(z_i) > 0.5 \iff z_i > 0 \iff \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{x}_i > 0$$ **Step 4 — Final decision rule:** $$\boxed{\hat{y}_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{x}_i \geq 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}}$$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style Problem) > **Problem:** A logistic regression model is trained on 3 samples with two features. Initial coefficients are $\beta_0 = 0.1, \beta_1 = 0.2, \beta_2 = -0.1$. Learning rate $\alpha = 0.5$. Perform **one gradient descent step**. | Sample | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $y$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | | 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **Step 1 — Compute linear predictor $z_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2$:** $$z_1 = 0.1 + 0.2(1) + (-0.1)(0) = 0.3$$ $$z_2 = 0.1 + 0.2(0) + (-0.1)(1) = 0.0$$ $$z_3 = 0.1 + 0.2(1) + (-0.1)(1) = 0.2$$ **Step 2 — Compute predictions $\hat{p}_i = \sigma(z_i)$:** $$\hat{p}_1 = \sigma(0.3) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.3}} = \frac{1}{1+0.7408} \approx 0.5744$$ $$\hat{p}_2 = \sigma(0.0) = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5000$$ $$\hat{p}_3 = \sigma(0.2) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.2}} = \frac{1}{1+0.8187} \approx 0.5498$$ **Step 3 — Compute errors $e_i = \hat{p}_i - y_i$:** $$e_1 = 0.5744 - 1 = -0.4256$$ $$e_2 = 0.5000 - 0 = +0.5000$$ $$e_3 = 0.5498 - 1 = -0.4502$$ **Step 4 — Compute gradients for each coefficient:** $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_0} = \frac{1}{3}(e_1 + e_2 + e_3) = \frac{1}{3}(-0.4256 + 0.5000 - 0.4502) = -0.1253$$ $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_1} = \frac{1}{3}(e_1 x_{11} + e_2 x_{21} + e_3 x_{31}) = \frac{1}{3}(-0.4256(1) + 0.5000(0) + (-0.4502)(1))$$ $$= \frac{1}{3}(-0.8758) = -0.2919$$ $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_2} = \frac{1}{3}(e_1 x_{12} + e_2 x_{22} + e_3 x_{32}) = \frac{1}{3}(-0.4256(0) + 0.5000(1) + (-0.4502)(1))$$ $$= \frac{1}{3}(0.0498) = 0.0166$$ **Step 5 — Apply the gradient descent update rule:** $$\beta_0^{\text{new}} = 0.1 - (0.5)(-0.1253) = 0.1 + 0.0627 = \mathbf{0.1627}$$ $$\beta_1^{\text{new}} = 0.2 - (0.5)(-0.2919) = 0.2 + 0.1460 = \mathbf{0.3460}$$ $$\beta_2^{\text{new}} = -0.1 - (0.5)(0.0166) = -0.1 - 0.0083 = \mathbf{-0.1083}$$ **Final Updated Coefficients:** $$\boxed{\beta_0 = 0.1627, \quad \beta_1 = 0.3460, \quad \beta_2 = -0.1083}$$ --- ## 3.6 Multinomial Logistic Regression (Softmax Regression) For $K > 2$ classes, the binary sigmoid is replaced by the **softmax function**: $$P(Y = k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{\boldsymbol{\beta}_k^T \mathbf{x}}}{\sum_{l=1}^{K} e^{\boldsymbol{\beta}_l^T \mathbf{x}}}, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, K$$ - The outputs form a valid probability distribution (sum to 1). - One class is typically chosen as reference (multinomial logit). - Reduces to binary logistic regression when $K = 2$. # SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 End-to-End Logistic Regression Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR inputSet[Training Dataset
