|---|---|---|
| **Actual: Fraud (+)** | 90 | 10 |
| **Actual: Legit (-)** | 60 | 340 |

**(a)** Compute the **Precision**, **Recall**, and **Accuracy** of the model. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain why the bank should focus on improving **Precision** rather than Recall for this application. Provide a domain-specific justification. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Extract Confusion Matrix Values.**

From the table, we identify:

$$
TP = 90, \quad FN = 10, \quad FP = 60, \quad TN = 340
$$

**Step 2: Verify the Total Sample Count.**

$$
N = TP + FP + FN + TN = 90 + 60 + 10 + 340 = 500 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3: Compute Precision.**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{90}{90 + 60} = \frac{90}{150} = 0.60
$$

$$
P = 60.00\%
$$

**Step 4: Compute Recall.**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90
$$

$$
R = 90.00\%
$$

**Step 5: Compute Accuracy.**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN} = \frac{90 + 340}{500} = \frac{430}{500} = 0.86
$$

$$
\text{Accuracy} = 86.00\%
$$

> **Summary of Results (3-Mark Component):**
> - **Precision = 0.60 (60%)**
> - **Recall = 0.90 (90%)**
> - **Accuracy = 0.86 (86%)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

The bank's fraud detection model has **high Recall (90%)** but **moderate Precision (60%)**. This means the model catches most fraudulent transactions, but it also generates a substantial number of **false alarms** (60 legitimate transactions wrongly flagged as fraud).

**Why Precision matters more for the bank:**

1. **Customer Trust and Retention:** A False Positive blocks a legitimate customer's transaction. If this happens frequently, customers will be frustrated, file complaints, and may switch to competitor banks, resulting in *revenue loss* and *brand damage*.
2. **Operational Cost of Manual Review:** Each flagged transaction must be manually reviewed by the bank's fraud investigation team. With 60 false positives out of 150 alerts, **40% of the team's effort is wasted** on legitimate transactions.
3. **Regulatory and Compliance Risks:** In some jurisdictions, wrongly blocking a customer's transaction without sufficient cause can lead to *regulatory fines* and *legal liability*.
4. **Precision-Recall Trade-off:** While the model achieves high recall, improving precision (for example, by tightening the classification threshold) will reduce false alarms without significantly increasing missed fraud cases (since fraud patterns are usually distinctive).

**Conclusion:** The bank should focus on improving **Precision** to reduce false alarms, improve customer experience, and lower operational overhead, while still maintaining an acceptable recall level for fraud detection.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Q: (a)** Define the **Confusion Matrix** for a binary classifier and explain all four of its components with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Precision** and **Recall** using formulas, interpretation, and a real-world example. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition:** A Confusion Matrix is a tabular visualization of the performance of a binary (or multi-class) classifier. It cross-tabulates the *Actual* labels (rows) against the *Predicted* labels (columns), yielding four cells in the binary case.

**Structure:**

|  | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP | FN |
| **Actual Negative** | FP | TN |

**Explanation of the Four Components:**

1. **True Positive (TP):** The model predicts *Positive* and the actual label is also *Positive*. *Example:* A spam filter correctly identifies a spam email as spam.
2. **False Negative (FN):** The model predicts *Negative* but the actual label is *Positive* (a "miss"). *Example:* A spam filter fails to catch a spam email, letting it land in the inbox (Type II Error).
3. **False Positive (FP):** The model predicts *Positive* but the actual label is *Negative* (a "false alarm"). *Example:* A spam filter wrongly flags a legitimate email as spam (Type I Error).
4. **True Negative (TN):** The model predicts *Negative* and the actual label is also *Negative*. *Example:* A spam filter correctly allows a legitimate email into the inbox.

**Significance:** All four cells together form the denominator of most evaluation metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Specificity). For any KTU numerical problem, the first step is always to *correctly extract* these four values from the confusion matrix.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

| **Aspect** | **Precision** | **Recall** |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ |
| **Denominator** | Total *Predicted* Positives | Total *Actual* Positives |
| **What it Measures** | Exactness of positive predictions | Coverage of actual positives |
| **Question Answered** | Of all predicted positives, how many were correct? | Of all actual positives, how many were found? |
| **Penalizes** | False Positives | False Negatives |
| **Synonym** | Positive Predictive Value (PPV) | Sensitivity, True Positive Rate (TPR) |
| **Focus** | Quality of "Yes" decisions | Quantity of "Yes" cases captured |
| **Real-World Example** | Spam filter (avoid false alarms) | Cancer screening (avoid missed diagnoses) |

**Real-World Example for Clarity:**

Consider a **medical test for a rare disease**:
- A model with **high Precision (95%)** ensures that when it flags a patient as diseased, there is a 95% chance the patient truly has the disease (low false alarm rate).
- A model with **high Recall (95%)** ensures that it catches 95% of all patients who actually have the disease (low miss rate).

In practice, an ideal medical test should have **both high precision and high recall**, which is why the **F1-Score** (harmonic mean) is often used as a balanced summary metric.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Swapping TP and FP:** Students often confuse the rows and columns of the confusion matrix. Always remember: *Rows = Actual*, *Columns = Predicted* (this is the KTU convention). Misreading the matrix will cascade into wrong precision and recall values.
> 2. **Forgetting the Denominator:** A common 1-mark deduction occurs when students write the formula as "Precision = TP" without including the denominator $TP + FP$.
> 3. **Using Accuracy Instead of Precision:** When the dataset is *imbalanced* (for example, 95% negative, 5% positive), accuracy is misleading. A naive "always predict negative" model achieves 95% accuracy but 0% recall. KTU examiners deduct 2 marks for using the wrong metric in imbalanced scenarios.
> 4. **No Unit Mention:** Always state precision as a decimal in $[0, 1]$ **or** as a percentage in $[0\%, 100\%]$. Do not write a bare fraction without context.
> 5. **Skipping Interpretation:** A 14-mark question that ends with a numerical answer but no business interpretation will lose 2-3 marks. KTU follows OBE-based evaluation and *always rewards domain-specific interpretation*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid-Revision Checklist
- **Definition:** Precision = $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ — the *exactness* of positive predictions.
- **Range:** $[0, 1]$ or $[0\%, 100\%]$. Value of **1** is perfect precision.
- **Synonym:** Positive Predictive Value (PPV).
- **Confusion Matrix Location:** Computed from the **first column** (Predicted Positive).
- **Denominator:** Total predicted positives ($TP + FP$).
- **Penalizes:** False Positives only (NOT False Negatives).
- **Best Used When:** Cost of False Positive is high (spam filter, fraud detection, medical diagnosis).
- **Complement Metric:** Recall (which is the *coverage* metric).
- **Summary Metric:** F1-Score = harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- **Multi-class Extension:** Use *One-vs-Rest* strategy and compute per-class precision.
- **Boundary Case:** $P = 1$ when $FP = 0$ (no false alarms).
- **KTU Numerical Strategy:** Always (1) extract TP, FP, FN, TN from matrix, (2) verify total, (3) substitute into formula, (4) report decimal and percentage, (5) provide domain-specific interpretation.
- **Exam Tip:** If a KTU question asks *which metric to use*, justify with the **cost asymmetry** between False Positives and False Negatives in the given application domain.

### Final Mnemonic
> **"Precision = Positive Prediction Purity"**
> *"Of everything I labeled Positive, how much was actually Positive?"*

This single sentence captures the essence of Precision and is sufficient to anchor any KTU Module 2 short-answer or long-answer question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recall
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Recall # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Supervised Machine Learning — Classification**, **Recall** is a **model evaluation metric** that quantifies the proportion of **actual positive instances** in a dataset that are **correctly identified as positive** by a classifier. It is one of the four fundamental metrics derived from the **Confusion Matrix**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Recall (R)** is formally defined as the ratio of **True Positives (TP)** to the **total number of actual positive samples** (True Positives + False Negatives). $$ R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP \;+\; FN} \;\;\in\; [0,\; 1] $$ Recall is also known by several synonymous names across literature and KTU textbooks: | Synonym | Common Usage | |---|---| | **Sensitivity** | Medical / Bio-informatics domain | | **True Positive Rate (TPR)** | ROC analysis | | **Hit Rate** | Information retrieval | | **Detection Rate** | Anomaly / Fraud detection | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Imagine you are a security officer at an airport metal-detector checkpoint.** 100 terrorists walk through your checkpoint in a day (positives), along with 990 normal passengers (negatives). - Your job is to **catch** as many terrorists as possible. - If you **catch 90 terrorists** and let **10 slip through** → your **Recall = 90/100 = 0.90 (90%)**. - The 10 you missed are **False Negatives** (terrorists labeled as "normal"). - The normal passengers you wrongly arrested are **False Positives** — *these affect Precision, not Recall.* **Key Insight:** Recall cares **only** about how many real positives you missed. A high Recall means the model is **"failing safe"** — it rarely lets a true positive escape undetected. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Boundaries > [!NOTE] > - **Range:** $0 \;\le\; R \;\le\; 1$ (or 0% to 100%) > - **Perfect Recall:** $R = 1$ when $FN = 0$ (no positive missed) > - **Worst Recall:** $R = 0$ when $TP = 0$ (no positive detected) > - **Unitless ratio** — but often expressed in **percentage (%)**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Building Blocks — The Confusion Matrix The Confusion Matrix is a $2 \times 2$ tabular structure that records the performance of a **binary classifier** on a labeled test set. Every metric in Module 2 (Precision, Recall, F1, Accuracy, Specificity) is derived directly from it. | | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** | |---|---|---| | **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) | | **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Row-wise interpretation (Actual class):** > - **TP** — Model correctly predicted Positive when the truth was Positive. ✓ > - **FN** — Model wrongly predicted Negative when the truth was Positive. ✗ (a *miss*) > - **FP** — Model wrongly predicted Positive when the truth was Negative. ✗ (a *false alarm*) > - **TN** — Model correctly predicted Negative when the truth was Negative. ✓ ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Recall 1. **Identify the positive class** — The class of *interest* in the problem (e.g., *disease present*, *fraud*, *spam*). 2. **Count True Positives (TP)** — Predictions where the model said "positive" and the truth was indeed "positive". 3. **Count False Negatives (FN)** — Predictions where the model said "negative" but the truth was "positive". 4. **Sum them** — $TP + FN$ equals the **total number of real positive cases** in the dataset. 5. **Divide** — $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$. > [!NOTE] > The denominator $TP + FN$ is **fixed by the ground-truth labels** of the test set. It does **not** change with the model. Hence, **improving Recall is equivalent to reducing False Negatives**. ## 2.3 The "Why" — Engineering Utility of Recall Recall is the **dominant metric** in high-stakes, asymmetric-cost domains: - **Medical Diagnosis (Cancer Detection):** A missed cancer (FN) can cost a life. Recall is prioritized. - **Fraud / Intrusion Detection:** Letting a fraudster pass (FN) is far more costly than a false alarm (FP). - **Search & Rescue Operations:** Missing a survivor is catastrophic. - **Quality Control in Manufacturing:** A defective product reaching the market (FN) damages brand and safety. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever the question uses the words *"missing a positive is dangerous"* or *"false negatives are expensive"*, the answer is **Recall**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Metric | Formula | Range | Best Value | Penalizes | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Recall (R)** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ | False Negatives | | 2 | **Precision (P)** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ | False Positives | | 3 | **Accuracy (A)** | $A = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+TN+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ | All errors | | 4 | **Specificity (TNR)** | $S = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ | False Positives | | 5 | **F1-Score** | $F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ | Both FN & FP | | 6 | **FPR (Fall-out)** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN} = 1 - S$ | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ | False Positives | | 7 | **Prevalence** | $\text{Prev} = \dfrac{TP+FN}{N}$ | $[0, 1]$ | — | — | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for KTU exams:** *"**R**ecall = **R**eal positives Recovered"* — and *"`TP+FN` = the whole positive population of ground truth."* ## 2.5 The Precision–Recall Tradeoff > [!WARNING] > Recall and Precision move in **opposite directions** as the **decision threshold** changes. - **Lower the threshold** → Model says "positive" more often → Recall ↑ but Precision ↓. - **Raise the threshold** → Model says "positive" only when very confident → Precision ↑ but Recall ↓. This is the famous **Precision–Recall Tradeoff**, and the curve of $P$ vs $R$ as the threshold varies is the **PR-Curve**. The **Area Under the PR-Curve (PR-AUC)** is a robust metric for imbalanced datasets. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Recall from First Principles (Bayes-Inspired) We start from the fundamental definition of **conditional probability**: the probability that the model predicts positive ($\hat{Y}=1$) **given** that the true label is positive ($Y=1$). $$ P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1) \;=\; \frac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \;\cap\; Y=1)}{P(Y=1)} $$ In empirical (frequency) form, with a test set of size $N$: $$ R \;=\; \frac{\#\{\hat{Y}=1 \;\cap\; Y=1\}}{N \cdot P(Y=1)} \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} $$ $$ \boxed{\;R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN}\;} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation of the F1-Score from Precision and Recall F1 is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall. The harmonic mean is used (not the arithmetic mean) because it **punishes extreme values** — a model with $P=1$ and $R=0$ must NOT get a high score. $$ F_1 \;=\; \frac{2}{\dfrac{1}{P} + \dfrac{1}{R}} $$ Step-by-step simplification: $$ F_1 \;=\; \frac{2}{\dfrac{1}{P} + \dfrac{1}{R}} \;=\; \frac{2}{\dfrac{R + P}{PR}} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot PR}{P + R} $$ Substituting $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ and $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$: $$ F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} $$ ## 3.3 Worked Example — Numerical Calculation of Recall > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** A spam-classifier is tested on **1000 emails**. Of these, **200** are actually spam (positives) and **800** are genuine (negatives). The classifier produces the following confusion matrix: > > - $TP = 170$ (spam correctly flagged) > - $FN = 30$ (spam missed, landed in inbox) > - $FP = 20$ (genuine wrongly flagged) > - $TN = 780$ (genuine correctly allowed) ### Step 1 — Identify the positive class Positive class = **Spam**. Total actual positives = $TP + FN = 170 + 30 = 200$. ### Step 2 — Apply the Recall formula $$ R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \;=\; \frac{170}{170 + 30} \;=\; \frac{170}{200} \;=\; 0.85 $$ $$ \boxed{\;R \;=\; 0.85 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 85\%\;} $$ ### Step 3 — Interpretation (Engineering) The model **recovers 85% of all spam emails**. **30 spam emails (15%) leaked into the inbox** — these are the False Negatives. ## 3.4 Multi-Class Extension — Macro & Micro Recall In a $K$-class problem (e.g., digit recognition with 10 classes), Recall is computed **per class** and then averaged. **Per-class Recall** for class $c$: $$ R_c \;=\; \frac{TP_c}{TP_c + FN_c} $$ **Macro-Recall** (treat all classes equally): $$ R_{\text{macro}} \;=\; \frac{1}{K} \sum_{c=1}^{K} R_c $$ **Micro-Recall** (pool all class contributions): $$ R_{\text{micro}} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{c} TP_c}{\sum_{c} (TP_c + FN_c)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key difference:** Micro-Recall **equals Accuracy** in single-label multi-class problems. Macro-Recall gives equal weight to rare classes — useful for **imbalanced datasets**. ## 3.5 Edge Case — Division by Zero If the test set contains **no positive samples** ($TP + FN = 0$), Recall is **undefined**. In Scikit-learn-style implementations, this returns **0.0 with a warning**. The KTU-recommended convention is to explicitly note this in any problem solution. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Confusion Matrix — Visual Block Diagram The block diagram below maps how the **ground truth** (rows) and **model predictions** (columns) combine to form the four cells of the Confusion Matrix, with the **Recall denominator** highlighted. ```mermaid graph TD A[Total Test Set N] --> B{Actual Label} B -->|Y = 1
Positive Class| C[Actual Positives
TP + FN] B -->|Y = 0
Negative Class| D[Actual Negatives
FP + TN] C -->|Model says Positive| E[TP
True Positive] C -->|Model says Negative| F[FN
False Negative
Missed] D -->|Model says Positive| G[FP
False Positive
False Alarm] D -->|Model says Negative| H[TN
True Negative] E --> I[Recall Numerator] F --> J[Recall Denominator Term 2] I --> K[Recall = TP / TP+FN] J --> K G --> L[Precision Denominator Term 2] E --> M[Precision = TP / TP+FP] L --> M style E fill:#2ecc71,stroke:#000,color:#000 style F fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#000,color:#000 style G fill:#e67e22,stroke:#000,color:#000 style H fill:#3498db,stroke:#000,color:#000 style K fill:#f1c40f,stroke:#000,color:#000 style M fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **green** cell feeds both Precision and Recall. The **red FN cell** is what Recall punishes. The **orange FP cell** is what Precision punishes. ## 4.2 Precision–Recall Tradeoff Curve (Schematic) The diagram below illustrates the **inverse relationship** between Precision and Recall as the classification **threshold $\tau$** is swept from 0 to 1. ```mermaid graph LR T1[Threshold tau = 0.0
Predict Positive for All] --> R1[Recall = 1.0
Precision = Low] T2[Threshold tau = 0.5
Balanced Operating Point] --> R2[Recall = Medium-High
Precision = Medium-High] T3[Threshold tau = 0.9
Predict Positive Only if Very Confident] --> R3[Recall = Low
Precision = High] T1 --> T2 T2 --> T3 R1 --> R2 R2 --> R3 style T1 fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#000,color:#000 style T2 fill:#f39c12,stroke:#000,color:#000 style T3 fill:#2ecc71,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R1 fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R2 fill:#f39c12,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R3 fill:#2ecc71,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Recall Computation Pipeline ```mermaid graph TD S1[Step 1: Train Classifier
e.g., Logistic Regression] --> S2[Step 2: Predict Labels on Test Set] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compare Predictions vs Ground Truth] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Build Confusion Matrix
TP, FP, FN, TN] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Extract TP and FN] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Compute Recall
R = TP / TP+FN] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Compare with Business Requirement
e.g., R greater than 0.95] S7 --> S8{Threshold Met?} S8 -->|Yes| S9[Deploy Model] S8 -->|No| S10[Adjust Threshold or Re-train] S10 --> S2 style S6 fill:#f1c40f,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S7 fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S9 fill:#2ecc71,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S10 fill:#e67e22,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` > Define **Recall** in the context of a binary classification problem. Why is it also called **Sensitivity** or **True Positive Rate**? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Recall** is an evaluation metric that measures the fraction of actual positive samples that a classifier correctly identifies as positive. Mathematically, for a binary classifier: $$ R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} $$ It is called **Sensitivity** because it measures how *sensitive* the model is to the positive class — a highly sensitive model rarely misses a true positive. It is called the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** because it is the ratio of true positives to the *total* actual positives, and in ROC analysis, TPR is plotted on the y-axis against the False Positive Rate (FPR) on the x-axis. **(3 Marks)** --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` > Differentiate between **Precision** and **Recall**. In which type of application is Recall the more important metric? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Precision | Recall | |---|---|---| | Question answered | *"Of those predicted positive, how many are truly positive?"* | *"Of all actual positives, how many did we catch?"* | | Formula | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | | Penalizes | False Positives | False Negatives | | Focus | Quality of positive predictions | Coverage of positive class | Recall is the more important metric in **asymmetric, high-stakes applications** such as **cancer detection, fraud detection, or terrorism screening**, where a *False Negative* (missing a real positive) is far more dangerous or costly than a False Positive. **(3 Marks)** --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **Part (a) — 7 marks** and **Part (b) — 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** out of the given choice. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]` **(a)** For a binary classifier evaluated on a test set, the following confusion matrix was obtained: | | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** | |---|---|---| | **Actual Positive** | 50 | 10 | | **Actual Negative** | 5 | 35 | Compute the **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score** of the classifier. Comment on the **class imbalance**, if any. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Precision–Recall Tradeoff** in detail. With a suitable diagram, show how the **decision threshold** affects Precision and Recall. Mention one real-world scenario where you would deliberately **sacrifice Precision for higher Recall**. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **MODEL ANSWER — Question A** #### Part (a) — Numerical Solution **Step 1 — Extract values from the Confusion Matrix:** $$ TP = 50, \quad FN = 10, \quad FP = 5, \quad TN = 35 $$ $$ N \;=\; TP + FN + FP + TN \;=\; 50 + 10 + 5 + 35 \;=\; 100 $$ **Step 2 — Compute Accuracy:** $$ \text{Accuracy} \;=\; \frac{TP + TN}{N} \;=\; \frac{50 + 35}{100} \;=\; \frac{85}{100} \;=\; 0.85 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Precision:** $$ P \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \;=\; \frac{50}{50 + 5} \;=\; \frac{50}{55} \;\approx\; 0.909 $$ **Step 4 — Compute Recall:** $$ R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \;=\; \frac{50}{50 + 10} \;=\; \frac{50}{60} \;\approx\; 0.833 $$ **Step 5 — Compute F1-Score:** $$ F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.909 \times 0.833}{0.909 + 0.833} \;=\; \frac{1.514}{1.742} \;\approx\; 0.869 $$ **Step 6 — Class Imbalance Analysis:** Actual Positives = $TP + FN = 60$; Actual Negatives = $FP + TN = 40$. The ratio is 60:40, which is **moderately imbalanced (1.5 : 1)**. Accuracy alone (0.85) can be misleading in such cases — a model that always predicts "positive" would get 60% accuracy. Therefore, **Recall (0.833)** is the more trustworthy metric here. **(7 Marks — Awarded)** #### Part (b) — Theoretical Explanation The **Precision–Recall Tradeoff** arises because, in probabilistic classifiers (e.g., Logistic Regression), the predicted class depends on a **decision threshold $\tau$** applied to the predicted probability $P(Y=1 \mid X)$. - If $P(Y=1 \mid X) \;\ge\; \tau$, predict **Positive**. - Else, predict **Negative**. **Effect of varying $\tau$:** - **Decrease $\tau$** (e.g., 0.5 → 0.3) → More samples classified as positive → **Recall increases** (catching more true positives) but **Precision decreases** (more false alarms). - **Increase $\tau$** (e.g., 0.5 → 0.8) → Fewer samples classified as positive → **Precision increases** (predictions are more reliable) but **Recall decreases** (more true positives are missed). **Diagram (drawn during exam):** ``` Precision |● | ● | ● ● ← P-R curve (inverse relationship) | ● |_____________●_____ Recall 1.0 ``` *(Refer to Section 4.2 for the structural schematic.)* **Real-world scenario — Sacrificing Precision for Recall:** In **COVID-19 mass screening** using rapid antigen tests, health authorities *deliberately* accept many false positives (low precision) because missing even one infected person (low recall) could trigger a community outbreak. Hence, they lower the threshold and prioritize Recall. **(7 Marks — Awarded)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **(a)** Define **Confusion Matrix** for a binary classifier. Derive the formula for **Recall** from the matrix and explain its significance with a **medical diagnosis** example. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A spam-detection model has the following performance on a test set of 500 emails: $TP = 90$, $FP = 10$, $FN = 60$, $TN = 340$. Compute: **(i) Recall**, **(ii) Specificity**, **(iii) Precision**, **(iv) F1-Score**, and **(v) Accuracy**. Which metric should the email service prioritize and why? **(7 Marks)** --- ### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B** #### Part (a) — Definition and Derivation A **Confusion Matrix** is a $2 \times 2$ table that summarizes the performance of a binary classifier by comparing predicted labels with actual labels. | | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative | |---|---|---| | **Actual Positive** | TP | FN | | **Actual Negative** | FP | TN | **Derivation of Recall:** Recall is the probability that a randomly chosen actual positive sample is classified as positive: $$ R \;=\; P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1) \;=\; \frac{P(\hat{Y}=1, Y=1)}{P(Y=1)} \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} $$ **Medical Diagnosis Significance:** Consider a model screening 1000 patients for **diabetes**, of whom 100 actually have it. If $TP = 95$ and $FN = 5$: $$ R \;=\; \frac{95}{100} \;=\; 0.95 $$ The model correctly identifies 95% of diabetic patients. The 5 missed patients (FN) can be sent for **confirmatory tests**. In medical screening, **high Recall is critical** because undiagnosed disease is dangerous. **(7 Marks)** #### Part (b) — Numerical Computation Given: $TP = 90$, $FP = 10$, $FN = 60$, $TN = 340$, $N = 500$. **(i) Recall:** $$ R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \;=\; \frac{90}{90 + 60} \;=\; \frac{90}{150} \;=\; 0.60 $$ **(ii) Specificity:** $$ S \;=\; \frac{TN}{TN + FP} \;=\; \frac{340}{340 + 10} \;=\; \frac{340}{350} \;\approx\; 0.971 $$ **(iii) Precision:** $$ P \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \;=\; \frac{90}{90 + 10} \;=\; \frac{90}{100} \;=\; 0.90 $$ **(iv) F1-Score:** $$ F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.90 \times 0.60}{0.90 + 0.60} \;=\; \frac{1.08}{1.50} \;=\; 0.72 $$ **(v) Accuracy:** $$ A \;=\; \frac{TP + TN}{N} \;=\; \frac{90 + 340}{500} \;=\; \frac{430}{500} \;=\; 0.86 $$ **Which metric to prioritize?** The email service should prioritize **Precision (0.90)** rather than Recall (0.60). Reason: a legitimate email wrongly classified as spam (FP) may be **lost forever** — causing user dissatisfaction and business harm. Whereas missing some spam emails (FN) is inconvenient but tolerable, as users can still mark them manually. Therefore, for spam filtering, **Precision matters more than Recall** — the opposite of medical diagnosis. **(7 Marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Confusing the denominator:** Students often write $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ — this is **Precision**, not Recall. Always remember: Recall's denominator is the **actual positive** count ($TP + FN$). > 2. **Forgetting the class imbalance caveat:** In a 1:100 imbalanced dataset, Accuracy > 99% can be **trivial** (predicting all-negative). Always pair Accuracy with Recall/Precision. > 3. **Reporting the wrong unit:** Recall is a ratio in $[0,1]$ — KTU expects either a decimal (0.85) or percentage (85%), not raw integers. > 4. **Skipping the interpretation step:** A numerical answer without a concluding sentence ("Thus, the model recovers 85% of all spam") loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Mixing up Sensitivity with Specificity:** Sensitivity = Recall (TPR). Specificity = TNR = $TN/(TN+FP)$. These are **mirror metrics** — don't interchange them. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Recall** measures *coverage* of the positive class: $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$. - **Range:** $[0, 1]$; **Best value:** 1.0 (no false negatives). - **Synonyms:** Sensitivity, TPR, Hit Rate, Detection Rate. - **Denominator is fixed by ground truth** — only the numerator $TP$ can change with the model. - **Prioritize Recall** when *missing a positive* is dangerous (cancer, fraud, terrorism, defect detection). - **Prioritize Precision** when *false alarms* are costly (spam filtering, legal evidence). - **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of P and R — penalizes extreme imbalance between them. - **Multi-class:** Use **Macro-Recall** for balanced class-weighting, **Micro-Recall** (= Accuracy) for overall performance. - **Edge case:** If $TP + FN = 0$, Recall is **undefined** (division by zero). - **PR-Tradeoff:** Lowering the threshold increases Recall but decreases Precision, and vice versa. - **KTU one-liner to remember:** *"Recall = How many real positives did we catch?"*

topics.title: Accuracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Accuracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Machine Learning (ML) under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Accuracy** is defined as a fundamental **classification performance metric** that quantifies the proportion of correct predictions made by a classification model relative to the total number of predictions (or instances) evaluated.

Mathematically, it is the ratio of the number of **correctly classified instances** to the total number of instances in the dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT755 - Module 2):**
> Accuracy is one of the simplest and most intuitive evaluation metrics used in supervised learning, particularly for **classification problems**. However, KTU examiners expect students to critically analyze when accuracy is *not* the right metric (e.g., imbalanced datasets).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **doctor screening 100 patients** for a disease:

- Out of 100 patients, the doctor correctly identifies **90 patients** (both healthy and diseased).
- The doctor misdiagnoses **10 patients**.

In this scenario, the doctor's diagnostic **accuracy** is 90%, meaning the doctor got 9 out of every 10 patients right.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Accuracy tells us, *"Out of all the guesses my model made, how many were right?"*

**Formally:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Number of Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Number of Predictions}}$$

The value of accuracy lies in the closed interval **[0, 1]**, where:
- **1.0 (or 100%)** → Perfect classification (every prediction is correct)
- **0.0 (or 0%)** → Completely wrong classification
- **0.5 (or 50%)** → For binary classification, equivalent to random guessing in a balanced dataset

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Limitation:** Accuracy can be **misleading on imbalanced datasets**. For example, in a dataset with 95% healthy patients and 5% diseased, a trivial model that always predicts "healthy" would achieve 95% accuracy despite being clinically useless.

### 1.3 The Confusion Matrix Connection

Accuracy is fundamentally derived from a **Confusion Matrix**, which is a tabular summary of a classifier's performance:

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **TP (True Positive)** | Model correctly predicts the positive class |
| **TN (True Negative)** | Model correctly predicts the negative class |
| **FP (False Positive)** | Model incorrectly predicts the positive class (Type I Error) |
| **FN (False Negative)** | Model incorrectly predicts the negative class (Type II Error) |

> [!NOTE]
> The terms **True Positive**, **True Negative**, **False Positive**, and **False Negative** are **standard KTU Board Examiner keywords**. Memorize them thoroughly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Accuracy

Accuracy is computed through a sequence of logical steps:

- **Step 1:** Obtain predictions from the trained classification model on a labeled dataset (test set).
- **Step 2:** Compare each predicted label with the corresponding true (actual) label.
- **Step 3:** Count the number of matches (correct predictions) — these constitute **TP + TN**.
- **Step 4:** Count the total number of instances — this constitutes **TP + TN + FP + FN**.
- **Step 5:** Compute the ratio of correct predictions to total instances.

### 2.2 The Core Mathematical Form

The fundamental formula for accuracy in terms of the confusion matrix components is:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Purpose / Use Case |
|------------------|------------|--------------------|
| **Basic Accuracy** | $\frac{\text{Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Predictions}}$ | General classification performance |
| **Accuracy (Confusion Matrix)** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Derived from confusion matrix |
| **Misclassification Rate (Error Rate)** | $\frac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = 1 - \text{Accuracy}$ | Complement of accuracy |
| **Accuracy in Percentage** | $\text{Accuracy} \times 100$ | Expressing accuracy as a percentage |
| **Accuracy for Multi-class** | $\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{C} \text{Correct}_i}{\text{Total Instances}}$ | Where $C$ is the number of classes |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Evaluating the reliability of disease detection models (with caution due to class imbalance).
- **Spam Detection:** Measuring how well an email classifier separates spam from legitimate mail.
- **Image Classification:** Used in computer vision tasks like object recognition.
- **Sentiment Analysis:** Evaluating NLP models in classifying text polarity.
- **Fraud Detection:** Critical metric alongside precision and recall due to extreme imbalance.

### 2.5 When to Use and When NOT to Use Accuracy

| Scenario | Is Accuracy Reliable? |
|----------|----------------------|
| Balanced dataset (roughly equal class distribution) | ✅ **Yes** |
| Imbalanced dataset (one class dominates) | ❌ **No** — use Precision, Recall, F1-Score |
| Multi-class classification with balanced classes | ✅ **Yes** |
| Cost-sensitive applications (e.g., cancer detection) | ❌ **No** — use Recall |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** Examiners frequently test whether students can **justify** or **criticize** the use of accuracy. Always discuss limitations in long-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Binary Classification Accuracy Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A binary classifier is tested on a dataset of **200 instances**. The confusion matrix obtained is as follows:

| Actual \ Predicted | Positive | Negative |
|--------------------|----------|----------|
| **Positive** | TP = 50 | FN = 10 |
| **Negative** | FP = 20 | TN = 120 |

**Task:** Compute the accuracy of the classifier.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the values from the confusion matrix.

$$TP = 50, \quad FN = 10, \quad FP = 20, \quad TN = 120$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator (correct predictions).

$$\text{Numerator} = TP + TN = 50 + 120 = 170$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator (total instances).

$$\text{Denominator} = TP + TN + FP + FN = 50 + 120 + 20 + 10 = 200$$

**Step 4:** Apply the accuracy formula.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85$$

**Step 5:** Express as a percentage.

$$\text{Accuracy (\%)} = 0.85 \times 100 = 85\%$$

**Conclusion:** The classifier correctly predicts **85%** of the instances.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Deriving Misclassification Rate from Accuracy

**Derivation:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

Let $N = TP + TN + FP + FN$ (total instances).

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N}$$

The misclassified instances are $FP + FN$. So the error rate is:

$$\text{Error Rate} = \frac{FP + FN}{N}$$

$$\text{Error Rate} = 1 - \frac{TP + TN}{N} = 1 - \text{Accuracy}$$

**Verification with Worked Example 1:**

$$\text{Error Rate} = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$$

This means **15 instances out of 200** were misclassified, which matches: $FP + FN = 20 + 10 = 30$... 

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction:** $20 + 10 = 30$ misclassified, so error rate = $30 / 200 = 0.15$. ✓ The formula holds.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Multi-Class Accuracy Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A 3-class classifier (Classes A, B, C) is evaluated on **150 instances**. The confusion matrix shows:

- Class A correctly classified: **40 instances**
- Class B correctly classified: **35 instances**
- Class C correctly classified: **25 instances**
- Total misclassifications across all classes: **50 instances**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute total correct predictions.

$$\text{Total Correct} = 40 + 35 + 25 = 100$$

**Step 2:** Verify total instances.

$$\text{Total Instances} = 100 + 50 = 150$$

**Step 3:** Apply the multi-class accuracy formula.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{100}{150} = 0.6667$$

$$\text{Accuracy (\%)} = 66.67\%$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4: Imbalanced Dataset Pitfall

**Problem Statement:**
A dataset has **1000 patients**, of which **990 are healthy** and **10 are diseased**. A trivial classifier predicts every patient as "healthy."

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Determine the confusion matrix values.

$$TP = 0, \quad FN = 10, \quad FP = 0, \quad TN = 990$$

**Step 2:** Compute accuracy.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{0 + 990}{1000} = 0.99 = 99\%$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result.

$$\text{Despite 99\% accuracy, the model fails to detect ANY diseased patient.}$$

**Conclusion:** This illustrates why accuracy alone is insufficient for imbalanced datasets. **Recall** would be more appropriate:

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{0}{0 + 10} = 0\%$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart: Accuracy Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Start: Trained ML Model"] --> nodeB["Feed Test Dataset"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Generate Predictions"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Compare with True Labels"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Is Prediction Correct?"}
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeF["Increment Correct Counter"]
    nodeE -- No --> nodeG["Increment Incorrect Counter"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Check All Instances"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["Compute Accuracy Ratio"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Output Accuracy Score"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["End"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Accuracy from Confusion Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph confmat["Confusion Matrix Components"]
        nodeA["TP: True Positive"]
        nodeB["TN: True Negative"]
        nodeC["FP: False Positive"]
        nodeD["FN: False Negative"]
    end
    confmat --> nodeE["Numerator: TP plus TN"]
    confmat --> nodeF["Denominator: TP plus TN plus FP plus FN"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Division Operation"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["Accuracy Score"]
```

### 4.3 Decision Diagram: When to Use Accuracy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Select Evaluation Metric"] --> nodeQ1{"Is Dataset Balanced?"}
    nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeQ2{"Is Cost of Error Equal for All Classes?"}
    nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeAlt1["Use Precision, Recall, F1-Score"]
    nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeUse["Use Accuracy"]
    nodeQ2 -- No --> nodeAlt2["Use Weighted F1-Score or ROC-AUC"]
    nodeUse --> nodeEnd["Final Metric: Accuracy"]
```

### 4.4 Confusion Matrix Visualization (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph actual["Actual Class"]
        nodeA1["Positive"]
        nodeA2["Negative"]
    end
    subgraph predicted["Predicted Class"]
        nodeP1["Positive"]
        nodeP2["Negative"]
    end
    nodeP1 --> nodeTP["TP: Correct Positive"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeFP["FP: False Alarm Type I Error"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeFN["FN: Missed Detection Type II Error"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeTN["TN: Correct Negative"]
    nodeTP --> nodeFinal["Correct: TP plus TN"]
    nodeTN --> nodeFinal
    nodeFP --> nodeWrong["Incorrect: FP plus FN"]
    nodeFN --> nodeWrong
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define accuracy in classification. Write its mathematical formula.**

**Model Answer:**

Accuracy is a classification performance metric that measures the proportion of correct predictions out of the total number of predictions made by the model.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Number of Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Number of Predictions}} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

It is expressed as a value between **0 and 1** (or 0% to 100%).

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
> **Why is accuracy not a reliable metric for imbalanced datasets? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

In imbalanced datasets, one class significantly outnumbers the other. A trivial model that always predicts the majority class can achieve high accuracy while completely failing to identify the minority class.

**Example:** In a dataset of 1000 emails with 990 legitimate and 10 spam, a model predicting "legitimate" for every email achieves **99% accuracy** but detects **zero spam emails**. Hence, **precision, recall, and F1-score** are preferred for imbalanced data.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define the confusion matrix. Explain TP, TN, FP, and FN with an example of a binary classification problem. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the formula for accuracy from the confusion matrix. A classifier produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of 250 samples: TP = 80, FN = 20, FP = 30, TN = 120. Calculate the accuracy and the misclassification rate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

A **Confusion Matrix** is a table that summarizes the performance of a classification model by comparing predicted labels against actual labels.

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **TP (True Positive)** | Model predicts positive, and actual is positive. E.g., correctly diagnosing a diseased patient. |
| **TN (True Negative)** | Model predicts negative, and actual is negative. E.g., correctly identifying a healthy patient. |
| **FP (False Positive)** | Model predicts positive, but actual is negative. E.g., falsely diagnosing a healthy patient as diseased (Type I Error). |
| **FN (False Negative)** | Model predicts negative, but actual is positive. E.g., missing a diseased patient (Type II Error). |

**Example:** In a spam detection system — TP = correctly flagged spam; TN = correctly identified legitimate email; FP = legitimate email marked as spam; FN = spam that reached the inbox.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation:**

The accuracy formula in terms of confusion matrix components is:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

This is derived by recognizing that:
- Correct predictions = TP + TN
- Total predictions = TP + TN + FP + FN

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Identify values.

$$TP = 80, \quad FN = 20, \quad FP = 30, \quad TN = 120$$

**Step 2:** Compute numerator.

$$\text{Correct Predictions} = TP + TN = 80 + 120 = 200$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator.

$$\text{Total Instances} = 80 + 120 + 30 + 20 = 250$$

**Step 4:** Calculate accuracy.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{200}{250} = 0.80 = 80\%$$

**Step 5:** Calculate misclassification rate.

$$\text{Error Rate} = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20 = 20\%$$

**Verification:** Misclassified instances = FP + FN = 30 + 20 = 50; 50 / 250 = 0.20 ✓

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of accuracy in machine learning. Discuss its advantages and limitations with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A medical diagnosis model is tested on 500 patients. The results show: 450 healthy patients correctly identified, 30 diseased patients correctly identified, 10 healthy patients misclassified as diseased, and 10 diseased patients misclassified as healthy. Compute the accuracy, precision, and recall. Comment on whether accuracy alone is sufficient. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:** Accuracy is the ratio of correctly classified instances to the total number of instances.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

**Advantages:**
- Simple to understand and compute.
- Useful for balanced datasets.
- Provides a single-number summary of model performance.

**Limitations:**
- **Misleading on imbalanced datasets** (e.g., 99% accuracy on a fraud detection dataset with 99% legitimate transactions).
- Does not distinguish between **Type I and Type II errors**.
- Treats all classes equally, which may not reflect real-world priorities.

**Example:** In cancer detection, a model with 95% accuracy may still miss 50% of cancer cases if the dataset has only 5% cancer patients.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Identify confusion matrix values.

Given:
- Diseased correctly identified: TP = 30
- Healthy correctly identified: TN = 450
- Healthy misclassified as diseased: FP = 10
- Diseased misclassified as healthy: FN = 10

**Step 2:** Compute Accuracy.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{30 + 450}{30 + 450 + 10 + 10} = \frac{480}{500} = 0.96 = 96\%$$

**Step 3:** Compute Precision.

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{30}{30 + 10} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

**Step 4:** Compute Recall.

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{30}{30 + 10} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

**Step 5:** Comment on sufficiency of accuracy.

Despite a high accuracy of **96%**, the precision and recall are only **75%**, meaning the model misses 25% of diseased patients and produces false alarms. In medical diagnosis, **recall is critical** since missing a diseased patient (FN) has severe consequences. Therefore, **accuracy alone is insufficient**; precision, recall, and F1-score provide a more complete picture.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting to define TP, TN, FP, FN explicitly** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these terms are used without proper definitions.
> 2. **Not stating the range of accuracy** [0, 1] — A frequently missed point worth 1 mark.
> 3. **Failing to mention limitations of accuracy** — In 14-mark questions, omitting the discussion of imbalanced datasets costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Incorrect confusion matrix mapping** — Confusing FP with FN or vice versa is a critical error that invalidates the entire calculation.
> 5. **Not verifying the total** — Always ensure TP + TN + FP + FN equals the given total dataset size.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **Accuracy** = (TP + TN) / Total Instances, ranging from **0 to 1**.
> - **Error Rate** = 1 − Accuracy = (FP + FN) / Total Instances.
> - Accuracy is derived from the **Confusion Matrix**.
> - **TP, TN, FP, FN** are the four foundational components.
> - Accuracy is **misleading for imbalanced datasets** — use Precision, Recall, F1-Score instead.
> - **Type I Error** = FP (False Positive); **Type II Error** = FN (False Negative).
> - In **multi-class classification**, accuracy = (sum of all correctly classified instances) / (total instances).
> - Always **verify** that TP + TN + FP + FN = Total Dataset Size.
> - For **cost-sensitive applications** (e.g., medical, fraud), prioritize recall over accuracy.
> - **KTU 2024 Scheme emphasis:** Examiners expect both the formula AND its critical analysis in long-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: F-Measure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** F-Measure ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of binary and multi-class classification under the **Confusion Matrix** evaluation framework, the **F-Measure (also called F-Score)** is defined as the **harmonic mean of Precision and Recall** of a classifier. It provides a single scalar metric that simultaneously balances the concerns of *false positives* and *false negatives*, producing a balanced evaluation even when the class distribution in the dataset is **highly imbalanced**. The general form, the **Fβ-Measure**, weights Recall β times more heavily than Precision (or vice-versa depending on the formulation), and is formally defined for β ≥ 0 as: $$F_{\beta} = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{Precision \times Recall}{(\beta^2 \cdot Precision) + Recall}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the PEMUT755 / AIML course, the most frequently examined variant is the **F1-Score** (where β = 1), which gives *equal weight* to Precision and Recall. Students must be able to compute F1 directly from a given confusion matrix without intermediate text-book references. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **lie detector in a courtroom** evaluating 100 statements: - **Precision (P)** = "Of all the statements I flagged as LIES, how many were actually lies?" — It answers: *When I accuse someone, am I accurate?* - **Recall (R)** = "Of all the REAL lies told, how many did I catch?" — It answers: *Did I miss any?* If your detector is very **conservative** (flags only one statement as a lie, and it's truly a lie), your **Precision = 1.0** (perfect!), but your **Recall will be very low** because you missed 99 other lies. If your detector is very **trigger-happy** (flags every statement as a lie), your **Recall = 1.0** (caught all lies), but **Precision collapses** because 50 honest people are wrongly accused. The **F-Measure** is the *judge's balanced score* — it punishes you for being either too cautious or too reckless. The **harmonic mean** (not the arithmetic mean) is used specifically because it heavily penalizes extreme imbalances: if *either* Precision or Recall is near zero, the F-Measure is dragged close to zero, regardless of how perfect the other metric is. > [!NOTE] > **Why Harmonic Mean and not Arithmetic Mean?** > > Arithmetic Mean of (1.0, 0.02) = 0.51 — sounds decent. > Harmonic Mean of (1.0, 0.02) = 0.0388 — reveals the truth. > > The harmonic mean correctly reflects the **true weak link** in the system, which is exactly what KTU examiners test in numerical problems. ### 1.3 Key Components (Prerequisites) Before F-Measure can be applied, the following foundational building blocks from **Module 2 – Evaluation Metrics** must be clearly understood: - **True Positive (TP)** — Correctly predicted positive instances - **False Positive (FP)** — Incorrectly predicted positive (Type I Error) - **True Negative (TN)** — Correctly predicted negative instances - **False Negative (FN)** — Incorrectly predicted negative (Type II Error) - **Precision (P)** — Quality of positive predictions - **Recall (R) / Sensitivity / True Positive Rate (TPR)** — Coverage of actual positives > [!IMPORTANT] > F-Measure is **not independent** of Precision and Recall — it is a *composite metric*. KTU questions will typically give you a confusion matrix and ask you to compute Precision, Recall, and F1 in sequence. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — How F-Measure Works The F-Measure operates on a three-tier logical pipeline: **Step 1 — Build the Confusion Matrix** - Tabulate TP, FP, TN, FN from the classifier's predictions versus ground truth. **Step 2 — Compute Precision and Recall Independently** - Precision focuses on the **column of predicted positives** (TP / (TP + FP)). - Recall focuses on the **row of actual positives** (TP / (TP + FN)). **Step 3 — Combine via Weighted Harmonic Mean** - Apply the Fβ formula to fuse both metrics. The parameter **β** is the **tuning knob** that tells the formula: *"How much more do I care about Recall than Precision?"* ### 2.2 The β Parameter — Interpretation Table | Value of β | Effect on F-Measure | When to Use in Engineering | |------------|---------------------|----------------------------| | β = 0 | Reduces to **Precision** alone | When false positives are very costly (e.g., spam filtering where legit emails must not be deleted) | | 0 < β < 1 | Precision is **weighted higher** | When FP cost > FN cost | | **β = 1** | **Equal weight to P and R** (F1-Score) | **Default KTU exam case**; general balanced classification | | β > 1 | Recall is **weighted higher** | When FN cost > FP cost (e.g., cancer detection — missing a tumor is worse than a false alarm) | | β → ∞ | Reduces to **Recall** alone | Theoretical extreme | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol is used inside table cells only for absolute value expressions using `\vert` or `\mid` — never as a raw character that would break markdown parsing. | # | Metric | Mathematical Formula | Range | Unit / Type | |---|--------|---------------------|-------|-------------| | 1 | Precision (P) | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | Recall (R) | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | F1 (alt form) | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless | | 5 | General Fβ | $F_{\beta} = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless | | 6 | F2-Score | $F_2 = 5 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{4P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless | | 7 | F0.5-Score | $F_{0.5} = 1.25 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{0.25P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless | | 8 | Accuracy (for context) | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Medical Diagnosis Systems** — F1 is used to evaluate cancer, diabetic retinopathy, and cardiac arrest prediction models. - **Information Retrieval & Search Engines** — F-Measure ranks document classifiers and search relevance. - **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** — Used in Named Entity Recognition (NER), sentiment analysis, and machine translation (BLEU's conceptual cousin). - **Anomaly & Fraud Detection** — In credit-card fraud datasets where 99.9% of transactions are legitimate, accuracy is misleading; F1 is the gold standard. - **Object Detection (Computer Vision)** — Used in Pascal VOC and COCO benchmarks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When the question states *"balanced dataset"* → use **Accuracy**. When it says *"imbalanced"* or asks for a *single robust metric* → use **F1-Score**. ### 2.5 Macro vs Micro F-Measure (Advanced) For **multi-class classification**, F-Measure is computed in two ways: - **Macro-F1**: Compute F1 for each class independently, then take the unweighted mean. Treats all classes equally regardless of support. - **Micro-F1**: Aggregate TP, FP, FN across all classes globally, then compute a single F1. Dominated by the majority class. > [!NOTE] > KTU Module 2 typically focuses on **binary classification** F-Measure, but the macro/micro distinction is a frequent 3-mark question. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why Harmonic Mean for F-Measure We start from the **generalized mean** formula. For two positive numbers $P$ and $R$ with weights $a$ and $b$ (where $a + b = 1$), the **weighted harmonic mean** is: $$H = \frac{1}{\dfrac{a}{P} + \dfrac{b}{R}}$$ For **F1-Score**, we want equal weights, so $a = b = \dfrac{1}{2}$: $$F_1 = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1/2}{P} + \dfrac{1/2}{R}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{P} + \dfrac{1}{R}\right)} = \frac{2}{\dfrac{1}{P} + \dfrac{1}{R}}$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $P \cdot R$: $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$ This is the **standard F1 formula**. ✓ ### 3.2 Generalization to Fβ For Fβ, we assign weight $\beta^2$ to Recall and weight $1$ to Precision (Recall's weight grows quadratically with β). With $a = \dfrac{1}{1 + \beta^2}$ and $b = \dfrac{\beta^2}{1 + \beta^2}$: $$F_{\beta} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1/(1+\beta^2)}{P} + \dfrac{\beta^2/(1+\beta^2)}{R}}$$ Simplifying: $$F_{\beta} = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$$ When $\beta = 1$: $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ ✓ (matches F1 derivation) ### 3.3 Alternative Derivation: F1 in terms of TP, FP, FN We know $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ and $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$. Step 1 — Express $P \cdot R$: $$P \cdot R = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{TP^2}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}$$ Step 2 — Express $P + R$: $$P + R = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = TP \cdot \frac{(TP+FN) + (TP+FP)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)} = \frac{TP(2 \cdot TP + FP + FN)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}$$ Step 3 — Divide $P \cdot R$ by $P + R$ and multiply by 2: $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP^2}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}}{\frac{TP(2 \cdot TP + FP + FN)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}} = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP(2 \cdot TP + FP + FN)} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$ **Final result:** $$\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **most exam-friendly form** — KTU 14-mark numerical problems often provide a raw confusion matrix and expect you to plug values directly into this equation. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A spam classifier produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of 1000 emails: | | Predicted Spam | Predicted Not-Spam | |--|----------------|---------------------| | **Actual Spam** | TP = 90 | FN = 10 | | **Actual Not-Spam** | FP = 20 | TN = 880 | **Step 1 — Extract values:** - $TP = 90$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 10$, $TN = 880$ **Step 2 — Compute Precision:** $$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{90}{90 + 20} = \frac{90}{110} = 0.8182$$ **Step 3 — Compute Recall:** $$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.9000$$ **Step 4 — Compute F1 using harmonic mean formula:** $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8182 \times 0.9000}{0.8182 + 0.9000} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7364}{1.7182} = 2 \times 0.4286 = 0.8571$$ **Step 5 — Verification using TP/FP/FN form:** $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \times 90}{2 \times 90 + 20 + 10} = \frac{180}{210} = 0.8571 \checkmark$$ **Step 6 — Interpret:** The classifier has an F1-Score of **0.8571 (85.71%)**, indicating strong balanced performance. ### 3.5 Comparative Edge Case Analysis Consider three hypothetical classifiers with varying Precision-Recall trade-offs to illustrate harmonic mean behavior: | Classifier | Precision (P) | Recall (R) | Arithmetic Mean $\frac{P+R}{2}$ | F1-Score | Observation | |------------|---------------|------------|-------------------------------|----------|-------------| | Model A | 1.00 | 0.10 | 0.550 | 0.1818 | High P, very low R → F1 collapses | | Model B | 0.55 | 0.55 | 0.550 | 0.5500 | Balanced → F1 = mean | | Model C | 0.90 | 0.80 | 0.850 | 0.8471 | Both high → F1 ≈ mean | > [!NOTE] > **Insight for KTU exam:** Only when $P = R$ does the F1 equal the arithmetic mean. Any deviation causes F1 ≤ arithmetic mean, exposing the **weak link principle** of the harmonic mean. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Confusion Matrix Reference Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Classifier Predictions on Test Set] --> B{Build Confusion Matrix} B --> C[True Positive TP
Correctly predicted positive] B --> D[False Positive FP
Type I Error
False Alarm] B --> E[True Negative TN
Correctly predicted negative] B --> F[False Negative FN
Type II Error
Missed Detection] C --> G[Compute Precision P] D --> G C --> H[Compute Recall R] F --> H G --> I[Apply F-Measure Formula] H --> I I --> J{Choose Beta Value} J --> K[Beta equals 0
F0 equals Precision] J --> L[Beta equals 1
F1 Score
Balanced] J --> M[Beta greater than 1
F2 Recall weighted] L --> N[Final F Score
Range 0 to 1] K --> N M --> N N --> O{High F Score?} O --> P[Yes
Good Classifier] O --> Q[No
Needs Tuning] ``` ### 4.2 Precision-Recall Trade-off Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT [INPUT METRICS] P1[Precision P] R1[Recall R] end subgraph COMPUTE [HARMONIC MEAN COMPUTATION] M1[Step 1
Multiply P times R] M2[Step 2
Add P and R] M3[Step 3
Divide Step 1 by Step 2] M4[Step 4
Multiply by 2] end subgraph OUTPUT [OUTPUT METRIC] O1[F1 Score
0 to 1] end P1 --> M1 R1 --> M1 P1 --> M2 R1 --> M2 M1 --> M3 M2 --> M3 M3 --> M4 M4 --> O1 style P1 fill:#e1f5ff style R1 fill:#e1f5ff style O1 fill:#d4edda style M1 fill:#fff3cd style M2 fill:#fff3cd style M3 fill:#fff3cd style M4 fill:#fff3cd ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: F-Measure in ML Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Dataset] --> B[Train Test Split] B --> C[Train Classifier Model] C --> D[Generate Predictions on Test Set] D --> E[Construct Confusion Matrix] E --> F[Extract TP FP TN FN] F --> G[Branch 1
Compute Precision] F --> H[Branch 2
Compute Recall] G --> I[Harmonic Mean Combiner
F1 equals 2PR over P plus R] H --> I I --> J[F1 Score Output] J --> K{Beta Selection} K --> L[F1 Balanced] K --> M[F2 Recall Heavy] K --> N[F0.5 Precision Heavy] L --> O[Final Model Evaluation Report] M --> O N --> O style A fill:#f8d7da style O fill:#d4edda style I fill:#fff3cd ``` ### 4.4 Multi-Class Extension: Macro vs Micro F1 ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Multi Class Classifier] --> B[Per Class F1 Calculation] B --> C1[Class 1 F1] B --> C2[Class 2 F1] B --> C3[Class 3 F1] C1 --> D{Macro or Micro} C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> E[Macro F1
Simple Average of per class F1] D --> F[Micro F1
Aggregate TP FP FN globally first] E --> G[Equal weight per class] F --> H[Weighted by class frequency] style A fill:#e1f5ff style E fill:#fff3cd style F fill:#fff3cd style G fill:#d4edda style H fill:#d4edda ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the F1-Score metric. Why is the harmonic mean used instead of the arithmetic mean in its computation?* **Model Answer:** The **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Precision (P) and Recall (R), defined as: $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$$ The **harmonic mean** is used (instead of the arithmetic mean) because it imposes a **strict penalty on imbalanced performance**. If either Precision or Recall is close to zero, the F1-Score is also driven close to zero, correctly reflecting the classifier's weakness. The arithmetic mean would mask such imbalances — e.g., arithmetic mean of (1.0, 0.1) = 0.55, misleadingly suggesting moderate performance, while the harmonic mean yields 0.181, truthfully exposing the poor Recall. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the Fβ-Measure? Explain the role of the parameter β with suitable examples.* **Model Answer:** The **Fβ-Measure** is a generalized form of the F-Score that allows weighting Precision and Recall differently: $$F_{\beta} = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$$ The parameter **β** controls the relative importance of Recall over Precision: - **β = 1** → F1-Score: Equal weight (balanced). - **β > 1** (e.g., β = 2) → Recall is weighted more heavily. Used in **medical diagnosis** where missing a positive case (false negative) is more dangerous than a false alarm. - **β < 1** (e.g., β = 0.5) → Precision is weighted more. Used in **spam filtering** where wrongly flagging a legitimate email is worse than missing a spam. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice #### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]* **(a)** Derive the F1-Score formula starting from the concept of weighted harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. Show that the formula simplifies to $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ when expressed in terms of confusion matrix entries. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A medical diagnosis model for detecting a rare disease is tested on 500 patients. The confusion matrix obtained is: | | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative | |--|--------------------|--------------------| | **Actually Positive** | 40 | 10 | | **Actually Negative** | 30 | 420 | Compute the Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Comment on the model's performance and whether F2-Score would be a more appropriate metric here. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Derivation:** Starting with the weighted harmonic mean of two positive numbers $P$ and $R$ with equal weights $\frac{1}{2}$: $$F_1 = \frac{1}{\frac{1/2}{P} + \frac{1/2}{R}} = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$ Substituting $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ and $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$: After algebraic simplification (as shown in Section 3.3), we obtain: $$\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}}$$ **(b) Numerical Computation:** Extracted values: $TP = 40$, $FP = 30$, $FN = 10$, $TN = 420$. **Precision:** $$P = \frac{40}{40 + 30} = \frac{40}{70} = 0.5714$$ **Recall:** $$R = \frac{40}{40 + 10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.8000$$ **F1-Score:** $$F_1 = \frac{2 \times 40}{2 \times 40 + 30 + 10} = \frac{80}{120} = 0.6667$$ **F2-Score Computation:** $$F_2 = 5 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{4P + R} = 5 \cdot \frac{0.5714 \times 0.8000}{4 \times 0.5714 + 0.8000} = 5 \cdot \frac{0.4571}{3.0856} = 5 \times 0.1481 = 0.7407$$ **Commentary:** The model's Recall (0.80) is significantly higher than its Precision (0.57), meaning it catches 80% of actual disease cases but produces many false alarms. Since this is a **medical diagnosis scenario** where missing a positive case (False Negative) can be life-threatening, the **F2-Score (0.7407) > F1-Score (0.6667)** is a more appropriate evaluation metric because it gives Recall higher weight. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]* **(a)** Define Precision, Recall, and F-Measure. Explain with a real-world example why Accuracy alone is insufficient for evaluating classifiers on imbalanced datasets. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An e-commerce website's recommendation engine produces the following results on 10,000 products: TP = 4500, FP = 500, FN = 1500, TN = 3500. Calculate (i) Accuracy, (ii) Precision, (iii) Recall, and (iv) F1-Score. Compare the four metrics and explain which one best represents the system's performance. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Definitions and Imbalanced Dataset Discussion:** - **Precision (P)** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ — Fraction of predicted positives that are actually positive. - **Recall (R)** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — Fraction of actual positives that were correctly identified. - **F-Measure** = Harmonic mean of P and R, providing a single balanced metric. **Real-World Example:** In a credit-card fraud detection system, 99.9% of transactions are legitimate. A trivial classifier that predicts *"no fraud"* for every transaction achieves **99.9% accuracy**, but Recall = 0% because it catches no fraud cases at all. Accuracy is thus dangerously misleading. The **F1-Score** would correctly evaluate this classifier as near-zero, exposing its uselessness. **(b) Numerical Computation:** Extracted values: $TP = 4500$, $FP = 500$, $FN = 1500$, $TN = 3500$. **(i) Accuracy:** $$Acc = \frac{4500 + 3500}{10000} = \frac{8000}{10000} = 0.80$$ **(ii) Precision:** $$P = \frac{4500}{4500 + 500} = \frac{4500}{5000} = 0.90$$ **(iii) Recall:** $$R = \frac{4500}{4500 + 1500} = \frac{4500}{6000} = 0.75$$ **(iv) F1-Score:** $$F_1 = \frac{2 \times 4500}{2 \times 4500 + 500 + 1500} = \frac{9000}{11000} = 0.8182$$ **Comparison and Conclusion:** | Metric | Value | Insight | |--------|-------|---------| | Accuracy | 0.80 | Deceptively high — masks poor positive-class detection | | Precision | 0.90 | Strong — when it recommends, it's usually right | | Recall | 0.75 | Moderate — misses 25% of relevant recommendations | | **F1-Score** | **0.8182** | **Best single representative metric** — balances both | The **F1-Score (0.8182)** is the most representative single metric because it penalizes the relatively low Recall while acknowledging high Precision. Accuracy alone would overstate the system's effectiveness. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Confusing Precision/Recall formulas** — A frequent error is swapping FP and FN in the denominator. Remember: **Precision uses FP (column error)**, **Recall uses FN (row error)**. > 2. **Using arithmetic mean** — If a question asks "average of P and R", students often write $\frac{P+R}{2}$. The F-Measure specifically requires the **harmonic mean**, $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$. > 3. **Forgetting the β² factor** — In Fβ, Recall's weight is $\beta^2$, not $\beta$. Writing $(1+\beta)$ instead of $(1+\beta^2)$ costs 2 marks. > 4. **Ignoring the TN term in F1** — F1 depends only on TP, FP, FN. Students sometimes incorrectly include TN in the denominator, which is wrong. > 5. **Rounding errors** — KTU expects answers to **4 decimal places** for fractional metrics. Writing "0.81" instead of "0.8182" may cost 0.5 marks in a 7-mark sub-question. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — F-Measure** - **Definition**: F-Measure = Harmonic mean of Precision (P) and Recall (R); ranges from 0 to 1. - **Core Formula (F1)**: $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ - **TP/FP/FN Form**: $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ — exam-friendly, no intermediate steps needed. - **General Fβ Formula**: $F_{\beta} = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$ - **β Interpretation**: - β = 1 → Balanced (default for KTU) - β > 1 → Recall-heavy (medical, safety-critical) - β < 1 → Precision-heavy (spam filters, search ranking) - **Why Harmonic Mean?** Penalizes imbalance; if either P or R → 0, F-Measure → 0. - **F1 = Arithmetic Mean only when P = R**; otherwise F1 < Arithmetic Mean. - **Multi-class variants**: Macro-F1 (unweighted per-class average) vs Micro-F1 (globally aggregated). - **Use F1 over Accuracy when**: dataset is imbalanced or false positives/negatives have asymmetric costs. - **Do NOT include TN** in the F1 denominator — it depends only on TP, FP, FN. - **Sanity check**: $F_1 \leq \min(P, R)$ always holds; verify your computed F1 against this. - **Common KTU traps**: wrong denominator (FP vs FN), using arithmetic mean, forgetting $\beta^2$, omitting decimal precision.

topics.title: Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (ROC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (ROC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve** is a fundamental evaluation metric in machine learning used to assess the performance of a **binary classifier** across all possible classification thresholds. It is a two-dimensional graphical plot in which the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** is plotted on the **Y-axis** and the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** is plotted on the **X-axis**, thereby illustrating the *trade-off between sensitivity and specificity*.

The ROC curve was originally developed during **World War II** for radar signal detection analysis and was later adopted into psychology and medical statistics before becoming a cornerstone of modern machine learning evaluation. In the KTU 2024 PEMUT755 syllabus, the ROC curve is positioned as the **standard tool for threshold-independent model evaluation**.

## 1.2 The Two Axes: TPR and FPR

The two fundamental quantities that define the ROC space are:

$$TPR \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \quad \text{(also called Recall or Sensitivity)}$$

$$FPR \;=\; \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \quad \text{(equals 1 − Specificity)}$$

Where:
- **TP** = True Positives (correctly predicted positive cases)
- **FN** = False Negatives (missed positive cases)
- **FP** = False Positives (incorrectly flagged negative cases)
- **TN** = True Negatives (correctly rejected negative cases)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spam Filter Tuner" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a spam email filter that uses a "spamminess score" from 0 to 100.**

When you set the threshold at **50**, every email scoring above 50 goes to the spam folder. But what if you lower the threshold to **20**? You will *catch more spam* (higher TPR) but you will also *wrongly tag some genuine emails as spam* (higher FPR). If you raise the threshold to **90**, you will *rarely misclassify genuine mail* (low FPR) but you will *miss a lot of spam* (low TPR).

**The ROC curve is simply the complete "scoreboard" of every possible threshold setting** — it traces how TPR and FPR move together as you sweep the threshold from 0 to 100. A perfect filter would jump straight to catching 100% of spam (TPR=1) while keeping zero genuine emails out of the spam folder (FPR=0) — landing at the top-left corner **(0, 1)**.

## 1.4 Why ROC? The Threshold-Independence Argument

> [!NOTE]
> Most classification metrics (accuracy, precision, F1-score) require you to **commit to one threshold** before measuring performance. The ROC curve lifts this restriction by showing performance at **every conceivable threshold simultaneously**, giving a complete picture of a model's discriminative power.

This is crucial in real-world AI deployments:
- **Medical diagnosis**: A cancer-detection model may need very high TPR (catching all sick patients) at the cost of some FPR (extra biopsies).
- **Fraud detection in banking**: The acceptable FPR depends on the cost of investigation.
- **Spam filtering**: Cost of missing a real email vs. cost of an extra spam in inbox.

## 1.5 The Four Critical Reference Points in ROC Space

| Point (FPR, TPR) | Interpretation | Model Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **(0, 0)** | No positives predicted | Predicts "negative" for everything |
| **(1, 1)** | Everything predicted positive | Predicts "positive" for everything |
| **(0, 1)** | **Perfect classifier** | All positives caught, no false alarms |
| **(1, 0)** | Worst possible | Missed every positive, false-alarmed on every negative |
| **Diagonal line (y = x)** | Random guessing | No discriminative power |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown: How the ROC Curve Is Constructed

A binary classifier (e.g., Logistic Regression, SVM, Neural Network) does **not** output a hard 0/1 label directly. It outputs a **continuous probability score** $P(y=1 \mid x) \in [0, 1]$. The threshold $\tau$ converts this into a decision:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } P(y=1 \mid x) \geq \tau \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every unique threshold τ generates a unique (FPR, TPR) pair**, and the ROC curve is the locus of all such pairs as τ sweeps from 1 down to 0.

### Step-by-Step Construction Logic

1. **Train the classifier** and obtain probability scores for every test sample.
2. **Sort** all $N$ test samples in descending order of predicted probability $P(y=1 \mid x)$.
3. **Initialize** the ROC plot at point $(0, 0)$.
4. **Walk down the sorted list**, one sample at a time. For each sample:
   - If the sample is a **true positive** → move **up** by $\frac{1}{P}$ (where $P$ = total positives), so TPR increases.
   - If the sample is a **false positive** (i.e., a negative sample that the model scores high) → move **right** by $\frac{1}{N}$ (where $N$ = total negatives), so FPR increases.
5. **Connect all visited points** to obtain the ROC curve.

## 2.2 The Confusion Matrix Foundation

The four-cell foundation upon which ROC analysis is built is the **Confusion Matrix**:

|  | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP | FN |
| **Actual Negative** | FP | TN |

From this 2×2 matrix, every metric in ROC analysis is derived.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every formula a KTU student is expected to know** for ROC-related questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

| # | Metric | Formula | Range | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **True Positive Rate (TPR)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of actual positives correctly identified |
| 2 | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of actual negatives wrongly flagged |
| 3 | **True Negative Rate (TNR) / Specificity** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of actual negatives correctly rejected |
| 4 | **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $\dfrac{FN}{FN+TP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of actual positives missed |
| 5 | **Precision (PPV)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Of those predicted positive, how many are correct |
| 6 | **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Same as TPR — recall = sensitivity |
| 7 | **F1-Score** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall |
| 8 | **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Overall correctness — misleading on imbalanced data |
| 9 | **AUC (Area Under Curve)** | $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Probability that the model ranks a random positive higher than a random negative |
| 10 | **Youden's J Statistic** | $J = TPR - FPR$ | $[0, 1]$ | Optimal threshold maximises $J$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
