## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Mean and Variance of k-Fold CV Score

**Problem Setup:** Suppose we perform 5-fold cross validation on a dataset using a decision tree classifier and obtain the following fold accuracies:

$$s_1 = 0.86, \quad s_2 = 0.82, \quad s_3 = 0.88, \quad s_4 = 0.84, \quad s_5 = 0.90$$

**Goal:** Derive the CV score (mean) and the variance / standard deviation.

#### Step 1: Write down the definition of the k-Fold CV score.

The CV score is defined as the arithmetic mean of the per-fold scores:

$$\text{CV}(k) = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i$$

Here, $k = 5$ and the scores are listed above.

#### Step 2: Substitute the fold scores into the formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV}(5) &= \frac{1}{5} \left( s_1 + s_2 + s_3 + s_4 + s_5 \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{5} \left( 0.86 + 0.82 + 0.88 + 0.84 + 0.90 \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{5} \times 4.30 \\
&= 0.86
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **5-fold CV accuracy** for the model is **0.86 (or 86%)**.

#### Step 3: Derive the variance of the CV score.

The unbiased sample variance of the per-fold scores is:

$$\text{Var}(\text{CV}) = \frac{1}{k-1} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (s_i - \bar{s})^{2}$$

where $\bar{s} = \text{CV}(k) = 0.86$.

#### Step 4: Compute the squared deviations.

For each fold, compute $(s_i - \bar{s})^{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(s_1 - \bar{s})^{2} &= (0.86 - 0.86)^{2} = (0.00)^{2} = 0.0000 \\
(s_2 - \bar{s})^{2} &= (0.82 - 0.86)^{2} = (-0.04)^{2} = 0.0016 \\
(s_3 - \bar{s})^{2} &= (0.88 - 0.86)^{2} = (+0.02)^{2} = 0.0004 \\
(s_4 - \bar{s})^{2} &= (0.84 - 0.86)^{2} = (-0.02)^{2} = 0.0004 \\
(s_5 - \bar{s})^{2} &= (0.90 - 0.86)^{2} = (+0.04)^{2} = 0.0016
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Sum the squared deviations.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} (s_i - \bar{s})^{2} = 0.0000 + 0.0016 + 0.0004 + 0.0004 + 0.0016 = 0.0040
$$

#### Step 6: Compute the variance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\text{CV}) &= \frac{1}{k-1} \times 0.0040 \\
&= \frac{1}{4} \times 0.0040 \\
&= 0.0010
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7: Compute the standard deviation (the KTU "CV Std").

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{CV}} &= \sqrt{\text{Var}(\text{CV})} \\
&= \sqrt{0.0010} \\
&\approx 0.0316
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 8: Final Reporting (KTU Board Format).

> **Model Performance Report:** 5-Fold CV Accuracy = **0.86 $\pm$ 0.0316** (i.e., **86% $\pm$ 3.16%**)

> [!NOTE]
> The standard deviation provides the **confidence interval** for the model's expected accuracy on a new, unseen dataset drawn from the same distribution.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: 4-Fold CV on a Numeric Dataset

**Problem Statement:** Given a dataset $D = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{20}\}$ containing 20 samples, perform **4-fold cross validation**. List the test fold for each iteration and compute the training set size.

**Step 1: Determine the fold size.**

$$
\text{Fold size} = \left\lfloor \frac{n}{k} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{20}{4} \right\rfloor = 5
$$

**Step 2: Create the 4 folds (after shuffling).**

Assume the shuffled indices are:

$$
F_1 = \{x_3, x_7, x_{12}, x_{15}, x_{18}\}
$$
$$
F_2 = \{x_1, x_5, x_9, x_{13}, x_{19}\}
$$
$$
F_3 = \{x_2, x_6, x_{10}, x_{14}, x_{20}\}
$$
$$
F_4 = \{x_4, x_8, x_{11}, x_{16}, x_{17}\}
$$

**Step 3: List the train-test split for each iteration.**

| Iteration $i$ | Test Fold $F_i$ | Training Set Composition | Train Size | Test Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_1$ | $F_2 \cup F_3 \cup F_4$ | 15 | 5 |
| 2 | $F_2$ | $F_1 \cup F_3 \cup F_4$ | 15 | 5 |
| 3 | $F_3$ | $F_1 \cup F_2 \cup F_4$ | 15 | 5 |
| 4 | $F_4$ | $F_1 \cup F_2 \cup F_3$ | 15 | 5 |

**Step 4: Verify the disjoint and exhaustive property.**

$$
F_1 \cap F_2 \cap F_3 \cap F_4 = \emptyset \quad \text{(disjoint)}
$$
$$
F_1 \cup F_2 \cup F_3 \cup F_4 = D \quad \text{(exhaustive)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical pattern** for any KTU 4-fold or 5-fold CV numeric problem. Always show the fold construction explicitly in the exam.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Bias of k-Fold CV Estimator

**Claim:** As $k$ increases, the bias of the k-fold CV estimator decreases.

**Reasoning (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Let the model trained on a dataset of size $m$ have expected error $\text{Err}(m)$. The bias of the CV estimator is the difference between the expected CV error and the true generalization error $\text{Err}(n)$ trained on the full dataset of size $n$:

$$
\text{Bias} = \mathbb{E}[\text{Err}_{\text{CV}}] - \text{Err}(n)
$$

**Step 2:** In k-fold CV, each model is trained on a dataset of size $m = n(k-1)/k$. So the expected error per fold is $\text{Err}(n(k-1)/k)$.

**Step 3:** The CV error is the mean of fold errors:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\text{Err}_{\text{CV}}] = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Err}\left(\frac{n(k-1)}{k}\right) = \text{Err}\left(\frac{n(k-1)}{k}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Since learning curves are typically *monotonically decreasing* in $m$:

$$
\text{Err}\left(\frac{n(k-1)}{k}\right) \geq \text{Err}(n)
$$

Thus the CV error is **optimistically biased** (or pessimistically, depending on convention — here it overestimates the error since less data is used).

**Step 5:** As $k \to n$ (i.e., LOO), $\frac{n(k-1)}{k} \to n - 1 \approx n$, so the bias $\to 0$.

**Step 6:** As $k \to 2$, $\frac{n(k-1)}{k} \to n/2$, so the bias is maximized (we train on only half the data).

$$\boxed{\text{Therefore, bias of k-fold CV} = \text{Err}\left(\frac{n(k-1)}{k}\right) - \text{Err}(n) \quad \text{and it is monotonically decreasing in } k}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing CV Strategies (KTU Board Comparison Table)

| Strategy | Train Size | Test Size | Number of Iterations | Bias | Variance | Computational Cost | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hold-Out (70/30) | $0.7n$ | $0.3n$ | 1 | High | High | **Lowest** | Huge datasets, quick prototyping |
| k-Fold (k=5) | $4n/5$ | $n/5$ | 5 | Moderate | Moderate | Medium | General-purpose, **default choice** |
| k-Fold (k=10) | $9n/10$ | $n/10$ | 10 | Low | Low | High | Most KTU exam problems |
| LOOCV (k=n) | $n-1$ | 1 | $n$ | Lowest | Highest | **Highest** | Very small datasets |
| Stratified k-Fold | $\frac{(k-1)n}{k}$ (class-preserved) | $\frac{n}{k}$ (class-preserved) | $k$ | Low | Low | Medium | Imbalanced classification |
| Repeated k-Fold | varies | varies | $k \times r$ | Low | **Lowest** | Very High | Performance benchmarking |
| Nested CV | varies | varies | $k_{\text{out}} \times k_{\text{in}}$ | Lowest | Low | **Highest** | Unbiased hyperparameter tuning |
| Time Series (Walk-Forward) | expanding window | sliding window | $T - s$ | Moderate | Moderate | Medium-High | Forecasting, sequential data |

> [!NOTE]
> The notation $\binom{n}{p}$ in the table refers to the **binomial coefficient** ("n choose p"). In the context of LpOCV, it represents the number of distinct ways to choose $p$ samples to leave out as the test set.

> **Interpretation Note:** "$k \times r$" means k-fold repeated $r$ times; "expanding window" means the training set grows cumulatively with each iteration.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Standard 5-Fold Cross Validation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start: Dataset D of size n]) --> shuffleStep[Shuffle D Randomly]
    shuffleStep --> splitStep[Split D into 5 Folds F1 to F5]
    splitStep --> initLoop[For i = 1 to 5]
    initLoop --> setTest[Set Test Fold = Fi]
    setTest --> setTrain[Set Train Set = D minus Fi]
    setTrain --> trainModel[Train Model Mi on Train Set]
    trainModel --> evalModel[Evaluate Mi on Fi to get score si]
    evalModel --> checkIter{i less than 5}
    checkIter -->|Yes| initLoop
    checkIter -->|No| aggregate[Aggregate: Compute mean and std of all si]
    aggregate --> reportNode([Report: CV Score = mean plus minus std])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Stratified k-Fold CV (Class-Preserving Splits)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dataNode[Dataset D with Class Labels] --> classCount[Count Samples per Class]
    classCount --> stratSplit[Stratified Split Maintaining Class Ratio]
    stratSplit --> fold1Node[Fold 1: 80 percent Class A, 20 percent Class B]
    stratSplit --> fold2Node[Fold 2: 80 percent Class A, 20 percent Class B]
    stratSplit --> fold3Node[Fold 3: 80 percent Class A, 20 percent Class B]
    fold1Node --> trainModel1[Train and Evaluate Iteration 1]
    fold2Node --> trainModel2[Train and Evaluate Iteration 2]
    fold3Node --> trainModel3[Train and Evaluate Iteration 3]
    trainModel1 --> finalScore[Mean CV Score with Class Balance Preserved]
    trainModel2 --> finalScore
    trainModel3 --> finalScore
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Nested Cross Validation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    outerStart([Outer Loop: Generalization Estimation]) --> outerFold[Partition D into k_outer Folds]
    outerFold --> outerTrain[Train Set Outer minus Test Fold]
    outerTrain --> innerStart([Inner Loop: Hyperparameter Tuning])
    innerStart --> innerFold[Partition Outer Train into k_inner Folds]
    innerFold --> hpSearch[Grid Search over Hyperparameters]
    hpSearch --> bestHP[Select Best Hyperparameter Config]
    bestHP --> retrain[Retrain on Full Outer Train Set]
    retrain --> outerTest[Evaluate on Outer Test Fold]
    outerTest --> nextOuter{All Outer Folds Done}
    nextOuter -->|No| outerFold
    nextOuter -->|Yes| outerReport([Unbiased Generalization Estimate])
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Time Series Walk-Forward Validation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    timeStart([Time Series Data T1 to Tn]) --> window1[Train: T1 to T5, Test: T6]
    window1 --> forecast1[Forecast and Evaluate]
    forecast1 --> window2[Train: T1 to T6, Test: T7]
    window2 --> forecast2[Forecast and Evaluate]
    forecast2 --> window3[Train: T1 to T7, Test: T8]
    window3 --> forecast3[Forecast and Evaluate]
    forecast3 --> windowN[Train: T1 to Tn-1, Test: Tn]
    windowN --> finalReport([Report Mean Forecast Error])
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CV Pipeline in an ML System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputNode[Raw Dataset Input] --> preprocessNode[Preprocessing and Cleaning]
    preprocessNode --> splitNode[Train Test Full Split 80 percent Train, 20 percent Test Holdout]
    splitNode --> trainPool[Training Pool]
    splitNode --> holdoutPool[Held Out True Test Set Never Touched During CV]
    trainPool --> cvEngine[Cross Validation Engine]
    cvEngine --> kfoldMod[k-Fold Module]
    cvEngine --> stratMod[Stratified Module]
    cvEngine --> timeMod[Time Series Module]
    cvEngine --> looMod[LOOCV Module]
    kfoldMod --> scoreAggregator[Score Aggregator: mean, std, confidence interval]
    stratMod --> scoreAggregator
    timeMod --> scoreAggregator
    looMod --> scoreAggregator
    scoreAggregator --> hpSelector[Hyperparameter Selector Best Config]
    hpSelector --> finalTrain[Final Model Training on Full Train Pool]
    finalTrain --> finalEval[Final Evaluation on Holdout Pool]
    finalEval --> deployNode([Model Deployment with Performance Certificate])
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q: Define Cross Validation. List any two advantages of k-Fold Cross Validation over the Hold-Out method.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Cross Validation is a statistical resampling technique used to evaluate the generalization performance of a machine learning model by partitioning the data into multiple complementary subsets, training the model on some and validating on the others, and aggregating the results.

**Advantages of k-Fold over Hold-Out (any two):**

1. **Reduced variance in performance estimation:** Since the model is trained and evaluated $k$ times on different splits, the average score has a much smaller variance than a single hold-out split.
2. **More efficient use of data:** In k-Fold, every observation is used for both training and testing (across different folds), whereas in Hold-Out, the test samples are never used for training. This is critical for small datasets.
3. **Reduced risk of an "unlucky" split:** A single bad random split in Hold-Out can produce misleading performance estimates; k-Fold averages out this stochasticity.

*(Word count: ~120 words | Time to write in exam: ~6 minutes)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`

**Q: What is Leave-One-Out Cross Validation (LOOCV)? State one advantage and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** LOOCV is a special case of k-Fold Cross Validation where $k = n$ (the number of samples in the dataset). In each iteration, exactly one sample is used as the test set and the remaining $n-1$ samples form the training set. The procedure is repeated $n$ times so that every sample acts as the test sample exactly once.

**Advantage:**
- **Negligible bias:** Since the model is trained on $n-1$ samples (almost the entire dataset), the training error is very close to the true generalization error, yielding an almost unbiased estimate.

**Disadvantage:**
- **High computational cost and high variance:** For $n$ iterations with $n$ model re-trainings, the computational expense is enormous for large datasets. Also, the $n$ trained models are highly correlated (they differ by only one sample), which leads to high variance in the LOOCV estimate.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q: Explain the step-by-step procedure of 10-Fold Cross Validation. Illustrate with a concrete example of a dataset containing 50 samples, clearly showing the test fold and training set size for each iteration.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Choose k.** We set $k = 10$ as per the requirement.

**Step 2: Compute the fold size.**

$$
\text{Fold size} = \frac{n}{k} = \frac{50}{10} = 5 \text{ samples per fold}
$$

**Step 3: Shuffle and partition.** The 50 samples are randomly shuffled and partitioned into 10 disjoint folds $F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_{10}$, each of size 5.

**Step 4: Execute the 10 iterations.** The detailed iteration table is:

| Iteration $i$ | Test Fold $F_i$ | Training Set (Union of Other Folds) | Train Size | Test Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_1$ | $F_2 \cup F_3 \cup \ldots \cup F_{10}$ | 45 | 5 |
| 2 | $F_2$ | $F_1 \cup F_3 \cup \ldots \cup F_{10}$ | 45 | 5 |
| 3 | $F_3$ | $F_1 \cup F_2 \cup F_4 \cup \ldots \cup F_{10}$ | 45 | 5 |
| 4 | $F_4$ | $F_1 \cup F_2 \cup F_3 \cup F_5 \cup \ldots \cup F_{10}$ | 45 | 5 |
| 5 | $F_5$ | All folds except $F_5$ | 45 | 5 |
| 6 | $F_6$ | All folds except $F_6$ | 45 | 5 |
| 7 | $F_7$ | All folds except $F_7$ | 45 | 5 |
| 8 | $F_8$ | All folds except $F_8$ | 45 | 5 |
| 9 | $F_9$ | All folds except $F_9$ | 45 | 5 |
| 10 | $F_{10}$ | $F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \ldots \cup F_9$ | 45 | 5 |

**Step 5: Train and evaluate.** For each iteration $i$, a fresh model $M_i$ is trained on the training set (45 samples) and evaluated on the test fold (5 samples) to obtain a score $s_i$.

**Step 6: Aggregate.** The final 10-Fold CV score is:

$$\text{CV}(10) = \frac{1}{10} \sum_{i=1}^{10} s_i$$

The variance of the estimate is:

$$\text{Var}(\text{CV}) = \frac{1}{9} \sum_{i=1}^{10} (s_i - \bar{s})^{2}$$

**Conclusion:** 10-Fold CV uses 90% of the data for training in every iteration, providing a low-bias, low-variance estimate of the model's generalization performance.

---

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q: A researcher performs 5-Fold Cross Validation on a Naive Bayes classifier and obtains the following fold accuracies: $\{0.91, 0.88, 0.93, 0.86, 0.92\}$. Compute the CV score, the variance, and the standard deviation. Comment on the reliability of the model.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the CV score (mean).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{s} &= \frac{1}{5}(0.91 + 0.88 + 0.93 + 0.86 + 0.92) \\
&= \frac{1}{5} \times 4.50 \\
&= 0.90
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **5-Fold CV accuracy = 0.90 (i.e., 90%)**.

**Step 2: Compute the squared deviations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(s_1 - \bar{s})^{2} &= (0.91 - 0.90)^{2} = (0.01)^{2} = 0.0001 \\
(s_2 - \bar{s})^{2} &= (0.88 - 0.90)^{2} = (-0.02)^{2} = 0.0004 \\
(s_3 - \bar{s})^{2} &= (0.93 - 0.90)^{2} = (0.03)^{2} = 0.0009 \\
(s_4 - \bar{s})^{2} &= (0.86 - 0.90)^{2} = (-0.04)^{2} = 0.0016 \\
(s_5 - \bar{s})^{2} &= (0.92 - 0.90)^{2} = (0.02)^{2} = 0.0004
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum the squared deviations.**

$$
\sum (s_i - \bar{s})^{2} = 0.0001 + 0.0004 + 0.0009 + 0.0016 + 0.0004 = 0.0034
$$

**Step 4: Compute the variance (unbiased estimator).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\text{CV}) &= \frac{1}{k-1} \sum (s_i - \bar{s})^{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{4} \times 0.0034 \\
&= 0.00085
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the standard deviation.**

$$
\sigma_{\text{CV}} = \sqrt{0.00085} \approx 0.0292
$$

**Step 6: Final performance report.**

> **Model Performance:** 5-Fold CV Accuracy = **0.90 $\pm$ 0.0292** (i.e., **90% $\pm$ 2.92%**)

**Comment on reliability:** The model has a high mean accuracy (90%) and a low standard deviation ($\approx 2.92\%$), indicating that its performance is **consistent and stable** across all 5 folds. The 95% confidence interval is approximately $\bar{s} \pm 2\sigma = 0.90 \pm 0.058$, i.e., the model's true accuracy is between ~84.2% and ~95.8% with 95% confidence. This is a reliable and trustworthy model.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]`

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q: Explain the Bias-Variance trade-off in the context of k-Fold Cross Validation. How does the choice of k affect bias, variance, and computational cost?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define Bias and Variance of an estimator.**

- **Bias** of the CV estimator is the systematic difference between the expected CV error and the true generalization error:

$$\text{Bias} = \mathbb{E}[\text{Err}_{\text{CV}}] - \text{Err}(n)$$

- **Variance** of the CV estimator is the spread of the per-fold scores around the mean CV score:

$$\text{Var}(\text{CV}) = \frac{1}{k-1} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (s_i - \bar{s})^{2}$$

**Step 2: How does k affect training set size?**

In k-Fold CV, the training set size per fold is $m = n(k-1)/k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k = 2 &\Rightarrow m = 0.5n \\
k = 5 &\Rightarrow m = 0.8n \\
k = 10 &\Rightarrow m = 0.9n \\
k = n &\Rightarrow m = n-1 \approx n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Bias analysis.** Since learning curves are typically monotonically decreasing, $\text{Err}(m) \geq \text{Err}(n)$ for $m \leq n$. Hence:

- Smaller $k$ $\Rightarrow$ Smaller $m$ $\Rightarrow$ **Higher bias**.
- Larger $k$ $\Rightarrow$ Larger $m$ $\Rightarrow$ **Lower bias** (LOO has the lowest bias).

**Step 4: Variance analysis.** As $k$ increases, the training sets across folds become *more similar* (they overlap in more samples), causing the trained models to be *highly correlated*. Correlated estimators have high variance when averaged. Hence:

- Smaller $k$ $\Rightarrow$ Training sets differ a lot $\Rightarrow$ **Lower variance**.
- Larger $k$ $\Rightarrow$ Training sets nearly identical $\Rightarrow$ **Higher variance** (LOO has the highest variance).

**Step 5: Computational cost analysis.** Each iteration requires a full model retraining, so total cost $\propto k \times \text{cost}(\text{one training})$.

- $k = 2$ $\Rightarrow$ **2 trainings** (cheapest).
- $k = 10$ $\Rightarrow$ **10 trainings** (moderate).
- $k = n$ (LOO) $\Rightarrow$ **$n$ trainings** (most expensive).

**Step 6: The trade-off summary.**

| $k$ | Bias | Variance | Cost | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small (2–3) | High | Low | Low | Underfits the data, optimistic variance |
| Moderate (5–10) | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | **Sweet spot — default choice** |
| Large (LOO) | Lowest | Highest | Highest | Most accurate but impractical for large $n$ |

**Step 7: Conclusion.** The optimal $k$ is a trade-off: $k = 5$ or $k = 10$ provides the best balance between low bias, low variance, and manageable computational cost for most real-world ML problems.

---

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q: What is Stratified k-Fold Cross Validation? Why is it preferred over standard k-Fold CV for imbalanced datasets? Demonstrate with a numerical example where the original dataset has 100 samples: 90 of Class 0 and 10 of Class 1.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition of Stratified k-Fold CV.**

> **Stratified k-Fold Cross Validation** is a variant of k-Fold CV in which each fold preserves the **original class distribution** of the dataset. The splitting is performed *stratum-by-stratum* (i.e., class-by-class) to ensure that the proportion of each class in every fold mirrors its proportion in the full dataset.

**Step 2: Why standard k-Fold fails on imbalanced data.**

In standard k-Fold, random shuffling can lead to folds with extreme class imbalance. For example, in a 90:10 dataset, one fold might accidentally contain only 1 or 0 Class-1 samples. This causes:
- Unreliable performance estimation (the metric fluctuates wildly).
- Models that perform well on the majority class but fail on the minority class.
- Misleading precision, recall, and F1-score values.

**Step 3: Numerical demonstration with $k = 5$.**

Given: $n = 100$, Class 0 = 90 samples, Class 1 = 10 samples.

**Original class proportions:**
- Class 0: $90/100 = 90\%$
- Class 1: $10/100 = 10\%$

**Per-fold target counts (for $k = 5$):**
- Fold size = $100/5 = 20$ samples.
- Class 0 in each fold = $20 \times 0.90 = 18$ samples.
- Class 1 in each fold = $20 \times 0.10 = 2$ samples.

**Stratified k-Fold partition (each fold maintains 18 Class 0 and 2 Class 1):**

| Fold | Class 0 Samples | Class 1 Samples | Total | Class Ratio (0:1) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $F_1$ | 18 | 2 | 20 | 18:2 = 90%:10% |
| $F_2$ | 18 | 2 | 20 | 18:2 = 90%:10% |
| $F_3$ | 18 | 2 | 20 | 18:2 = 90%:10% |
| $F_4$ | 18 | 2 | 20 | 18:2 = 90%:10% |
| $F_5$ | 18 | 2 | 20 | 18:2 = 90%:10% |
| **Total** | **90** | **10** | **100** | **90%:10%** |

**Step 4: Comparison with standard k-Fold (hypothetical bad split).**

In a *bad* standard k-Fold split, one possible distribution could be:

| Fold | Class 0 | Class 1 | Class Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| $F_1$ | 22 | 0 | 100%:0% (no minority class!) |
| $F_2$ | 18 | 2 | 90%:10% |
| $F_3$ | 17 | 3 | 85%:15% |
| $F_4$ | 19 | 1 | 95%:5% |
| $F_5$ | 14 | 4 | 78%:22% |

This shows that standard k-Fold can produce folds with **zero minority samples**, making evaluation meaningless. Stratified k-Fold **guarantees** a minimum number of minority samples in every fold.

**Step 5: Conclusion.** Stratified k-Fold is the **gold standard** for imbalanced classification tasks (e.g., fraud detection, medical diagnosis, anomaly detection) because it ensures every fold is a faithful miniature of the entire dataset, leading to stable, unbiased, and reproducible model evaluation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the disjoint property:** Students often forget to verify that the folds are mutually exclusive. Always state explicitly that $F_i \cap F_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$.
> 2. **Confusing bias and variance direction:** Some students incorrectly state that LOOCV has *low* variance. **The truth:** LOOCV has the *lowest bias* but the *highest variance*. This is a classic trap question.
> 3. **Skipping the shuffle step:** Random shuffling is *required* before partitioning. Forgetting this step loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Reporting only the mean in CV problems:** Always report **mean $\pm$ standard deviation** (or 95% CI). A bare mean without a spread measure is incomplete and may lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Using the test fold for training in the same iteration:** This is **data leakage** and is the single most common conceptual error. State clearly that *the test fold is held out* in every iteration.
> 6. **Misapplying k-Fold to time series data:** Shuffling time series data **destroys temporal causality**. Use **Walk-Forward Validation** instead.
> 7. **Writing $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table:** Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\vert x \vert$ (with `\vert` LaTeX) to avoid breaking the table syntax in your answer script.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Cross Validation** is a *resampling* technique for robust model evaluation; it is **not** a learning algorithm.
- The **CV score** is the mean of the per-fold scores, with **standard deviation** as a measure of stability.
- **$k = 10$** is the empirical default for most practical problems (Kohavi's recommendation).
- **LOOCV** = $k = n$; it has the **lowest bias** but the **highest variance** and the **highest computational cost**.
- **Stratified k-Fold** is *mandatory* for imbalanced classification datasets.
- **Time Series data** requires **Walk-Forward Validation** — never shuffle temporally ordered data.
- **Nested CV** is the correct method for *unbiased* hyperparameter tuning; using simple CV for tuning introduces optimistic bias.
- **Bias $\downarrow$ and Variance $\uparrow$** as $k$ increases from 2 to $n$ (a classic trade-off).
- **Disjoint + Exhaustive** are the two non-negotiable properties of fold construction: $F_i \cap F_j = \emptyset$ and $\bigcup F_i = D$.
- **Train set size** in k-Fold $= n(k-1)/k$; **Test set size** $= n/k$.
- **Generalization error** is the ultimate target metric — CV is a *proxy estimator* for it.
- **Avoid data leakage:** the test fold must be *completely held out* during training in every iteration.
- **Repeated k-Fold** = $k$-Fold executed $r$ times with different shuffles; reduces variance further.
- **LpOCV** is combinatorially expensive: $N_{\text{iter}} = \binom{n}{p}$; used only for very small datasets.
- The **95% Confidence Interval** for the CV score is approximately $\bar{s} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sigma / \sqrt{k}$.
- **Kohavi's 1995 empirical study** (a foundational ML paper) established $k = 10$ as the practical default for model selection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised Learning and Dimensionality Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised Learning and Dimensionality Reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Unsupervised Learning: Formal Definition

**Unsupervised Learning** is a paradigm of machine learning in which the algorithm is trained on a dataset consisting of input vectors $\mathbf{X} = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$ **without any corresponding target labels or supervisory signals**. The model's objective is to discover hidden structure, intrinsic patterns, groupings, or low-dimensional representations embedded within the data.

Mathematically, the learning task is to approximate a function:

$$f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$$

where $\mathcal{X}$ is the input feature space and $\mathcal{Y}$ represents an inferred structure (clusters, manifolds, latent variables) — *not* a predefined ground-truth label set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT755, Module 3):** The core unsupervised tasks explicitly required are (i) **Clustering** (K-Means, Hierarchical, DBSCAN) and (ii) **Dimensionality Reduction** (PCA, LDA, t-SNE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Sorting Laundry Without Instructions":**
> Imagine you are handed a basket of mixed clothes with **no labels, no categories, and no instructions**. Without being told "these are socks" or "these are shirts," you naturally group them based on *similarity* — color, size, fabric type. **Unsupervised learning works exactly like this**: the algorithm discovers groupings based purely on the inherent statistical structure of the data, without ever being told what the "correct" group is.

- **Supervised learning** = Teacher guiding a student with answer keys.
- **Unsupervised learning** = Student exploring a new city alone, finding patterns (streets, neighborhoods) by themselves.

## 1.3 Sub-Domains in This Module

| Sub-domain | Goal | Typical Output |
|------------|------|----------------|
| **Clustering** | Partition data into homogeneous groups | Cluster assignments $\{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$ |
| **Dimensionality Reduction** | Project data to a lower-dimensional space | Reduced features $\mathbf{Z} \in \mathbb{R}^{d'}, \, d' < d$ |
| **Association Mining** (advanced) | Find co-occurrence rules | Rules $\{X \Rightarrow Y\}$ |

## 1.4 The "Curse of Dimensionality" — Why Reduction Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As the number of features $d$ grows, the volume of the feature space grows **exponentially**, causing data to become sparse. This phenomenon, termed the **Curse of Dimensionality** (Bellman, 1961), degrades model performance and inflates computation. Dimensionality reduction techniques counter this by preserving essential information in $d' \ll d$ dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Clustering Algorithms — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 K-Means Clustering

K-Means is a **centroid-based, partitioning clustering algorithm** that iteratively refines $k$ cluster centroids to minimize intra-cluster variance.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. **Initialization:** Randomly select $k$ initial centroids $\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_k$.
2. **Assignment Step:** Assign each data point $\mathbf{x}_i$ to the nearest centroid:

$$c_i = \arg\min_{j} \, \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$$

3. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of points assigned to it:

$$\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_j} \mathbf{x}_i$$

4. **Convergence Check:** Repeat steps 2–3 until centroid positions stabilize or maximum iterations are reached.

**Objective Function (to be minimized):**

$$J(\mathbf{C}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_j} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$$

### 2.1.2 Hierarchical Clustering

Builds a **dendrogram** (tree of clusters) using either:
- **Agglomerative (Bottom-Up):** Start with $n$ singleton clusters → iteratively merge the two closest clusters.
- **Divisive (Top-Down):** Start with one cluster → recursively split.

**Linkage Criteria** (how inter-cluster distance is computed):

| Linkage | Formula (between clusters $A$ and $B$) |
|---------|----------------------------------------|
| Single | $d(A,B) = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in A, \mathbf{y} \in B} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert$ |
| Complete | $d(A,B) = \max_{\mathbf{x} \in A, \mathbf{y} \in B} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert$ |
| Average | $d(A,B) = \frac{1}{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in A, \mathbf{y} \in B} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert$ |
| Ward's | Minimizes increase in total within-cluster SSE on merging |

### 2.1.3 DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)

Groups together points that are **closely packed** (high-density regions), marking points in low-density regions as **outliers**.

**Key Parameters:**
- $\varepsilon$ (epsilon): Radius of the neighborhood.
- $\text{MinPts}$: Minimum number of points required to form a dense region.

**Point Classifications:**
- **Core Point:** Has $\geq \text{MinPts}$ neighbors within $\varepsilon$.
- **Border Point:** Lies within $\varepsilon$ of a core point but has fewer than MinPts neighbors.
- **Noise Point (Outlier):** Neither core nor border.

## 2.2 Dimensionality Reduction — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.2.1 Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — Linear, Unsupervised

**Goal:** Find orthogonal directions (principal components) that maximize variance.

**Procedure:**
1. Center the data: $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \bar{\mathbf{x}}$.
2. Compute the covariance matrix:

$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^\top \mathbf{X}_c$$

3. Perform eigen-decomposition: $\Sigma \mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i$.
4. Sort eigenvectors by descending eigenvalues.
5. Select top-$d'$ eigenvectors to form the projection matrix $\mathbf{W}$.
6. Project: $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}$.

### 2.2.2 Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) — Linear, Supervised Reduction

**Goal:** Find a projection that **maximizes between-class separability** while **minimizing within-class scatter**.

**Optimization (Fisher's Criterion):**

$$J(\mathbf{W}) = \frac{\mathbf{W}^\top \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{W}}{\mathbf{W}^\top \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{W}}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{S}_B = \sum_{c} n_c (\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top$ (between-class scatter)
- $\mathbf{S}_W = \sum_{c} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in c} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)^\top$ (within-class scatter)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LDA requires class labels** — it is supervised reduction, unlike PCA. KTU examiners love this contrast question.

### 2.2.3 t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) — Nonlinear, Unsupervised

**Goal:** Preserve **local neighborhood structure** for visualization in 2D/3D.

**Symmetric probability of similarity in high-dimensional space:**

$$p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \vert i} + p_{i \vert j}}{2n}, \quad p_{j \vert i} = \frac{\exp(-\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(-\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_k \Vert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}$$

**Low-dimensional similarity (Student-t with 1 df):**

$$q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \Vert^2)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l} (1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_k - \mathbf{y}_l \Vert^2)^{-1}}$$

**Loss function (KL-Divergence):**

$$C = \text{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Quick-Reference)

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_j} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$ | K-Means objective (WCSS) |
| 2 | $\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_j} \mathbf{x}_i$ | Centroid update rule |
| 3 | $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^\top \mathbf{X}_c$ | Covariance matrix |
| 4 | $\Sigma \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ | Eigen-system for PCA |
| 5 | $\text{Var}_{\text{retained}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d'} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}$ | Variance explained ratio |
| 6 | $J(\mathbf{W}) = \frac{\mathbf{W}^\top \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{W}}{\mathbf{W}^\top \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{W}}$ | Fisher LDA criterion |
| 7 | $C = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}$ | t-SNE cost (KL divergence) |
| 8 | Silhouette Score $= \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Clustering quality metric |
| 9 | Davies-Bouldin Index (lower = better) | Cluster compactness/separability |
| 10 | $\varepsilon$-neighborhood: $N_\varepsilon(\mathbf{x}) = \{\mathbf{y} : \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert \leq \varepsilon\}$ | DBSCAN neighborhood |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Technique | Industry Application |
|-----------|----------------------|
| **K-Means** | Customer segmentation in e-commerce, image compression (color quantization), document clustering |
| **Hierarchical** | Phylogenetic tree construction in bioinformatics, social network analysis |
| **DBSCAN** | Anomaly detection in network intrusion, GPS trajectory clustering, astronomy |
| **PCA** | Face recognition (Eigenfaces), signal denoising, feature engineering in pipelines |
| **LDA** | Face recognition (Fisherfaces), speaker recognition, disease classification preprocessing |
| **t-SNE** | Visualizing MNIST digits, single-cell RNA-seq analysis, NLP word embeddings visualization |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 PCA: Complete Mathematical Derivation

### Step 1 — Data Centering

We begin with data matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ where each row is a sample. Subtract the column means:

$$\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{1} \bar{\mathbf{x}}^\top$$

where $\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}_i$ is the mean vector and $\mathbf{1}$ is an $n$-vector of ones.

**Reason:** Centering ensures the first principal component aligns with the direction of maximum variance passing through the data origin.

### Step 2 — Covariance Matrix Computation

The empirical covariance matrix is computed as:

$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{n - 1} \mathbf{X}_c^\top \mathbf{X}_c$$

The $(j, k)$-th entry $\Sigma_{jk}$ represents the covariance between the $j$-th and $k$-th features:

$$\Sigma_{jk} = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j)(x_{ik} - \bar{x}_k)$$

### Step 3 — Eigen-Decomposition

Solve the characteristic equation:

$$\Sigma \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$$

For each eigenvector $\mathbf{v}_i$, the corresponding eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ represents the variance of the data along that direction. Since $\Sigma$ is symmetric positive semi-definite:
- Eigenvalues $\lambda_i \geq 0$.
- Eigenvectors are mutually orthogonal: $\mathbf{v}_i^\top \mathbf{v}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$.

### Step 4 — Variance Maximization via Lagrangian

We want to find $\mathbf{v}$ maximizing the projected variance subject to $\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert = 1$:

$$\max_{\mathbf{v}} \mathbf{v}^\top \Sigma \mathbf{v} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mathbf{v}^\top \mathbf{v} = 1$$

Set up the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{v}, \beta) = \mathbf{v}^\top \Sigma \mathbf{v} - \beta (\mathbf{v}^\top \mathbf{v} - 1)$$

Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{v}$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{v}} = 2\Sigma \mathbf{v} - 2\beta \mathbf{v} = 0$$

This simplifies to the eigen-equation:

$$\Sigma \mathbf{v} = \beta \mathbf{v}$$

Thus the optimal direction is the eigenvector with the **largest** eigenvalue.

### Step 5 — Construct Projection Matrix

Sort eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_d$. Pick the top $d'$:

$$\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{v}_1 \,\, \mathbf{v}_2 \,\, \ldots \,\, \mathbf{v}_{d'}] \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d'}$$

### Step 6 — Project to Lower-Dimensional Space

$$\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d'}$$

**Variance retained ratio:**

$$R^2 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d'} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):** Given 2D data with $\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, eigenvalues satisfy $\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0$:
>
> $(4 - \lambda)(2 - \lambda) - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda^2 - 6\lambda + 7 = 0$
>
> $\lambda = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 28}}{2} = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{8}}{2} = 3 \pm \sqrt{2}$
>
> So $\lambda_1 \approx 4.414$, $\lambda_2 \approx 1.586$.

## 3.2 K-Means: Convergence Proof Outline (Cost Minimization)

The K-Means objective is:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} r_{ij} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$$

where $r_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ indicates assignment of $\mathbf{x}_i$ to cluster $j$.

**Step 1 — Fix $\mu_j$, optimize $r_{ij}$:**

For each $i$, choose $j$ minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$. This gives the assignment step.

**Step 2 — Fix $r_{ij}$, optimize $\mu_j$:**

Set $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_j} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_j} \sum_{i} r_{ij} (\mathbf{x}_i - \mu_j)^\top (\mathbf{x}_i - \mu_j) = -2 \sum_i r_{ij} (\mathbf{x}_i - \mu_j) = 0$$

Solving yields the mean update:

$$\mu_j = \frac{\sum_i r_{ij} \mathbf{x}_i}{\sum_i r_{ij}}$$

**Step 3 — Monotonic Decrease:** Each step monotonically decreases $J$, and since $J \geq 0$ and the number of possible partitions is finite, K-Means is **guaranteed to converge** (though possibly to a local minimum).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | PCA | LDA | t-SNE |
|--------|-----|-----|-------|
| Linearity | Linear | Linear | Nonlinear |
| Supervised? | No | Yes | No |
| Objective | Max variance | Max class separability | Preserve local neighborhoods |
| Computational Cost | $O(d^3)$ for eigen-decomposition | $O(d^3)$ | $O(n^2)$, slow for large $n$ |
| Output Dimensions | Any $d' \leq d$ | At most $C-1$ | Typically 2 or 3 |
| Preserves | Global structure | Class structure | Local structure |
| Interpretability | High | High | Low |
| Best For | Preprocessing, denoising | Classification prep | Visualization |

## 3.4 Distance Metrics Used in Clustering

| Metric | Formula | Use Case |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Euclidean | $\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Default; isotropic clusters |
| Manhattan | $\sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | High-dimensional sparse data |
| Cosine | $1 - \frac{\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert}$ | Text / high-dim embeddings |
| Mahalanobis | $\sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y})^\top \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y})}$ | Correlated features |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 K-Means Clustering Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Dataset X with n points"] --> B["Choose number of clusters k"]
    B --> C["Random Initialization of k Centroids"]
    C --> D["Assignment Step: Assign each point to nearest centroid"]
    D --> E["Update Step: Recompute centroids as cluster means"]
    E --> F{"Convergence: Centroids stable?"}
    F -- "No" --> D
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Output: Final Cluster Assignments and Centroids"]
    H["Loop terminates after max iterations"] -.-> F
```

## 4.2 PCA Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Raw Data Matrix X (n x d)"] --> B["Step 1: Center Data - subtract mean"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Compute Covariance Matrix Sigma"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Eigen-decomposition Sigma v equals lambda v"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Sort Eigenvalues Descending"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Select top d-prime Eigenvectors"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Form Projection Matrix W"]
    G --> H["Step 7: Project Z equals X_c times W"]
    H --> I["Reduced Representation Z (n x d-prime)"]
```

## 4.3 DBSCAN Classification Logic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Point p in Dataset"] --> B{"Neighbors within epsilon >= MinPts?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Classify as CORE POINT"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Within epsilon of any Core Point?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Classify as BORDER POINT"]
    D -- "No" --> F["Classify as NOISE / OUTLIER"]
    C --> G["Form cluster via density reachability"]
    E --> G
    F --> H["Mark as outlier, exclude from clusters"]
```

## 4.4 Clustering Hierarchy (Taxonomy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Unsupervised Learning"] --> B["Clustering"]
    A --> C["Dimensionality Reduction"]
    A --> D["Association Rule Mining"]
    B --> B1["Partition-based: K-Means, K-Medoids"]
    B --> B2["Hierarchical: Agglomerative, Divisive"]
    B --> B3["Density-based: DBSCAN, OPTICS"]
    B --> B4["Model-based: GMM, Expectation-Maximization"]
    C --> C1["Linear: PCA, LDA, ICA"]
    C --> C2["Nonlinear: t-SNE, UMAP, Autoencoders"]
```

## 4.5 PCA Geometric Interpretation (Text Schematic)

```
        Original 2D Data              After PCA Projection (1D)
                                    
        .  .                           . .
       .  .                           . .
      •  •         ←── PCA ──→       • •  ← projected onto PC1
       .  .                           . .
        .  .                           . .

    PC2 captures residual variance
    PC1 captures direction of maximum spread
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning. Give two examples of unsupervised learning tasks. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Labels | Requires labeled training data $(x_i, y_i)$ | Only inputs $x_i$, no labels |
| Goal | Learn mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ | Discover hidden structure |
| Feedback | Direct error signal | No error signal |
| Examples | Classification, Regression | Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction, Anomaly Detection |
| Evaluation | Accuracy, MSE, F1-score | Silhouette score, reconstruction error |

Two examples of unsupervised tasks: **(1) Customer segmentation using K-Means**, **(2) Image compression using PCA**. *(Award 3 marks: 1 for table differentiation, 1 for correct definitions, 1 for valid examples)*

### Q2. What is the curse of dimensionality? How does dimensionality reduction mitigate it? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **curse of dimensionality** refers to the various phenomena that arise when analyzing data in high-dimensional spaces ($d \to \infty$). As dimensions grow:
- Data becomes extremely sparse — the volume grows exponentially.
- Distance metrics lose discriminative power (all pairwise distances converge).
- Models overfit and require exponentially more training data.

**Mitigation:** Dimensionality reduction techniques like **PCA**, **LDA**, and **t-SNE** project the data onto a lower-dimensional manifold $d' \ll d$ while preserving the most informative structure (variance, class separability, or local neighborhoods), thereby improving computational efficiency, reducing overfitting, and enabling visualization.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Cluster Analysis Focus

**Q.A (a) Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm in detail. Discuss its objective function, initialization methods, and the elbow method for choosing $k$. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Solution:**

**K-Means Algorithm** is an iterative, partition-based clustering technique introduced by Lloyd (1957). It aims to partition $n$ data points into $k$ disjoint clusters $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k$ such that the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS) is minimized.

**Objective Function:**

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_j} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$$

where $\mu_j$ is the centroid of cluster $C_j$.

**Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
1. Choose $k$; initialize centroids $\mu_1^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_k^{(0)}$.
2. **Assignment:** $c_i^{(t)} = \arg\min_j \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mu_j^{(t)} \Vert^2$.
3. **Update:** $\mu_j^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_j^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_j^{(t)}} \mathbf{x}_i$.
4. Repeat until convergence.

**Initialization Methods (2 marks):**
- **Random Initialization:** Simple but prone to poor local minima.
- **K-Means++:** Smarter seeding — first centroid chosen randomly; subsequent centroids chosen with probability proportional to squared distance from nearest existing centroid. Reduces convergence to poor local minima.

**Choosing $k$ — The Elbow Method (3 marks):**
- Compute $J(k)$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, k_{\max}$.
- Plot $J$ vs $k$.
- The "elbow point" (where the rate of decrease sharply drops) indicates the optimal $k$.

---

**Q.A (b) Compare DBSCAN with K-Means. Explain the concepts of core, border, and noise points with a suitable diagram. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | K-Means | DBSCAN |
|--------|---------|--------|
| Cluster Shape | Spherical only | Arbitrary shapes |
| Requires $k$? | Yes | No |
| Handles Outliers | No (assigns them) | Yes (marks as noise) |
| Density Assumption | Equal density | Varying density sensitive |
| Parameters | $k$ | $\varepsilon$, MinPts |
| Complexity | $O(nkt)$ | $O(n \log n)$ with indexing |

**Point Types (4 marks):**

Let $N_\varepsilon(\mathbf{x}) = \{\mathbf{y} : \text{dist}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \leq \varepsilon\}$.

- **Core Point:** $\vert N_\varepsilon(\mathbf{x}) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$
- **Border Point:** $\vert N_\varepsilon(\mathbf{x}) \vert < \text{MinPts}$ BUT $\mathbf{x} \in N_\varepsilon(\mathbf{x}_c)$ for some core point $\mathbf{x}_c$.
- **Noise Point:** Neither core nor border.

**Diagram:**

```
        Cluster A (Dense)         Cluster B (Dense)
       ⊙ ⊙ ⊙ ⊙                    ⊙ ⊙
      ⊙ ⊙ ⊙ ⊙ ⊙                  ⊙ ⊙ ⊙
       ⊙ ⊙ ⊙                      ⊙ ⊙
                                   
        Δ Δ Δ Δ   ← Noise points (isolated)
        
        ★ = Core    ● = Border    Δ = Noise
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Dimensionality Reduction Focus

**Q.B (a) Derive the mathematical formulation of Principal Component Analysis (PCA). Show how the principal components are obtained via eigen-decomposition of the covariance matrix. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Goal:** Find an orthonormal basis $\{\mathbf{v}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_d\}$ such that the projection of centered data $\mathbf{X}_c$ onto this basis maximizes variance.

**Step 1 — Data Centering (1 mark):**
$$\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{1}\bar{\mathbf{x}}^\top$$

**Step 2 — Covariance Matrix (1 mark):**
$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^\top \mathbf{X}_c \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$$

**Step 3 — Variance Maximization as Constrained Optimization (2 marks):**
Maximize $\mathbf{v}^\top \Sigma \mathbf{v}$ subject to $\mathbf{v}^\top \mathbf{v} = 1$.

Lagrangian: $\mathcal{L} = \mathbf{v}^\top \Sigma \mathbf{v} - \lambda(\mathbf{v}^\top \mathbf{v} - 1)$

Setting $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{v}} = 0$:
$$2\Sigma \mathbf{v} - 2\lambda \mathbf{v} = 0 \Rightarrow \Sigma \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$$

**Step 4 — Eigen-Decomposition (2 marks):**
Since $\Sigma$ is symmetric PSD, it has $d$ orthogonal eigenvectors with non-negative eigenvalues. The eigenvector corresponding to the **largest** eigenvalue is the first principal component (PC1).

Sort: $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_d$.

**Step 5 — Projection (1 mark):**
$$\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}, \quad \mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{v}_1 \,\, \mathbf{v}_2 \,\, \ldots \,\, \mathbf{v}_{d'}]$$

---

**Q.B (b) Explain t-SNE and how it differs from PCA. Why is t-SNE preferred for visualization? (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Solution:**

**t-SNE (van der Maaten & Hinton, 2008)** is a nonlinear dimensionality reduction technique specifically designed for **high-dimensional data visualization** in 2D or 3D.

**Mechanism (4 marks):**

1. **High-Dimensional Similarities:** Compute conditional probabilities $p_{j \vert i}$ using a Gaussian distribution centered at each point:
$$p_{j \vert i} = \frac{\exp(-\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(-\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_k \Vert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}$$

2. **Low-Dimensional Similarities:** Use a **Student-t distribution** (heavy-tailed) for $q_{ij}$:
$$q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \Vert^2)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l} (1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_k - \mathbf{y}_l \Vert^2)^{-1}}$$

The heavy tail prevents the "crowding problem."

3. **Gradient Descent:** Minimize KL-divergence $C = \text{KL}(P \Vert Q)$ using gradient descent on $\mathbf{y}_i$.

**Why t-SNE vs PCA (3 marks):**

| Feature | PCA | t-SNE |
|---------|-----|-------|
| Linearity | Linear | Nonlinear |
| Preserves | Global structure | Local neighborhood |
| Output | Any $d'$ | Usually 2 or 3 |
| Computational cost | Fast ($O(d^3)$) | Slow ($O(n^2)$) |
| Interpretability of axes | High (linear combinations) | Low (axes are meaningless) |
| Use case | Preprocessing | Visualization |

**Why Preferred for Visualization:** t-SNE preserves **local cluster structure** — neighboring points in high-D remain close in low-D, while far-apart clusters stay separated. PCA can only capture linear global variance and may collapse distinct clusters that lie on nonlinear manifolds. For example, on MNIST, t-SNE reveals clear digit clusters, while PCA shows heavy overlap.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PCA with LDA:** Many students incorrectly state LDA is unsupervised. **LDA is supervised** — it requires class labels to compute $S_B$ and $S_W$. This single mistake costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the Lagrangian in PCA derivation:** Examiners award full marks for the constrained optimization step. Just writing "compute eigenvectors" without showing $\Sigma \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ loses up to 3 marks.
> 3. **Not stating convergence guarantees:** For K-Means, you must explicitly state the algorithm converges because $J$ is monotonically non-increasing and bounded below by 0. Just writing "it converges" is incomplete.
> 4. **Mixing up DBSCAN point types:** Border points ARE reachable from core points — they are not noise. Examiners will deduct marks if these are conflated.
> 5. **Using "$\vert x \vert$" in formulas:** Inside derivation tables, write $\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert$ for norms — vertical bars in markdown tables break formatting. Examiners reading your script will appreciate clean notation.
> 6. **Forgetting to standardize data before PCA:** PCA is **scale-sensitive**. Always mention standardization: $\mathbf{x}_{\text{std}} = (\mathbf{x} - \mu)/\sigma$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Unsupervised Learning & Dimensionality Reduction**

- ☐ **Unsupervised Learning** has no labels — model discovers structure.
- ☐ **K-Means** minimizes WCSS via iterative assignment + centroid update.
- ☐ K-Means **converges** (monotonic $J$ decrease, bounded below).
- ☐ **K-Means++** improves initialization, reducing poor local minima.
- ☐ **Elbow method** chooses $k$ by finding the "bend" in $J$ vs $k$ plot.
- ☐ **Hierarchical clustering** uses a dendrogram; linkage types are Single/Complete/Average/Ward's.
- ☐ **DBSCAN** uses $\varepsilon$ + MinPts; defines Core, Border, Noise points.
- ☐ DBSCAN finds **arbitrary-shaped clusters** and **identifies outliers** (unlike K-Means).
- ☐ **PCA** finds orthogonal directions of maximum variance.
- ☐ PCA is solved via **eigen-decomposition** of the covariance matrix $\Sigma$.
- ☐ **Always center (and ideally standardize)** data before PCA.
- ☐ Variance retained ratio: $\sum_{i=1}^{d'} \lambda_i / \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$.
- ☐ **LDA is supervised**, maximizes $S_B/S_W$ — Fisher's criterion.
- ☐ LDA gives at most $C - 1$ discriminant components (where $C$ is the number of classes).
- ☐ **t-SNE** is nonlinear, used for visualization, preserves local structure.
- ☐ t-SNE uses **Gaussian in high-D**, **Student-t in low-D** to avoid crowding.
- ☐ **Curse of dimensionality** motivates reduction.
- ☐ **Silhouette score** ranges $[-1, 1]$ — higher = better clustering.
- ☐ PCA: linear + global; t-SNE: nonlinear + local; LDA: linear + class-discriminative.
- ☐ Distance metric choice matters: Euclidean (default), Manhattan (sparse), Cosine (text), Mahalanobis (correlated).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ensemble Methods - Voting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ensemble Methods - Voting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ensemble Learning: The Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ensemble Learning** is a machine learning paradigm where multiple individual models (called **base learners** or **weak learners**) are strategically combined to solve a particular problem, with the goal of producing a single composite model that performs **significantly better** than any of the constituent models alone.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT755)** syllabus, ensemble methods are studied as a direct solution to the high-variance and high-bias problems faced by individual classifiers. The underlying principle is rooted in the **Condorcet's Jury Theorem (1785)**, which mathematically proves that a group of independent, moderately competent decision-makers consistently outperforms even the single best expert in the group, provided the experts are reasonably accurate and their errors are uncorrelated.

For a problem involving $n$ independent classifiers, each with an accuracy $p > 0.5$, the probability that the **majority vote** of the group arrives at the correct answer is given by the cumulative binomial distribution:

$$P(\text{Ensemble Correct}) = \sum_{k=\lceil n/2 \rceil}^{n} \binom{n}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}$$

This equation demonstrates a critical KTU point: **the ensemble's accuracy monotonically increases with the number of diverse, moderately accurate base learners**.

## 1.2 The Voting Method: A Targeted Ensemble Strategy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voting** is the most fundamental and pedagogically important ensemble technique, where multiple *diverse* classifiers are trained independently on the same dataset, and their predictions are aggregated through a **voting rule** to produce the final output.

The voting ensemble belongs to the **averaging methods** family of ensembles (alongside bagging and random forests) where the principle of **"wisdom of the crowd"** is operationalized through statistical aggregation rather than sequential correction (as in boosting).

### 1.2.1 Two Principal Variants of Voting

| Variant | Aggregation Rule | Output Type | KTU Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hard Voting (Majority Voting)** | Modal class label across all classifiers | Discrete class | Used when classifiers only output labels |
| **Soft Voting (Probabilistic Voting)** | Average of predicted class probabilities | Continuous probabilities | Used when classifiers expose `predict_proba` |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The Medical Board

Imagine a patient visits a hospital with a complex, ambiguous set of symptoms. Instead of relying on a single doctor's diagnosis, the hospital convenes a **medical board of 5 specialists** (a cardiologist, a neurologist, a general physician, an endocrinologist, and an immunologist).

- **Hard Voting Analogy**: Each specialist independently writes down their diagnosis on a slip of paper (e.g., "Diabetes", "Thyroid", "Diabetes", "Diabetes", "Anemia"). The diagnoses are tallied, and the majority diagnosis ("Diabetes" — 3 out of 5 votes) becomes the final diagnosis. This mirrors the **Hard Voting** mechanism where only the discrete final class label matters.

- **Soft Voting Analogy**: Instead of writing down a single disease, each specialist estimates a probability distribution across all possible diseases and reports their confidence levels (e.g., "70% Diabetes, 20% Thyroid, 10% Anemia"). The hospital then averages these confidence scores across all 5 specialists and selects the disease with the **highest average confidence**. This mirrors the **Soft Voting** mechanism, which leverages the *uncertainty information* of each model.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Students**: Hard voting only uses the "tip of the iceberg" (the final decision), while soft voting uses the "entire iceberg" (the probability distribution). This is why soft voting **strictly outperforms** hard voting when the base classifiers are well-calibrated.

## 1.4 Why Voting Works: The Three Pillars of Ensemble Success

The success of any voting ensemble rests on three necessary conditions identified by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Accuracy (Statistical Condition)**: Each base classifier must perform **better than random guessing**. For a binary classification problem, each base learner must achieve an accuracy of **$p > 0.5$**.

2. **Diversity (Independence Condition)**: The base classifiers must make **uncorrelated errors**. If all classifiers make the same mistakes, the ensemble provides no benefit regardless of how many models are added. Diversity can be achieved by:
   - Using different algorithms (e.g., Logistic Regression + SVM + Decision Tree)
   - Using different training subsets (Bagging)
   - Using different feature subsets (Random Subspace Method)

3. **Aggregation Rule**: The combination strategy (voting rule) must be **robust to outliers** and exploit the consensus signal effectively.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of Voting

Let us formalize the voting framework. Consider an ensemble of $M$ base classifiers, denoted as $\{C_1, C_2, C_3, \ldots, C_M\}$, each trained on the same dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

For a given test input $\mathbf{x}$, each classifier produces either:
- A discrete prediction: $C_j(\mathbf{x}) \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ (for hard voting)
- A probability vector: $P_j(y=k \mid \mathbf{x})$ for $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ (for soft voting)

### 2.1.1 Hard Voting (Plurality/Majority Voting)

In hard voting, the ensemble's prediction is the **mode** (most frequent class) of the predictions from all base classifiers:

$$\hat{y}_{\text{hard}} = \underset{k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}}{\operatorname{arg\,max}} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \mathbb{I}(C_j(\mathbf{x}) = k)$$

where $\mathbb{I}(\cdot)$ is the **indicator function** that equals $1$ when the condition is true and $0$ otherwise.

For **binary classification** with $M$ being odd, hard voting reduces to:

$$\hat{y}_{\text{hard}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \sum_{j=1}^{M} \mathbb{I}(C_j(\mathbf{x}) = 1) > M/2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 2.1.2 Soft Voting (Probability Averaging)

In soft voting, the predicted probability for class $k$ is the **arithmetic mean** of the probabilities assigned by all base classifiers:

$$P_{\text{ensemble}}(y=k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{j=1}^{M} P_j(y=k \mid \mathbf{x})$$

The final prediction is the class with the highest averaged probability:

$$\hat{y}_{\text{soft}} = \underset{k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}}{\operatorname{arg\,max}} \frac{1}{M} \sum_{j=1}^{M} P_j(y=k \mid \mathbf{x})$$

### 2.1.3 Weighted Voting (Advanced Variant)

In weighted voting, each classifier is assigned a weight $w_j$ reflecting its reliability or past performance. The weights are typically **non-negative** and sum to unity ($\sum_{j=1}^{M} w_j = 1$):

$$P_{\text{weighted}}(y=k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j=1}^{M} w_j \cdot P_j(y=k \mid \mathbf{x})$$

$$\hat{y}_{\text{weighted}} = \underset{k}{\operatorname{arg\,max}} \sum_{j=1}^{M} w_j \cdot P_j(y=k \mid \mathbf{x})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note**: Weighted voting is often used when base classifiers have demonstrably different accuracies on a validation set. The weights are usually proportional to the validation accuracy: $w_j \propto \text{Acc}(C_j)$.

## 2.2 Bias-Variance Decomposition Perspective

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes the **bias-variance tradeoff** as the lens through which ensemble methods should be understood:

$$\text{Expected Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}$$

| Ensemble Type | Effect on Bias | Effect on Variance | Voting Position |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bagging | Slight increase | **Significant decrease** | Averaging reduces variance |
| Boosting | **Significant decrease** | Slight increase | Sequential correction |
| **Voting (Independent)** | **Slight decrease** | **Significant decrease** | Best of both worlds |

The voting ensemble simultaneously reduces both bias (through model diversity) and variance (through averaging), making it the most "balanced" ensemble technique in the KTU curriculum.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Boundary Condition** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Condorcet's Probability of Correct Majority | $P = \sum_{k=\lceil n/2 \rceil}^{n} \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$ | Requires $p > 0.5$ | Estimating ensemble accuracy |
| Hard Voting Prediction | $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{C_1(x), \ldots, C_M(x)\}$ | Odd $M$ recommended | Tied binary classification |
| Soft Voting Probability | $P(y=k \mid x) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{j=1}^{M} P_j(y=k \mid x)$ | Requires calibrated probabilities | Multi-class classification |
| Weighted Voting Probability | $P(y=k \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{M} w_j P_j(y=k \mid x)$ | $\sum_j w_j = 1$, $w_j \geq 0$ | Heterogeneous base learners |
| Weighted Majority Error Bound | $\epsilon \leq \prod_{j=1}^{M} 2\sqrt{\epsilon_j (1-\epsilon_j)}$ | $\epsilon_j < 0.5$ | Theoretical error upper bound |
| Ensemble Variance (Uncorrelated) | $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{M}$ | Independent base models | Variance reduction estimate |
| Diversity Measure (Disagreement) | $D_{ij} = \frac{N^{10} + N^{01}}{N^{11} + N^{10} + N^{01} + N^{00}}$ | $D_{ij} \in [0, 1]$ | Quantifying base learner diversity |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Voting ensembles are deployed extensively in **high-stakes engineering applications** where reliability is paramount:

- **Medical Diagnosis Systems**: Combining outputs of CNN, Random Forest, and SVM for tumor detection in histopathology images.
- **Financial Credit Scoring**: Aggregating predictions from Logistic Regression, Gradient Boosted Trees, and Neural Networks to minimize the cost of false negatives (defaulters classified as safe).
- **Autonomous Vehicle Perception**: Sensor fusion where outputs from LiDAR, camera, and radar classifiers are voted upon for object detection.
- **Cybersecurity Intrusion Detection**: Combining anomaly detectors with signature-based classifiers to reduce both false positive and false negative rates.
- **Remote Sensing & Agriculture**: Multi-classifier crop classification from satellite imagery, where each classifier captures a different spectral feature.

## 2.5 Assumptions and Limitations

> [!WARNING]
> Students must explicitly state these limitations in KTU exams. Voting ensembles assume: (1) base classifiers are **independent**, (2) base classifiers are **moderately accurate** ($p > 0.5$), and (3) the **aggregation cost is acceptable**. When these assumptions are violated (e.g., highly correlated models), voting provides minimal benefit over a single strong learner.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Error Reduction Through Majority Voting

This is a **high-yield derivation** frequently tested in KTU ESE Module Question 14-mark problems. We derive the mathematical proof that majority voting reduces error compared to a single classifier.

### Step 1: Problem Setup

Consider $M$ independent classifiers, each with the **same accuracy** $p$ (probability of being correct). For a binary classification task, the probability that the majority of the $M$ classifiers vote correctly is given by the binomial probability that **more than half** of the classifiers are correct.

For simplicity, let $M$ be **odd** (e.g., $M = 2n+1$), so there are no ties.

### Step 2: Binomial Distribution Formulation

The number of correct classifiers $K$ follows a Binomial distribution:

$$K \sim \text{Binomial}(M, p)$$

The probability mass function is:

$$P(K = k) = \binom{M}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{M-k}$$

### Step 3: Majority Vote is Correct When $K > M/2$

The ensemble makes a correct prediction when the number of correct classifiers exceeds $M/2$. Since $M$ is odd, $M/2$ is not an integer, and we require $K \geq \lceil M/2 \rceil = n+1$.

$$P(\text{Ensemble Correct}) = \sum_{k=n+1}^{2n+1} \binom{2n+1}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{2n+1-k}$$

### Step 4: Concrete Example Calculation

Let $M = 5$ and $p = 0.7$. The ensemble is correct when at least 3 classifiers (out of 5) are correct.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Ensemble Correct}) &= \sum_{k=3}^{5} \binom{5}{k} (0.7)^{k} (0.3)^{5-k} \\[6pt]
&= \binom{5}{3}(0.7)^{3}(0.3)^{2} + \binom{5}{4}(0.7)^{4}(0.3)^{1} + \binom{5}{5}(0.7)^{5}(0.3)^{0} \\[6pt]
&= (10)(0.343)(0.09) + (5)(0.2401)(0.3) + (1)(0.16807)(1) \\[6pt]
&= 10 \times 0.03087 + 5 \times 0.07203 + 0.16807 \\[6pt]
&= 0.3087 + 0.36015 + 0.16807 \\[6pt]
&= 0.83692
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: With 5 classifiers each having 70% accuracy, the ensemble achieves **83.69% accuracy**, a **13.69 percentage point improvement** over a single classifier. This is the **quantitative proof** of the wisdom of the crowd principle.

### Step 5: Limiting Behavior as $M \to \infty$

A fundamental result is that as $M \to \infty$ (under the assumption of independence and $p > 0.5$):

$$\lim_{M \to \infty} P(\text{Ensemble Correct}) = 1$$

This is a consequence of the **Weak Law of Large Numbers**. By Hoeffding's inequality, the convergence rate is exponentially fast:

$$P\left(\left|\frac{1}{M}\sum_{j=1}^{M} \mathbb{I}(C_j = y) - p\right| \geq \epsilon\right) \leq 2\exp(-2M\epsilon^{2})$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Variance Reduction via Averaging

This derivation proves why soft voting (averaging) reduces variance.

### Step 1: Setup

Consider $M$ independent random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_M$ (the predictions of base classifiers), each with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.

### Step 2: Define the Averaged Predictor

The ensemble's prediction is the sample mean:

$$\bar{X} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{j=1}^{M} X_j$$

### Step 3: Compute the Mean of $\bar{X}$

By linearity of expectation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}] &= \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{1}{M} \sum_{j=1}^{M} X_j\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{M} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \mathbb{E}[X_j] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{M} \cdot M \cdot \mu = \mu
\end{aligned}
$$

**The ensemble is unbiased** — its expected prediction equals the expected prediction of a single classifier.

### Step 4: Compute the Variance of $\bar{X}$

Using independence ($\text{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\bar{X}) &= \text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{M} \sum_{j=1}^{M} X_j\right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{M^{2}} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \text{Var}(X_j) \quad \text{(independence)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{M^{2}} \cdot M \cdot \sigma^{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\sigma^{2}}{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: The variance of the ensemble is reduced by a factor of $M$ compared to a single classifier. This is the **fundamental mathematical justification** for using averaging (soft voting) in ensembles.

### Step 5: Extension to Correlated Classifiers

If base classifiers are correlated with pairwise correlation $\rho$, the variance becomes:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \rho \sigma^{2} + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^{2}}{M}$$

This shows that **even with moderate correlation**, the ensemble still provides variance reduction. However, if $\rho \to 1$ (perfectly correlated models), the variance reduction vanishes — confirming the KTU syllabus emphasis on **diversity**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hard vs Soft Voting Trade-offs

| **Decision Criterion** | **Hard Voting** | **Soft Voting** | **KTU Recommendation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Required base learner output | Class label only | `predict_proba` (probabilities) | Prefer soft when available |
| Information utilization | Only final label | Full probability distribution | Soft is strictly more informative |
| Computational cost | Lower (just labels) | Higher (need calibrated probs) | Hard for resource-constrained systems |
| Robustness to miscalibration | Not affected | Highly sensitive | Hard when models are uncalibrated |
| Performance with strong learners | Comparable | Slightly better | Soft preferred when models are calibrated |
| Tied votes handling | Problematic (especially with even $M$) | Smooth (probabilities rarely tie) | Soft avoids tie issues |
| Hyperparameter sensitivity | Low | Moderate | Hard for stable baselines |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Weighted Voting

**Problem Statement**: A KTU board examination-style problem presents 3 classifiers with the following validation accuracies and weights. Compute the ensemble's predicted probabilities for classes A, B, C.

**Given**:
- Classifier $C_1$ (Decision Tree): Validation Accuracy = 85%, Weight $w_1 = 0.2$
- Classifier $C_2$ (SVM): Validation Accuracy = 92%, Weight $w_2 = 0.5$
- Classifier $C_3$ (Logistic Regression): Validation Accuracy = 78%, Weight $w_3 = 0.3$

For input $\mathbf{x}$, the predicted probabilities are:

| Classifier | $P(y=A)$ | $P(y=B)$ | $P(y=C)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | 0.60 | 0.30 | 0.10 |
| $C_2$ | 0.40 | 0.55 | 0.05 |
| $C_3$ | 0.50 | 0.35 | 0.15 |

### Solution Steps

**Step 1: Verify weights sum to unity.**

$$w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 0.2 + 0.5 + 0.3 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2: Compute the weighted probability for class A.**

$$
P_{\text{ens}}(y=A) = (0.2)(0.60) + (0.5)(0.40) + (0.3)(0.50)
$$

$$
P_{\text{ens}}(y=A) = 0.120 + 0.200 + 0.150 = 0.470
$$

**Step 3: Compute the weighted probability for class B.**

$$
P_{\text{ens}}(y=B) = (0.2)(0.30) + (0.5)(0.55) + (0.3)(0.35)
$$

$$
P_{\text{ens}}(y=B) = 0.060 + 0.275 + 0.105 = 0.440
$$

**Step 4: Compute the weighted probability for class C.**

$$
P_{\text{ens}}(y=C) = (0.2)(0.10) + (0.5)(0.05) + (0.3)(0.15)
$$

$$
P_{\text{ens}}(y=C) = 0.020 + 0.025 + 0.045 = 0.090
$$

**Step 5: Verify probabilities sum to 1.**

$$0.470 + 0.440 + 0.090 = 1.000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6: Determine final prediction.**

$$\hat{y} = \underset{k \in \{A, B, C\}}{\operatorname{arg\,max}} \, P_{\text{ens}}(y=k) = A \quad (\text{since } 0.470 > 0.440 > 0.090)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Note how the SVM (highest weight 0.5)** "pulls" the prediction toward class B's confidence of 0.55, but the ensemble still chooses class A because the other two classifiers strongly favor A. This demonstrates the **stabilizing effect** of weighted ensembles.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Voting Ensemble

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DS[("Training Dataset D")]

    C2 -->|p2| AGG
    C3 -->|p3| AGG
    C4 -->|p4| AGG
    C5 -->|p5| AGG

    AGG -->|Hard Vote: mode| HV["Hard Voting: Majority Class"]
    AGG -->|Soft Vote: mean| SV["Soft Voting: Avg Probabilities"]

    HV --> OUT["Final Prediction y-hat"]
    SV --> OUT
```

## 4.2 Detailed Hard Voting vs Soft Voting Flowchart

flowchart LR
    INPUT[/"Test Input x"/]

    INPUT --> H1["C1 predicts: Class A"]

    H2 --> HVOTE
    H3 --> HVOTE
