## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Full Manual Calculation)

This is a **classic KTU examination problem**. We are given 8 two-dimensional data points, $K=2$ clusters, and initial centroids. We will perform 2 full iterations and demonstrate convergence.

**Dataset:** $\mathcal{D} = \{A(2,10), B(2,5), C(8,4), D(5,8), E(7,5), F(6,4), G(1,2), H(4,9)\}$

**Given:** $K = 2$, Initial centroids $\mu_1^{(0)} = (2, 10)$ and $\mu_2^{(0)} = (8, 4)$.

**Distance Formula (Euclidean, 2D):**

$$d(P, Q) = \sqrt{(x_P - x_Q)^2 + (y_P - y_Q)^2}$$

---

#### ITERATION 1 — Assignment Step

We compute $d(\cdot, \mu_1^{(0)})$ and $d(\cdot, \mu_2^{(0)})$ for every point.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Point A}(2,10): \quad & d(A, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (10-10)^2} = \sqrt{0} = 0.00 \\
& d(A, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(2-8)^2 + (10-4)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 36} = \sqrt{72} \approx 8.49 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Assign A to Cluster 1} \quad [0.00 < 8.49] \\[6pt]
\text{Point B}(2,5): \quad & d(B, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (5-10)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 25} = 5.00 \\
& d(B, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(2-8)^2 + (5-4)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 1} = \sqrt{37} \approx 6.08 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Assign B to Cluster 1} \quad [5.00 < 6.08] \\[6pt]
\text{Point C}(8,4): \quad & d(C, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(8-2)^2 + (4-10)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 36} \approx 8.49 \\
& d(C, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(8-8)^2 + (4-4)^2} = 0.00 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Assign C to Cluster 2} \quad [0.00 < 8.49] \\[6pt]
\text{Point D}(5,8): \quad & d(D, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(5-2)^2 + (8-10)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61 \\
& d(D, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(5-8)^2 + (8-4)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5.00 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Assign D to Cluster 1} \quad [3.61 < 5.00] \\[6pt]
\text{Point E}(7,5): \quad & d(E, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(7-2)^2 + (5-10)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 25} \approx 7.07 \\
& d(E, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(7-8)^2 + (5-4)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 1} \approx 1.41 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Assign E to Cluster 2} \quad [1.41 < 7.07] \\[6pt]
\text{Point F}(6,4): \quad & d(F, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(6-2)^2 + (4-10)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 36} \approx 7.21 \\
& d(F, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(6-8)^2 + (4-4)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 0} = 2.00 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Assign F to Cluster 2} \quad [2.00 < 7.21] \\[6pt]
\text{Point G}(1,2): \quad & d(G, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (2-10)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 64} \approx 8.06 \\
& d(G, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(1-8)^2 + (2-4)^2} = \sqrt{49 + 4} \approx 7.28 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Assign G to Cluster 1} \quad [8.06 \text{ and } 7.28, \text{ closer to } \mu_1] \\[6pt]
\text{Point H}(4,9): \quad & d(H, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(4-2)^2 + (9-10)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24 \\
& d(H, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(4-8)^2 + (9-4)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 25} \approx 6.40 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Assign H to Cluster 1} \quad [2.24 < 6.40] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 1 — Cluster Assignment Summary:**

| Cluster | Members |
| :--- | :--- |
| $C_1^{(1)}$ | A, B, D, G, H |
| $C_2^{(1)}$ | C, E, F |

---

#### ITERATION 1 — Update Step

We recompute the centroids as the arithmetic mean of all points in each cluster.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1^{(1)} & = \frac{1}{5}\left[ (2,10) + (2,5) + (5,8) + (1,2) + (4,9) \right] \\
& = \frac{1}{5}\left( (2+2+5+1+4), (10+5+8+2+9) \right) \\
& = \frac{1}{5}\left( 14, 34 \right) \\
& = (2.80, 6.80) \\[10pt]
\mu_2^{(1)} & = \frac{1}{3}\left[ (8,4) + (7,5) + (6,4) \right] \\
& = \frac{1}{3}\left( (8+7+6), (4+5+4) \right) \\
& = \frac{1}{3}\left( 21, 13 \right) \\
& = (7.00, 4.33) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**New centroids after Iteration 1:** $\mu_1^{(1)} = (2.80, 6.80)$, $\mu_2^{(1)} = (7.00, 4.33)$.

---

#### ITERATION 2 — Assignment Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Point A}(2,10): \quad & d(A, \mu_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(2-2.8)^2 + (10-6.8)^2} = \sqrt{0.64 + 10.24} = \sqrt{10.88} \approx 3.30 \\
& d(A, \mu_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(2-7)^2 + (10-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 32.15} \approx 7.56 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Cluster 1} \\[4pt]
\text{Point B}(2,5): \quad & d(B, \mu_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(2-2.8)^2 + (5-6.8)^2} = \sqrt{0.64 + 3.24} = \sqrt{3.88} \approx 1.97 \\
& d(B, \mu_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(2-7)^2 + (5-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 0.45} \approx 5.04 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Cluster 1} \\[4pt]
\text{Point C}(8,4): \quad & d(C, \mu_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(8-2.8)^2 + (4-6.8)^2} = \sqrt{27.04 + 7.84} \approx 5.91 \\
& d(C, \mu_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(8-7)^2 + (4-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0.11} \approx 1.05 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Cluster 2} \\[4pt]
\text{Point D}(5,8): \quad & d(D, \mu_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(5-2.8)^2 + (8-6.8)^2} = \sqrt{4.84 + 1.44} = \sqrt{6.28} \approx 2.51 \\
& d(D, \mu_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(5-7)^2 + (8-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 13.47} \approx 4.18 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Cluster 1} \\[4pt]
\text{Point E}(7,5): \quad & d(E, \mu_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(7-2.8)^2 + (5-6.8)^2} = \sqrt{17.64 + 3.24} \approx 4.57 \\
& d(E, \mu_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(7-7)^2 + (5-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 0.45} \approx 0.67 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Cluster 2} \\[4pt]
\text{Point F}(6,4): \quad & d(F, \mu_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(6-2.8)^2 + (4-6.8)^2} = \sqrt{10.24 + 7.84} \approx 4.25 \\
& d(F, \mu_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(6-7)^2 + (4-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0.11} \approx 1.05 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Cluster 2} \\[4pt]
\text{Point G}(1,2): \quad & d(G, \mu_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(1-2.8)^2 + (2-6.8)^2} = \sqrt{3.24 + 23.04} \approx 5.13 \\
& d(G, \mu_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(1-7)^2 + (2-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 5.43} \approx 6.44 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Cluster 1} \\[4pt]
\text{Point H}(4,9): \quad & d(H, \mu_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(4-2.8)^2 + (9-6.8)^2} = \sqrt{1.44 + 4.84} = \sqrt{6.28} \approx 2.51 \\
& d(H, \mu_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(4-7)^2 + (9-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 21.81} \approx 5.55 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Cluster 1} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2 — Cluster Assignment (unchanged from Iteration 1):**

| Cluster | Members |
| :--- | :--- |
| $C_1^{(2)}$ | A, B, D, G, H |
| $C_2^{(2)}$ | C, E, F |

---

#### ITERATION 2 — Update Step & Convergence Check

Since cluster membership is unchanged, the new centroids are identical:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1^{(2)} & = (2.80, 6.80) = \mu_1^{(1)} \\
\mu_2^{(2)} & = (7.00, 4.33) = \mu_2^{(1)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Because $\mu_i^{(2)} = \mu_i^{(1)}$ for all $i$, the **convergence criterion** is satisfied. The algorithm terminates after 2 iterations.

---

#### Final WCSS (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares) Calculation

We now compute the final objective function value using squared Euclidean distances to the converged centroids.

**Cluster 1: $C_1 = \{A(2,10), B(2,5), D(5,8), G(1,2), H(4,9)\}$, centroid $\mu_1 = (2.8, 6.8)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SSE_1 & = (2-2.8)^2 + (10-6.8)^2 + (2-2.8)^2 + (5-6.8)^2 \\
& \quad + (5-2.8)^2 + (8-6.8)^2 + (1-2.8)^2 + (2-6.8)^2 \\
& \quad + (4-2.8)^2 + (9-6.8)^2 \\
& = 0.64 + 10.24 + 0.64 + 3.24 + 4.84 + 1.44 + 3.24 + 23.04 + 1.44 + 4.84 \\
& = 53.60 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cluster 2: $C_2 = \{C(8,4), E(7,5), F(6,4)\}$, centroid $\mu_2 = (7, 4.33)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SSE_2 & = (8-7)^2 + (4-4.33)^2 + (7-7)^2 + (5-4.33)^2 + (6-7)^2 + (4-4.33)^2 \\
& = 1 + 0.11 + 0 + 0.45 + 1 + 0.11 \\
& = 2.67 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total WCSS:**

$$J = SSE_1 + SSE_2 = 53.60 + 2.67 = \mathbf{56.27}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **distortion score** of the converged clustering. A *lower* WCSS indicates tighter, more compact clusters. The Elbow Method leverages this metric to choose $K$.

### 3.2 Choosing Optimal K — The Elbow Method

When $K$ is not specified, plot WCSS vs $K$. The point where the rate of decrease sharply bends (like an elbow) is the optimal $K$. Formally, we look for the largest $K$ such that adding another cluster does not provide a significantly better model.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 K-Means Algorithm — Control Flow Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of the K-Means algorithm, mapping data flow, decision points, and iteration control.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Input Dataset X and Number of Clusters K]
    B --> C[Initialize K Centroids mu_1, mu_2, ..., mu_K]
    C --> D[Set Iteration Counter t = 0]
    D --> E[Assignment Step: For each data point x_j compute distance to all centroids]
    E --> F[Assign x_j to nearest centroid forming clusters C_1, C_2, ..., C_K]
    F --> G[Update Step: Recompute each centroid as mean of its cluster members]
    G --> H[Increment Iteration Counter: t = t + 1]
    H --> I{Convergence Check: Have centroids stopped moving?}
    I -->|No| E
    I -->|Yes| J[Output Final Clusters and Centroids]
    J --> K[Compute Final WCSS J for quality evaluation]
    K --> L[End]
```

### 4.2 Geometric Block Diagram — Cluster Convergence

The following Mermaid visualization captures the **geometric evolution** of K-Means iteration in a 2D feature space, with distinct functional zones:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Initialization Phase]
        I1[Raw Unlabeled Data Points]
        I2[Random Centroid Placement K seeds]
    end
    subgraph AS[Assignment Phase Iteration t]
        S1[Compute Euclidean Distance from each point to each centroid]
        S2[Color-coded Voronoi Partition: points grouped by nearest centroid]
    end
    subgraph US[Update Phase Iteration t]
        U1[Compute new centroid = arithmetic mean of cluster members]
        U2[Shift centroid position toward cluster center of mass]
    end
    subgraph CV[Convergence Verification]
        V1[Compare mu_t with mu_t_minus_1]
        V2{Difference less than epsilon?}
    end
    subgraph OP[Output Phase]
        O1[Final Cluster Membership Labels]
        O2[Final Centroid Coordinates]
        O3[WCSS Quality Score]
    end
    I1 --> S1
    I2 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> U1
    U1 --> U2
    U2 --> V1
    V1 --> V2
    V2 -->|Yes| O1
    V2 -->|No| S1
    O1 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows from the **Convergence Verification** block back to the **Assignment Phase** represent the iterative loop. The loop terminates only when the convergence check returns "Yes", passing control to the final output block.

### 4.3 Functional Role Mapping Table

| Block | Mathematical Equivalent | Engineering Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Initialization | $\mu_i^{(0)}$ for $i=1..K$ | Random seed / K-Means++ seeding |
| Assignment | $\arg\min_i \Vert x_j - \mu_i \Vert^2$ | Voronoi tessellation update |
| Update | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{N_i}\sum_{x \in C_i} x$ | Centroid re-centering |
| Convergence | $\Vert \mu_i^{(t)} - \mu_i^{(t-1)} \Vert < \epsilon$ | Stability controller |
| Output | Final $C_i$ and $\mu_i$ | Cluster map / label vector |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define clustering. Why is K-Means classified as an unsupervised learning algorithm?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Clustering** is the process of grouping similar data points together based on intrinsic patterns, without using pre-defined class labels.
> K-Means is **unsupervised** because:
> 1. It does **not** require labeled training data.
> 2. The algorithm discovers cluster structure purely from the geometric distribution of input features.
> 3. There is no ground-truth label to compare predictions against during training.

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Elbow Method? How is it used to choose the optimal number of clusters in K-Means?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Elbow Method** is a heuristic used to determine the optimal number of clusters $K$ in K-Means. The procedure is:
> 1. Run K-Means for a range of $K$ values (e.g., $K = 1$ to $10$).
> 2. Compute the **WCSS (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares)** for each $K$.
> 3. Plot WCSS versus $K$.
> 4. The optimal $K$ is the point where the curve forms an **"elbow"** — i.e., the point after which adding more clusters yields only marginal WCSS reduction.
> Beyond this point, additional clusters cause overfitting without meaningful gain in cluster compactness.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the steps of the K-Means clustering algorithm with a suitable block diagram. Discuss the role of the WCSS objective function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following 2D dataset with $K = 2$ clusters:

| Point | X1 | X2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| P1 | 1 | 1 |
| P2 | 2 | 1 |
| P3 | 4 | 3 |
| P4 | 5 | 4 |

Initial centroids: $\mu_1 = (1, 1)$ and $\mu_2 = (5, 4)$. Perform **two complete iterations** of K-Means and verify convergence. Compute the final WCSS. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**

1. **Input:** Dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$, number of clusters $K$, convergence threshold $\epsilon$.
2. **Initialize:** Select $K$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \dots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$ using Forgy or K-Means++.
3. **Repeat until convergence:**
   - **Assignment Step:** Assign each $x_j$ to the cluster with the nearest centroid using $C_i^{(t)} = \{x_j : \Vert x_j - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert^2 \leq \Vert x_j - \mu_p^{(t)} \Vert^2, \ \forall p\}$.
   - **Update Step:** Recompute $\mu_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i^{(t)}} x_j$.
4. **Termination:** Stop when $\Vert \mu_i^{(t+1)} - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert < \epsilon$ or maximum iterations reached.

**Role of WCSS (3 marks):**

- WCSS is the **objective function** $J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ that K-Means minimizes.
- It quantifies the **total intra-cluster variance** — lower WCSS means tighter clusters.
- K-Means performs **coordinate descent** on $J$: the assignment step minimizes $J$ w.r.t. labels, the update step minimizes $J$ w.r.t. centroids.
- The Elbow Method uses WCSS to choose the optimal $K$.

> **Block Diagram:** See Section 4.1 above for the canonical K-Means flow diagram.

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**ITERATION 1 — Assignment (using Euclidean distance):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(P_1, \mu_1) = \sqrt{0+0} = 0.00; \quad & d(P_1, \mu_2) = \sqrt{16+9} = 5.00 \Rightarrow P_1 \to C_1 \\
d(P_2, \mu_1) = \sqrt{1+0} = 1.00; \quad & d(P_2, \mu_2) = \sqrt{9+9} \approx 4.24 \Rightarrow P_2 \to C_1 \\
d(P_3, \mu_1) = \sqrt{9+4} \approx 3.61; \quad & d(P_3, \mu_2) = \sqrt{1+1} \approx 1.41 \Rightarrow P_3 \to C_2 \\
d(P_4, \mu_1) = \sqrt{16+9} = 5.00; \quad & d(P_4, \mu_2) = \sqrt{0+0} = 0.00 \Rightarrow P_4 \to C_2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 1 result:** $C_1 = \{P_1, P_2\}$, $C_2 = \{P_3, P_4\}$.

**ITERATION 1 — Update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1^{(1)} & = \frac{1}{2}[(1,1) + (2,1)] = (1.5, 1.0) \\
\mu_2^{(1)} & = \frac{1}{2}[(4,3) + (5,4)] = (4.5, 3.5) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**ITERATION 2 — Assignment (recompute with new centroids):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(P_1, \mu_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{0.25+0} = 0.50; \quad & d(P_1, \mu_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{16+6.25} \approx 4.72 \Rightarrow C_1 \\
d(P_2, \mu_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{0.25+0} = 0.50; \quad & d(P_2, \mu_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{6.25+6.25} \approx 3.54 \Rightarrow C_1 \\
d(P_3, \mu_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{6.25+4} \approx 3.20; \quad & d(P_3, \mu_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{0.25+0.25} \approx 0.71 \Rightarrow C_2 \\
d(P_4, \mu_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{12.25+9} \approx 4.61; \quad & d(P_4, \mu_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{0.25+0.25} \approx 0.71 \Rightarrow C_2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2 result:** $C_1 = \{P_1, P_2\}$, $C_2 = \{P_3, P_4\}$ — **unchanged**.

**Convergence:** Centroids remain at $(1.5, 1.0)$ and $(4.5, 3.5)$. **Algorithm converged in 2 iterations.**

**Final WCSS Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SSE_1 & = (1-1.5)^2 + (1-1)^2 + (2-1.5)^2 + (1-1)^2 = 0.25 + 0 + 0.25 + 0 = 0.50 \\
SSE_2 & = (4-4.5)^2 + (3-3.5)^2 + (5-4.5)^2 + (4-3.5)^2 = 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 = 1.00 \\
J_{total} & = 0.50 + 1.00 = \mathbf{1.50} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Discuss the **K-Means++ initialization** algorithm. Why is it preferred over random initialization? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **advantages and limitations** of K-Means. How does the **Silhouette Score** help in validating the quality of clusters formed? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**K-Means++ Algorithm (4 marks):**

K-Means++ is a smart centroid initialization strategy proposed to overcome the random initialization pitfall of standard K-Means. The steps are:

1. **Pick first centroid** $\mu_1$ uniformly at random from the dataset.
2. **For each subsequent centroid** $i = 2, 3, \dots, K$:
   - Compute the squared distance $D(x)^2 = \min_{j < i} \Vert x - \mu_j \Vert^2$ from each data point $x$ to its **nearest already-chosen centroid**.
   - Select the next centroid $\mu_i$ with probability proportional to $D(x)^2$ — i.e., points far from existing centroids have a **higher chance** of being selected.
3. Proceed with the standard K-Means algorithm using these smartly-chosen initial centroids.

**Why K-Means++ is Preferred (3 marks):**

| Issue with Random Initialization | How K-Means++ Solves It |
| :--- | :--- |
| Centroids may cluster together, leaving regions empty. | Probabilistic spread ensures diverse seeding. |
| High risk of converging to a poor **local minimum**. | Better seed leads to better convergence basin. |
| Inconsistent results across multiple runs. | Provides stable, reproducible clusterings. |
| Slow convergence due to bad starting point. | Typically converges in fewer iterations. |

K-Means++ has been proven (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007) to provide an $O(\log K)$-competitive approximation to the optimal WCSS.

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Advantages of K-Means (3 marks):**

1. **Simplicity:** Easy to understand, implement, and interpret.
2. **Computational Efficiency:** Linear complexity $O(n \cdot K \cdot D \cdot I)$ — scales well to large datasets.
3. **Tight Clusters:** Produces compact, globular clusters with low intra-cluster variance.
4. **Easy Adaptation:** Trivially extendable to fuzzy C-Means, spherical K-Means, etc.

**Limitations of K-Means (2 marks):**

1. **Must specify $K$ in advance** — non-trivial for unknown data.
2. **Sensitive to initialization** — random seeds may yield poor local optima.
3. **Assumes spherical, equally-sized clusters** — fails on elongated or irregular shapes.
4. **Sensitive to outliers** — single outliers can heavily distort the centroid.
5. **Euclidean-only by default** — struggles with categorical or high-dimensional data.

**Silhouette Score (2 marks):**

For each point $x$, the Silhouette Score is:

$$s(x) = \frac{b(x) - a(x)}{\max\{a(x), b(x)\}}$$

where:
- $a(x)$ = mean intra-cluster distance (average distance to all other points in the same cluster)
- $b(x)$ = mean nearest-cluster distance (lowest average distance to points in any other cluster)

Score range: $-1 \leq s(x) \leq +1$.
- $s \approx +1$: point is well-matched to its own cluster.
- $s \approx 0$: point lies on the cluster boundary.
- $s \approx -1$: point may be assigned to the wrong cluster.

**Higher average silhouette score = better clustering quality.** It is widely used to validate the choice of $K$ alongside the Elbow Method.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Distance formula error:** Students often forget to take the **square root** in Euclidean distance during manual calculation, leading to wrong cluster assignments. Always write the full $\sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ form.
> 2. **Centroid calculation error:** The new centroid is the **mean of coordinates**, not the geometric center of bounding box. Use the formula $\mu_i = \frac{1}{N_i} \sum x_j$ explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting to re-assign in Iteration 2:** A common mistake is declaring convergence after computing new centroids without re-running the assignment step. **Convergence requires checking that BOTH centroid positions AND cluster memberships are stable.**
> 4. **Mixing up Elbow Method with Silhouette:** The Elbow Method uses WCSS vs $K$ plot; the Silhouette Method uses average silhouette score vs $K$. They are **complementary**, not interchangeable.
> 5. **Failing to state convergence criterion explicitly:** Always end the numerical problem with: *"Centroids did not change between iterations, hence the algorithm has converged."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **K-Means is unsupervised, iterative, and centroid-based** — it groups $n$ data points into $K$ clusters without using labels.
- The **objective function** is the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**: $J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$.
- The algorithm has **two main steps per iteration**:
  - **Assignment** (E-step equivalent): Assign each point to the nearest centroid via Euclidean distance.
  - **Update** (M-step equivalent): Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points.
- **Convergence** is reached when centroids stop moving (positions stabilize) or a max iteration limit is hit.
- **Complexity:** $O(n \cdot K \cdot D \cdot I)$ where $I$ is the number of iterations.
- **Choosing $K$:**
  - **Elbow Method** — plot WCSS vs $K$, find the bend.
  - **Silhouette Score** — measure cluster cohesion vs separation.
  - **Gap Statistic** — compare WCSS to null reference distribution.
- **K-Means++** is the standard smart-initialization strategy that ensures diverse, well-spread starting centroids.
- **Limitations** to memorize: requires specifying $K$, sensitive to outliers, assumes spherical clusters, can converge to local optima.
- **Numerical exam tip:** Always show (i) distance table, (ii) cluster assignment, (iii) new centroid computation, (iv) convergence statement, and (v) WCSS value.
- **Clustering vs Classification:** Clustering is unsupervised (no labels); Classification is supervised (uses labels). Do NOT confuse the two in the exam.
- **Variants worth knowing:** Fuzzy C-Means (soft assignment), Mini-Batch K-Means (scalable), Spherical K-Means (cosine similarity), Bisecting K-Means (hierarchical flavor).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clustering - Hierarchical Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Clustering - Hierarchical Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical Clustering** is an unsupervised machine learning algorithm that organizes a dataset into a nested tree-like structure (a **dendrogram**) of clusters, without requiring the number of clusters ($k$) to be specified in advance. It produces a multi-level hierarchy where clusters at one level are formed by merging or splitting clusters at the adjacent level.

Hierarchical clustering operates purely on a **dissimilarity (distance) matrix** $D = [d_{ij}]_{n \times n}$ computed over $n$ data points, and produces a strict binary hierarchy (in the agglomerative case) representing $\mathcal{O}(n)$ levels of partitioning.

## 1.2 The Two Paradigms

| Paradigm | Direction | Strategy | Starting State | Ending State |
|----------|-----------|----------|----------------|--------------|
| **Agglomerative** | Bottom-Up | Repeatedly merge the two closest clusters | $n$ singleton clusters | $1$ mega-cluster |
| **Divisive** | Top-Down | Repeatedly split the most heterogeneous cluster | $1$ mega-cluster | $n$ singleton clusters |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering (AHC) is the most frequently examined variant in KTU papers. It has **time complexity** $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ in its naive form, and **space complexity** $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ to store the proximity matrix.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree" of Data

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a Classroom Photo:**
> Imagine 10 students standing randomly in a playground. An **agglomerative** approach works like a teacher grouping photos: first pair the two students standing closest (twins/siblings), then group pairs of friends, then group friend-circles, and finally unite the whole class. A **divisive** approach is the opposite: start with the entire class, identify the two subgroups that are most different (say, juniors vs seniors) and split them, continue splitting until every student is alone. The resulting "family tree" is the **dendrogram**.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

- **Dendrogram** — A tree diagram whose vertical axis records the **dissimilarity (height)** at which clusters merge and whose horizontal axis lists the data points.
- **Linkage Criterion** — The rule that defines the distance between two *clusters* (not just two points).
- **Cophenetic Distance** — The height in the dendrogram at which two points first become members of the same cluster.
- **Agglomerative Coefficient (AC)** — A scalar in $[0, 1]$ measuring the *strength of clustering structure*; values closer to **1** indicate strong, well-defined clusters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Flow of Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering (AHC)

The AHC algorithm follows a strict, deterministic 5-step procedure:

1. **Initialization:** Treat every data point $x_i$ ($i = 1, 2, \dots, n$) as an independent singleton cluster $C_i^{(0)} = \{x_i\}$.
2. **Proximity Matrix Construction:** Compute the $n \times n$ symmetric distance matrix $D^{(0)}$, where $D^{(0)}_{ij} = d(x_i, x_j)$ using a chosen point-wise distance metric.
3. **Iterative Merging Loop:**
   - Scan the upper triangle of $D^{(k)}$ and locate the pair $(C_a, C_b)$ achieving the **minimum** inter-cluster distance under the chosen **linkage criterion** $L$.
   - Merge them: $C_{new} = C_a \cup C_b$.
   - Update $D^{(k)}$ by recomputing distances between $C_{new}$ and every remaining cluster $C_r$ using the Lance–Williams recurrence formula.
4. **Termination:** Stop when exactly **1 cluster** remains (or when the user-specified number of clusters $k$ is reached by cutting the dendrogram at a chosen height $h$).
5. **Visualization:** Render the dendrogram with merge heights preserved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it is Greedy and Irreversible:** Once two clusters merge, the decision is never revisited. This makes AHC **deterministic** (given the same linkage and metric) but also **sensitive to early merge errors** — there is no backtracking.

## 2.2 The Four Standard Linkage Criteria

Let $C_p, C_q$ be two clusters. The inter-cluster distance $L(C_p, C_q)$ is defined as:

| Linkage Type | Formula | Behavior | Tendency |
|--------------|---------|----------|----------|
| **Single Linkage** (MIN) | $L(C_p, C_q) = \min_{x \in C_p, \, y \in C_q} d(x, y)$ | Connects nearest neighbours | Produces **chained**, elongated clusters |
| **Complete Linkage** (MAX) | $L(C_p, C_q) = \max_{x \in C_p, \, y \in C_q} d(x, y)$ | Connects farthest pair | Produces **compact, spherical** clusters |
| **Average Linkage** (UPGMA) | $L(C_p, C_q) = \frac{1}{\vert C_p \vert \cdot \vert C_q \vert} \sum_{x \in C_p} \sum_{y \in C_q} d(x, y)$ | Average pairwise distance | Compromise between MIN and MAX |
| **Ward's Linkage** | $L(C_p, C_q) = \sqrt{\frac{2 \vert C_p \vert \vert C_q \vert}{\vert C_p \vert + \vert C_q \vert}} \, \Vert \mu_p - \mu_q \Vert$ | Minimizes within-cluster SSE increase | Produces **balanced, minimum-variance** clusters |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Lance–Williams Unified Update Formula** allows *any* of the above linkages to be computed in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time after a merge:
>
> $$D(C_r, C_{p \cup q}) = \alpha_p \, D(C_r, C_p) + \alpha_q \, D(C_r, C_q) + \beta \, D(C_p, C_q) + \gamma \, \vert D(C_r, C_p) - D(C_r, C_q) \vert$$
>
> The constants $(\alpha_p, \alpha_q, \beta, \gamma)$ uniquely identify the linkage. This is the *theoretical backbone* of the AHC computational efficiency.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---------|---------|---------------|
| Euclidean distance | $d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Most common, sensitive to scale |
| Manhattan distance | $d(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| Minkowski distance (order $p$) | $d(x, y) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{m}\vert x_i - y_i \vert^{p}\right)^{1/p}$ | $p=1 \Rightarrow$ Manhattan; $p=2 \Rightarrow$ Euclidean |
| Cosine distance | $d(x, y) = 1 - \frac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x \Vert \, \Vert y \Vert}$ | For text / sparse data |
| Single linkage | $L = \min d(x, y)$ | Prone to chaining |
| Complete linkage | $L = \max d(x, y)$ | Sensitive to outliers |
| Average linkage | $L = \frac{1}{n_p n_q} \sum \sum d(x, y)$ | Balanced |
| Ward's linkage increase | $\Delta I = \frac{n_p \, n_q}{n_p + n_q} \, \Vert \mu_p - \mu_q \Vert^2$ | Variance-based |
| Agglomerative Coefficient | $AC = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \left(1 - \frac{h(i)}{\frac{1}{2} h_{max}}\right)$ | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| Time complexity (naive) | $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n^3)$ | $n$ points |
| Time complexity (priority queue) | $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$ | Optimized variant |
| Space complexity | $S(n) = \mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Stores proximity matrix |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Bioinformatics** — Phylogenetic tree construction (gene expression, evolutionary distance).
- **Customer Segmentation** — Marketing teams group buyers by purchase similarity.
- **Image Segmentation** — Multi-resolution region grouping in computer vision.
- **Document Clustering** — Organizing news articles, legal judgments, or research papers.
- **Anomaly / Outlier Detection** — Isolated branches in a dendrogram indicate outliers.
- **Remote Sensing** — Grouping satellite pixels of similar spectral signature.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Agglomerative, Single Linkage)

Consider 5 data points in $\mathbb{R}^2$:

$$A = (2, 10), \quad B = (2, 5), \quad C = (8, 4), \quad D = (5, 8), \quad E = (7, 5)$$

We use **Euclidean distance** and **single linkage**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Initial Proximity Matrix

For any two points $P = (p_1, p_2)$ and $Q = (q_1, q_2)$:

$$d(P, Q) = \sqrt{(p_1 - q_1)^2 + (p_2 - q_2)^2}$$

**Compute $d(A, B)$:**

$$d(A, B) = \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (10-5)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 25} = 5.00$$

**Compute $d(A, C)$:**

$$d(A, C) = \sqrt{(2-8)^2 + (10-4)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 36} = \sqrt{72} \approx 8.49$$

**Compute $d(A, D)$:**

$$d(A, D) = \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (10-8)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$$

**Compute $d(A, E)$:**

$$d(A, E) = \sqrt{(2-7)^2 + (10-5)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$$

**Compute $d(B, C)$:**

$$d(B, C) = \sqrt{(2-8)^2 + (5-4)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 1} = \sqrt{37} \approx 6.08$$

**Compute $d(B, D)$:**

$$d(B, D) = \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (5-8)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24$$

**Compute $d(B, E)$:**

$$d(B, E) = \sqrt{(2-7)^2 + (5-5)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 0} = 5.00$$

**Compute $d(C, D)$:**

$$d(C, D) = \sqrt{(8-5)^2 + (4-8)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5.00$$

**Compute $d(C, E)$:**

$$d(C, E) = \sqrt{(8-7)^2 + (4-5)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$$

**Compute $d(D, E)$:**

$$d(D, E) = \sqrt{(5-7)^2 + (8-5)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$$

**Initial Symmetric Distance Matrix $D^{(0)}$:**

| | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 5.00 | 8.49 | 3.61 | 7.07 |
| **B** | 5.00 | 0 | 6.08 | 4.24 | 5.00 |
| **C** | 8.49 | 6.08 | 0 | 5.00 | **1.41** |
| **D** | 3.61 | 4.24 | 5.00 | 0 | 3.61 |
| **E** | 7.07 | 5.00 | **1.41** | 3.61 | 0 |

### Step 2 — First Merge

The minimum off-diagonal entry is $\min = 1.41$, located at $d(C, E)$.

$$\Rightarrow \text{Merge } C \text{ and } E \text{ into cluster } C_1 = \{C, E\}, \text{ height } h_1 = 1.41$$

### Step 3 — Update Matrix (Single Linkage Rule)

For each remaining singleton $X \in \{A, B, D\}$, under **single linkage**:

$$d(X, C_1) = \min\{d(X, C), \, d(X, E)\}$$

**Compute $d(A, C_1)$:**

$$d(A, C_1) = \min\{d(A, C), d(A, E)\} = \min\{8.49, 7.07\} = 7.07$$

**Compute $d(B, C_1)$:**

$$d(B, C_1) = \min\{d(B, C), d(B, E)\} = \min\{6.08, 5.00\} = 5.00$$

**Compute $d(D, C_1)$:**

$$d(D, C_1) = \min\{d(D, C), d(D, E)\} = \min\{5.00, 3.61\} = 3.61$$

**Updated Distance Matrix $D^{(1)}$:**

| | A | B | D | C1 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 5.00 | 3.61 | 7.07 |
| **B** | 5.00 | 0 | 4.24 | 5.00 |
| **D** | 3.61 | 4.24 | 0 | 3.61 |
| **C1** | 7.07 | 5.00 | 3.61 | 0 |

### Step 4 — Second Merge

The minimum is $\min = 3.61$, occurring twice: $d(A, D) = d(D, C_1) = 3.61$.

Choosing the **first discovered minimum** lexicographically:

$$\Rightarrow \text{Merge } A \text{ and } D \text{ into cluster } C_2 = \{A, D\}, \text{ height } h_2 = 3.61$$

### Step 5 — Update Matrix (Single Linkage)

For $X \in \{B, C_1\}$:

$$d(X, C_2) = \min\{d(X, A), d(X, D)\}$$

**Compute $d(B, C_2)$:**

$$d(B, C_2) = \min\{d(B, A), d(B, D)\} = \min\{5.00, 4.24\} = 4.24$$

**Compute $d(C_1, C_2)$:**

$$d(C_1, C_2) = \min\{d(C_1, A), d(C_1, D)\} = \min\{7.07, 3.61\} = 3.61$$

**Updated Distance Matrix $D^{(2)}$:**

| | B | C1 | C2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **B** | 0 | 5.00 | 4.24 |
| **C1** | 5.00 | 0 | **3.61** |
| **C2** | 4.24 | **3.61** | 0 |

### Step 6 — Third (Final) Merge

The minimum is $3.61$ at $d(C_1, C_2)$:

$$\Rightarrow \text{Merge } C_1, C_2 \text{ into root cluster } C_3 = \{A, C, D, E\}, \text{ height } h_3 = 3.61$$

Then $B$ automatically joins at $h_4 = 4.24$ (the only remaining distance), yielding the **single root cluster** $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$.

### Step 7 — Summary of Merge Sequence (Merge Log)

| Step | Merged Pair | Height | Resulting Cluster |
|------|-------------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | $(C, E)$ | $1.41$ | $\{C, E\}$ |
| 2 | $(A, D)$ | $3.61$ | $\{A, D\}$ |
| 3 | $(\{C, E\}, \{A, D\})$ | $3.61$ | $\{A, C, D, E\}$ |
| 4 | $(B, \{A, C, D, E\})$ | $4.24$ | $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ |

## 3.2 Derivation: Ward's Linkage Variance Increase

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation of $\Delta I$ in Ward's Method:** When merging clusters $C_p$ and $C_q$ of sizes $n_p$ and $n_q$, the increase in total within-cluster Sum of Squared Errors (SSE) is:
>
> $$\Delta I(C_p, C_q) = SSE(C_p \cup C_q) - SSE(C_p) - SSE(C_q)$$
>
> Recall that for any cluster $C_k$ with mean $\mu_k$:
>
> $$SSE(C_k) = \sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^2$$
>
> Using the **parallel axis theorem (Huygens' theorem)**:
>
> $$SSE(C_p \cup C_q) = SSE(C_p) + SSE(C_q) + \frac{n_p \, n_q}{n_p + n_q} \, \Vert \mu_p - \mu_q \Vert^2$$
>
> Therefore the increase reduces to:
>
> $$\Delta I(C_p, C_q) = \frac{n_p \, n_q}{n_p + n_q} \, \Vert \mu_p - \mu_q \Vert^2$$
>
> Ward's method chooses the merge $(p, q)$ that **minimizes** $\Delta I$ — i.e., the merge causing the *smallest* increase in total intra-cluster variance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of AHC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Dataset X of n points in Rm"] --> B["Compute Proximity Matrix D0 using chosen metric"]
    B --> C["Initialize n singleton clusters Ci = xi"]
    C --> D{"Number of clusters > 1?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Scan upper triangle of Dk to find min dij"]
    E --> F["Apply Linkage Criterion L Single, Complete, Average, Ward"]
    F --> G["Merge Ca and Cb into Cnew = Ca union Cb"]
    G --> H["Update Dk using Lance Williams recurrence"]
    H --> I["Record merge height h and pair"]
    I --> D
    D -- No --> J["Construct Dendrogram with recorded heights"]
    J --> K["Cut dendrogram at threshold h to obtain k clusters"]
    K --> L["Output Final Cluster Labels"]
```

## 4.2 Dendrogram Schematic for the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["C"] --- M1["height = 1.41"]
    P2["E"] --- M1
    M1 --- M3["height = 3.61"]
    P3["A"] --- M2["height = 3.61"]
    P4["D"] --- M2
    M2 --- M3
    M3 --- M4["height = 4.24"]
    P5["B"] --- M4
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Data Characteristics"] --> B{"Shape of clusters?"}
    B -- "Elongated / Snake-like" --> C["Single Linkage"]
    B -- "Compact / Spherical" --> D["Complete Linkage"]
    B -- "Balanced / General-purpose" --> E["Average Linkage"]
    A --> F{"Goal?"}
    F -- "Minimize variance" --> G["Ward's Linkage"]
    F -- "Find outliers" --> C
    A --> H{"Noise level?"}
    H -- "High noise" --> D
    H -- "Low noise" --> C
```

## 4.4 Lance–Williams Recurrence Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Distance Matrix D k"] --> B["Locate min dpq"]
    B --> C["Merge p and q"]
    C --> D["Compute D r, new using alpha p, alpha q, beta, gamma"]
    D --> E["New Distance Matrix D k+1"]
    E --> F{"More clusters?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> B
    F -- "No" --> G["Output Dendrogram"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Agglomerative and Divisive Hierarchical Clustering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Agglomerative | Divisive |
|--------|---------------|----------|
| **Strategy** | Bottom-up merging | Top-down splitting |
| **Initial state** | $n$ singleton clusters | $1$ cluster with all $n$ points |
| **Final state** | $1$ mega-cluster | $n$ singleton clusters |
| **Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ naive | $\mathcal{O}(2^{n-1})$ worst-case |
| **Practical use** | Widely used (scipy, R) | Rare, computationally expensive |

**(1 mark per correct point × 3 = 3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define dendrogram and explain how the number of clusters is decided from it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **dendrogram** (1 mark) is a tree-like visual representation of the merge sequence produced by agglomerative hierarchical clustering, where the **vertical axis** denotes the dissimilarity (height) at which two clusters were merged and the **horizontal axis** lists the data points (1 mark).

The number of clusters is decided by drawing a **horizontal cut line** at a chosen dissimilarity threshold $h^*$: each distinct vertical branch intersected by this line corresponds to one cluster (1 mark). Equivalently, one can look for the **largest vertical gap** in the dendrogram — the cut is placed just below that gap, which maximizes inter-cluster dissimilarity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not skip linkage formulas in numerical problems** — explicitly write the chosen linkage criterion (1 mark reserved).
> - **Always update the distance matrix** after each merge (2 marks reserved for matrix updates).
> - **Mention the height** at which each merge occurs — the dendrogram step is worth 1–2 marks.
> - **Ward linkage questions** specifically require the variance increase formula $\Delta I = \frac{n_p n_q}{n_p + n_q}\Vert \mu_p - \mu_q \Vert^2$ — students who forget this lose 3 marks.
> - **Do not confuse centroid linkage with average linkage** — centroid uses cluster means; average uses all pairwise distances.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering algorithm in detail. Discuss the role of the proximity matrix and the Lance–Williams recurrence formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
1. Start with $n$ clusters, each containing a single point: $C_i^{(0)} = \{x_i\}$, $i = 1, \ldots, n$.
2. Compute the $n \times n$ proximity matrix $D^{(0)}$ where $D^{(0)}_{ij} = d(x_i, x_j)$ (Euclidean / Manhattan / Minkowski).
3. Repeat until one cluster remains:
   - Find the pair $(C_p, C_q)$ with the minimum inter-cluster distance $L(C_p, C_q)$.
   - Merge them: $C_{new} = C_p \cup C_q$.
   - Recompute distances from $C_{new}$ to all other clusters.
4. Output the merge history as a dendrogram.

**Role of the Proximity Matrix (2 marks):**
- Stores pairwise dissimilarities between all data points.
- Reduces repeated distance computations.
- Underpins the merging decision at every iteration.

**Lance–Williams Recurrence Formula (2 marks):**
$$D(C_r, C_{p \cup q}) = \alpha_p D(C_r, C_p) + \alpha_q D(C_r, C_q) + \beta D(C_p, C_q) + \gamma \vert D(C_r, C_p) - D(C_r, C_q) \vert$$

The constants $(\alpha_p, \alpha_q, \beta, \gamma)$ define the linkage:
- Single: $\alpha_p = \alpha_q = \frac{1}{2}$, $\beta = 0$, $\gamma = -\frac{1}{2}$
- Complete: $\alpha_p = \alpha_q = \frac{1}{2}$, $\beta = 0$, $\gamma = +\frac{1}{2}$
- Average: $\alpha_p = \frac{n_p}{n_p + n_q}$, $\alpha_q = \frac{n_q}{n_p + n_q}$, $\beta = 0$, $\gamma = 0$

This unifies all linkages in one $\mathcal{O}(1)$ update rule.

---

**Q. (b)** Consider the following five 2-D data points:
$A(2, 10), B(2, 5), C(8, 4), D(5, 8), E(7, 5)$.

Apply **Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering** using **Euclidean distance** and **complete linkage**. Show all distance matrices and the final dendrogram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial Distance Matrix** (computed exactly as in Section 3.1, with Euclidean metric):

| | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 5.00 | 8.49 | 3.61 | 7.07 |
| B | 5.00 | 0 | 6.08 | 4.24 | 5.00 |
| C | 8.49 | 6.08 | 0 | 5.00 | 1.41 |
| D | 3.61 | 4.24 | 5.00 | 0 | 3.61 |
| E | 7.07 | 5.00 | 1.41 | 3.61 | 0 |

**Step 2 — First Merge:** Minimum = $1.41$ at $(C, E)$ → merge $C_1 = \{C, E\}$ at $h_1 = 1.41$. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Update with Complete Linkage** (2 marks):
$$d(X, C_1) = \max\{d(X, C), d(X, E)\}$$

- $d(A, C_1) = \max\{8.49, 7.07\} = 8.49$
- $d(B, C_1) = \max\{6.08, 5.00\} = 6.08$
- $d(D, C_1) = \max\{5.00, 3.61\} = 5.00$

| | A | B | D | C1 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 5.00 | 3.61 | 8.49 |
| B | 5.00 | 0 | 4.24 | 6.08 |
| D | 3.61 | 4.24 | 0 | 5.00 |
| C1 | 8.49 | 6.08 | 5.00 | 0 |

**Step 4 — Second Merge:** Minimum = $3.61$ at $(A, D)$ → merge $C_2 = \{A, D\}$ at $h_2 = 3.61$. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Update** (1 mark):
- $d(B, C_2) = \max\{5.00, 4.24\} = 5.00$
- $d(C_1, C_2) = \max\{8.49, 5.00\} = 8.49$

| | B | C1 | C2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| B | 0 | 6.08 | 5.00 |
| C1 | 6.08 | 0 | 8.49 |
| C2 | 5.00 | 8.49 | 0 |

**Step 6 — Final Merge:** Minimum = $5.00$ at $(B, C_2)$ → merge $C_3 = \{A, B, D\}$ at $h_3 = 5.00$, then auto-join with $C_1$ at $h_4 = 6.08$. (1 mark)

**Step 7 — Final Dendrogram** (1 mark):

```
       6.08 ─┐                     ← C1 joins C3
             ├──────────────────────
       5.00 ─┤      ┌── A
             ├── B ─┤             ← C3 = {A, B, D}
             │      └── D
       3.61 ─┤
             ├── C
       1.41 ─┤
             └── E
```

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Compare and contrast the four major linkage criteria used in Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Linkage | Formula | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---------|---------|-----------|--------------|
| **Single** | $L = \min d(x, y)$ | (1) Can detect non-ellipsoidal, elongated shapes. (2) Conceptually simple. | (1) Suffers from **chaining effect** — noisy bridge points connect otherwise distinct clusters. |
| **Complete** | $L = \max d(x, y)$ | (1) Produces compact, equal-diameter clusters. (2) Less sensitive to chaining. | (1) Breaks large clusters; (2) Biased toward spherical clusters. (3) Sensitive to outliers. |
| **Average** | $L = \frac{1}{n_p n_q} \sum \sum d(x, y)$ | (1) Compromise between MIN and MAX; (2) Uses all pairwise info → robust. | (1) Computationally heavier; (2) Still biased toward spherical shapes. |
| **Ward's** | $L = \frac{n_p n_q}{n_p + n_q}\Vert \mu_p - \mu_q \Vert^2$ | (1) Minimizes information loss (variance increase); (2) Produces balanced, tight clusters. | (1) Only valid for Euclidean distance; (2) Tends to produce equally-sized clusters. |

**Marks Distribution:** 4 × (1.5 marks each) = 6 marks + 1 mark for comparative summary statement.

---

**Q. (b)** Consider the dataset:
$P_1 = (1, 1), \; P_2 = (1, 2), \; P_3 = (2, 1), \; P_4 = (10, 10), \; P_5 = (10, 11)$.

Apply single-linkage agglomerative clustering using Euclidean distance. Identify the cluster structure at cut-height $h = 5$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Initial Matrix** (2 marks):

| | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 | P5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 12.73 | 13.45 |
| P2 | 1.00 | 0 | 1.00 | 11.70 | 12.41 |
| P3 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 0 | 11.40 | 12.08 |
| P4 | 12.73 | 11.70 | 11.40 | 0 | 1.00 |
| P5 | 13.45 | 12.41 | 12.08 | 1.00 | 0 |

**Step 2 — Merges** (3 marks):

- **Merge 1:** $d(P_1, P_2) = d(P_1, P_3) = d(P_2, P_3) = 1.00$ → merge $P_1, P_2$ first at $h = 1.00$.
- **Merge 2:** $\{P_1, P_2\}$ joins with $P_3$ at $h = 1.00$ (single-linkage min is $1.00$).
- **Merge 3:** $d(P_4, P_5) = 1.00$ → merge $P_4, P_5$ at $h = 1.00$.
- **Merge 4:** The two clusters $\{P_1, P_2, P_3\}$ and $\{P_4, P_5\}$ merge at $h = 11.40$ (their single-linkage distance).

**Step 3 — Cut at $h = 5$** (1 mark):
A horizontal cut at $h = 5$ separates the dendrogram into **2 clusters**:
$$C_A = \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}, \quad C_B = \{P_4, P_5\}$$

**Step 4 — Final Dendrogram** (1 mark):

```
       11.40 ─┐
              ├── C1
        1.00 ─┤   ├── P1
              ├── P2
              ├── P3
        1.00 ─┐
              ├── C2 ── P4
              ├── P5
```

**Verification:** Intra-cluster distances are all $\leq 1.41 \ll 5$, while inter-cluster distance is $\geq 11.40 \gg 5$. The choice of $h = 5$ cleanly separates the two well-separated sub-groups.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hierarchical Clustering**

- **Definition** — Builds a *hierarchy* of clusters without pre-specifying $k$; output is a **dendrogram**.
- **Two paradigms** — **Agglomerative** (bottom-up, $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$) and **Divisive** (top-down, $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$).
- **Distance metrics** — Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski, Cosine; choice must precede linkage selection.
- **Four linkages** — Single (MIN, chaining), Complete (MAX, compact), Average (UPGMA, balanced), Ward (variance-minimizing).
- **Lance–Williams formula** — Unified $\mathcal{O}(1)$ update rule; constants $(\alpha_p, \alpha_q, \beta, \gamma)$ encode the linkage.
- **Merge heights** must be **recorded** and **reported** — they form the dendrogram.
- **Ward's $\Delta I$** — Minimizes within-cluster SSE increase; only valid for Euclidean distance.
- **Agglomerative Coefficient (AC)** — Scalar in $[0, 1]$ measuring cluster structure strength; closer to 1 is better.
- **Irreversibility** — AHC is greedy and non-backtracking; sensitive to noise and early merge errors.
- **Cophenetic distance** — Dendrogram height at which two points first co-cluster; used to validate clustering quality via the **cophenetic correlation coefficient**.
- **Cut the dendrogram** at the largest vertical gap to get the most natural $k$.
- **Scalability** — For $n > 10{,}000$, prefer **BIRCH** or **Mini-Batch K-Means** over vanilla AHC.
- **KTU keywords to memorize**: proximity matrix, dendrogram, linkage, Lance–Williams, Ward's method, agglomerative coefficient, cophenetic distance, $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ complexity, chaining effect.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Density-Based Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Density-Based Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Density-Based Clustering** is a non-parametric, unsupervised machine learning methodology that identifies arbitrarily shaped clusters as dense regions of data points separated by low-density regions (sparse areas) in the feature space. Unlike partition-based methods (e.g., K-Means) or hierarchical methods, density-based clustering does not require the user to pre-specify the number of clusters, making it particularly suitable for exploratory data analysis and anomaly detection tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)** is the canonical and most widely cited density-based clustering algorithm, formally introduced by Ester, Kriegel, Sander, and Xu in 1996. It defines clusters as **maximal sets of density-connected points**.

The two pivotal hyperparameters in DBSCAN are:
- **ε (Epsilon)**: The radius of the neighborhood around a data point.
- **MinPts (Minimum Points)**: The minimum number of data points required to form a dense region.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a City Map Analogy**: Imagine you are a cartographer looking at a city map dotted with restaurants. You want to group these restaurants into "food districts." K-Means would force you to draw a perfect circle around each district — failing to capture the organic, sprawling shape of a real food district. Density-Based Clustering, however, is like saying: *"A district exists wherever there is a high concentration of restaurants (a dense crowd). Any isolated restaurant standing alone in the wilderness is noise."* You walk from one restaurant to the next within a fixed walking distance (ε), and if you find enough neighbors (MinPts) at every step, you have discovered a district.

## 1.3 Three Types of Points in DBSCAN

| Point Type | Formal Definition | Intuitive Meaning |
|------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Core Point** | A point having at least **MinPts** points (including itself) within its ε-neighborhood. | A "popular hub" — a point surrounded by many neighbors. |
| **Border Point** | A point that is not a core point itself, but lies within the ε-neighborhood of a core point. | A "neighbor of a hub" — reachable from a core point but lacks enough neighbors itself. |
| **Noise Point (Outlier)** | A point that is neither a core point nor a border point. | A "lonely wanderer" — an isolated point in a sparse region. |

## 1.4 Why Density-Based Clustering Matters in Engineering

- **Anomaly Detection**: Identifying fraudulent credit card transactions, network intrusions, and defective manufactured products.
- **Geospatial Analysis**: Clustering GPS trajectories, identifying accident-prone zones, and environmental hotspot detection.
- **Bioinformatics**: Detecting cell populations in flow cytometry and disease cluster identification in epidemiology.
- **Image Segmentation**: Separating objects of irregular shapes from backgrounds in computer vision tasks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Definitions

Let $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ be a dataset of $n$ points in a $d$-dimensional feature space. The distance metric is typically Euclidean, but any metric satisfying the triangle inequality is valid.

### 2.1.1 ε-Neighborhood ($N_{\varepsilon}$)

The ε-neighborhood of a point $p \in \mathcal{D}$ is defined as the set of all points within a radius ε centered at $p$.

$$N_{\varepsilon}(p) = \{q \in \mathcal{D} \mid \text{dist}(p, q) \leq \varepsilon\}$$

### 2.1.2 Core Point Condition

A point $p$ is classified as a **core point** if and only if the cardinality of its ε-neighborhood is at least **MinPts**.

$$p \text{ is a core point} \iff \vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$$

### 2.1.3 Direct Density-Reachability

A point $q$ is **directly density-reachable** from $p$ if:
1. $q \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$, AND
2. $p$ is a core point (i.e., $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note**: Direct density-reachability is an **asymmetric** relation. If $p$ is a core point and $q$ is in $p$'s neighborhood, then $q$ is directly density-reachable from $p$, but the converse may not hold (because $q$ might be a border point).

### 2.1.4 Density-Reachability

A point $q$ is **density-reachable** from $p$ if there exists a chain of points $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_m$ such that:
- $p_1 = p$, $p_m = q$
- Each $p_{i+1}$ is directly density-reachable from $p_i$

### 2.1.5 Density-Connectivity

Two points $p$ and $q$ are **density-connected** if there exists a point $o$ such that both $p$ and $q$ are density-reachable from $o$.

## 2.2 DBSCAN Algorithm — Operational Logic

The DBSCAN algorithm proceeds in the following structured steps:

1. **Initialization**: Select the parameters ε and MinPts. Mark all points as unvisited.
2. **Point Selection**: Pick an arbitrary unvisited point $p$ from the dataset.
3. **Neighborhood Retrieval**: Compute $N_{\varepsilon}(p)$.
4. **Classification Check**:
   - If $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert < \text{MinPts}$, mark $p$ as a **noise point** (temporarily) and move to the next point.
   - Otherwise, mark $p$ as a core point, create a new cluster $C$, and add $p$ to $C$.
5. **Cluster Expansion**: For each point $q$ in $N_{\varepsilon}(p)$:
   - If $q$ was previously marked as noise, reassign $q$ to cluster $C$ (border point promotion).
   - If $q$ is unvisited, mark $q$ as visited, compute $N_{\varepsilon}(q)$, and if $q$ is a core point, add its neighborhood to the seed set of $C$.
6. **Termination**: When the seed set is exhausted, output cluster $C$. Repeat from Step 2 until all points have been visited.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "temporarily" mark noise?** A point initially marked as noise may later be discovered to be in the neighborhood of a core point. DBSCAN handles this by reclassifying such points as **border points** of the corresponding cluster.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description |
|---------|------------------------|-------------|
| ε-Neighborhood | $N_{\varepsilon}(p) = \{q \in \mathcal{D} \mid \text{dist}(p, q) \leq \varepsilon\}$ | Set of points within radius ε of $p$ |
| Euclidean Distance | $d(p, q) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(p_i - q_i)^2}$ | Default distance metric |
| Core Point Test | $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$ | Defines a dense region anchor |
| Direct Density-Reachability | $q \in N_{\varepsilon}(p) \land p$ is a core | Asymmetric reachability relation |
| Density-Reachability | $\exists$ chain $p_1, \ldots, p_m$ of direct reachability | Transitive closure of direct reachability |
| Density-Connectivity | $\exists \, o : p \leftarrow o \rightarrow q$ density-reachable | Symmetric connectivity |
| Heuristic for MinPts | $\text{MinPts} \geq d + 1$ | Recommended minimum for $d$-dimensional data |
| Heuristic for ε | $k$-distance plot elbow | Use $k = \text{MinPts}$ to find optimal ε |

## 2.4 Choice of Parameters — Practical Engineering Guidelines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selecting MinPts**: A common heuristic is to set **MinPts ≥ D + 1**, where $D$ is the dimensionality of the dataset. For 2D data, MinPts = 4 is a common starting point. Larger MinPts values produce more robust clusters but may merge distinct clusters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selecting ε (Epsilon)**: Use the **k-distance plot** method. Compute the distance of every point to its $k$-th nearest neighbor (where $k = \text{MinPts}$), sort these distances in descending order, and plot them. The "elbow" or "knee" point in the plot indicates the optimal ε value.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Manufacturing Quality Control**: DBSCAN identifies defective products on assembly lines as noise points, enabling real-time rejection of anomalies without pre-defining a fixed number of defect types.
- **Urban Planning and Traffic Engineering**: Analyzing taxi GPS data to discover traffic congestion patterns of irregular shapes that K-Means cannot capture.
- **Medical Imaging**: Segmenting tumors in MRI scans where tumors have arbitrary, non-spherical shapes.
- **Cybersecurity**: Detecting distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks by identifying isolated, anomalous network traffic patterns.

## 2.6 OPTICS — An Extension of DBSCAN

**OPTICS (Ordering Points To Identify the Clustering Structure)** addresses DBSCAN's sensitivity to parameter selection. It produces an **augmented cluster ordering** representing the density-based clustering structure across multiple ε values simultaneously. Key outputs include:

- **Reachability Distance**: The minimum distance at which a point must be reached to be part of a cluster.
- **Core-Distance**: The minimum ε such that a point becomes a core point.

$$ \text{core-dist}(p) = \begin{cases} \text{undefined} & \text{if } \vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert < \text{MinPts} \\ \text{dist}(p, \text{MinPts-th nearest neighbor}) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

$$ \text{reach-dist}(p, o) = \max(\text{core-dist}(o), \text{dist}(o, p)) $$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Reachability Distance

The reachability distance in OPTICS is a key concept. We derive it systematically.

**Step 1**: Define the core-distance of a point $o$.

The core-distance of $o$ is the distance from $o$ to its MinPts-th nearest neighbor, provided that $o$ is a core point. If $o$ is not a core point, the core-distance is undefined.

**Step 2**: Define the reachability distance from $o$ to $p$.

The reachability distance of $p$ with respect to $o$ is the maximum of:
- The core-distance of $o$ (the minimum radius that makes $o$ a core point)
- The actual Euclidean distance between $o$ and $p$

**Step 3**: Mathematical Expression

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{reach-dist}_{\varepsilon, \text{MinPts}}(p, o) &= \max(\text{core-dist}(o), \, \text{dist}(o, p)) \\
&= \begin{cases}
\text{undefined} & \text{if } \vert N_{\varepsilon}(o) \vert < \text{MinPts} \\
\max(\text{core-dist}(o), \text{dist}(o, p)) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation of the `max` operation**: The reachability distance is the minimum radius ε at which $p$ would be directly density-reachable from $o$. It cannot be smaller than the core-distance of $o$ (because $o$ must first become a core point), and it cannot be smaller than the actual distance between $o$ and $p$.

## 3.2 Worked Example: DBSCAN Step-by-Step on a 2D Dataset

Consider the following 2D dataset with **MinPts = 3** and **ε = 1.5**:

$$
\mathcal{D} = \{A(1,1), \, B(1,2), \, C(2,1), \, D(2,2), \, E(8,8), \, F(8,9), \, G(9,8), \, H(15,15)\}
$$

**Step 1: Compute the Pairwise Euclidean Distances**

We compute key distances (only those relevant to ε-neighborhoods). For instance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(A, B) &= \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 1} = 1.0 \\
d(A, C) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-1)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0} = 1.0 \\
d(A, D) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 1} \approx 1.414 \\
d(B, C) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 1} \approx 1.414 \\
d(B, D) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (2-2)^2} = 1.0 \\
d(C, D) &= \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (1-2)^2} = 1.0 \\
d(E, F) &= \sqrt{(8-8)^2 + (8-9)^2} = 1.0 \\
d(E, G) &= \sqrt{(8-9)^2 + (8-8)^2} = 1.0 \\
d(F, G) &= \sqrt{(8-9)^2 + (9-8)^2} \approx 1.414 \\
d(H, \text{anything}) &\geq 6.36
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Determine ε-Neighborhoods for Each Point**

For ε = 1.5, we list each point's neighbors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\varepsilon}(A) &= \{A, B, C, D\} \quad (\vert N_{\varepsilon}(A) \vert = 4) \\
N_{\varepsilon}(B) &= \{A, B, C, D\} \quad (\vert N_{\varepsilon}(B) \vert = 4) \\
N_{\varepsilon}(C) &= \{A, B, C, D\} \quad (\vert N_{\varepsilon}(C) \vert = 4) \\
N_{\varepsilon}(D) &= \{A, B, C, D\} \quad (\vert N_{\varepsilon}(D) \vert = 4) \\
N_{\varepsilon}(E) &= \{E, F, G\} \quad (\vert N_{\varepsilon}(E) \vert = 3) \\
N_{\varepsilon}(F) &= \{E, F, G\} \quad (\vert N_{\varepsilon}(F) \vert = 3) \\
N_{\varepsilon}(G) &= \{E, F, G\} \quad (\vert N_{\varepsilon}(G) \vert = 3) \\
N_{\varepsilon}(H) &= \{H\} \quad (\vert N_{\varepsilon}(H) \vert = 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Classify Each Point**

- **A, B, C, D**: $\vert N_{\varepsilon} \vert = 4 \geq 3$ → **Core Points**
- **E, F, G**: $\vert N_{\varepsilon} \vert = 3 \geq 3$ → **Core Points**
- **H**: $\vert N_{\varepsilon} \vert = 1 < 3$ → **Noise Point** (not in any core point's neighborhood since all other points are more than 1.5 away)

**Step 4: Form Clusters**

- **Cluster 1**: {A, B, C, D} — all core points, mutually density-connected.
- **Cluster 2**: {E, F, G} — all core points, mutually density-connected.
- **Noise**: {H}

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Advantages vs. Disadvantages of DBSCAN

| Aspect | Advantage | Limitation |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| Cluster Shape | Detects **arbitrarily shaped** clusters (non-spherical) | Not optimal for clusters with very **varying densities** |
| Number of Clusters | Does **not** require pre-specifying $k$ | Sensitive to ε and MinPts selection |
| Outlier Handling | Has **built-in noise detection** | Border points may be ambiguously assigned to nearest cluster |
| Computational Cost | $O(n \log n)$ with spatial indexing (e.g., KD-tree) | $O(n^2)$ in worst case for high-dimensional data |
| Scalability | Efficient for large spatial datasets | Suffers from **curse of dimensionality** |
| Determinism | Produces same clusters across runs | Does not work well when data has **overlapping densities** |

## 3.4 Mathematical Justification: Why Density-Reachability is Asymmetric

> [!NOTE]
> Consider points $p$ (a core point with many neighbors) and $q$ (a border point with few neighbors). Although $q \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$, it is **not** necessarily true that $p \in N_{\varepsilon}(q)$ (or even if it is, $q$ may not be a core point). Hence, $q$ is directly density-reachable from $p$, but $p$ is **not** directly density-reachable from $q$. This asymmetry is fundamental to DBSCAN and is resolved by using **density-connectivity** (a symmetric relation) for final cluster definition.

## 3.5 Performance Complexity Analysis

The time complexity of DBSCAN depends on the spatial index used:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{DBSCAN}} &= O(n \cdot T_{\text{query}}) \\
T_{\text{query}} &= \begin{cases}
O(\log n) & \text{with KD-tree or R-tree indexing} \\
O(n) & \text{naive linear scan (worst case)}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, in the best case with proper indexing:

$$
T_{\text{DBSCAN, best}} = O(n \log n)
$$

And in the worst case (no spatial index, high-dimensional data):

$$
T_{\text{DBSCAN, worst}} = O(n^2)
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DBSCAN Algorithm Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Dataset D, epsilon, MinPts] --> B[Mark all points as unvisited]
    B --> C{Any unvisited point p?}
    C -- No --> Z[End: Output clusters and noise]
    C -- Yes --> D[Mark p as visited]
    D --> E[Compute N_epsilon of p]
    E --> F{Is N_epsilon of p greater than or equal to MinPts?}
    F -- No --> G[Mark p as noise temporarily]
    G --> C
    F -- Yes --> H[Create new cluster C]
    H --> I[Add p to cluster C]
    I --> J[Add all points in N_epsilon of p to seed set S]
    J --> K{Is seed set S non-empty?}
    K -- No --> C
    K -- Yes --> L[Pop a point q from S]
    L --> M{Was q previously marked as noise?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Add q to cluster C]
    N --> O
    M -- No --> O{Was q unvisited?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Mark q as visited]
    P --> Q[Compute N_epsilon of q]
    Q --> R{Is N_epsilon of q greater than or equal to MinPts?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Add points in N_epsilon of q to seed set S]
    S --> O
    R -- No --> O
    O -- No --> T[Add q to cluster C]
    T --> K
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Density-Based Clustering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        I1[Raw Dataset D]
        I2[Epsilon Parameter]
        I3[MinPts Parameter]
    end

    subgraph PROC["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        P1[Distance Metric Module]
        P2[Neighborhood Query Engine]
        P3[Point Classifier]
        P4[Cluster Expander]
    end

    subgraph CLASS["CLASSIFICATION SUBMODULE"]
        C1[Core Point Detector]
        C2[Border Point Detector]
        C3[Noise Point Detector]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1[Cluster Set 1]
        O2[Cluster Set 2]
        O3[Cluster Set N]
        O4[Noise Points]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P2
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> C1
    P3 --> C2
    P3 --> C3
    C1 --> P4
    C2 --> P4
    C3 --> O4
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
```

```mermaid
        A1[Core Point A]

    B1 --- B3
    N1 -.-> N2
```
