# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Core Concept Definition `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define density-based clustering. Explain the terms core point, border point, and noise point with reference to DBSCAN.**

**Model Answer:**

Density-based clustering is an unsupervised learning technique that groups together data points that are closely packed (high-density regions) and marks points in low-density regions as outliers. DBSCAN is the most popular algorithm in this category.

- **Core Point**: A data point that has at least **MinPts** points (including itself) within its ε-radius neighborhood. These points lie in the interior of a cluster.
- **Border Point**: A data point that is not a core point but falls within the ε-neighborhood of a core point. It lies on the edge of a cluster.
- **Noise Point**: A data point that is neither a core point nor a border point. It is treated as an outlier or anomaly.

---

### Question 2: Parameter Significance `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the role of the parameters ε (epsilon) and MinPts in DBSCAN? How does the k-distance plot help in choosing ε?**

**Model Answer:**

- **ε (Epsilon)**: Defines the radius of the neighborhood around each data point. A larger ε results in larger neighborhoods and potentially fewer, larger clusters. A smaller ε may fragment clusters or label points as noise.
- **MinPts**: Specifies the minimum number of points required within the ε-neighborhood for a point to qualify as a core point. Higher MinPts values lead to more conservative cluster formation and better noise detection.

- **k-Distance Plot Method**: The k-distance plot is constructed by computing the distance of every point to its $k$-th nearest neighbor (where $k = \text{MinPts}$), sorting these distances in descending order, and plotting them. The "elbow" or "knee" point in the resulting curve indicates the optimal value of ε. Points to the left of the knee belong to clusters, while points to the right are likely noise.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: DBSCAN Algorithm and Reachability Analysis `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the DBSCAN algorithm in detail. Describe how the algorithm identifies core points, border points, and noise points. (7 marks)

**(b)** Define the following relations in DBSCAN: direct density-reachability, density-reachability, and density-connectivity. Explain why density-reachability is asymmetric with a suitable example. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The DBSCAN algorithm operates on the principle that clusters are dense regions in the feature space. The detailed procedure is as follows:

**Step 1: Parameter Initialization**
The algorithm accepts two input parameters: ε (neighborhood radius) and MinPts (minimum number of points to form a dense region). All points in the dataset $\mathcal{D}$ are initially marked as "unvisited."

**Step 2: Point Selection and Neighborhood Computation**
An arbitrary unvisited point $p$ is selected and marked as "visited." The ε-neighborhood of $p$ is computed as $N_{\varepsilon}(p) = \{q \in \mathcal{D} \mid d(p, q) \leq \varepsilon\}$.

**Step 3: Classification of $p$**
- If $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$, then $p$ is classified as a **core point**, and a new cluster $C$ is initiated with $p$ as its first member.
- If $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert < \text{MinPts}$, then $p$ is temporarily marked as a **noise point**.

**Step 4: Cluster Expansion**
For every point $q \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$:
- If $q$ was previously marked as noise, it is reclassified as a **border point** of cluster $C$.
- If $q$ is unvisited, it is marked as visited. Its ε-neighborhood is computed; if $q$ is also a core point, all points in its neighborhood are added to the seed set of $C$.

**Step 5: Iteration and Termination**
The algorithm iterates until the seed set of the current cluster is exhausted. It then proceeds to the next unvisited point. When all points are visited, the algorithm terminates, producing a set of clusters and a set of noise points.

**Classification Summary**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Core Point: } & p \text{ such that } \vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts} \\
\text{Border Point: } & p \text{ such that } p \in N_{\varepsilon}(q) \text{ for some core point } q, \text{ but } \vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert < \text{MinPts} \\
\text{Noise Point: } & p \text{ such that } p \text{ is neither a core point nor a border point}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Direct Density-Reachability**: A point $q$ is directly density-reachable from $p$ if:
1. $q \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$, AND
2. $p$ is a core point (i.e., $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$).

**Density-Reachability**: A point $q$ is density-reachable from $p$ if there exists a finite chain of points $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_m$ where $p_1 = p$, $p_m = q$, and each $p_{i+1}$ is directly density-reachable from $p_i$.

**Density-Connectivity**: Two points $p$ and $q$ are density-connected if there exists a point $o$ such that both $p$ and $q$ are density-reachable from $o$.

**Asymmetry of Density-Reachability — Example**:

Consider three points $A$, $B$, and $C$ in a 1D line with positions $A = 0$, $B = 1$, $C = 2$, where ε = 1.5 and MinPts = 3.

- $N_{\varepsilon}(A) = \{A, B\}$ (size 2) — $A$ is **not** a core point.
- $N_{\varepsilon}(B) = \{A, B, C\}$ (size 3) — $B$ **is** a core point.
- $N_{\varepsilon}(C) = \{B, C\}$ (size 2) — $C$ is **not** a core point.

Now, $C$ is directly density-reachable from $B$ because $C \in N_{\varepsilon}(B)$ and $B$ is a core point. However, $B$ is **not** directly density-reachable from $C$ because $A \notin N_{\varepsilon}(C)$ and $C$ is not a core point. This demonstrates the **asymmetric** nature of direct density-reachability. Density-connectivity resolves this by introducing a common anchor point.

---

### Question B: OPTICS and Parameter Selection `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** What are the limitations of DBSCAN? Explain how OPTICS addresses these limitations. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider the 2D dataset: $P_1(1,1)$, $P_2(2,1)$, $P_3(2,2)$, $P_4(1,2)$, $P_5(10,10)$, $P_6(11,10)$, $P_7(20,20)$. Apply DBSCAN with MinPts = 3 and ε = 1.5. Identify the clusters and noise points. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Limitations of DBSCAN**:

1. **Sensitivity to Parameters**: DBSCAN's output heavily depends on the choice of ε and MinPts. A poor choice can merge distinct clusters or fragment a single cluster.
2. **Difficulty with Varying Densities**: DBSCAN struggles when clusters have significantly different densities. A single ε value cannot simultaneously capture both dense and sparse clusters.
3. **Curse of Dimensionality**: In high-dimensional spaces, distance metrics become less meaningful, degrading DBSCAN's performance.
4. **Border Point Ambiguity**: Border points in overlapping regions may be assigned to the nearest cluster arbitrarily, leading to non-deterministic edge cases in some implementations.

**How OPTICS Addresses These Limitations**:

**OPTICS (Ordering Points To Identify the Clustering Structure)** is an extension of DBSCAN that addresses the parameter sensitivity issue. Key features include:

1. **Augmented Cluster Ordering**: OPTICS produces a linear ordering of all data points along with two additional attributes per point: **core-distance** and **reachability-distance**. This ordering captures the multi-scale density structure of the data.

2. **Core-Distance Definition**: The core-distance of a point $p$ is the minimum ε value at which $p$ becomes a core point.

3. **Reachability-Distance Definition**: The reachability distance from $o$ to $p$ is defined as:

$$
\text{reach-dist}(p, o) = \max(\text{core-dist}(o), \, d(o, p))
$$

4. **Reachability Plot**: OPTICS generates a reachability plot (distance vs. ordered index) where valleys indicate clusters and peaks indicate noise. By varying a threshold on the reachability distance, clusters at different density levels can be extracted, eliminating the need to pre-commit to a single ε value.

5. **Multi-Density Capability**: By adjusting the reachability threshold, OPTICS can identify both dense and sparse clusters in the same dataset, overcoming DBSCAN's single-density limitation.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Compute Pairwise Distances**

The relevant distances for ε-neighborhood computation (ε = 1.5):

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(P_1, P_2) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-1)^2} = 1.0 \\
d(P_1, P_3) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \\
d(P_1, P_4) &= \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (1-2)^2} = 1.0 \\
d(P_2, P_3) &= \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (1-2)^2} = 1.0 \\
d(P_2, P_4) &= \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \\
d(P_3, P_4) &= \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (2-2)^2} = 1.0 \\
d(P_5, P_6) &= \sqrt{(10-11)^2 + (10-10)^2} = 1.0 \\
d(P_5, P_7) &= \sqrt{(10-20)^2 + (10-20)^2} = \sqrt{200} \approx 14.14 \\
d(P_6, P_7) &= \sqrt{(11-20)^2 + (10-20)^2} = \sqrt{181} \approx 13.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Determine ε-Neighborhoods (ε = 1.5)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\varepsilon}(P_1) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\} \quad (\text{size} = 4) \\
N_{\varepsilon}(P_2) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\} \quad (\text{size} = 4) \\
N_{\varepsilon}(P_3) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\} \quad (\text{size} = 4) \\
N_{\varepsilon}(P_4) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\} \quad (\text{size} = 4) \\
N_{\varepsilon}(P_5) &= \{P_5, P_6\} \quad (\text{size} = 2) \\
N_{\varepsilon}(P_6) &= \{P_5, P_6\} \quad (\text{size} = 2) \\
N_{\varepsilon}(P_7) &= \{P_7\} \quad (\text{size} = 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Classify Points (MinPts = 3)**

- $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$: All have $\vert N_{\varepsilon} \vert = 4 \geq 3$ → **Core Points**
- $P_5, P_6$: Both have $\vert N_{\varepsilon} \vert = 2 < 3$ → Not core points. Check if they are in any core point's neighborhood. Since no other point has distance ≤ 1.5 to $P_5$ or $P_6$, they are **Noise Points**.
- $P_7$: Has $\vert N_{\varepsilon} \vert = 1 < 3$ and no neighbors within ε → **Noise Point**.

**Step 4: Final Cluster Assignment**

- **Cluster 1**: $\{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\}$
- **Noise**: $\{P_5, P_6, P_7\}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often forget to include the point $p$ itself when computing $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert$. The point $p$ is always a member of its own neighborhood. Failure to include $p$ leads to off-by-one errors in classification.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: When a point is initially marked as noise, students forget to reclassify it as a border point if it is later found within a core point's neighborhood. Always remember the two-phase nature of noise classification in DBSCAN.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Students confuse direct density-reachability with density-reachability. Direct requires $p$ to be a core point; density-reachability involves a chain. Writing one when the other is asked will cost 2-3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Failing to state that the k-distance plot uses $k = \text{MinPts}$ and that the elbow method is applied on the sorted distances. Partial marks are awarded for incomplete parameter selection rationale.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Density-Based Clustering**

- **Core Definition**: Density-based clustering groups dense regions and isolates sparse points as noise. DBSCAN is the canonical algorithm.
- **Two Key Parameters**: ε (neighborhood radius) and MinPts (density threshold).
- **Three Point Types**:
  - **Core Point**: $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$
  - **Border Point**: Within ε of a core point, but not a core point itself
  - **Noise Point**: Neither a core point nor a border point
- **Three Key Relations**:
  - **Direct Density-Reachability**: Asymmetric, requires core point origin
  - **Density-Reachability**: Transitive closure via a chain
  - **Density-Connectivity**: Symmetric, via a common anchor point
- **Strengths of DBSCAN**: Arbitrary cluster shapes, no need to pre-specify $k$, built-in outlier detection, deterministic output.
- **Weaknesses of DBSCAN**: Sensitive to ε and MinPts, struggles with varying densities, curse of dimensionality.
- **Parameter Selection Heuristic**:
  - $\text{MinPts} \geq d + 1$ (where $d$ is dimensionality)
  - Use **k-distance plot** (with $k = \text{MinPts}$) to find optimal ε via the elbow method.
- **OPTICS Extension**: Generates an augmented cluster ordering with core-distance and reachability-distance, enabling multi-density cluster extraction without committing to a single ε.
- **Time Complexity**: $O(n \log n)$ with spatial indexing; $O(n^2)$ in the worst case without indexing.
- **Engineering Applications**: Anomaly detection, geospatial clustering, image segmentation, bioinformatics, cybersecurity, and manufacturing quality control.
- **Exam Tip**: Always include the point $p$ itself when computing $N_{\varepsilon}(p)$ — this is a frequent KTU examiner trap.
- **Comparison with K-Means**: K-Means requires $k$ and assumes spherical clusters; DBSCAN requires ε/MinPts and handles arbitrary shapes with built-in noise detection.
- **Reachability Distance Formula**: $\text{reach-dist}(p, o) = \max(\text{core-dist}(o), d(o, p))$ — remember the `max` operation ensures $o$ must first become a core point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Expectation Maximization (EM) for Soft Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Expectation Maximization (EM) for Soft Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Expectation Maximization (EM)** is an iterative numerical optimization algorithm used to find **Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE)** or **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** estimates for the parameters of statistical models that involve **latent (unobserved) variables**.

In the specific context of **soft clustering**, the EM algorithm is used to fit a **Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)** to a given dataset. Unlike *hard clustering* (e.g., K-Means) where each data point is assigned exclusively to one cluster, **soft clustering** assigns each data point a *probability distribution* over all the clusters. This means a single data point has a fractional membership in every cluster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEMUT755):** The EM algorithm is introduced as the canonical solution for parameter estimation when the model contains latent variables. In clustering, the cluster assignment itself is treated as the latent variable $Z$, and EM is the bridge that lets us iteratively estimate both the cluster assignments and the underlying distribution parameters simultaneously.

### Formal Statement

Given:
- A dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ where each $x_n \in \mathbb{R}^d$.
- A model with parameters $\theta = \{\pi_k, \mu_k, \Sigma_k\}_{k=1}^K$.
- Latent variables $Z = \{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_N\}$, where $z_n$ indicates which Gaussian component generated $x_n$.

The EM algorithm finds:
$$\theta^* = \arg\max_{\theta} \, \ln p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \arg\max_{\theta} \, \sum_{n=1}^{N} \ln \left[ \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]$$

by alternating between two steps until convergence:
- **E-step (Expectation):** Compute the expected value of the latent variables given the current parameters.
- **M-step (Maximization):** Update the parameters to maximize the expected log-likelihood found in the E-step.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Hidden Group Identity" Analogy**
> Imagine you are a school teacher with **100 students** but **no class lists**. You know their exam scores, but you don't know which student belongs to which of the **3 streams** (Science, Commerce, Arts). The streams might have different score distributions — Science students cluster around 85, Commerce around 70, and Arts around 55.
>
> **The Challenge:** You don't know the stream averages (means), and you don't know the spread (variance), and you don't know the student-to-stream mapping. *All three unknowns are entangled.*

**How EM solves this:**
1. **E-step (Guess the group of each student):** Based on your *current best guess* of the stream averages, calculate how likely each student is to belong to each stream. Student A might be 70% Science, 20% Commerce, 10% Arts.
2. **M-step (Update the stream averages):** Now that every student has fractional membership in every stream, recompute the average score for each stream as a *weighted* average. A student who is 70% Science contributes 70% of their score to the Science stream's new average.
3. **Repeat** until the averages stop changing meaningfully.

This is **soft clustering** because every student has a probability of belonging to every stream, not just one.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Reference

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Observed Data | $X = \{x_n\}$ | The dataset we can see. |
| Latent Variable | $Z = \{z_n\}$ | Hidden cluster assignment, $z_n \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$. |
| Mixing Coefficient | $\pi_k$ | Prior probability of cluster $k$; $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$. |
| Cluster Mean | $\mu_k$ | Mean vector of the $k$-th Gaussian. |
| Cluster Covariance | $\Sigma_k$ | Covariance matrix of the $k$-th Gaussian. |
| Responsibility | $\gamma(z_{nk})$ | Posterior probability that $x_n$ belongs to cluster $k$. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Is the EM Algorithm Necessary?

The log-likelihood of a Gaussian Mixture Model is given by:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \ln \left\{ \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right\}$$

The summation **inside** the logarithm (due to marginalizing out the latent variable $z_n$) makes direct differentiation **non-trivial**. Setting $\frac{\partial \ln p(X \mid \theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$ yields a system of equations with no closed-form solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Insight:** EM transforms a difficult, unobserved-variable problem into a sequence of *easy, complete-data* problems by using the current parameter estimate to "fill in" the missing data, then re-optimizing.

## 2.2 The Two-Step Operational Logic

### Step A — The E-Step (Expectation Step)

**Goal:** Compute the *responsibility* $\gamma(z_{nk})$ — the posterior probability that data point $x_n$ was generated by component $k$.

Using Bayes' theorem:

$$\gamma(z_{nk}) = p(z_n = k \mid x_n, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \frac{\pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$$

The E-step also defines the **expected complete-data log-likelihood** (the **Q-function**):

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \mathbb{E}_{Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}}} \left[ \ln p(X, Z \mid \theta) \right] = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \left[ \ln \pi_k + \ln \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]$$

### Step B — The M-Step (Maximization Step)

**Goal:** Maximize the Q-function with respect to each parameter $\theta = \{\pi_k, \mu_k, \Sigma_k\}$.

**Update for the mixing coefficients:**
$$\pi_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_k}{N} \quad \text{where} \quad N_k = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})$$

**Update for the means:**
$$\mu_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, x_n$$

**Update for the covariances:**
$$\Sigma_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, (x_n - \mu_k^{\text{new}})(x_n - \mu_k^{\text{new}})^T$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — It contains the highest-weight formulas for KTU 2024 exams.**

| # | Formula | Description / Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p(x \mid \theta) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$ | GMM marginal distribution; $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$, each $\pi_k \geq 0$. |
| 2 | $\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu, \Sigma) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}\vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu)\right)$ | Multivariate Gaussian density in $d$ dimensions. |
| 3 | $\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$ | Responsibility (E-step); $\sum_k \gamma(z_{nk}) = 1$. |
| 4 | $N_k = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})$ | Effective number of data points in cluster $k$. |
| 5 | $\mu_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, x_n$ | M-step mean update. |
| 6 | $\Sigma_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, (x_n - \mu_k^{\text{new}})(x_n - \mu_k^{\text{new}})^T$ | M-step covariance update. |
| 7 | $\pi_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_k}{N}$ | M-step mixing coefficient update. |
| 8 | $\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \ln \left[ \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]$ | Observed-data log-likelihood (the quantity EM maximizes). |
| 9 | $\ln p(X,Z \mid \theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{I}(z_n = k) \left[ \ln \pi_k + \ln \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]$ | Complete-data log-likelihood. |
| 10 | $Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \left[ \ln \pi_k + \ln \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]$ | Expected complete-data log-likelihood (Q-function). |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Computer Vision** | Image segmentation, background subtraction, skin-color modeling. |
| **Speech Processing** | Speaker identification via GMM-HMM acoustic models. |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Density estimation for fraud detection in finance. |
| **Bioinformatics** | Gene expression clustering, single-cell RNA-seq analysis. |
| **Recommender Systems** | Latent-factor modeling of user-item interactions. |

## 2.5 Soft Clustering vs Hard Clustering — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | Hard Clustering (e.g., K-Means) | Soft Clustering (e.g., GMM + EM) |
|---|---|---|
| Assignment Type | Binary: $z_n \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$ | Probabilistic: $p(z_n = k \mid x_n) \in [0, 1]$ |
| Cluster Shape | Spherical (equal variance) | Arbitrary elliptical (full covariance) |
| Membership | Each point belongs to exactly 1 cluster | Each point belongs to *all* clusters with weights |
| Uncertainty | No measure of confidence | Confidence encoded in $\gamma(z_{nk})$ |
| Optimization | Lloyd's algorithm (closed-form per step) | EM algorithm (closed-form per step) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse K-Means with GMM. K-Means is a *special case* of GMM when all covariances are isotropic ($\Sigma_k = \sigma^2 I$) and $\sigma \to 0$. The E-step responsibilities then become 0/1, recovering hard assignments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The E-Step Responsibility Formula

We want to compute $p(z_n = k \mid x_n, \theta^{\text{old}})$.

By the definition of the generative model for GMM, the prior probability of component $k$ is $\pi_k$, and the likelihood of $x_n$ given $z_n = k$ is the Gaussian density $\mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$.

Using Bayes' theorem:

$$p(z_n = k \mid x_n, \theta) = \frac{p(z_n = k \mid \theta) \, p(x_n \mid z_n = k, \theta)}{p(x_n \mid \theta)}$$

Substituting the GMM components:

$$p(z_n = k \mid x_n, \theta) = \frac{\pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} p(z_n = j \mid \theta) \, p(x_n \mid z_n = j, \theta)}$$

$$= \frac{\pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$$

This yields the responsibility:

$$\boxed{\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Q-Function from Jensen's Inequality

The EM algorithm is fundamentally justified by **Jensen's Inequality**, which states that for a concave function $\ln(\cdot)$:

$$\ln \mathbb{E}[Y] \geq \mathbb{E}[\ln Y]$$

Consider the observed-data log-likelihood:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \ln p(x_n \mid \theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \ln \sum_{k=1}^{K} p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta)$$

We introduce an *arbitrary* distribution $q(z_n)$ over the latent variable $z_n$ for each $n$:

$$\ln p(x_n \mid \theta) = \ln \sum_{k=1}^{K} q(z_n = k) \, \frac{p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta)}{q(z_n = k)}$$

Since $\ln(\cdot)$ is concave, Jensen's inequality gives a **lower bound**:

$$\ln p(x_n \mid \theta) \geq \sum_{k=1}^{K} q(z_n = k) \, \ln \frac{p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta)}{q(z_n = k)}$$

Summing over all $n$:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) \geq \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} q(z_n = k) \, \ln \frac{p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta)}{q(z_n = k)} \equiv \mathcal{L}(q, \theta)$$

To make this bound **tight** (i.e., turn inequality into equality), we choose $q(z_n)$ equal to the true posterior:

$$q(z_n = k) = p(z_n = k \mid x_n, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \gamma(z_{nk})$$

Substituting:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, \ln \frac{p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta)}{\gamma(z_{nk})}$$

Decomposing the log:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \left[ \ln p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta) - \ln \gamma(z_{nk}) \right]$$

Since $\ln \gamma(z_{nk})$ does not depend on $\theta$, maximizing $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to $\theta$ is equivalent to maximizing:

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, \ln p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta)$$

Expanding the complete-data joint:

$$\ln p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta) = \ln \pi_k + \ln \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \left[ \ln \pi_k + \ln \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — M-Step Mean Update

We maximize $Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}})$ with respect to $\mu_k$.

Starting from the Q-function (keeping only terms that depend on $\mu_k$):

$$Q_{\mu_k} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, \ln \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) + \text{const}$$

Expanding the Gaussian log-density:

$$\ln \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) = -\frac{d}{2} \ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2} \ln \vert \Sigma_k \vert - \frac{1}{2} (x_n - \mu_k)^T \Sigma_k^{-1} (x_n - \mu_k)$$

Differentiate $Q_{\mu_k}$ with respect to $\mu_k$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial Q_{\mu_k}}{\partial \mu_k} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, \Sigma_k^{-1} (x_n - \mu_k) = 0$$

Multiplying both sides by $\Sigma_k$:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, (x_n - \mu_k) = 0$$

$$\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, x_n - \mu_k \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) = 0$$

Defining $N_k = \sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})$:

$$\boxed{\mu_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, x_n}$$

This is the **weighted average** of all data points, where the weights are the responsibilities — confirming the "soft membership" interpretation.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — M-Step Covariance Update

Similarly, differentiate $Q$ with respect to $\Sigma_k$:

$$\frac{\partial Q_{\Sigma_k}}{\partial \Sigma_k} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \left[ -\frac{1}{2} \Sigma_k^{-1} + \frac{1}{2} \Sigma_k^{-1} (x_n - \mu_k)(x_n - \mu_k)^T \Sigma_k^{-1} \right] = 0$$

Pre-multiplying and post-multiplying by $\Sigma_k$:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, \Sigma_k - \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, (x_n - \mu_k)(x_n - \mu_k)^T = 0$$

Solving for $\Sigma_k$:

$$\boxed{\Sigma_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, (x_n - \mu_k^{\text{new}})(x_n - \mu_k^{\text{new}})^T}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — M-Step Mixing Coefficient Update (with Lagrange Constraint)

We must respect the constraint $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$. Construct the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\pi} = Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) + \lambda \left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k - 1 \right)$$

The relevant terms:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\pi} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left[ \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \right] \ln \pi_k + \lambda \left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k - 1 \right) + \text{const}$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $\pi_k$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\pi}}{\partial \pi_k} = \frac{N_k}{\pi_k} + \lambda = 0 \implies \pi_k = -\frac{N_k}{\lambda}$$

Apply the constraint:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k = -\frac{1}{\lambda} \sum_{k=1}^{K} N_k = -\frac{N}{\lambda} = 1 \implies \lambda = -N$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\pi_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_k}{N}}$$

## 3.6 Convergence Property

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monotonic Increase Theorem:** EM guarantees that the observed-data log-likelihood is *non-decreasing* at every iteration:
> $$\ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{new}}) \geq \ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{old}})$$
> Equality holds only at a stationary point (typically a local maximum).

EM is **guaranteed to converge**, but only to a **local maximum**, not necessarily the global maximum. This is why initialization matters.

## 3.7 Convergence Criteria (Stopping Rule)

Practical stopping conditions used in KTU-approved implementations:

1. **Likelihood Tolerance:** $\vert \ln p(X \mid \theta^{t+1}) - \ln p(X \mid \theta^t) \vert < \epsilon$, typically $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$.
2. **Parameter Tolerance:** $\max_k \Vert \mu_k^{t+1} - \mu_k^t \Vert < \epsilon$.
3. **Maximum Iterations:** Hard cap, typically **100–500 iterations**.

## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example (Illustrative)

Consider $N = 3$ data points $x_1 = 0, x_2 = 1, x_3 = 2$ in $\mathbb{R}^1$ and $K = 2$ components.

**Initialization:** $\pi_1 = \pi_2 = 0.5$, $\mu_1 = -1, \mu_2 = 3$, $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = 1$.

**Iteration 1 — E-Step:**

For $x_1 = 0$:

$$\gamma(z_{11}) = \frac{0.5 \cdot \mathcal{N}(0 \mid -1, 1)}{0.5 \cdot \mathcal{N}(0 \mid -1, 1) + 0.5 \cdot \mathcal{N}(0 \mid 3, 1)}$$

Computing Gaussian values:

$$\mathcal{N}(0 \mid -1, 1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-0.5 \cdot (0-(-1))^2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-0.5} \approx 0.2420$$

$$\mathcal{N}(0 \mid 3, 1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-0.5 \cdot (0-3)^2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-4.5} \approx 0.0017$$

Thus:

$$\gamma(z_{11}) = \frac{0.5 \cdot 0.2420}{0.5 \cdot 0.2420 + 0.5 \cdot 0.0017} = \frac{0.1210}{0.1219} \approx 0.993$$

And $\gamma(z_{21}) \approx 0.007$.

Repeating for $x_2 = 1$ and $x_3 = 2$:

For $x_2 = 1$:

$$\mathcal{N}(1 \mid -1, 1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-2} \approx 0.0540$$

$$\mathcal{N}(1 \mid 3, 1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-2} \approx 0.0540$$

So $\gamma(z_{22}) \approx 0.500$.

For $x_3 = 2$:

$$\mathcal{N}(2 \mid -1, 1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-4.5} \approx 0.0017$$

$$\mathcal{N}(2 \mid 3, 1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-0.5} \approx 0.2420$$

Thus $\gamma(z_{32}) \approx 0.993$.

**Iteration 1 — M-Step:**

Compute $N_1 = 0.993 + 0.007 + 0.500 = 1.500$ and $N_2 = 0.007 + 0.493 + 0.993 = 1.493$ (≈ $\approx 1.500$ by symmetry).

Update means:

$$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{1.500} \left( 0.993 \cdot 0 + 0.007 \cdot 1 + 0.500 \cdot 2 \right) = \frac{1.007}{1.500} \approx 0.671$$

$$\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{1.500} \left( 0.007 \cdot 0 + 0.493 \cdot 1 + 0.993 \cdot 2 \right) = \frac{2.479}{1.500} \approx 1.653$$

Update mixing coefficients:

$$\pi_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{1.500}{3} = 0.500, \quad \pi_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{1.500}{3} = 0.500$$

The means have moved from $\{-1, 3\}$ to $\{0.671, 1.653\}$ — clearly *attracted* toward the data centroid at $x = 1$. Iterations continue until convergence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation from the Example:** Notice how the responsibilities are *not* 0 or 1 in general — for $x_2 = 1$ (right between the initial means), it has nearly equal probability of belonging to either cluster. This is the defining feature of **soft clustering**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EM Algorithm — High-Level Block Flow

The diagram below maps the iterative interaction between the E-step, M-step, and the convergence monitor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input dataset X = x1 to xN] --> B[Initialize parameters: pi_k, mu_k, Sigma_k for k = 1 to K]
    B --> C[Set iteration counter t = 0]
    C --> D[E-Step: Compute responsibilities gamma_znk using current parameters]
    D --> E[M-Step: Update pi_k_new, mu_k_new, Sigma_k_new using gamma_znk]
    E --> F[Increment t = t + 1]
    F --> G{Convergence Check: likelihood change less than epsilon}
    G -- No --> D
    G -- Yes --> H[Output: Final parameters theta_star and responsibilities gamma_znk]
    H --> I[End: Cluster soft assignments ready]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style D fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    style E fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
```

## 4.2 GMM Generative Process — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Latent Variable z_n] -- Sample k from Categorical pi_1 to pi_K --> B[Choose component k]
    B -- Sample x_n from Gaussian N mu_k Sigma_k --> C[Observed Data Point x_n]
    D[Mixing Coefficients pi_k] --> A
    E[Component Means mu_k] --> B
    F[Component Covariances Sigma_k] --> B

    style A fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style B fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    style C fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    style D fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    style E fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    style F fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
```

## 4.3 E-Step and M-Step Functional Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ESP[Expectation Step - E-Step]
        E1[Input: x_n and theta_old] --> E2[Compute log p x_n, z_n=k given theta_old]
        E2 --> E3[Compute responsibility gamma_znk via Bayes rule]
        E3 --> E4[Output: Q-function expectation]
    end

    subgraph MSP[Maximization Step - M-Step]
        M1[Input: gamma_znk and Q-function] --> M2[Set partial derivative of Q wrt mu_k = 0]
        M2 --> M3[Update mu_k_new = weighted average]
        M1 --> M4[Set partial derivative of Q wrt Sigma_k = 0]
        M4 --> M5[Update Sigma_k_new = weighted scatter]
        M1 --> M6[Apply Lagrange multiplier for pi_k]
        M6 --> M7[Update pi_k_new = N_k over N]
    end

    E4 --> M1
    M7 --> CONV{Likelihood Converged}
    CONV -- No --> E1
    CONV -- Yes --> DONE[Output theta_star]

    style E3 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style M3 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style M5 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style M7 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style DONE fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block — K-Means vs GMM-EM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HC[Hard Clustering - K-Means]
        H1[Assign each x_n to nearest centroid] --> H2[Binary membership 0 or 1]
        H2 --> H3[Update centroid = mean of assigned points]
    end

    subgraph SC[Soft Clustering - GMM plus EM]
        S1[Compute responsibility gamma_znk for each x_n] --> S2[Probabilistic membership in 0 to 1]
        S2 --> S3[Update mu_k = weighted average with weights gamma_znk]
        S2 --> S4[Update Sigma_k = weighted covariance]
        S2 --> S5[Update pi_k = fraction of total responsibility]
    end

    style H2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Notice the cyclical arrow in the EM block diagram (E-step → M-step → Convergence Check → back to E-step). This loop is the heart of EM. The K-Means vs GMM comparison shows how soft clustering generalizes hard clustering by replacing 0/1 assignments with continuous responsibilities.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between hard clustering and soft clustering with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hard Clustering | Soft Clustering |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Each data point is assigned to *exactly one* cluster. | Each data point has a *probability distribution* over all clusters. |
| Output | Discrete label $z_n \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$ | Probability vector $(\gamma_1, \gamma_2, \ldots, \gamma_K)$ with $\sum \gamma_k = 1$. |
| Algorithm | K-Means, K-Medoids | GMM with EM, Fuzzy C-Means |
| Example | Customer segmentation: a customer is "VIP" or "Regular". | Customer segmentation: a customer is 60% VIP, 40% Regular. |

**Example:** Consider three customers with spending $x = 100, 500, 900$ and two clusters (Low Spender, High Spender). K-Means assigns 100 → Low, 500 → either, 900 → High (binary). GMM+EM gives $p(\text{High} \mid 500) = 0.5$ for the borderline customer.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *What is the role of latent variables in the EM algorithm?*

**Model Answer:**

In the EM algorithm, **latent variables** are unobserved quantities whose values are necessary for the model but are not directly available in the dataset. They play three critical roles:

1. **Model Completeness:** They complete the probabilistic model by specifying which component generated each data point (e.g., $z_n \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$ in GMM).

2. **Enabling Tractable Optimization:** Without latent variables, the log-likelihood contains $\ln \sum_k$ (intractable). With them, the complete-data log-likelihood $\ln p(X, Z \mid \theta)$ decomposes into a sum that is easy to maximize.

3. **Information Source for the E-Step:** The E-step computes the posterior distribution $p(Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}})$ over these latent variables using the current parameters, effectively "filling in" the missing information needed for the M-step.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **E-step responsibility formula** $\gamma(z_{nk})$ for the Gaussian Mixture Model using Bayes' theorem. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the expected complete-data log-likelihood $Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}})$, derive the **M-step update rule for the mixing coefficient** $\pi_k$, explaining the role of the Lagrange multiplier. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of the E-Step Responsibility Formula:**

**Step 1:** In the GMM, each data point $x_n$ is assumed to be generated from a latent cluster assignment $z_n \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$. The prior over the latent variable is:

$$p(z_n = k \mid \theta) = \pi_k, \quad \text{where} \quad \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k = 1$$

**Step 2:** Conditioned on the cluster assignment, the data follows a multivariate Gaussian:

$$p(x_n \mid z_n = k, \theta) = \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$

**Step 3:** The joint distribution of $x_n$ and $z_n$ is:

$$p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta) = p(z_n = k \mid \theta) \, p(x_n \mid z_n = k, \theta) = \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$

**Step 4:** The marginal likelihood of $x_n$ is obtained by summing over all cluster assignments:

$$p(x_n \mid \theta) = \sum_{j=1}^{K} p(x_n, z_n = j \mid \theta) = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)$$

**Step 5:** Applying Bayes' theorem:

$$\gamma(z_{nk}) \equiv p(z_n = k \mid x_n, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \frac{p(z_n = k \mid \theta^{\text{old}}) \, p(x_n \mid z_n = k, \theta^{\text{old}})}{p(x_n \mid \theta^{\text{old}})}$$

**Step 6:** Substituting the GMM components:

$$\boxed{\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}}$$

**Assumption stated:** The data points are i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) given the latent assignments.

---

**Part (b) — Derivation of the M-Step Update for $\pi_k$:**

**Step 1:** The M-step maximizes the Q-function with respect to the parameters. The terms involving $\pi_k$ in the Q-function are:

$$Q_{\pi_k} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, \ln \pi_k + \text{terms independent of } \pi_k$$

**Step 2:** Since $\pi_k$ does not depend on $n$ inside the logarithm, this simplifies to:

$$Q_{\pi_k} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left[ \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \right] \ln \pi_k = \sum_{k=1}^{K} N_k \, \ln \pi_k$$

where $N_k = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})$ is the effective number of points in cluster $k$.

**Step 3:** The optimization must respect the constraint $\sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k = 1$. Construct the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}(\pi, \lambda) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} N_k \, \ln \pi_k + \lambda \left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k - 1 \right)$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate with respect to $\pi_k$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \pi_k} = \frac{N_k}{\pi_k} + \lambda = 0 \implies \pi_k = -\frac{N_k}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5:** Sum over all $k$ and apply the constraint:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k = -\frac{1}{\lambda} \sum_{k=1}^{K} N_k = -\frac{N}{\lambda} = 1 \implies \lambda = -N$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back:

$$\boxed{\pi_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_k}{N} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})}$$

**Role of Lagrange Multiplier:** The multiplier $\lambda$ enforces the simplex constraint that the mixing coefficients must sum to unity, ensuring the result remains a valid probability distribution over the clusters.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)** with its generative interpretation. Write down the marginal likelihood expression and discuss why direct maximization of this likelihood is intractable. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Show how the **EM algorithm overcomes the intractability** by introducing the Q-function. Use Jensen's inequality in your explanation and write the final Q-function expression for GMM. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Gaussian Mixture Model Explanation:**

A Gaussian Mixture Model represents a complex distribution as a *convex combination* of $K$ simpler Gaussian distributions. Each Gaussian component $k$ is characterized by:

- A **mean vector** $\mu_k \in \mathbb{R}^d$,
- A **covariance matrix** $\Sigma_k \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ (positive definite),
- A **mixing coefficient** $\pi_k \in [0, 1]$ with $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$.

**Generative Interpretation:** To generate a data point $x_n$:
1. Sample a cluster index $z_n \sim \text{Categorical}(\pi_1, \ldots, \pi_K)$.
2. Sample $x_n \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{z_n}, \Sigma_{z_n})$.

The marginal likelihood of the observed data is obtained by marginalizing out $z_n$:

$$p(X \mid \theta) = \prod_{n=1}^{N} p(x_n \mid \theta) = \prod_{n=1}^{N} \left[ \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]$$

The log-likelihood is:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \ln \left[ \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]$$

**Why Direct Maximization Is Intractable:**

Setting $\frac{\partial \ln p(X \mid \theta)}{\partial \mu_k} = 0$ yields:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{N} \frac{\pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)} \, \Sigma_k^{-1} (x_n - \mu_k) = 0$$

Notice that $\mu_k$ appears *both inside* the Gaussian density (inside the exponent) *and* inside the responsibility term in the summation. The two occurrences **couple** together, making the equation have no closed-form solution.

---

**Part (b) — How EM Overcomes the Intractability:**

The EM algorithm avoids direct maximization by introducing **auxiliary distributions** $q(z_n)$ over the latent variables and using **Jensen's inequality**.

**Step 1:** Start with the log-likelihood of a single data point:

$$\ln p(x_n \mid \theta) = \ln \sum_{k=1}^{K} p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta)$$

**Step 2:** Introduce $q(z_n = k)$ — any valid probability distribution over clusters:

$$\ln p(x_n \mid \theta) = \ln \sum_{k=1}^{K} q(z_n = k) \, \frac{p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta)}{q(z_n = k)}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize this as $\ln \mathbb{E}_q[Y]$ where $Y = \frac{p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta)}{q(z_n = k)}$. Since $\ln(\cdot)$ is **concave**, Jensen's inequality gives:

$$\ln \mathbb{E}_q[Y] \geq \mathbb{E}_q[\ln Y]$$

Therefore:

$$\ln p(x_n \mid \theta) \geq \sum_{k=1}^{K} q(z_n = k) \, \ln \frac{p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta)}{q(z_n = k)}$$

**Step 4:** Sum over all $n$ to get a lower bound $\mathcal{L}(q, \theta)$ on the log-likelihood:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) \geq \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} q(z_n = k) \, \ln \frac{p(x_n, z_n = k \mid \theta)}{q(z_n = k)} \equiv \mathcal{L}(q, \theta)$$

**Step 5:** Make the bound **tight** by setting $q(z_n = k) = p(z_n = k \mid x_n, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \gamma(z_{nk})$. This is the **E-step**.

**Step 6:** Maximize this tight lower bound with respect to $\theta$ to get $\theta^{\text{new}}$. This is the **M-step**. The resulting Q-function is:

$$\boxed{Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \left[ \ln \pi_k + \ln \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]}$$

**Why This Works:** With $\gamma(z_{nk})$ fixed (from the E-step), the Q-function is now a *linear combination* of $\ln \pi_k$ and $\ln \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$, each of which is easy to maximize separately. The coupling that made direct maximization intractable is broken because the latent variable contribution is now treated as a known weight.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on EM/GMM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the constraint $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$** when deriving $\pi_k^{\text{new}}$. Without the Lagrange multiplier, students lose 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Confusing complete-data vs observed-data log-likelihood.** The Q-function uses the *expected* complete-data log-likelihood, NOT the observed-data log-likelihood.
> 3. **Omitting the sum in the denominator of $\gamma(z_{nk})$.** A very common slip — students write the numerator only, ignoring the marginal likelihood normalization.
> 4. **Using K-Means formulas in a GMM question.** The covariance update $\Sigma_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_n \gamma(z_{nk}) (x_n - \mu_k^{\text{new}})(x_n - \mu_k^{\text{new}})^T$ is unique to GMM, NOT K-Means.
> 5. **Not stating "i.i.d. assumption"** in the derivation. Examiners often deduct 1 mark for this.
> 6. **Missing the link to Jensen's inequality.** The Q-function is *not* pulled out of thin air — it is rigorously derived from a lower bound made tight.
> 7. **Skipping the convergence property.** Always mention that EM monotonically increases the log-likelihood and converges to a (local) maximum.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for EM for Soft Clustering:**

- **Soft Clustering** = each point has a *probability distribution* over clusters (not a single label).
- **GMM** = convex combination of $K$ Gaussians; parameterized by $\pi_k$, $\mu_k$, $\Sigma_k$.
- **EM Algorithm** = iterative two-step procedure for MLE with latent variables.
- **E-step** computes **responsibilities** $\gamma(z_{nk})$ via Bayes' theorem using *current* parameters.
- **M-step** updates $\{\pi_k, \mu_k, \Sigma_k\}$ by maximizing the Q-function.
- **Mean update:** $\mu_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_n \gamma(z_{nk}) x_n$ — a *weighted average*.
- **Covariance update:** $\Sigma_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_n \gamma(z_{nk}) (x_n - \mu_k^{\text{new}})(x_n - \mu_k^{\text{new}})^T$ — a *weighted scatter*.
- **Mixing coefficient:** $\pi_k^{\text{new}} = N_k / N$ — derived with **Lagrange multiplier** to enforce $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$.
- **Q-function** = expected complete-data log-likelihood, obtained from Jensen's inequality applied to observed-data log-likelihood.
- **Convergence:** EM *guarantees* monotonic increase in $\ln p(X \mid \theta)$; converges to a *local* (not global) maximum.
- **Stopping criteria:** $\Delta \ln p < 10^{-6}$ OR max iterations reached.
- **Initialization matters:** K-Means pre-clustering is a common, KTU-approved initialization heuristic.
- **K-Means is a special case** of GMM where $\Sigma_k = \sigma^2 I$ and $\sigma \to 0$, making $\gamma(z_{nk}) \in \{0, 1\}$.
- **Jensen's inequality** $\ln \mathbb{E}[Y] \geq \mathbb{E}[\ln Y]$ is the mathematical foundation that justifies the EM lower-bound optimization.
- **Effective cluster size** $N_k = \sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})$ is a key diagnostic quantity — it tells you how many "fractional" points belong to cluster $k$.
- **Applications:** Image segmentation, speech recognition (GMM-HMM), anomaly detection, bioinformatics clustering.
- **Advantages:** Soft assignments carry uncertainty information; supports elliptical clusters via full $\Sigma_k$.
- **Disadvantages:** Sensitive to initialization; can converge to local maxima; computationally heavier than K-Means.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionality Reduction - Subset Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dimensionality Reduction - Subset Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Dimensionality Reduction** is the process of reducing the number of input features (predictors) in a dataset while preserving as much relevant information and predictive power as possible. It is broadly classified into two paradigms:

1. **Feature Selection (Subset Selection)** — Selecting a *subset* of the original features without transforming them.
2. **Feature Extraction (Projection)** — Creating *new* features by projecting the data onto a lower-dimensional space (e.g., PCA, LDA).

**Subset Selection (Feature Selection)** is the specific family of dimensionality reduction techniques that identifies and retains a meaningful subset of the original variables, discarding irrelevant, redundant, or noisy ones. The original semantics and units of the selected variables are preserved, which is a major advantage over projection methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** Subset selection refers to the process of identifying and selecting a relevant subset $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_p\}$ of the original $p$ features such that the predictive model built on $\mathcal{S}$ performs at par with (or better than) the model built on the full set of $p$ features, while reducing model complexity, training time, and overfitting risk.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a doctor diagnosing a patient with 50 possible symptoms, but only **8 symptoms** are actually medically relevant for the disease. Reading all 50 wastes time, confuses the diagnosis, and might mislead you. **Subset selection is like an experienced doctor's diagnostic instinct** — it filters out the noise and focuses only on the symptoms that matter.

In machine learning:
- **Symptoms** = Features ($X_1, X_2, \dots, X_p$)
- **Relevant symptoms** = Selected subset $\mathcal{S}$
- **Irrelevant symptoms** = Discarded features
- **Diagnosis** = Prediction target $Y$

By eliminating the irrelevant features, the model becomes simpler, faster, more interpretable, and — crucially — *less prone to overfitting* the training data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Subset Selection Matters in KTU Context:**
> - Reduces **curse of dimensionality**
> - Mitigates **overfitting** (high variance, low bias → low variance, controlled bias)
> - Improves **model interpretability**
> - Decreases **computational cost** (training and inference)
> - Often improves **generalization** accuracy on unseen data

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Subset Selection

| Pillar | Description | Standard KTU Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Relevance** | Does feature $X_j$ have a strong statistical relationship with target $Y$? | Correlation $r$, Mutual Information $I(X_j; Y)$ |
| **Redundancy** | Are selected features duplicates of one another? | Inter-feature correlation $\vert r_{X_iX_j} \vert$ |
| **Relevance ≠ Causation** | A feature may correlate with $Y$ but not cause $Y$ | Domain knowledge, experimentation |

### 1.4 Mathematical Setup (Standardized Notation)

Let the dataset be represented as a matrix:

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{11} & x_{12} & \cdots & x_{1p} \\ x_{21} & x_{22} & \cdots & x_{2p} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ x_{n1} & x_{n2} & \cdots & x_{np} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_n \end{bmatrix}
$$

where:
- $n$ = number of observations (samples)
- $p$ = number of original features
- $k$ = number of selected features, with $k < p$
- $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$, $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$

The **goal** is to find the optimal subset index set $\mathcal{S}^* \subseteq \{1, 2, \dots, p\}$ with $\vert \mathcal{S}^* \vert = k$ that minimizes a loss function:

$$
\mathcal{S}^* = \arg\min_{\mathcal{S} \subseteq \{1,\dots,p\},\; \vert \mathcal{S} \vert = k} L(\mathbf{X}_{\mathcal{S}}, \mathbf{y})
$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Subset Selection Methods

Subset selection is broadly classified into **three methodological families**, each evaluated against the criteria of *search strategy*, *evaluation criterion*, and *computational cost*.

#### Family A — Filter Methods (Pre-processing)
- **Independent of any model.** Features are scored using a statistical measure applied directly to the data.
- Computationally cheap, model-agnostic, fast for high-dimensional data.
- Tends to select redundant features (does not account for feature interactions).

#### Family B — Wrapper Methods (Search + Model)
- Use a **predictive model as a black-box evaluator**. Search for subsets that maximize predictive performance.
- Account for feature interactions but are computationally expensive.
- Risk of overfitting to the validation set if not cross-validated carefully.

#### Family C — Embedded Methods (Built-in Regularization)
- Feature selection is performed **during model training** as part of the optimization objective.
- Efficient and model-specific (e.g., Lasso for linear regression, tree-based importance).

### 2.2 Theoretical Breakdown of Major Subset Selection Algorithms

#### 2.2.1 Best Subset Selection (Exhaustive Search)

**Logic Steps:**
1. For every $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, p$, enumerate *all* $\binom{p}{k}$ possible subsets of size $k$.
2. For each subset $\mathcal{S}_k$, fit the model $M_k$ and compute a chosen metric (RSS, $R^2$, AIC, BIC, adjusted $R^2$, $C_p$, or cross-validated error).
3. Identify the best subset $\mathcal{S}_k^*$ for each $k$.
4. Apply a parsimony rule (e.g., minimum BIC, minimum cross-validated error) to choose the final $\mathcal{S}_{\text{final}}$ across all $k$.

**Pros:** Mathematically optimal (within the chosen metric).
**Cons:** Computationally infeasible for $p > 40$ because $\sum_{k=0}^{p} \binom{p}{k} = 2^p$ grows exponentially.

#### 2.2.2 Forward Stepwise Selection (Greedy)

**Logic Steps:**
1. Start with the null model $\mathcal{M}_0$ (no predictors).
2. For $k = 1$ to $p-1$:
   - Fit $p - k$ models, each adding one of the remaining $p - k$ features to the current set.
   - Select the feature whose addition produces the **largest improvement** in fit (largest drop in RSS or largest increase in $R^2$).
3. Stop when a stopping criterion is met (e.g., no improvement, predefined $k$, or $C_p$/BIC increases).

**Pros:** Tractable, fast. Considers $1 + \sum_{k=0}^{p-1}(p-k) = 1 + \frac{p(p+1)}{2} = O(p^2)$ models.
**Cons:** Greedy → may miss the globally optimal subset. Once added, a feature is never removed.

#### 2.2.3 Backward Stepwise Selection (Greedy)

**Logic Steps:**
1. Start with the full model $\mathcal{M}_p$ containing all $p$ predictors.
2. For $k = p$ down to $1$:
   - Fit $k$ models, each removing one feature from the current set.
   - Remove the feature whose deletion causes the **smallest increase in RSS** (or smallest drop in $R^2$).
3. Stop when a stopping criterion is met.

**Pros:** Considers the joint effect of all features; may find better subsets than forward selection.
**Cons:** Requires $n > p$ (cannot start with full model if $p > n$). Computationally heavier than forward.

#### 2.2.4 Hybrid Stepwise Selection
Combines forward and backward: at each step, both add and remove candidates are evaluated. Allows "reconsideration" of features.

#### 2.2.5 Filter — Correlation-Based Selection
Compute the Pearson correlation between each feature and target $Y$:

$$
r_{X_j, Y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j)(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j)^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}
$$

Select the top $k$ features by $\vert r_{X_j, Y} \vert$. **Limitation:** captures only linear relationships.

#### 2.2.6 Filter — Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Test (Categorical Features)
Tests independence between a categorical feature $X_j$ and categorical target $Y$:

$$
\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}
$$

where $O_{ij}$ = observed frequency, $E_{ij}$ = expected frequency under independence. Large $\chi^2$ → reject independence → feature is relevant.

#### 2.2.7 Filter — Mutual Information (MI)
Quantifies the non-linear statistical dependence between $X$ and $Y$:

$$
I(X; Y) = \sum_{x \in X}\sum_{y \in Y} p(x, y) \log \frac{p(x, y)}{p(x)p(y)}
$$

Higher $I(X; Y)$ indicates stronger dependency.

#### 2.2.8 Wrapper — Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE)
1. Train model on all features.
2. Rank features by importance (e.g., coefficient magnitude, tree importance).
3. Remove the *least* important feature.
4. Repeat until desired number of features remains.

#### 2.2.9 Embedded — Lasso ($\ell_1$ Regularization)
Solves:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{\text{Lasso}} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j x_{ij})^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \right\}
$$

The $\ell_1$ penalty forces many $\beta_j \to 0$, performing *automatic feature selection*.

#### 2.2.10 Embedded — Tree-Based Importance
Random Forest / Gradient Boosting compute **Gini importance** or **permutation importance** for each feature. Features with importance below a threshold are removed.

### 2.3 Selection Criteria for Choosing the Best Subset (Statistical Metrics)

These criteria explicitly trade off **model fit** against **model complexity**:

| Criterion | Formula | Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **RSS** (Residual Sum of Squares) | $\text{RSS} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Always decreases as $k$ increases → must be paired with penalty. |
| **$R^2$** | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{RSS}}{\text{TSS}}$ | Always increases → must be paired with penalty. |
| **Adjusted $R^2$** | $\text{Adj-}R^2 = 1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n - k - 1}$ | Penalizes extra variables. |
| **Mallow's $C_p$** | $C_p = \frac{1}{n}(\text{RSS} + 2k\hat{\sigma}^2)$ | Estimates test MSE. Pick $k$ with smallest $C_p$. |
| **AIC** (Akaike) | $\text{AIC} = \frac{1}{n\hat{\sigma}^2}(\text{RSS} + 2k\hat{\sigma}^2)$ | Information-theoretic penalty. |
| **BIC** (Bayesian) | $\text{BIC} = \frac{1}{n}(\text{RSS} + \log(n) \cdot k\hat{\sigma}^2)$ | Stronger penalty than AIC; favors smaller models. |
| **CV-Error** (k-Fold) | $\text{CV}_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\text{MSE}_i$ | Most reliable empirical estimate. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bias-Variance Tradeoff Insight:** As $k$ increases, model **bias** decreases (more flexible), but **variance** increases. The criteria above pinpoint the "sweet spot" where the increase in variance begins to outweigh the decrease in bias.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Pearson Correlation | $r_{X_j,Y} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X_j,Y)}{\sigma_{X_j}\sigma_Y}$ | Range: $[-1, +1]$ |
| Mutual Information | $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \vert Y)$ | Bits / Nats |
| $\chi^2$ Statistic | $\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O-E)^2}{E}$ | Degrees of freedom = $(r-1)(c-1)$ |
| Lasso Objective | $\min \Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} \Vert_2^2 + \lambda \Vert \boldsymbol{\beta} \Vert_1$ | $\lambda \geq 0$ |
| Ridge Objective | $\min \Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} \Vert_2^2 + \lambda \Vert \boldsymbol{\beta} \Vert_2^2$ | $\lambda \geq 0$ |
| Adjusted $R^2$ | $1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-k-1}$ | Dimensionless |
| Mallow's $C_p$ | $\frac{1}{n}(\text{RSS} + 2k\hat{\sigma}^2)$ | Dimensionless |
| BIC | $\frac{1}{n}(\text{RSS} + \log(n) k \hat{\sigma}^2)$ | Dimensionless |
| Number of subsets of size $k$ | $\binom{p}{k} = \frac{p!}{k!(p-k)!}$ | Combinatorial |
| Best subset total | $\sum_{k=0}^{p} \binom{p}{k} = 2^p$ | Exhaustive search complexity |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Subset Selection |
|---|---|
| **Biomedical Engineering** | Selecting relevant genes from thousands of micro-array features for cancer diagnosis. |
| **Text Classification (NLP)** | Removing rare/non-informative words from 100,000+ feature vocabularies. |
| **Financial Engineering** | Picking the most predictive macroeconomic indicators from 500+ candidates. |
| **IoT / Edge Devices** | Reducing sensor dimensions to fit limited compute/memory budget. |
| **Computer Vision** | Selecting the most discriminative HOG/SIFT features before classification. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Best Subset Selection Algorithm (with $C_p$ Criterion)

**Setup:** Linear regression with $n$ observations, $p$ predictors, true error variance $\sigma^2$. The training RSS is $\text{RSS}_{\mathcal{S}}$ for subset $\mathcal{S}$.

**Step 1 — Define the loss for a model fit on subset $\mathcal{S}$ of size $k$:**

$$
L(\mathcal{S}) = \text{RSS}_{\mathcal{S}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \hat{y}_i^{(\mathcal{S})}\right)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Expected test MSE decomposition (Bias² + Variance + Irreducible Error):**

$$
\mathbb{E}[(y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2] = \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x_0)) + [\text{Bias}(\hat{f}(x_0))]^2 + \text{Var}(\varepsilon)
$$

**Step 3 — In classical linear-model theory, an unbiased estimate of the test MSE is given by Mallow's $C_p$ statistic. Derive it:**

The expected value of training RSS for a model fit with $k$ parameters (plus intercept) is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\text{RSS}_{\mathcal{S}}] = \mathbb{E}[(n - k - 1)\sigma^2] - \sigma^2 \cdot (k+1) + \text{(Bias contribution)}
$$

For an **unbiased** model (true model is among candidates), this simplifies to:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\text{RSS}_{\mathcal{S}}] = (n - 2k - 2)\sigma^2
$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging to estimate the test error:**

$$
\widehat{\text{MSE}}_{\text{test}} \approx \frac{1}{n}\left(\text{RSS}_{\mathcal{S}} + 2k\hat{\sigma}^2\right) = C_p
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
- $\text{RSS}_{\mathcal{S}}$ measures training fit.
- $2k\hat{\sigma}^2$ is a **penalty** proportional to the number of features.
- The optimal subset $\mathcal{S}^*$ minimizes $C_p$.

**Step 6 — Algorithm Pseudocode (conceptual, no code):**

For $k = 0, 1, \dots, p$:
    For every subset $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \{1,\dots,p\}$ with $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert = k$:
        Fit OLS on $(\mathbf{X}_{\mathcal{S}}, \mathbf{y})$
        Compute $\text{RSS}_{\mathcal{S}}$ and $C_p(\mathcal{S})$
Choose $\mathcal{S}^* = \arg\min_{\mathcal{S}} C_p(\mathcal{S})$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Forward Stepwise Selection Convergence

**Step 1 — Initialize:** $\mathcal{S}_0 = \emptyset$, current model $\mathcal{M}_0$ = null.

**Step 2 — At iteration $t$, the candidate set is** $\mathcal{C}_t = \{1, 2, \dots, p\} \setminus \mathcal{S}_{t-1}$.

**Step 3 — For each candidate $j \in \mathcal{C}_t$, the marginal RSS reduction is:**

$$
\Delta \text{RSS}_{j}^{(t)} = \text{RSS}(\mathcal{S}_{t-1}) - \text{RSS}(\mathcal{S}_{t-1} \cup \{j\})
$$

**Step 4 — Choose the best candidate:**

$$
j^* = \arg\max_{j \in \mathcal{C}_t} \Delta \text{RSS}_{j}^{(t)}
$$

**Step 5 — Update subset:** $\mathcal{S}_t = \mathcal{S}_{t-1} \cup \{j^*\}$.

**Step 6 — Stopping rule:** Stop when $\Delta \text{RSS}_{j^*}^{(t)} < \tau$ for a pre-chosen threshold $\tau > 0$, or when $C_p$ / BIC begins to increase.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Mallow's $C_p$ Application)

**Problem:** A dataset has $n = 50$ observations, $p = 4$ predictors $X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4$, with full-model RSS $= 200$ and $\hat{\sigma}^2 = 10$. Compute $C_p$ for the following candidate subsets and identify the best.

| Subset | Size $k$ | RSS | $C_p$ Calculation | $C_p$ Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Null | 0 | 800 | $\frac{1}{50}(800 + 2(0)(10))$ | $16.00$ |
| $\{X_1\}$ | 1 | 400 | $\frac{1}{50}(400 + 2(1)(10))$ | $8.40$ |
| $\{X_1, X_2\}$ | 2 | 300 | $\frac{1}{50}(300 + 2(2)(10))$ | $6.80$ |
| $\{X_1, X_2, X_3\}$ | 3 | 240 | $\frac{1}{50}(240 + 2(3)(10))$ | $6.00$ |
| $\{X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4\}$ | 4 | 200 | $\frac{1}{50}(200 + 2(4)(10))$ | $5.60$ |

**Decision Rule (using $C_p \approx p$ heuristic):** The model with $C_p$ closest to or below $p + 1 = 5$ (a common rule of thumb where $p$ here is the number of parameters including intercept — we use the simpler "smallest $C_p$" rule):
- **Best subset = $\{X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4\}$** with $C_p = 5.60$, since it yields the lowest $C_p$ and is approximately equal to the number of parameters (4 features + 1 intercept = 5 parameters; $C_p \approx p$ indicates low bias).

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative rule:** If we apply the parsimony principle, $\{X_1, X_2, X_3\}$ with $C_p = 6.00$ is "good enough" with fewer features — illustrating the **parsimony vs. fit** tradeoff that students are expected to articulate in KTU exams.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Methods

| Method | Search Strategy | Computations | Handles Redundancy | Handles Interaction | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Best Subset** | Exhaustive | $2^p$ | ✅ | ✅ | Infeasible for large $p$ |
| **Forward Stepwise** | Greedy Add | $O(p^2)$ | ⚠ Partial | ⚠ Limited | Greedy local optima |
| **Backward Stepwise** | Greedy Remove | $O(p^2)$ | ⚠ Partial | ⚠ Limited | Requires $n > p$ |
| **Filter (Correlation)** | Ranking | $O(np)$ | ❌ | ❌ | Ignores interaction |
| **Filter (Mutual Info)** | Ranking | $O(np)$ | ❌ | ✅ | Ignores redundancy |
| **Wrapper (RFE)** | Iterative | $O(p \cdot T)$ | ✅ | ✅ | Slow for large $p$ |
| **Embedded (Lasso)** | Joint optimization | $O(np)$ | ✅ | ⚠ Limited | Requires linear/log-linear |
| **Tree Importance** | Built-in | $O(pn \log n)$ | ✅ | ✅ | Biased toward high-cardinality features |

### 3.5 Derivation of Lasso's Sparsity Inducing Property (Geometric Intuition)

**Step 1 — Lasso's constrained form:**

$$
\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} \Vert_2^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \Vert \boldsymbol{\beta} \Vert_1 \leq t
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric insight:** The $\ell_1$ ball $\Vert \boldsymbol{\beta} \Vert_1 \leq t$ has *corners* on the axes. When the elliptical RSS contour first touches the constraint region, the contact point frequently lands on a **corner** → one or more $\beta_j = 0$.

**Step 3 — Comparison with Ridge:** The $\ell_2$ ball $\Vert \boldsymbol{\beta} \Vert_2 \leq t$ is a smooth sphere; the contact point is rarely on an axis → Ridge shrinks but does **not** zero out coefficients.

**Step 4 — Consequence:** Lasso = subset selection *embedded* into a convex optimization problem. It is provably consistent for sparse high-dimensional settings under certain conditions (irrepresentable condition).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Architecture of Subset Selection (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Raw Dataset X with p features] --> preA[Step 1: Data Preprocessing]
    preA --> preB[Step 2: Handle Missing Values and Scaling]
    preB --> splitA[Step 3: Train Validation Split]

    splitA --> familyA[Filter Methods]
    splitA --> familyB[Wrapper Methods]
    splitA --> familyC[Embedded Methods]

    familyA --> filter1A[Pearson Correlation]
    familyA --> filter1B[Chi Square Test]
    familyA --> filter1C[Mutual Information]
    familyA --> filter1D[ANOVA F Test]

    familyB --> wrap1A[Forward Selection]
    familyB --> wrap1B[Backward Elimination]
    familyB --> wrap1C[Recursive Feature Elimination RFE]
    familyB --> wrap1D[Exhaustive Best Subset]

    familyC --> emb1A[Lasso L1 Regularization]
    familyC --> emb1B[Tree Based Importance RF XGB]
    familyC --> emb1C[Ridge L2 for Ranking]

    filter1A --> evalA[Step 4: Evaluation Criterion RSS R2 Cp BIC AIC CV]
    filter1B --> evalA
    filter1C --> evalA
    filter1D --> evalA
    wrap1A --> evalA
    wrap1B --> evalA
    wrap1C --> evalA
    wrap1D --> evalA
    emb1A --> evalA
    emb1B --> evalA
    emb1C --> evalA

    evalA --> rankA[Step 5: Rank and Select Subset S of size k]
    rankA --> trainA[Step 6: Train Final Model on S]
    trainA --> valA[Step 7: Validate on Holdout Set]
    valA --> decA{Performance Acceptable}
    decA -- Yes --> endA[Deploy Model with Selected Subset]
    decA -- No --> backA[Return to Step 4 and Adjust Criterion or k]
    backA --> evalA
```

### 4.2 Search Trajectory Diagram — Best Subset vs. Forward vs. Backward

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startN[Null Model M0] --> add1[Add X1]
    add1 --> add2[Add X3]
    add2 --> add3[Add X2]
    add3 --> add4[Final Subset M3 X1 X2 X3]
    fullM[Full Model M4 X1 X2 X3 X4] --> rem1[Remove X4]
    rem1 --> rem2[Remove X2]
    rem2 --> rem3[Final Subset M2 X1 X3]
    exStart[All 2 power 4 = 16 Subsets] --> exBest[Exhaustive Search picks Global Optimum]
    exBest --> finalEx[Final Best Subset]
    add4 --> finalEx
    rem3 --> finalEx
```

### 4.3 Bias-Variance Tradeoff Across Subset Sizes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    kAxis[k = Number of Selected Features] --> biasCurve[Bias Squared DECREASES with k]
    kAxis --> varCurve[Variance INCREASES with k]
    kAxis --> errCurve[Test Error = Bias Squared plus Variance plus Irreducible]
    biasCurve --> minA[Minimum Test Error at Optimal k star]
    varCurve --> minA
    errCurve --> minA
    minA --> markA[Parsimony Sweet Spot - Choose k star via Cp BIC CV]
```

### 4.4 Filter vs. Wrapper vs. Embedded Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qA{How many features p?} --> qB{p greater than 100?}
    qB -- Yes --> recA[Use Filter Method Correlation Chi Square MI]
    qB -- No --> qC{Need to preserve feature interactions?}
    qC -- Yes --> recB[Use Wrapper RFE or Embedded Lasso]
    qC -- No --> qD{Have an interpretable model?}
    qD -- Yes --> recC[Use Embedded Lasso Logistic with L1]
    qD -- No --> recD[Use Wrapper Forward Backward]
    recA --> endN[Select Subset S]
    recB --> endN
    recC --> endN
    recD --> endN
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]**
> Define **dimensionality reduction**. Distinguish between **feature selection** and **feature extraction**, giving one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Dimensionality reduction is the process of reducing the number of input variables in a dataset while retaining as much meaningful information as possible, in order to mitigate the curse of dimensionality, reduce overfitting, and improve computational efficiency.
- **Feature Selection (1 mark):** Selects a *subset* of the original features without altering them. Example: Forward Stepwise Selection, Recursive Feature Elimination.
- **Feature Extraction (1 mark):** *Transforms* the original features into a new lower-dimensional space. Example: Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA).

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 3]**
> What is **best subset selection**? State one major **limitation**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Best subset selection is an exhaustive search method that evaluates every possible combination of $p$ features taken $k$ at a time (for all $k = 0, 1, \dots, p$), fitting a model for each and selecting the subset that minimizes a chosen criterion (RSS, $C_p$, BIC, AIC, or CV-error).
- **Limitation (1 mark):** It is computationally infeasible for large $p$ because the total number of subsets is $2^p$, leading to exponential time complexity (e.g., $p = 40$ implies $\approx 10^{12}$ subsets).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **best subset selection** algorithm. Derive the **Mallow's $C_p$** statistic and explain its role in choosing the optimal subset size.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast **filter, wrapper, and embedded** methods of feature selection. Provide a clear schematic or table highlighting differences in **search strategy, evaluation criterion, computational cost, and ability to handle feature interactions**.

#### Model Solution — Q3(A) Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
1. Let $\mathcal{M}_k$ denote the best model containing exactly $k$ predictors, for $k = 0, 1, \dots, p$.
2. For each $k$, enumerate all $\binom{p}{k}$ subsets and fit OLS; identify the subset that minimizes the training RSS.
3. After computing $\text{RSS}$, $\hat{\sigma}^2$, and $C_p$/BIC for each $k$, choose the model that minimizes $C_p$ (or BIC) — this balances fit and complexity.
4. **Final model** = $\mathcal{M}_{k^*}$ where $k^* = \arg\min_k C_p(k)$.

**Derivation of Mallow's $C_p$ (3 marks):**
For an unbiased model fit on $k$ features, the expected training RSS is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\text{RSS}_k] = (n - 2k - 2)\sigma^2
$$

Adding $2k\sigma^2$ to RSS gives an unbiased estimator of the test error:

$$
C_p = \frac{1}{n}\left(\text{RSS}_k + 2k\hat{\sigma}^2\right)
$$

**Role in optimal subset selection (1 mark):** A small $C_p$ indicates a model with low estimated test error. The "best" subset is the one with $C_p \approx k + 1$ (parsimony threshold) or, more simply, the global minimum across all $k$.

#### Model Solution — Q3(A) Part (b) [7 Marks]

| Dimension | Filter | Wrapper | Embedded |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Search** | Ranking (no search) | Search (forward/backward/RFE) | Joint optimization |
| **Evaluation** | Statistical (corr, $\chi^2$, MI) | Predictive (cross-validation) | Loss + regularization |
| **Computation** | $O(np)$ | $O(p^2)$ to $O(2^p)$ | $O(np)$ |
| **Feature Interaction** | ❌ (mostly univariate) | ✅ | ⚠ Limited |
| **Model Dependency** | Model-agnostic | Model-dependent | Model-specific |
| **Overfitting Risk** | Low | High | Moderate |

**Examples (1 mark):** Filter → Pearson correlation. Wrapper → RFE. Embedded → Lasso.

**Examples of Use (1 mark):** Filter for high-dimensional genomics ($p \gg n$); Wrapper for medium $p$ ($< 50$); Embedded for sparse linear models.

---

> **Q3(B). [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **forward stepwise selection** and **backward stepwise selection** algorithms. State the conditions under which each is applicable, and explain why these greedy methods do not guarantee a globally optimal subset.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] With a **suitable mathematical derivation**, explain how the **Lasso ($\ell_1$ regularization)** performs *embedded feature selection*. Compare it briefly with **Ridge regression ($\ell_2$)** in terms of (i) sparsity of solution, (ii) computational complexity, and (iii) suitability for high-dimensional data.

#### Model Solution — Q3(B) Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Forward Stepwise (3 marks):**
1. Begin with null model $\mathcal{M}_0$.
2. For $k = 1$ to $p-1$: try adding each of the $p - k$ remaining features; pick the one that **most reduces RSS**.
3. Continue until stopping criterion (no significant improvement or chosen $k$ is reached).
- **Applicable when:** $n < p$ (cannot fit full model), high-dimensional data.
- **Models considered:** $1 + \frac{p(p+1)}{2} = O(p^2)$.

**Backward Stepwise (3 marks):**
1. Begin with full model $\mathcal{M}_p$.
2. For $k = p$ down to $1$: try removing each feature; drop the one that **least increases RSS**.
3. Continue until stopping criterion.
- **Applicable when:** $n > p$ (must start with full model), $p$ is moderate.
- **Models considered:** $O(p^2)$.

**Why greedy ≠ optimal (1 mark):** Greedy algorithms commit to locally optimal choices and never reconsider them. A feature that was "useless" early on may become highly relevant after other features are added — but forward selection cannot retroactively add it. Conversely, backward selection may keep a feature whose importance vanishes later. The true optimal subset may be missed.

#### Model Solution — Q3(B) Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Lasso Derivation (3 marks):**

Lasso solves:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{\text{Lasso}} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j x_{ij})^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \right\}
$$

The $\ell_1$ penalty $\lambda \sum_j \vert \beta_j \vert$ has a non-differentiable point at $\beta_j = 0$. Setting subgradient to zero gives the **soft-thresholding rule**:

$$
\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{Lasso}} = \text{sign}(\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}})\left(\vert \hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}} \vert - \frac{\lambda}{2}\right)_+
$$

where $(x)_+ = \max(x, 0)$. If $\vert \hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}} \vert \leq \lambda/2$, then $\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{Lasso}} = 0$ → **sparse solution**.

**Comparison with Ridge (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Lasso ($\ell_1$) | Ridge ($\ell_2$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Sparsity** | Yes (some $\beta_j = 0$) | No (all $\beta_j$ shrink but stay nonzero) |
| **Computational Complexity** | $O(np)$ via LARS / coordinate descent | $O(np^2)$ via closed-form |
| **High-Dimensional Suitability** | Excellent ($p \gg n$ supported) | Limited (no feature selection) |
| **Geometric Constraint** | Diamond (corners → sparsity) | Sphere (smooth → no sparsity) |

**Geometric intuition (1 mark):** The diamond-shaped $\ell_1$ constraint region has sharp corners; the elliptical RSS contour first contacts the boundary at a corner, forcing some coefficients to be exactly zero. Ridge's spherical region has no corners, so the contact point lies on a smooth face → no zero coefficients.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing filter with wrapper:** Filter methods do *not* use a predictive model — students often incorrectly attribute "model evaluation" to Pearson correlation or chi-square.
> 2. **Forgetting to justify the choice of criterion ($C_p$, BIC, AIC):** Examiners award marks for *why* a particular criterion is preferred (e.g., "BIC penalizes complexity more heavily than AIC → favored when $n$ is large and parsimony is desired").
> 3. **Saying "Forward is better than Backward" or vice versa** without context: Both are valid; the choice depends on whether $n > p$ (use Backward) or $n < p$ (use Forward).
> 4. **Ignoring the bias-variance tradeoff:** Subset selection is fundamentally about reducing variance at the cost of slight bias increase. Students who omit this lose 2–3 easy marks.
> 5. **Mis-stating the $C_p$ formula** as $\frac{1}{n}(\text{RSS} - 2k\hat{\sigma}^2)$ (sign error). The correct formula *adds* $2k\hat{\sigma}^2$ as a penalty.
