> 6. **Confusing Lasso and Ridge geometries:** Lasso's $\ell_1$ ball is a *diamond* (rotated square), not a circle. Writing it as a circle loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Subset Selection**

- **Definition:** Selecting a subset $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \{X_1, \dots, X_p\}$ of original features to retain predictive power with reduced complexity.
- **Two Paradigms of Dimensionality Reduction:** Subset selection (preserves original features) vs. feature extraction (creates new features via projection).
- **Three Method Families:** Filter (statistical, model-agnostic), Wrapper (search + model, expensive but accurate), Embedded (built into training, e.g., Lasso).
- **Best Subset:** Exhaustive; complexity $2^p$; infeasible for $p > 40$.
- **Forward Stepwise:** Greedy add; $O(p^2)$ complexity; works when $n < p$; never reconsiders features.
- **Backward Stepwise:** Greedy remove; $O(p^2)$ complexity; requires $n > p$; starts from full model.
- **Filter Metrics:** Pearson $r$ (linear), $\chi^2$ (categorical), Mutual Information $I(X;Y)$ (non-linear), ANOVA F (regression).
- **Wrapper Methods:** RFE, Forward/Backward selection — model-evaluated.
- **Embedded Methods:** Lasso ($\ell_1$) for sparsity, Tree importance for non-linear ranking.
- **Selection Criteria:** RSS always decreases with $k$; use Adjusted $R^2$, $C_p$, AIC, **BIC**, or **CV-error** to penalize complexity.
- **Mallow's $C_p$ Formula:** $C_p = \frac{1}{n}(\text{RSS} + 2k\hat{\sigma}^2)$; choose $k$ minimizing $C_p$.
- **Lasso Soft-Thresholding:** $\hat{\beta}_j = \text{sign}(\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}})(\vert \hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}} \vert - \lambda/2)_+$ → exact zeros → sparsity.
- **Ridge vs. Lasso:** Ridge = smooth shrinkage (no zeros); Lasso = sparse selection (exact zeros).
- **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Subset selection trades increased bias for substantially reduced variance → better test error.
- **Parsimony Principle:** Prefer the smallest model whose performance is statistically indistinguishable from the best (Occam's Razor).
- **Number of subsets of size $k$:** $\binom{p}{k}$; total for best subset: $\sum_{k=0}^{p} \binom{p}{k} = 2^p$.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Adjusted $R^2 = 1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-k-1}$
  - $C_p = \frac{1}{n}(\text{RSS} + 2k\hat{\sigma}^2)$
  - BIC penalty: $\log(n) k \hat{\sigma}^2$
  - $\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$
  - $I(X;Y) = \sum_{x,y} p(x,y) \log \frac{p(x,y)}{p(x)p(y)}$
- **KTU Exam Golden Rule:** Always state (a) which method, (b) why it's chosen, and (c) the bias-variance tradeoff in any subset-selection answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Component Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Principal Component Analysis ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a statistical procedure that uses an **orthogonal linear transformation** to convert a set of observations of possibly correlated variables into a set of values of linearly uncorrelated variables called **principal components**. This transformation is defined in such a way that the **first principal component** has the **largest possible variance** (i.e., accounts for as much of the variability in the data as possible), and each succeeding component, in turn, has the highest variance possible under the constraint that it is **orthogonal** to the preceding components. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, PCA belongs to the family of **Unsupervised Dimensionality Reduction** techniques within classical Machine Learning. It is a **feature extraction** method (as opposed to feature selection) because it constructs new features (the principal components) from linear combinations of the original features. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Shadow of a Complex 3D Object** > Imagine you have a complexly shaped 3D sculpture (your high-dimensional dataset). You shine a flashlight on it from a particular angle, and it casts a **2D shadow** on a flat wall. Different angles give you different shadows. The **best angle** is the one where the shadow preserves the **maximum spread, shape, and uniqueness** of the sculpture. > > PCA does exactly this. It finds the **best "shadow angle"** (a projection) that retains the **maximum variance (information)** of the original data. The principal components are simply those **best viewing angles** (directions in feature space). ### 1.3 Key Vocabulary Anchors | Term | Plain English Meaning | Mathematical Symbol | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Principal Component (PC)** | A new axis (direction) that captures the most variance. | $\mathbf{w}$ | | **Eigenvector** | The direction vector of a principal component. | $\mathbf{v}$ | | **Eigenvalue** | The amount of variance captured along its eigenvector. | $\lambda$ | | **Loading Scores** | Weights that form the projection matrix. | $W$ | | **Explained Variance Ratio** | Fraction of total dataset variance captured by one PC. | $\frac{\lambda_i}{\sum \lambda_j}$ | | **Dimensionality** | Number of original features. | $d$ or $p$ | | **Orthogonality** | Components are perpendicular (uncorrelated). | $\mathbf{w}_i \perp \mathbf{w}_j$ | --- ### 1.4 Why PCA Matters in KTU Engineering Curriculum - **Curse of Dimensionality Mitigation**: Reduces computation time for K-NN, SVM, Neural Networks. - **Data Visualization**: Projects 100-D data into 2-D for scatter plots. - **Noise Filtering**: Low-variance directions often represent noise; discarding them cleans data. - **Multicollinearity Removal**: PCs are orthogonal, eliminating correlation among inputs in regression. - **Engineering Applications**: Face recognition (Eigenfaces), Image compression, Gene expression analysis in Bioinformatics, Signal processing. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Geometric and Statistical Premise PCA operates on two parallel intuitions: 1. **Maximum Variance Formulation (Karhunen–Loève Transform):** Find directions $\mathbf{w}$ such that the projected data $X\mathbf{w}$ has maximum variance. 2. **Minimum Reconstruction Error Formulation:** Find a subspace where the orthogonal distance (reconstruction error) between original points and their projections is minimized. Both formulations yield the **same mathematical solution**. ### 2.2 Algorithmic Logic — Stepwise Decomposition - **Step 1 — Center the Data:** Subtract the mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ from every observation. This shifts the data cloud to the origin so that variance computations are valid. - **Step 2 — Standardize the Data (Optional but Recommended):** Divide each feature by its standard deviation $\sigma_j$ so all features contribute equally. Critical when features are on different scales (e.g., age in years vs. salary in lakhs). - **Step 3 — Compute the Covariance Matrix:** Build a symmetric $d \times d$ matrix $\Sigma$ that quantifies how features co-vary. - **Step 4 — Eigendecomposition:** Solve the characteristic equation $\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0$ to find eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ and corresponding eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_i$. - **Step 5 — Rank and Select:** Sort eigenvalues in descending order. The eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue is **PC1**, the next is **PC2**, and so on. - **Step 6 — Form the Projection Matrix:** Stack the top $k$ eigenvectors as columns to form $W_{d \times k}$. - **Step 7 — Transform the Data:** Compute $Y = X_{\text{centered}} W$ to obtain the new $k$-dimensional representation. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common trap is assuming PCA always uses the **covariance** matrix. If features are on different scales, PCA on raw covariance will be dominated by high-variance features. Always **standardize** first, which effectively uses the **correlation matrix** instead. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definition | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Mean Vector | $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ | Average of all $n$ samples. | | 2 | Centered Data | $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}_{\text{centered}} = \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | Origin-shifted data point. | | 3 | Covariance Matrix | $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{\top} X_c$ | $d \times d$ symmetric matrix. | | 4 | Eigenvalue Equation | $\Sigma \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ | Definition of eigen-pair. | | 5 | Characteristic Equation | $\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0$ | Solves for $\lambda$. | | 6 | Projection Direction | $\mathbf{w}^* = \arg\max_{\vert \mathbf{w} \vert = 1} \mathbf{w}^{\top} \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ | Optimal unit-length direction. | | 7 | Projected Variance | $\text{Var}(\mathbf{w}^{\top} X) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ | Variance along direction $\mathbf{w}$. | | 8 | Reconstruction | $\hat{X} = X_c W W^{\top} + \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | Approximation back to original space. | | 9 | Reconstruction Error | $J = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{(i)} \Vert^2$ | Sum of squared orthogonal distances. | | 10 | Explained Variance Ratio | $\text{EVR}_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$ | Fraction of total variance. | | 11 | Cumulative Variance | $C_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$ | Used to choose $k$ (e.g., $C_k \geq 0.95$). | | 12 | Scree Plot Choice Rule | Choose smallest $k$ such that $C_k \geq 0.95$ | Standard KTU threshold. | ### 2.4 Practical Engineering Utility - **Computer Vision:** Eigenfaces for face recognition — projected onto a face space spanned by top eigenfaces. - **Biomedical Signal Processing:** EEG/ECG feature extraction before classification. - **Finance:** Portfolio risk analysis — PCA on correlated stock returns decorrelates them into market factors. - **Telecommunications:** Source separation in MIMO channels using PCA over covariance of received signals. - **Manufacturing:** Quality control by projecting multivariate sensor readings onto principal component space to detect anomalies. > [!TIP] > **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) Implementation:** In practice, PCA is implemented via SVD of $X_c = U S V^{\top}$. The right-singular vectors $V$ are the principal directions, and the singular values $s_i$ relate to eigenvalues via $\lambda_i = s_i^2 / (n-1)$. SVD is **numerically more stable** than direct eigen-decomposition, especially for high-dimensional data. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Maximum Variance Formulation **Problem Setup:** Given centered data matrix $X_c \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ (rows are samples, columns are features) and a unit vector $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ ($\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = 1$), we seek the direction that maximizes the variance of the projected scalar values $z_i = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}_c$. **Step 1 — Express the projected variance.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Var}(z) &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (z_i - \bar{z})^2 \\ \end{aligned} $$ Since the data is centered, the projected mean is $\bar{z} = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \bar{\mathbf{x}}_c = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{0} = 0$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Var}(z) &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}_c)^2 \\ &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}_c \mathbf{x}^{(i)\top}_c \mathbf{w} \\ &= \mathbf{w}^{\top} \left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}_c \mathbf{x}^{(i)\top}_c \right) \mathbf{w} \\ &= \mathbf{w}^{\top} \Sigma \mathbf{w} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Set up the constrained optimization.** We want to maximize $\mathbf{w}^{\top} \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ subject to the constraint $\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} = 1$. Use the **Lagrange multiplier** $\alpha$: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \alpha) &= \mathbf{w}^{\top} \Sigma \mathbf{w} - \alpha (\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} - 1) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ and set to zero.** $$ \begin{aligned} \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} &= 2 \Sigma \mathbf{w} - 2 \alpha \mathbf{w} = 0 \\ \Sigma \mathbf{w} &= \alpha \mathbf{w} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Recognize the eigen-equation.** This is the standard eigenvalue problem. The maximum variance is achieved when $\mathbf{w}$ is the **eigenvector** of $\Sigma$, and $\alpha$ is the corresponding **eigenvalue**. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Maximum Variance} &= \mathbf{w}^{\top} \Sigma \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{\top} (\lambda \mathbf{w}) = \lambda \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The first principal component is the eigenvector associated with the **largest eigenvalue** of the covariance matrix. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard) **Dataset:** Given 2-D data points (already centered): $X_c = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 \\ -1 & -2 \\ -2 & -1 \\ -3 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$. **Step 1 — Compute the covariance matrix using $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{\top} X_c$** with $n = 6$. $$ \begin{aligned} X_c^{\top} X_c &= \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & 1 & -1 & -2 & -3 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 & -2 & -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 \\ -1 & -2 \\ -2 & -1 \\ -3 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ Compute element-wise: - $(1,1)$ entry: $4 + 9 + 1 + 1 + 4 + 9 = 28$ - $(1,2)$ entry: $2 + 6 - 1 + 2 + 2 + 6 = 17$ - $(2,2)$ entry: $1 + 4 + 1 + 4 + 1 + 4 = 15$ $$ \begin{aligned} X_c^{\top} X_c &= \begin{bmatrix} 28 & 17 \\ 17 & 15 \end{bmatrix} \\ \Sigma &= \frac{1}{5} \begin{bmatrix} 28 & 17 \\ 17 & 15 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.6 & 3.4 \\ 3.4 & 3.0 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Solve the characteristic equation.** $$ \begin{aligned} \det(\Sigma - \lambda I) &= 0 \\ \begin{vmatrix} 5.6 - \lambda & 3.4 \\ 3.4 & 3.0 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} &= 0 \\ (5.6 - \lambda)(3.0 - \lambda) - 3.4^2 &= 0 \\ \lambda^2 - 8.6 \lambda + 16.8 - 11.56 &= 0 \\ \lambda^2 - 8.6 \lambda + 5.24 &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Apply the quadratic formula $\lambda = \frac{8.6 \pm \sqrt{73.96 - 20.96}}{2} = \frac{8.6 \pm \sqrt{53.0}}{2} \approx \frac{8.6 \pm 7.28}{2}$. $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda_1 &\approx 7.94 \quad (\text{PC1}) \\ \lambda_2 &\approx 0.66 \quad (\text{PC2}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Find the eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 7.94$.** Solve $(\Sigma - \lambda_1 I)\mathbf{v}_1 = 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} -2.34 & 3.4 \\ 3.4 & -4.94 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ From the first row: $-2.34 v_{11} + 3.4 v_{12} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{11} = 1.453 v_{12}$. Choosing $v_{12} = 1$ and normalizing: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{v}_1 &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.453^2 + 1^2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1.453 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.824 \\ 0.567 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Verify explained variance.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EVR}_1 &= \frac{7.94}{7.94 + 0.66} \approx 0.923 = 92.3\% \\ \text{EVR}_2 &= \frac{0.66}{8.60} \approx 0.077 = 7.7\% \end{aligned} $$ PC1 alone captures **92.3%** of the variance. We can safely reduce 2-D data to 1-D. ### 3.3 Reconstruction Error Derivation The total variance equals the trace of the covariance matrix: $\text{Tr}(\Sigma) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$. The reconstruction error when keeping $k$ components is: $$ \begin{aligned} J(k) &= \sum_{i=k+1}^{d} \lambda_i \end{aligned} $$ This is the **mathematical proof** that keeping top-$k$ principal components minimizes reconstruction error among all rank-$k$ projections. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 PCA End-to-End Block Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Dataset
X in R n times d] --> B[Mean Centering
Subtract mu from each feature] B --> C{Features on
different scales?} C -->|Yes| D[Standardization
Divide by sigma] C -->|No| E[Skip to Covariance] D --> F[Compute Covariance Matrix
Sigma equals Xc transpose Xc over n minus 1] E --> F F --> G[Eigendecomposition
Sigma v equals lambda v] G --> H[Sort Eigen Pairs
Descending by lambda] H --> I[Choose k Components
Using Scree Plot or 95 percent rule] I --> J[Build Projection Matrix
W equals v1 v2 ... vk] J --> K[Transform Data
Y equals Xc times W] K --> L[Reduced Dataset
Y in R n times k] L --> M[Optional: Reconstruction
X hat equals Y W transpose plus mu] ``` ### 4.2 Geometric Intuition of Projection ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Original Point x1
in d dimensional space] -->|Project onto
principal axis| Q1[Projected z1
in k dimensional space] P2[Original Point x2
in d dimensional space] -->|Project onto
principal axis| Q2[Projected z2
in k dimensional space] P3[Original Point x3
in d dimensional space] -->|Project onto
principal axis| Q3[Projected z3
in k dimensional space] R[Original Data Cloud
High dimensional] --> S[Reconstruction Plane
Spanned by PCs] S --> T[Compressed Representation
Low dimensional] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Flowchart for Choosing $k$ ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Compute all eigenvalues lambda1 to lambda d] --> B{Method 1
Threshold Rule} B --> C[Select smallest k such that
Cumulative Variance >= 0.95] A --> D{Method 2
Scree Elbow Rule} D --> E[Plot lambda i vs i
Find elbow point] A --> F{Method 3
Kaiser Rule} F --> G[Keep only eigenvalues
greater than mean lambda] C --> H[Final k value] E --> H G --> H ``` ### 4.4 Variance vs. Component Number — Conceptual Visual ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph V[Variance Distribution] V1[PC1
Highest Variance
lambda 1] --> V2[PC2
Second Highest
lambda 2] V2 --> V3[PC3
lambda 3] V3 --> V4[PC d
Lowest Variance
lambda d] end V1 -.->|Cutoff at 95 percent| K[Selected k Components] V4 -.->|Discard| DISC[Noise / Discarded] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Define Principal Component Analysis. What is the significance of orthogonality among the principal components?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a linear dimensionality reduction technique that transforms correlated high-dimensional data into a smaller set of uncorrelated variables called principal components, ordered by the amount of variance they capture. **Orthogonality** ensures that: - Each principal component captures a **unique** portion of variance not shared with others. - The covariance between any two distinct PCs is exactly **zero**, eliminating multicollinearity. - PCs form an **orthonormal basis** for the projected subspace, allowing clean geometric interpretation. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Writing "PCs are independent" instead of "uncorrelated." For Gaussian data, uncorrelated implies independent, but in general PCA only guarantees **uncorrelatedness**, not statistical independence. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Distinguish between Covariance Matrix and Correlation Matrix. When would you prefer correlation-based PCA over covariance-based PCA?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Covariance Matrix $\Sigma$ | Correlation Matrix $R$ | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Diagonal entries | Variances $\sigma_j^2$ | All equal to $1$ | | Off-diagonal entries | Scale-dependent | Scale-invariant | | Use case | Features already on same scale | Features on different units/scales | **Preference Rule:** Use **correlation-based PCA** (which standardizes features to zero mean, unit variance) whenever features have different units or vastly different variances (e.g., height in cm vs. salary in lakhs). Otherwise, the first PC will be dominated by the high-variance feature. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the mathematical formulation of Principal Component Analysis as a **maximum variance projection problem**. Derive the eigenvalue equation that yields the principal directions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Consider the following centered 2-D dataset: $$ \begin{aligned} X_c = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \\ -3 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ Compute the covariance matrix, find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and project the data onto the first principal component. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **(a) Maximum Variance Formulation (7 Marks)** **Setup:** Let $X_c \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ be the centered data matrix with covariance matrix $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{\top} X_c$. We seek a unit vector $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ ($\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = 1$) such that the variance of the projection $z_i = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}_c$ is maximized. **Variance Computation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Var}(z) &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}_c - \overline{\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}_c})^2 \end{aligned} $$ Since the data is centered ($\bar{\mathbf{x}}_c = \mathbf{0}$), the projected mean is zero: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Var}(z) &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}_c)^2 = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \Sigma \mathbf{w} \end{aligned} $$ **Constrained Optimization via Lagrange Multiplier:** $$ \begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \alpha) &= \mathbf{w}^{\top} \Sigma \mathbf{w} - \alpha (\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} - 1) \end{aligned} $$ Differentiating and setting to zero: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} &= 2\Sigma \mathbf{w} - 2\alpha \mathbf{w} = 0 \\ \Sigma \mathbf{w} &= \alpha \mathbf{w} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **eigenvalue equation**. The direction of maximum variance is the eigenvector of $\Sigma$, and the maximum variance equals the corresponding eigenvalue $\alpha = \lambda$. **Maximum Subspace:** To get $k$ principal components, take the $k$ eigenvectors corresponding to the $k$ largest eigenvalues. These eigenvectors are mutually orthogonal because $\Sigma$ is real symmetric. **(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Covariance matrix:** $$ \begin{aligned} X_c^{\top} X_c &= \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 & 1 & -3 \\ 2 & 1 & -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \\ -3 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 16+4+1+9 & 8+2-1+6 \\ 8+2-1+6 & 4+1+1+4 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 30 & 15 \\ 15 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ With $n = 4$: $$ \begin{aligned} \Sigma = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 30 & 15 \\ 15 & 10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 5 \\ 5 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Characteristic equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \det(\Sigma - \lambda I) &= (10 - \lambda)(5 - \lambda) - 25 = 0 \\ \lambda^2 - 15\lambda + 50 - 25 &= 0 \\ \lambda^2 - 15\lambda + 25 &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Using the quadratic formula: $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{15 \pm \sqrt{225 - 100}}{2} = \frac{15 \pm \sqrt{125}}{2} = \frac{15 \pm 11.18}{2} \\ \lambda_1 &\approx 13.09 \quad (\text{PC1}) \\ \lambda_2 &\approx 1.91 \quad (\text{PC2}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 13.09$:** $$ \begin{aligned} (\Sigma - 13.09 I)\mathbf{v}_1 &= \begin{bmatrix} -3.09 & 5 \\ 5 & -8.09 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{0} \end{aligned} $$ From the first row: $-3.09 v_{11} + 5 v_{12} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{11} = 1.618 v_{12}$. Normalizing: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{v}_1 &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.618^2 + 1^2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1.618 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.851 \\ 0.526 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Projection of data onto PC1:** $$ \begin{aligned} Y = X_c \mathbf{v}_1 &= \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \\ -3 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.851 \\ 0.526 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 3.404 + 1.052 \\ 1.702 + 0.526 \\ 0.851 - 0.526 \\ -2.553 - 1.052 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.456 \\ 2.228 \\ 0.325 \\ -3.605 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ The 2-D data is reduced to a 1-D representation. **Explained variance ratio:** $\frac{13.09}{15.00} \approx 87.3\%$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Discuss the **Minimum Reconstruction Error** formulation of PCA and show that it is equivalent to the Maximum Variance formulation. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is the **Scree Plot**? Describe the practical steps involved in choosing the optimal number of principal components using the **Cumulative Explained Variance** method and the **Elbow Rule**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **(a) Minimum Reconstruction Error Formulation (7 Marks)** **Setup:** Suppose we project data onto a $k$-dimensional subspace spanned by orthonormal basis vectors $W = [\mathbf{w}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{w}_k]$. The reconstruction of a point $\mathbf{x}$ in the original $d$-dimensional space is: $$ \begin{aligned} \hat{\mathbf{x}} = W W^{\top} \mathbf{x} \end{aligned} $$ **Reconstruction Error:** The Mean Squared Error (MSE) between original points and reconstructions is: $$ \begin{aligned} J(W) &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - W W^{\top} \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \Vert^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Algebraic Simplification:** Using the fact that for any vector, $\Vert \mathbf{a} \Vert^2 = \mathbf{a}^{\top} \mathbf{a}$ and $W^{\top} W = I_k$: $$ \begin{aligned} J(W) &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \Vert^2 - \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}^{(i)\top} W W^{\top} \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \\ &= \text{Tr}(\Sigma) - \text{Tr}(W^{\top} \Sigma W) \end{aligned} $$ Since $\text{Tr}(\Sigma) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$ is constant, **minimizing** $J(W)$ is equivalent to **maximizing** $\text{Tr}(W^{\top} \Sigma W)$. **Maximum Trace Condition:** For orthonormal $W$, $\text{Tr}(W^{\top} \Sigma W) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \mathbf{w}_j^{\top} \Sigma \mathbf{w}_j$. By Rayleigh quotient theory, this sum is maximized when $\mathbf{w}_j$ are the top-$k$ eigenvectors of $\Sigma$. $$ \begin{aligned} J_{\min} &= \sum_{i=k+1}^{d} \lambda_i \end{aligned} $$ **Equivalence Proven:** The directions that minimize reconstruction error are exactly those that maximize projected variance — both yield the eigenvectors of $\Sigma$. **(b) Scree Plot and Component Selection (7 Marks)** **Scree Plot Definition:** > [!NOTE] > A **Scree Plot** is a line plot of eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ (y-axis) against component index $i$ (x-axis). It is named after the geological term "scree" — the rubble at the base of a cliff. **Step 1 — Cumulative Explained Variance Method:** - Compute all eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_d$. - Compute total variance: $T = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$. - Compute cumulative ratio: $$ \begin{aligned} C_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{T} \end{aligned} $$ - Choose the smallest $k$ such that $C_k \geq \theta$, where $\theta$ is typically **0.90, 0.95, or 0.99**. **Step 2 — Elbow Rule:** - Visually inspect the scree plot. - Locate the **elbow point** — the index where the curve sharply transitions from steep descent to gradual tapering. - Choose $k$ at this elbow — components before it capture significant variance; after it contribute little. **Step 3 — Kaiser Rule (Supplementary):** - Compute the mean eigenvalue $\bar{\lambda} = \frac{1}{d}\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$. - Retain only components with $\lambda_i > \bar{\lambda}$. **Step 4 — Cross-Validation (for downstream tasks):** - For supervised tasks, choose $k$ that maximizes downstream model performance on a validation set. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Loses 2 marks** if you confuse **feature selection** with **feature extraction**. PCA creates **new features** (linear combinations); it does not select a subset. > - **Loses 1 mark** if you forget to mention that PCA requires **centering** (and possibly **standardization**) as a preprocessing step. > - **Loses 1 mark** if you write that PCA assumes data is **Gaussian**. PCA does not assume Gaussianity; it only uses first and second moments (mean and covariance). > - **Loses 2 marks** in numerical problems if you skip showing the determinant expansion or the normalization step for the eigenvector. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — PCA** - **Definition:** Linear orthogonal transformation that maximizes projected variance (or equivalently, minimizes reconstruction error). - **Prerequisite:** Data must be **centered**; standardize if features have different scales. - **Core Math:** Solves the eigenvalue problem $\Sigma \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$. - **First PC:** Eigenvector of $\Sigma$ with the **largest** eigenvalue. - **Ordering:** PCs are sorted in **descending** order of eigenvalues. - **Orthogonality:** $\mathbf{w}_i^{\top} \mathbf{w}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$ — always true because $\Sigma$ is symmetric. - **Explained Variance Ratio:** $\text{EVR}_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_j \lambda_j}$. - **Total Variance Preserved:** $\text{Tr}(\Sigma) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$. - **Reconstruction Error:** Equals the sum of discarded eigenvalues $\sum_{i=k+1}^{d} \lambda_i$. - **Choosing $k$:** - Cumulative variance threshold ($C_k \geq 0.95$). - Scree plot elbow. - Kaiser rule ($\lambda_i > \bar{\lambda}$). - **Implementation:** Use **SVD** for numerical stability; relation $X_c = U S V^{\top} \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_i = V_{:,i},\ \lambda_i = s_i^2 / (n-1)$. - **Limits of PCA:** - **Linear** only — fails on manifolds (use Kernel PCA or t-SNE for non-linear). - **Sensitive to outliers** — use Robust PCA for contaminated data. - **Unsupervised** — discards class label information (use LDA if labels matter). - **Key Distinctions:** - PCA vs. LDA: PCA is unsupervised, LDA is supervised. - PCA vs. ICA: PCA decorrelates; ICA achieves independence (for non-Gaussian sources). - PCA vs. Factor Analysis: PCA is a linear transform; FA assumes a latent variable model. - **Real-World Applications:** Eigenfaces (face recognition), image compression, bioinformatics (gene expression), finance (risk factor decomposition), anomaly detection, noise filtering. - **Common KTU Traps:** - Forgetting to **mean-center**. - Forgetting to **normalize eigenvectors** to unit length. - Using PCA on **categorical** data (PCA is for continuous data). - Confusing eigenvalues (variance magnitude) with eigenvectors (directions).

topics.title: Neural Networks and SVM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT755 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Neural Networks and SVM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Artificial Neural Networks (ANN)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> An **Artificial Neural Network (ANN)** is a computational model inspired by the structure and functional aspects of biological neurons. It consists of interconnected processing elements (neurons/artificial neurons) organized in layers — input, hidden, and output — that learn complex non-linear mappings from input data $\mathbf{x}$ to output $\hat{y}$ by adjusting connection weights using optimization algorithms such as **Gradient Descent** and **Backpropagation**.

### Intuition / Real-World Analogy 🧠
Imagine you are learning to identify whether a fruit is an apple or an orange. You unconsciously consider features like color, shape, and texture. A **neural network** does the same thing mathematically: each "neuron" is a tiny decision-maker that looks at some features, multiplies them by learned importance values (weights), adds a bias, applies a non-linear function, and passes the result forward. After thousands of such decisions stacked in layers, the network can recognize even very complex patterns — like faces, voices, or diseases in medical scans.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biological Inspiration:** A biological neuron (dendrites → cell body → axon) is mimicked as: **inputs $\rightarrow$ weighted sum $\rightarrow$ activation function $\rightarrow$ output**.

---

## 1.2 The Perceptron (Building Block of ANN)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perceptron (Rosenblatt, 1958):** The simplest neural unit that computes a linear combination of inputs followed by a step activation function. It is the **fundamental linear classifier** of neural networks.

### Intuition
A perceptron is like a **judge at a weighing scale** — it puts each piece of evidence (feature $x_i$) on one side, multiplied by how important that evidence is (weight $w_i$). If the total score crosses a threshold (bias), it says "Yes" (class $+1$); otherwise "No" (class $-1$).

---

## 1.3 Support Vector Machine (SVM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a discriminative supervised learning classifier that finds the **optimal hyperplane** which maximizes the **margin** — the perpendicular distance between the decision boundary and the closest data points (called **support vectors**) from either class. It uses the **kernel trick** to handle non-linearly separable data by projecting it into higher-dimensional feature spaces.

### Intuition 🛣️
Imagine two rival groups of students standing on a playground. You are asked to draw a line (or a road divider) that separates them. The best line is **not** any line that separates them, but the one that is **furthest away from the nearest student on either side**. SVM automatically finds that "widest possible road." The students standing closest to the road are the **support vectors** — they alone define the road.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary:** **Hyperplane**, **Margin**, **Support Vectors**, **Kernel Trick**, **Soft Margin (C-parameter)**.

---

## 1.4 Why These Two Together?

| Aspect | Neural Network | SVM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin** | Brain-inspired (1958) | Statistical Learning Theory (Vapnik, 1995) |
| **Decision Boundary** | Learned non-linear manifold | Maximum-margin hyperplane |
| **Key Strength** | Universal function approximator | Robust to high-dimensional data |
| **Data Type** | Large datasets, deep learning | Small/medium datasets, high dimensions |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Perceptron Model

The perceptron takes an input vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$, computes a weighted sum, adds a bias, and passes it through an activation function.

**Operational Steps:**
- **Step 1 — Weighted Summation (Net Input):** Compute the dot product of weights and inputs, then add bias $b$.

$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$

- **Step 2 — Activation Function:** Apply a non-linear transformation to produce the output.

$$\hat{y} = f(z)$$

- **Step 3 — Threshold Decision (Binary Step Function):** For the original Rosenblatt perceptron:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } z \ge 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } z < 0 \end{cases}$$

- **Step 4 — Weight Update Rule (Perceptron Learning):** If the prediction is wrong, update weights and bias.

$$w_i \leftarrow w_i + \eta (y - \hat{y}) x_i$$

$$b \leftarrow b + \eta (y - \hat{y})$$

Here, $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (typically $0 < \eta \le 1$) and $y$ is the true label.

### Why Each Step?
- Step 1 aggregates evidence.
- Step 2 introduces **non-linearity**, vital for learning complex patterns.
- Step 3 produces a class label.
- Step 4 minimizes classification error using the **Perceptron Convergence Theorem** (guaranteed convergence for linearly separable data).

---

## 2.2 Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) & Backpropagation

A single perceptron can only solve linearly separable problems (e.g., AND, OR — but **not XOR**). To overcome this, multiple perceptrons are stacked into **layers** forming an MLP, and the **Backpropagation algorithm** (Rumelhart, Hinton, Williams — 1986) is used to train it.

**Standard Activation Functions:**

| Function | Formula | Range | Derivative | Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | Binary output |
| **Tanh** | $\tanh(z)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $1 - \tanh^2(z)$ | Zero-centered |
| **ReLU** | $\max(0, z)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $1 \text{ if } z>0, \text{ else } 0$ | Hidden layers (default) |
| **Softmax** | $\dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | $(0,1)$, sum=1 | — | Multi-class output |

### The Backpropagation Idea (Why & How)
- **Why:** The network must know how each weight contributes to the final error so it can adjust weights intelligently.
- **How:** It uses the **chain rule of calculus** to propagate the error backwards from output to input layer.

**Loss Function (Mean Squared Error):**

$$L = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left( y^{(k)} - \hat{y}^{(k)} \right)^2$$

**Weight Update via Gradient Descent:**

$$w_{ij} \leftarrow w_{ij} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial w_{ij}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A feedforward neural network with a single hidden layer containing a finite number of neurons can approximate **any continuous function** on compact subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$ to arbitrary accuracy.

---

## 2.3 Support Vector Machine — Linear Case

Given training data $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the decision hyperplane is:

$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$

**Classification rule:** $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)$

### 2.3.1 Functional & Geometric Margins

- **Functional margin** of the $i$-th point: $\gamma_i = y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b)$
- **Geometric margin** (true perpendicular distance from $\mathbf{x}_i$ to the hyperplane):

$$\gamma = \frac{y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2}$$

- $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2$ denotes the $\ell_2$-norm (Euclidean norm): $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^2}$

> [!NOTE]
> We write $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2$ in plain text (NOT $\vert w \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown tables.

### 2.3.2 Optimization Problem (Primal Form)

Maximize the margin $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2}$, equivalent to minimizing $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2$:

$$\boxed{\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1, \;\; \forall i}$$

### 2.3.3 Dual Form (Using Lagrange Multipliers)

Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \ge 0$:

$$L(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

Taking derivatives and substituting yields the **dual problem**:

$$\boxed{\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \alpha_i \ge 0, \;\; \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Support Vectors** are precisely the data points with $\alpha_i > 0$ — they lie ON the margin boundary and alone determine the hyperplane.

---

## 2.4 Soft-Margin SVM (Non-Separable Case)

When data is **not** linearly separable, we introduce **slack variables** $\xi_i \ge 0$ and the penalty parameter $C > 0$:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

$$\text{subject to:} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \ge 0$$

- $C$ balances **margin width** vs. **classification error** (large $C$ = hard margin, small $C$ = wider margin, more violations).

---

## 2.5 The Kernel Trick (Non-Linear SVM)

For non-linearly separable data, map inputs to a higher-dimensional feature space using a mapping function $\phi(\mathbf{x})$:

$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$$

This avoids computing $\phi$ explicitly — only the kernel is needed.

| Kernel | Formula $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linear** | $\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$ | Linearly separable |
| **Polynomial (degree $d$)** | $(\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$ | Curved boundaries |
| **RBF / Gaussian** | $\exp\!\left(-\gamma \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert_2^2 \right)$ | Most popular, infinite-dim |
| **Sigmoid** | $\tanh(\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + r)$ | Neural-like |

- $\gamma$ is the kernel coefficient; $\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert_2$ is the Euclidean distance.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Perceptron Net Input | $z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$ | Linear aggregation |
| 2 | Perceptron Update | $w_i \leftarrow w_i + \eta (y - \hat{y}) x_i$ | Misclassification correction |
| 3 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Output in $(0,1)$ |
| 4 | ReLU | $\max(0, z)$ | Default hidden activation |
| 5 | Gradient Descent | $w \leftarrow w - \eta \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial w}$ | Backprop engine |
| 6 | Geometric Margin | $\gamma = \dfrac{y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2}$ | True perpendicular distance |
| 7 | SVM Primal | $\min \dfrac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2^2$ s.t. $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1$ | Hard margin |
| 8 | SVM Dual | $\max \sum \alpha_i - \dfrac{1}{2} \sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Solved via QP |
| 9 | Soft-Margin | $\min \dfrac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2^2 + C \sum \xi_i$ | Adds slack variables |
| 10 | RBF Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = e^{-\gamma \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert_2^2}$ | Most-used non-linear kernel |
| 11 | Hinge Loss | $L = \max(0, 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b))$ | SVM's loss function |
| 12 | Kernel Condition | $K$ valid $\iff$ symmetric + PSD | Mercer's Theorem |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application | Model Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Healthcare** | Cancer / Tumor classification from MRI | Deep MLP, CNN |
| **NLP** | Sentiment analysis, machine translation | RNN / Transformer |
| **Finance** | Credit card fraud detection | SVM + RBF Kernel |
| **Computer Vision** | Face recognition, OCR | SVM (classical), CNN (modern) |
| **Bioinformatics** | Protein classification, gene expression | SVM with string kernels |
| **Speech Recognition** | Voice assistants (Alexa, Siri) | Deep Neural Networks |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Perceptron Learning Rule from the Loss Function

**Goal:** Show that the perceptron update rule $w_i \leftarrow w_i + \eta (y - \hat{y}) x_i$ emerges from **gradient descent** on a suitable loss.

### Step 1 — Define the Perceptron Loss
For a misclassified point, define the **perceptron criterion**:

$$L_{\text{perc}}(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b)$$

where $\mathcal{M}$ is the set of misclassified samples.

> Note: for correctly classified points, $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) > 0$, so they contribute nothing to the loss.

### Step 2 — Compute the Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$

$$\frac{\partial L_{\text{perc}}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

### Step 3 — Apply Gradient Descent Update
A single misclassified point $\mathbf{x}_i$ yields:

$$\mathbf{w}^{\text{new}} = \mathbf{w} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L_{\text{perc}}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \eta \left( - y_i \mathbf{x}_i \right) = \mathbf{w} + \eta y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

For binary outputs $\hat{y} \in \{-1, +1\}$, this matches:

$$\boxed{w_j \leftarrow w_j + \eta \, y_i \, x_{i,j}}$$

which is the well-known Perceptron Learning Rule. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation: SVM Margin Maximization

**Goal:** Derive the dual SVM optimization from the primal using **Lagrange multipliers**.

### Step 1 — Primal Problem
Hard-margin SVM is the convex optimization problem:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2^2$$

$$\text{subject to:} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1, \quad i = 1, \dots, N$$

### Step 2 — Form the Lagrangian
Attach a multiplier $\alpha_i \ge 0$ to every constraint:

$$L(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

### Step 3 — Stationarity (Set Partial Derivatives to Zero)

**Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$:**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i}$$

**Differentiate w.r.t. $b$:**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0}$$

### Step 4 — Back-Substitute into the Lagrangian
Substitute $\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ into $L$:

$$L = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i \right)^T \left( \sum_j \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j \right) - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i^T \sum_j \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j - b \sum_i \alpha_i y_i + \sum_i \alpha_i$$

Using $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$, the middle terms vanish:

$$L = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + \sum_i \alpha_i$$

### Step 5 — Dual Maximization Problem
The dual is obtained by flipping the sign (from minimization to maximization):

$$\boxed{\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \;\; W(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j}$$

subject to $\alpha_i \ge 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTT (Karush–Kuhn–Tucker) Complementary Slackness:** $\alpha_i \left[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right] = 0$. So $\alpha_i > 0 \Rightarrow y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$ → these are the **support vectors**.

### Step 6 — Replace Dot Product with Kernel (Final Generalization)
For non-linear SVM, replace $\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$ with the kernel $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$:

$$W(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$$

The final classifier becomes:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\!\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b \right)$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Perceptron on 2D Data

Suppose we have two points:
- $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1, 1)^T$, $y_1 = +1$
- $\mathbf{x}_2 = (0, 0)^T$, $y_2 = -1$

Initial weights $\mathbf{w} = (0, 0)^T$, $b = 0$, $\eta = 1$.

**Iteration 1:** Predict on $\mathbf{x}_1$ → $z = 0$. Output $\hat{y} = -1$ (incorrect).
Update:

$$\mathbf{w} = (0,0) + 1 \cdot (+1) \cdot (1,1) = (1, 1)$$

$$b = 0 + 1 \cdot (+1) = 1$$

**Iteration 2:** Predict on $\mathbf{x}_2$ → $z = 0 + 1 = 1 \Rightarrow \hat{y} = +1$ (incorrect, true $y = -1$).
Update:

$$\mathbf{w} = (1, 1) + 1 \cdot (-1) \cdot (0, 0) = (1, 1)$$

$$b = 1 + 1 \cdot (-1) = 0$$

**Iteration 3:** Verify on $\mathbf{x}_1$: $z = 1\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 1 + 0 = 2 \Rightarrow +1$ ✅
On $\mathbf{x}_2$: $z = 0 + 0 + 0 = 0 \Rightarrow$ (boundary, treated as $-1$ or $0$). ✅

The final decision boundary: $x_1 + x_2 = 0$, i.e., a line separating the two points correctly.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Perceptron | SVM (Linear) | SVM (Kernel) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Goal** | Minimize misclassifications | Maximize geometric margin | Maximize margin in feature space |
| **Loss** | Perceptron criterion / 0-1 | Hinge loss | Kernel hinge loss |
| **Solver** | Iterative update | QP / SMO | QP / SMO with kernel |
| **Output** | $\text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b)$ | Same | $\text{sign}\!\left(\sum \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b\right)$ |
| **Key Variable** | Weights $\mathbf{w}$ | Weights + bias + $\alpha_i$ | Support vectors + $\alpha_i$ |
| **Non-linearity** | Through hidden layers | None | Through kernel function |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN1[Input Layer]
        x1[Node x1]
        x2[Node x2]
        x3[Node x3]
    end
    subgraph H1[Hidden Layer 1]
        h1a[Node h1a]
        h1b[Node h1b]
        h1c[Node h1c]
        h1d[Node h1d]
    end
    subgraph H2[Hidden Layer 2]
        h2a[Node h2a]
        h2b[Node h2b]
        h2c[Node h2c]
    end
    subgraph OUT1[Output Layer]
        y1[Node y1]
        y2[Node y2]
    end
    x1 --> h1a
    x1 --> h1b
    x1 --> h1c
    x1 --> h1d
    x2 --> h1a
    x2 --> h1b
    x2 --> h1c
    x2 --> h1d
    x3 --> h1a
    x3 --> h1b
    x3 --> h1c
    x3 --> h1d
    h1a --> h2a
    h1a --> h2b
    h1a --> h2c
    h1b --> h2a
    h1b --> h2b
    h1b --> h2c
    h1c --> h2a
    h1c --> h2b
    h1c --> h2c
    h1d --> h2a
    h1d --> h2b
    h1d --> h2c
    h2a --> y1
    h2a --> y2
    h2b --> y1
    h2b --> y2
    h2c --> y1
    h2c --> y2
```

> **Reading guide:** Each arrow carries a weight $w_{ij}$ that is learned during backpropagation. Information flows **left → right** (feedforward); gradients flow **right → left** (backprop).

---

## 4.2 Backpropagation Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Input x] --> stepB[Forward Pass: z and a in each layer]
    stepB --> stepC[Compute Loss L y minus yhat]
    stepC --> stepD[Backward Pass: dL over dw via chain rule]
    stepD --> stepE[Gradient Descent: w new equals w minus eta times dL over dw]
    stepE --> stepF{Converged or Max Epochs}
    stepF -->|No| stepB
    stepF -->|Yes| stepG[Final Weights and Trained Network]
```

---

## 4.3 SVM Hyperplane and Support Vectors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NEG[Class minus 1 Region]
        s1[Support Vector x1]
        s2[Support Vector x2]
    end
    subgraph MARGIN_LEFT[Margin Boundary minus 1]
        b1[Hyperplane Parallel: wT x plus b equals minus 1]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Decision Region]
        hp[Optimal Hyperplane: wT x plus b equals 0]
    end
    subgraph MARGIN_RIGHT[Margin Boundary plus 1]
        b2[Hyperplane Parallel: wT x plus b equals plus 1]
    end
    subgraph POS[Class plus 1 Region]
        s3[Support Vector x3]
        s4[Support Vector x4]
    end
    NEG --> MARGIN_LEFT
    MARGIN_LEFT --> CORE
    CORE --> MARGIN_RIGHT
    MARGIN_RIGHT --> POS
```

> **Reading guide:** Only the **support vectors** (points lying on the dashed margin boundaries) determine the hyperplane. The margin width is $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2}$. Maximizing this width is the objective of SVM.

---

## 4.4 Kernel Trick Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Original Input Space X] --> map1[Mapping phi maps X to Feature Space F]
    map1 --> inner[Compute Inner Product phi xi transpose phi xj]
    inner --> eq[Equivalent Kernel: K xi xj]
    raw --> eq
    eq --> classifier[Train SVM with K]
    classifier --> out[Non-Linear Decision Boundary in X]
```

> **Why this works:** Mercer's theorem guarantees that certain symmetric positive semi-definite functions $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ implicitly compute an inner product in a (possibly infinite-dimensional) feature space **without ever computing** $\phi(\mathbf{x})$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 4]**
**Define an Artificial Neural Network. List any two activation functions used in hidden layers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** An Artificial Neural Network (ANN) is a parallel, distributed information-processing model inspired by the human brain, consisting of interconnected processing units (neurons) organized into input, hidden, and output layers. It learns the mapping $f: \mathbf{x} \to \hat{y}$ by adjusting connection weights to minimize a loss function.
- **Two activation functions (1 mark):** (i) **Sigmoid:** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, and (ii) **ReLU:** $f(z) = \max(0, z)$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam, Module 4]**
**What are support vectors in an SVM? Why are they important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Support vectors (2 marks):** Support vectors are the data points that lie **closest to the decision hyperplane** and satisfy the constraint $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$ exactly. They correspond to non-zero Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i > 0$.
- **Importance (1 mark):** They are the **only** training points that determine the position and orientation of the optimal hyperplane. Removing any other non-support-vector point has no effect on the model, but moving a support vector **changes** the hyperplane.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** *"Answer any ONE full question from each module. Each full question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks."*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Neural Networks Focus** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Sample Paper, Module 4]`

#### **A(a). Explain the architecture and working of a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) with a neat diagram. Discuss the backpropagation algorithm in detail.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. MLP Architecture (2 marks):**
An MLP consists of:
- An **input layer** (no computation, just data ingestion).
- One or more **hidden layers**, each containing neurons with non-linear activation functions.
- An **output layer** for prediction.
- Information flows strictly **left-to-right** (feedforward); each connection carries a learnable weight $w_{ij}^{(l)}$ for layer $l$.

**2. Forward Propagation (2 marks):**
For a layer $l$ with pre-activation $\mathbf{z}^{(l)}$ and activation $\mathbf{a}^{(l)}$:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}, \quad \mathbf{a}^{(l)} = f(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$$

where $f$ is the activation function (ReLU, sigmoid, etc.).

**3. Backpropagation Algorithm (3 marks):**
- **Step 1:** Forward pass to compute activations at every layer.
- **Step 2:** Compute output error $\delta^{(L)} = (\mathbf{a}^{(L)} - \mathbf{y}) \odot f'(\mathbf{z}^{(L)})$, where $\odot$ is the element-wise product and $L$ is the last layer.
- **Step 3:** Backpropagate the error: $\delta^{(l)} = \left( \mathbf{W}^{(l+1)} \right)^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot f'(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$.
- **Step 4:** Compute gradients: $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})^T$.
- **Step 5:** Update weights: $\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(l)} - \eta \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$.
- **Step 6:** Repeat for a fixed number of epochs or until convergence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Idea:** Backpropagation is **just the chain rule of calculus** applied recursively across layers.

---

#### **A(b). With a suitable example, explain the Perceptron learning algorithm. Show that it converges for linearly separable data.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Algorithm Statement (2 marks):**
The Perceptron learning algorithm iteratively updates weights to correctly classify all training points.

**2. Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
1. Initialize $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$, $b = 0$, learning rate $\eta$.
2. For each epoch and each $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$:
   - Compute $z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b$.
   - Predict $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(z)$.
   - If $y_i \neq \hat{y}$: $w_j \leftarrow w_j + \eta \, y_i \, x_{i,j}$ and $b \leftarrow b + \eta \, y_i$.
3. Repeat until no misclassifications.

**3. Numerical Example (2 marks):**
Let $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1, 1)^T, y_1 = +1$ and $\mathbf{x}_2 = (-1, -1)^T, y_2 = -1$. With $\eta = 1$, one epoch produces the correct separating line $w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 = 0$.

**4. Convergence Proof Sketch (1 mark):**
The **Perceptron Convergence Theorem** (Rosenblatt, 1962; Novikoff, 1962) states: *If the data is linearly separable with margin $\gamma > 0$, then the perceptron algorithm makes at most $\dfrac{R^2}{\gamma^2}$ mistakes*, where $R = \max_i \lVert \mathbf{x}_i \rVert_2$. Hence it converges in finite steps.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — SVM Focus** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Sample Paper, Module 4]`

#### **B(a). Formulate the SVM optimization problem for linearly separable binary classification. Derive the dual form using Lagrange multipliers.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Primal Formulation (2 marks):**
Given training data $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the goal is to find $\mathbf{w}, b$ minimizing $\dfrac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2^2$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1$ for all $i$.

**2. Lagrangian (2 marks):**
Attach multipliers $\alpha_i \ge 0$:

$$L = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

**3. KKT Stationarity (2 marks):**
Setting $\partial L / \partial \mathbf{w} = 0$ gives $\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$.
Setting $\partial L / \partial b = 0$ gives $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

**4. Dual Form (1 mark):**
Substituting back yields the dual:

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$$

subject to $\alpha_i \ge 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

> **Conclusion:** The dual depends only on inner products $\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$, which is the gateway to the **kernel trick**.

---

#### **B(b). Explain the kernel trick in SVM. List and briefly explain any three popular kernel functions with their mathematical forms.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Motivation (2 marks):**
Real-world data is often not linearly separable in the original input space. The kernel trick maps data to a higher-dimensional space $\mathcal{F}$ via $\phi: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathcal{F}$ where it becomes linearly separable — **without ever explicitly computing** $\phi(\mathbf{x})$. We only need the inner product $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$, which is cheaper.

**2. Mercer's Condition (1 mark):**
A function $K$ is a valid kernel if and only if its kernel matrix (Gram matrix) is **symmetric and positive semi-definite**.

**3. Three Popular Kernels (4 marks — to be split equally):**

| Kernel | Formula |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Polynomial (degree $d$)** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\gamma \, \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$ |
| **RBF (Gaussian)** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert_2^2 \right)$ |
| **Sigmoid** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \tanh(\gamma \, \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + r)$ |

- **Polynomial:** Creates feature spaces of degree $d$; good for image processing.
- **RBF:** Most widely used; maps to an infinite-dimensional space; $\gamma$ controls locality.
- **Sigmoid:** Behaves like a two-layer neural network; not always a valid kernel (depends on parameters).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing functional vs. geometric margin:** Functional margin does **not** account for $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2$; geometric margin does. Examiners specifically deduct for this.
> 2. **Forgetting KKT conditions:** Whenever deriving SVM, the KTT complementary slackness condition $\alpha_i[y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1] = 0$ **must** be written — it defines support vectors.
> 3. **Wrong direction of weight update:** Perceptron update is $w \leftarrow w + \eta (y - \hat{y}) x$ — **plus** sign for misclassification, **NOT** a gradient-descent minus sign in the standard form. Show both forms for full credit.
> 4. **Kernel matrix must be PSD:** If a student writes "any function is a kernel" — *zero marks*. Mercer's theorem is the cornerstone.
> 5. **Pipe symbol in tables:** In KTU answer sheets, never use $\vert \cdot \vert$ inside LaTeX tables — examiners often transcribe to plain text and break formatting. Use $\lVert \cdot \rVert_2$ for norms.
> 6. **Activation function derivatives:** Examiners expect the **exact** derivative forms ($\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$). Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Perceptron** is the smallest ANN — linear binary classifier using step activation.
- **Perceptron cannot solve XOR** — it fails on non-linearly separable data, motivating MLPs.
- **MLP + Backpropagation + Gradient Descent** = the backbone of all deep learning.
- **Activation functions** introduce non-linearity: **Sigmoid** for output (binary), **ReLU** for hidden layers (default).
- **Universal Approximation Theorem:** One hidden layer suffices to approximate any continuous function.
- **SVM** is a **maximum-margin classifier** — it finds the hyperplane that maximizes the geometric margin $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2}$.
- **Support Vectors** are the data points on the margin boundary with $\alpha_i > 0$ — they alone define the hyperplane.
- **SVM Primal** minimizes $\dfrac{1}{2}\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert_2^2$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1$.
- **SVM Dual** depends only on inner products — this is why the **kernel trick** works.
- **KTT Conditions** = Stationarity + Primal feasibility + Dual feasibility + Complementary slackness.
- **Kernel Trick** replaces $\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$ with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ to learn non-linear boundaries.
- **RBF Kernel** is the most popular non-linear kernel (default in scikit-learn `SVC`).
- **Soft-Margin SVM** uses slack variables $\xi_i$ and penalty $C$ for non-separable data.
- **Hinge Loss** is the loss function underlying SVM: $L = \max(0, 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b))$.
- **Compare**: NN = universal approximator with many hyperparameters; SVM = robust, works well on small/high-dim data, theoretically principled.
- **KTU Mantra:** "Margin, KKT, Kernel" — these three terms appear in **every** SVM exam answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perceptron (Rosenblatt, 1958):** A *Perceptron* is the earliest and mathematically simplest form of an artificial neuron, introduced by **Frank Rosenblatt** in 1958. It is a linear, binary classifier that maps an input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to a single binary output $y \in \{0, 1\}$ (or $\{-1, +1\}$) by computing a weighted sum of its inputs, adding a bias term, and passing the result through a **step activation function**.

Mathematically, a single-layer perceptron implements the discriminant function:

$$y = \phi\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right) = \phi(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^\top$ is the **input feature vector**
- $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n]^\top$ is the **weight vector** (synaptic strengths)
- $b$ is the **bias** (offsets the decision boundary from the origin)
- $\phi(\cdot)$ is the **activation function** (typically the Heaviside step function)
- $y$ is the **binary output**

The decision boundary, called the **hyperplane**, is the locus of points where:

$$\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$

This hyperplane divides the input space $\mathbb{R}^n$ into two half-spaces — one for class $+1$ and the other for class $0$ (or $-1$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Perceptron is studied as a *foundational building block* of modern neural networks. KTU examiners expect students to (a) derive the **perceptron learning rule**, (b) state the **perceptron convergence theorem**, and (c) explain the famous **XOR limitation** that led to the first "AI Winter".

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Think of a Perceptron as a **strict, old-school bank loan officer** sitting at a desk.

Imagine Mr. Rosenblatt, the bank officer. A customer walks in carrying a folder of documents. Mr. Rosenblatt looks at each document — annual income, credit score, existing debts, collateral value, age — and assigns an **importance factor (weight)** to each. Income might be highly positive; existing debt might be highly negative.

He mentally **sums up**: "income $\times 0.8$ + credit score $\times 0.5$ - debts $\times 0.7$ + bias" until he crosses some internal threshold. The moment the running total crosses his cutoff, the **decision snap** — *Approved* (1) or *Rejected* (0). There is no grey area. He never says "maybe".

Now, when a loan turns out to be a disaster (the bank loses money), the officer **learns from his mistake**: he nudges the weight on the misleading feature (say, "collateral value") downward and nudges the weight on the truthful feature (say, "credit score") upward. He repeats this after every mistake, and over thousands of customers, his decision rule tightens into a sharp, hyperplane-shaped policy.

That is **exactly** how a Perceptron behaves:
- **Inputs** $x_i$ = the customer's features
- **Weights** $w_i$ = the officer's learned importance factors
- **Bias** $b$ = his inherent risk appetite
- **Step function** = the moment of final decision
- **Error-driven weight update** = the officer learning from his past mistakes

---

## 1.3 Historical Context

| Year | Milestone | Contributor |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| 1943 | McCulloch–Pitts Neuron (fixed weights) | McCulloch & Pitts |
| 1958 | Perceptron (learnable weights, single layer) | Frank Rosenblatt |
| 1969 | XOR problem exposed — first AI winter | Minsky & Papert |
| 1986 | Backpropagation for multi-layer networks | Rumelhart, Hinton, Williams |
| 2010s+ | Deep learning revival (ReLU, GPUs) | Hinton, LeCun, Bengio |

> [!NOTE]
> The Perceptron is historically significant because it proved that **a machine could learn weights from data automatically** — a radical idea in 1958. The 2024 KTU syllabus treats it as the conceptual ancestor of the multi-layer perceptron (MLP) and modern deep networks.

---

## 1.4 Geometric Intuition of the Decision Boundary

A Perceptron with $n=2$ inputs draws a **straight line** in the 2-D plane:

$$w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_2 = -\frac{w_1}{w_2}x_1 - \frac{b}{w_2}$$

For $n=3$, it draws a **plane** in 3-D space. For $n>3$, it draws a **hyperplane** in $\mathbb{R}^n$. This is why the Perceptron is called a **linear classifier** — it can only separate classes that are linearly separable.

**The bias $b$ is geometrically crucial:** without $b$, the hyperplane is forced to pass through the origin, which is useless when the two classes are clustered away from the origin.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Single-Layer Perceptron

The architecture consists of **four distinct layers of computation** even though it is called "single-layer" (the term *layer* refers only to the *output* layer of trainable weights):

1. **Input Layer** — receives the raw feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ (no computation, no weights).
2. **Weighting Stage** — each input $x_i$ is multiplied by its corresponding synaptic weight $w_i$.
3. **Summation + Bias Stage** — computes the **net input** (also called the *pre-activation*):

$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$$

4. **Activation Stage** — applies the **step function** to produce the final output $y$:

$$y = \phi(z) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } z \geq \theta \\ 0 & \text{if } z < \theta \end{cases}$$

where $\theta$ is the **threshold**. The bias $b$ is algebraically equivalent to $-\theta$ when the threshold is moved to the input side.

---

## 2.2 The Activation (Step) Functions

| Name | Formula | Output Range | KTU Use |
|------|---------|--------------|---------|
| **Binary Step** (Unipolar) | $\phi(z) = \begin{cases} 1 & z \geq 0 \\ 0 & z < 0 \end{cases}$ | $\{0, 1\}$ | Original Perceptron |
| **Signum / Bipolar Step** | $\phi(z) = \begin{cases} +1 & z \geq 0 \\ -1 & z < 0 \end{cases}$ | $\{-1, +1\}$ | Mathematically symmetric form |
| **Sigmoid** (for comparison) | $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | Differentiable — used in MLP |
| **ReLU** (for comparison) | $\phi(z) = \max(0, z)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Modern deep networks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The step function is **non-differentiable** at $z=0$ and has **zero derivative** everywhere else. This is precisely why we **cannot use gradient descent** on a vanilla Perceptron — we must use the special **Perceptron Learning Rule** derived by Rosenblatt.

---

## 2.3 The Perceptron Learning Rule (Rosenblatt, 1958)

Given a training sample $(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}, t^{(k)})$ where $t^{(k)} \in \{0, 1\}$ is the target (desired output), the perceptron makes a prediction $y^{(k)}$. The error signal is:

$$e^{(k)} = t^{(k)} - y^{(k)} \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$$

The weight update rule is:

$$w_i^{\text{new}} = w_i^{\text{old}} + \Delta w_i \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta w_i = \eta \cdot e^{(k)} \cdot x_i^{(k)}$$

$$b^{\text{new}} = b^{\text{old}} + \eta \cdot e^{(k)}$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (typically $0 < \eta \leq 1$).

In **vector form** (very useful for KTU derivations):

$$\mathbf{w}^{\text{new}} = \mathbf{w}^{\text{old}} + \eta \, e^{(k)} \mathbf{x}^{(k)}$$

**Update logic:**
- If $e = 0$ (correct prediction) $\Rightarrow$ **no change**.
- If $e = +1$ (under-activated, $t=1, y=0$) $\Rightarrow$ **increase** weights along $\mathbf{x}$.
- If $e = -1$ (over-activated, $t=0, y=1$) $\Rightarrow$ **decrease** weights along $\mathbf{x}$.

---

## 2.4 The Perceptron Convergence Theorem (Novikoff, 1962)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement (KTU-favourite 14-mark theorem):** If the training data is **linearly separable**, the Perceptron Learning Algorithm is **guaranteed to converge** to a separating hyperplane in a **finite number of steps**.

**Formal Novikoff Theorem:** Let $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \ldots, \mathbf{x}^{(K)}$ be a sequence of training vectors with $\Vert \mathbf{x}^{(k)} \Vert \leq R$. Suppose there exists a unit-weight vector $\mathbf{w}^*$ and a margin $\gamma > 0$ such that $\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}^{(k)} \geq \gamma$ for all positively-labelled examples. Then the perceptron algorithm makes at most

$$N \leq \left(\frac{R}{\gamma}\right)^2$$

mistakes before converging.

This **does NOT** say anything about convergence for **non-linearly-separable** data (e.g., the XOR problem) — the algorithm will oscillate forever.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | KTU Significance |
|---|---------|---------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Net input | $z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$ | Core pre-activation |
| 2 | Output (binary step) | $y = \begin{cases} 1, & z \geq 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases}$ | Original Perceptron |
| 3 | Output (signum) | $y = \text{sgn}(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)$ | Symmetric form |
| 4 | Error signal | $e = t - y$ | 3-valued: $-1, 0, +1$ |
| 5 | Weight update | $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \eta \, e \, \mathbf{x}$ | Rosenblatt's rule |
| 6 | Bias update | $b \leftarrow b + \eta \, e$ | Same as weight on $x_0 = 1$ |
| 7 | Decision boundary | $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | A hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 8 | Margin of separation | $\gamma = \min_{k} \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + b^* \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w}^* \Vert}$ | Used in Novikoff bound |
| 9 | Novikoff bound | $N_{\text{mistakes}} \leq (R/\gamma)^2$ | Convergence guarantee |
| 10 | Perceptron loss | $L_{\text{perc}} = \max(0, -t \cdot \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x})$ | Hinge-like surrogate |
| 11 | Delta rule (LMS) | $\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta (t - y) \mathbf{x}$ | Gradient-based analogue |
| 12 | Perceptron as linear classifier | Decision region = open half-space | Limitation: no XOR |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Perceptron forms the conceptual core of every modern neural network. The **multi-layer perceptron (MLP)** stacks perceptrons in layers with differentiable activations (sigmoid, ReLU) and uses **backpropagation** (gradient descent + chain rule) instead of the discrete Rosenblatt rule. Understanding the Perceptron is therefore a prerequisite for understanding CNNs, RNNs, and Transformers at their lowest mathematical level.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->
