## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Layer RBF Network Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[INPUT LAYER]
        direction TB
        I1((X1))
        I2((X2))
        I3((X3))
    end

    subgraph HD[HIDDEN LAYER - RBF NEURONS]
        direction TB
        H1{{H1 phi}}
        H2{{H2 phi}}
        H3{{H3 phi}}
        H4{{H4 phi}}
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        direction TB
        O1((Y1))
        O2((Y2))
    end

    I1 --> H1
    I1 --> H2
    I1 --> H3
    I1 --> H4
    I2 --> H1
    I2 --> H2
    I2 --> H3
    I2 --> H4
    I3 --> H1
    I3 --> H2
    I3 --> H3
    I3 --> H4

    H1 --> O1
    H1 --> O2
    H2 --> O1
    H2 --> O2
    H3 --> O1
    H3 --> O2
    H4 --> O1
    H4 --> O2

    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef hidden fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    class I1,I2,I3 input
    class H1,H2,H3,H4 hidden
    class O1,O2 output
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows in the **Input → Hidden** connections represent **distance computations** (not weighted sums as in MLPs). Each hidden neuron computes $\phi(\Vert x - c_i \Vert)$ independently. The **Hidden → Output** connections are the **learnable linear weights** $w_i$.

### 4.2 Two-Stage Training Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Training Data X, Y] --> B{STAGE 1: Unsupervised}
    B --> B1[Step 1: Initialize K cluster centers]
    B1 --> B2[Step 2: Apply K-Means to find optimal centers c_i]
    B2 --> B3[Step 3: Compute width sigma from pairwise distances]
    B3 --> C[Fixed: Hidden Layer Parameters]
    C --> D{STAGE 2: Supervised}
    D --> D1[Step 4: Compute interpolation matrix Phi]
    D1 --> D2[Step 5: Solve normal equations for w]
    D2 --> D3[Step 6: Apply regularization if needed]
    D3 --> E[Trained RBF Network]
    E --> F[Inference: Predict on new data]

    classDef stage fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    class B,D stage
    class A,E,F data
    class B1,B2,B3,D1,D2,D3 result
```

### 4.3 Comparison: RBF vs. MLP Decision Boundaries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MLP[MLP Decision Boundary]
        M1[Global Hyperplane] --> M2[Linear in feature space]
    end
    subgraph RBN[RBF Network Decision Boundary]
        R1[Multiple Gaussian bumps] --> R2[Non-linear closed forms]
    end
    MLP -.->|Contrast| RBN
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** MLPs carve the input space with **global linear hyperplanes** (stacked to form non-linear regions), whereas RBF networks place **localized Gaussian "bumps"** whose superposition forms smooth, closed-form decision regions.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Radial Basis Function Network. List any two commonly used radial basis functions.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Radial Basis Function (RBF) Network is a type of feedforward artificial neural network that uses **radial basis functions** as activation functions in its hidden layer. The output of the network is a **linear combination** of these radially symmetric functions, each centered at a different point in the input space. Mathematically:

$$y(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \, \phi(\Vert x - c_i \Vert)$$

Two commonly used radial basis functions are:
1. **Gaussian Function:** $\phi(r) = e^{-r^2/\sigma^2}$ — smooth, localized, most widely used.
2. **Multiquadric Function:** $\phi(r) = \sqrt{r^2 + \sigma^2}$ — used in scattered data interpolation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5) + Listing two functions with formulas (1.5).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model]**
*Explain the role of the "center" and "width" parameters in a Gaussian RBF neuron.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In a Gaussian RBF neuron, the output is computed as:

$$\phi(x) = e^{-\Vert x - c \Vert^2 / 2\sigma^2}$$

- **Center ($c$):** This is the point in the input space where the neuron gives its **maximum response of 1**. It represents a "prototypical" input pattern. The neuron is most sensitive to inputs near $c$ and the response falls off symmetrically as the input moves away.
- **Width ($\sigma$):** This parameter controls the **spread** of the receptive field. A **small $\sigma$** produces a narrow, highly localized response (the neuron fires only for inputs very close to $c$). A **large $\sigma$** produces a broad response covering a wider region. It is typically set as the average distance between centers.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution:** Center explanation (1.5) + Width explanation (1.5).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the architecture of a Radial Basis Function Network with a neat diagram. Compare it with a Multi-Layer Perceptron. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **two-stage training procedure** of an RBF network in detail. Discuss how K-Means clustering is used to determine the centers. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Architecture of RBF Network (4 Marks):**

An RBF network has a **strictly three-layer** feedforward architecture:

1. **Input Layer:** Distributes the input vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to the hidden layer. No computation, no weights.

2. **Hidden Layer:** Comprises $N$ neurons, each computing a radial basis function:

$$h_i(x) = \phi(\Vert x - c_i \Vert) = e^{-\Vert x - c_i \Vert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2}$$

where $c_i$ is the center and $\sigma_i$ is the width of the $i$-th neuron.

3. **Output Layer:** Produces a linear combination:

$$y_k(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_{ik} h_i(x) + b_k$$

**Diagram (to be drawn by student):**

A three-layer feedforward structure with $n$ input nodes, $N$ hidden nodes (labeled as radial basis functions), and $m$ output nodes, with full connectivity between adjacent layers only.

**Comparison with MLP (3 Marks):**

| Feature | RBF Network | MLP |
|---------|-------------|-----|
| Hidden Activation | Radial (localized) | Sigmoidal (global) |
| Training | Two-stage (hybrid) | Backpropagation (end-to-end) |
| Hidden Layer Count | Single | One or more |
| Optimization Landscape | Convex in weights | Non-convex, prone to local minima |
| Universal Approximation | Yes | Yes |
| Interpretability | Centers are interpretable | Weights harder to interpret |

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Stage 1 — Unsupervised Center Selection (3.5 Marks):**

The hidden layer centers $c_i$ are determined without using target labels. The most common method is **K-Means Clustering**:

1. Initialize $N$ cluster centers $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_N$ randomly from the training data.
2. **Assignment Step:** For each data point $x_j$, assign it to the nearest center:

$$S_i^{(t)} = \{x_j : \Vert x_j - c_i^{(t)} \Vert^2 \leq \Vert x_j - c_l^{(t)} \Vert^2 \; \forall l\}$$

3. **Update Step:** Recompute each center as the mean of its assigned points:

$$c_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert S_i^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_j \in S_i^{(t)}} x_j$$

4. Iterate until convergence. The objective minimized is:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{x_j \in S_i} \Vert x_j - c_i \Vert^2$$

After convergence, the width $\sigma$ is set heuristically:

$$\sigma = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \min_{j \neq i} \Vert c_i - c_j \Vert$$

**Stage 2 — Supervised Weight Learning (3.5 Marks):**

With centers fixed, compute the **interpolation matrix** $\Phi$ where $\Phi_{ji} = \phi(\Vert x_j - c_i \Vert)$. The optimal weights minimizing squared error are:

$$w = (\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^T y$$

To handle ill-conditioning, **regularization** is added:

$$w = (\Phi^T \Phi + \lambda I)^{-1} \Phi^T y$$

This is a **closed-form solution** requiring no iterative search, and the problem is **convex**, guaranteeing a **global optimum**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse the **two training stages** and mistakenly apply backpropagation through the entire RBF network. Remember: Stage 1 is **unsupervised** (centers via K-Means), Stage 2 is **linear least-squares** (weights). No gradient descent through the hidden layer is needed!

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Jun 2025 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the closed-form solution for the output weights of an RBF network. Why is regularization often necessary? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how an RBF network can be used for **function approximation**. Show how it differs from polynomial approximation. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

Given $P$ training samples $(x_j, y_j)$ and $N$ fixed centers $c_i$, the network output is:

$$\hat{y}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \phi_{ji}, \quad \text{where } \phi_{ji} = \phi(\Vert x_j - c_i \Vert)$$

Define the **squared error objective**:

$$E(w) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{P} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \phi_{ji} - y_j \right)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \Phi w - y \Vert^2$$

Differentiating with respect to $w$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w} = \Phi^T (\Phi w - y) = 0$$

$$\Phi^T \Phi \, w = \Phi^T y$$

When $\Phi^T \Phi$ is non-singular:

$$w = (\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^T y = \Phi^+ y$$

**Need for Regularization (2 Marks):**

Regularization becomes necessary when:
- $N$ is **large** relative to $P$ (over-parameterization, overfitting).
- $\Phi^T \Phi$ is **ill-conditioned** (e.g., two centers are very close, causing near-collinear columns).
- We wish to **improve generalization** on unseen data.

The regularized objective is:

$$E_\lambda(w) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \Phi w - y \Vert^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2$$

Differentiating and solving:

$$w = (\Phi^T \Phi + \lambda I)^{-1} \Phi^T y$$

The added $\lambda I$ ensures the matrix is always invertible and **shrinks** large weights, improving numerical stability and generalization.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Function Approximation Example (4 Marks):**

Suppose we wish to approximate the function $f(x) = \sin(2\pi x)$ on $[0, 1]$ using an RBF network with $N = 5$ Gaussian neurons.

- **Step 1:** Place 5 centers uniformly: $c_1 = 0.1, c_2 = 0.3, c_3 = 0.5, c_4 = 0.7, c_5 = 0.9$.
- **Step 2:** Set $\sigma = 0.15$ (heuristically based on inter-center distance).
- **Step 3:** For a training input $x_j$, compute:

$$h_i(x_j) = e^{-(x_j - c_i)^2 / (2 \cdot 0.15^2)}$$

- **Step 4:** Approximate as:

$$\hat{f}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i h_i(x)$$

The weights $w_i$ are learned by least squares from sample points $(x_j, \sin(2\pi x_j))$.

The result is a **smooth, bell-shaped superposition** that closely matches $\sin(2\pi x)$ with as few as 5–10 Gaussians.

**Comparison with Polynomial Approximation (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | RBF Approximation | Polynomial Approximation |
|--------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Basis Functions | Localized (Gaussian bumps) | Global (powers of $x$) |
| Convergence | Local refinement possible | Global — affected by entire domain |
| Runge's Phenomenon | Less prone | Highly prone (oscillations at edges) |
| Number of Parameters | Scales with complexity locally | Must increase polynomial degree globally |
| Extrapolation | Poor far from training data | Often unstable |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In exam answers, students frequently:
> 1. Forget to specify the **distance metric** (always Euclidean unless stated).
> 2. Use **Sigmoid** instead of **Gaussian** as the RBF (these are different!).
> 3. Forget the $\sigma$ parameter or set it incorrectly (it must be **positive**).
> 4. Confuse the **bias** of the RBF network (added at the output layer, not the hidden layer).
> 5. Omit the **K-Means justification** when describing center selection.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Radial Basis Function (RBF)**

- [x] **RBF = Radial Basis Function** — depends only on distance $\Vert x - c \Vert$, not on direction.
- [x] **Gaussian RBF** is the most common: $\phi(r) = e^{-r^2/\sigma^2}$, peak value **1** at center.
- [x] **Width $\sigma$** controls receptive field spread; larger $\sigma$ = wider coverage.
- [x] **RBF Network has 3 layers:** Input (pass-through), Hidden (non-linear RBF), Output (linear).
- [x] **Two-stage training:** Stage 1 = K-Means (unsupervised, finds centers); Stage 2 = Least Squares (finds weights).
- [x] **Closed-form weight solution:** $w = (\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^T y$ — no backpropagation needed.
- [x] **Regularized form:** $w = (\Phi^T \Phi + \lambda I)^{-1} \Phi^T y$ — prevents overfitting and singularity.
- [x] **Interpolation matrix $\Phi$** has entries $\Phi_{ji} = \phi(\Vert x_j - c_i \Vert)$.
- [x] **Universal Approximation Theorem** guarantees RBF networks can approximate any continuous function.
- [x] **Advantage over MLP:** Convex optimization for weights, faster training, localized learning.
- [x] **Disadvantage:** Poor extrapolation; curse of dimensionality in high-dimensional input spaces.
- [x] **Cover's Theorem** justifies the hidden layer: non-linearly separable problems become linearly separable in higher-dimensional feature spaces produced by RBFs.
- [x] **Key KTU value:** Half-activation radius of Gaussian = $\sigma \sqrt{\ln 2} \approx 0.833 \sigma$.
